The study finds that superradiance is governed by a double-gate mechanism requiring both a local horizon condition and an outer propagation condition to be satisfied simultaneously, and that increasing the external magnetic field strength suppresses and ultimately closes the superradiant window at a critical field value. Near the propagation threshold, the amplification coefficient is shown to vanish linearly with the outer wave number.
items.citation: Hai Huang, Xudong Sun, Juhua Chen. Massless scalar scattering by Kerr-Bertotti-Robinson black holes:transparent-end channels and superradiance. arXiv. 2026

items.title: BayesPO: Bayesian Prompt Optimization via Parallel-Tempered Gradient-Guided Discrete MCMC
items.summary: This paper investigates prompt optimization for large language models through a Bayesian lens, framing the problem as posterior sampling over discrete prompt tokens rather than heuristic search. The authors define a posterior distribution combining a task likelihood term that rewards prompts fitting input-output examples with a language-model prior favoring fluent instructions, converting prompt optimization into an energy-based sampling problem. Their framework, BayesPO, employs a Metropolis-Hastings corrected Gibbs-with-Langevin proposal adapted for non-weight-tied LLM embeddings, augmented with parallel tempering to escape local optima in rugged energy landscapes. Experiments demonstrate that the approach discovers semantically meaningful prompts, that parallel tempering aids exploration in challenging tasks, and that post-optimizing existing prompts on instruction-induction benchmarks yields improved average accuracy. The authors also candidly identify key limitations, namely overfitting on small optimization sets and high computational cost, positioning the method as a principled post-optimization tool with promise for future probabilistic prompt optimization research.
items.citation: Junjie Zhou, Zhijian Ou. BayesPO: Bayesian Prompt Optimization via Parallel-Tempered Gradient-Guided Discrete MCMC. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Diagnostic value of cystatin C in acute kidney injury among patients with sepsis: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
items.summary: This systematic review and meta-analysis evaluated the diagnostic accuracy of cystatin C (CysC) for early detection of acute kidney injury in patients with sepsis. The authors systematically retrieved relevant studies from electronic databases, assessed their quality using a standardized diagnostic accuracy tool, and performed a random-effects meta-analysis combined with summary receiver operating characteristic curve analysis. Across the included studies, cystatin C demonstrated good overall diagnostic performance for sepsis-associated acute kidney injury, with strong sensitivity, specificity, and area under the curve values. Subgroup analyses indicated that studies applying Sepsis 3.0 diagnostic criteria showed improved specificity and diagnostic odds ratios with less heterogeneity, and combining cystatin C with other biomarkers further enhanced diagnostic accuracy. The authors conclude that cystatin C is a promising biomarker for early clinical identification of sepsis-associated acute kidney injury, though they acknowledge residual heterogeneity among the included studies.
items.citation: Cheng L, Zhang B, Hu X et al.. Diagnostic value of cystatin C in acute kidney injury among patients with sepsis: a systematic review and meta-analysis.. Frontiers in medicine. 2026

items.title: Pancreatic Stone Protein as an Emerging Biomarker in Paediatric Nosocomial and Postoperative Sepsis: An Integrative Review of Diagnostic and Prognostic Performance.
items.summary: This narrative review examined pancreatic stone protein (PSP) as a diagnostic and prognostic biomarker for sepsis in paediatric patients, particularly in nosocomial and postoperative settings where distinguishing infectious from sterile inflammation is difficult using conventional markers like C-reactive protein and procalcitonin. The authors conducted a structured literature search across two major databases, screening studies published over a recent multi-year period and ultimately analysing those meeting inclusion criteria focused on nosocomial infections, paediatric surgery, and PSP. The reviewed evidence indicates that PSP levels rise significantly in children with sepsis compared to those with non-infectious systemic inflammation, and elevated levels correlate with bacteraemia, organ dysfunction, and mortality. In multiple paediatric cohorts, PSP showed diagnostic performance comparable to or exceeding traditional biomarkers, with particularly encouraging sensitivity and specificity in neonatal and other high-risk populations. The authors conclude that PSP is a promising early biomarker for paediatric sepsis identification and risk stratification, though inter-study variability underscores the need for larger prospective multicentre trials and integration of PSP into multimarker diagnostic frameworks.
items.citation: Ramírez Quintana A, Gaman EL, Badea M et al.. Pancreatic Stone Protein as an Emerging Biomarker in Paediatric Nosocomial and Postoperative Sepsis: An Integrative Review of Diagnostic and Prognostic Performance.. International journal of molecular sciences. 2026

items.title: Early diagnostic accuracy of immature platelet fraction for sepsis: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
items.summary: This systematic review and meta-analysis examined whether the immature platelet fraction — a readily available measure of bone marrow thrombopoietic activity — could serve as an early diagnostic biomarker for sepsis. The authors searched major databases and identified studies meeting inclusion criteria, then pooled data to calculate sensitivity, specificity, diagnostic odds ratio, and summary receiver operating characteristic curves, including a subgroup analysis restricted to adult populations. The meta-analysis found that the immature platelet fraction demonstrated only moderate diagnostic accuracy for sepsis, with pooled sensitivity and specificity both around the low-to-mid seventies and a combined diagnostic odds ratio indicating limited but statistically meaningful discriminatory ability. Moderate heterogeneity was observed across the included studies, and the overall evidence base was restricted to a small number of studies spanning less than a decade. The authors conclude that while the immature platelet fraction may serve as a complementary biomarker alongside other diagnostic tools, the limited and methodologically heterogeneous evidence base warrants cautious interpretation and calls for larger, standardized prospective studies to clarify its clinical utility.
items.citation: Wiranata S, Manuaba IBAP, Supadmanaba IGP et al.. Early diagnostic accuracy of immature platelet fraction for sepsis: a systematic review and meta-analysis.. Hematology, transfusion and cell therapy. 2026

items.title: Diagnostic performance of interleukin-27 and C-reactive protein in neonatal sepsis: an updated systematic review and meta-analysis.
items.summary: This systematic review and meta-analysis evaluated and compared the diagnostic accuracy of interleukin-27 (IL-27) and C-reactive protein (CRP) as biomarkers for neonatal sepsis, addressing inconsistencies in prior clinical studies. Following PRISMA guidelines, the authors searched major medical databases spanning nearly three decades and included controlled studies involving neonates, applying pooled sensitivity, specificity, and summary receiver operating characteristic curve analyses. The results showed that IL-27 achieved higher pooled sensitivity and specificity than CRP, and its area under the curve was notably superior, indicating stronger overall diagnostic performance. The authors conclude that IL-27 demonstrates greater diagnostic accuracy than CRP for identifying neonatal sepsis and may serve as a more reliable biomarker to support early and accurate clinical diagnosis.
items.citation: Liu W, Zhang Y, Liu W et al.. Diagnostic performance of interleukin-27 and C-reactive protein in neonatal sepsis: an updated systematic review and meta-analysis.. Frontiers in pediatrics. 2026

items.title: Aligning neonatal sepsis triage diagnostics with WHO target product profiles in low- and middle-income countries.
items.summary: This paper examined the landscape of diagnostic approaches for neonatal sepsis in low- and middle-income countries, where the condition remains a major driver of infant death yet effective diagnostics are lacking. The authors synthesized evidence across a range of current and emerging strategies, including clinical algorithms, host-response biomarkers, haematological parameters, continuous monitoring, and artificial intelligence-based digital platforms, evaluating each against the WHO Target Product Profile for neonatal sepsis triage. They assessed diagnostic performance alongside operational feasibility and suitability for resource-constrained and non-hospital settings. The analysis identified several promising technologies, including more accurate host-response biomarkers and affordable tools capable of enhancing predictive algorithms, that could better meet WHO criteria than existing approaches. The authors conclude that integrating point-of-care testing, routine clinical data, and digital decision support into a unified, target-product-profile-aligned strategy offers the most viable path toward earlier detection, more rational antibiotic use, and reduced neonatal mortality in these settings.
items.citation: Gleeson B, Blumel B, Ibarz Pavon AB et al.. Aligning neonatal sepsis triage diagnostics with WHO target product profiles in low- and middle-income countries.. BMJ global health. 2026

items.title: Calprotectin as a Diagnostic Marker for Lower Respiratory Tract Infection and Sepsis in the Emergency Department.
items.summary: This study investigated whether calprotectin, a neutrophil activation marker measured in plasma, could serve as a useful diagnostic biomarker for sepsis and for identifying the source of infection in emergency department patients. Researchers prospectively enrolled patients presenting with suspected sepsis to a university hospital emergency department and measured calprotectin levels at admission, comparing its diagnostic performance alone and in combination with C-reactive protein. Calprotectin showed modest ability to distinguish sepsis from non-infectious systemic inflammatory response syndrome, but was not an independent predictor when combined with C-reactive protein in multivariable analysis. However, calprotectin demonstrated notable utility in differentiating lower respiratory tract infections from both upper respiratory tract infections and urinary tract infections, and adding calprotectin to C-reactive protein significantly improved discriminatory performance for these comparisons. The authors conclude that calprotectin may complement existing markers in identifying lower respiratory tract infections among emergency department patients with sepsis, though further research is needed to establish its value within standard clinical assessment.
items.citation: Wollmer M, Wessman T, Melander O et al.. Calprotectin as a Diagnostic Marker for Lower Respiratory Tract Infection and Sepsis in the Emergency Department.. Open forum infectious diseases. 2026

items.title: Lipid metabolism in homeostasis and disease
items.summary: This review examines the role of lipid metabolism in maintaining cellular homeostasis and how its dysregulation contributes to a broad range of diseases. The authors synthesize the research history of lipid metabolism and focus particularly on how immune cells—including macrophages, T cells, B cells, and neutrophils—depend on lipid metabolites for proliferation, differentiation, and activation in both innate and adaptive immunity. The review covers lipid transport, signaling pathways, and multilevel regulatory mechanisms spanning genetic, epigenetic, posttranscriptional, and posttranslational processes. These principles are applied to understanding diseases such as cancer, cardiovascular disease, autoimmune disorders, neurodegeneration, and metabolic conditions. The authors conclude by surveying therapeutic strategies targeting lipid metabolism, ongoing clinical efforts, and emerging opportunities including lipid-derived biomarkers and novel treatment approaches.
items.citation: Zhili Li, Wende Deng, Lanxuan Yang et al.. Lipid metabolism in homeostasis and disease. Signal Transduction and Targeted Therapy. 2026

items.title: Surviving Sepsis Campaign: International Guidelines for Management of Sepsis and Septic Shock 2026
items.summary: This paper presents the 2026 Surviving Sepsis Campaign international clinical practice guidelines for the management of sepsis and septic shock in adults. The guidelines were developed by a large, geographically diverse committee organized into clinical subgroups covering screening, infection management, hemodynamics, respiratory support, adjunctive therapies, and goals of care, with input from a patient and family advisory panel. Evidence synthesis followed the GRADE methodology, incorporating the Evidence to Decision framework to weigh balance of effects, certainty of evidence, patient values, resource availability, equity, and cost-effectiveness across numerous structured clinical questions. The resulting guidelines provide a comprehensive set of strong and conditional recommendations spanning early identification, antimicrobial stewardship, diagnostic strategies, and treatment de-escalation across hospital, prehospital, and post-hospital settings. The guidelines emphasize that early recognition and prompt, evidence-based intervention are critical to improving outcomes, while acknowledging that conditional recommendations must be adapted to local clinical context and resource availability.
items.citation: Hallie C. Prescott, Massimo Antonelli, Waleed Alhazzani et al.. Surviving Sepsis Campaign: International Guidelines for Management of Sepsis and Septic Shock 2026. Critical Care Medicine. 2026

items.title: Microbiota in cancer: current understandings and future perspectives
items.summary: This review paper examines the relationship between the microbiota and cancer, covering how microbial communities influence carcinogenesis, tumor progression, and patient prognosis. The authors trace the historical development of this research field and analyze the molecular mechanisms through which the microbiota modulates oncogenic signaling pathways, immune responses, and tumor metabolism. The review addresses the dual nature of microbial communities in cancer biology, as they can both promote tumor growth and serve as targets for therapeutic intervention. Particular attention is given to how the microbiota influences responses to immunotherapy, chemotherapy, and radiotherapy, alongside emerging anticancer strategies such as probiotics, antibiotics, and fecal microbiota transplantation. The authors conclude by advocating for personalized, microbiota-targeted approaches in oncology and propose a comprehensive framework to guide future research and clinical applications in this field.
items.citation: Yanxi Yao, Yifei Zhu, Keji Chen et al.. Microbiota in cancer: current understandings and future perspectives. Signal Transduction and Targeted Therapy. 2026

items.title: Insulin Resistance and Inflammation
items.summary: This paper examined the bidirectional relationship between insulin resistance and inflammation, with a focus on how each condition perpetuates and amplifies the other in the context of cardiometabolic and vascular disease. The authors conducted a conceptual narrative review synthesizing evidence across multiple levels of biological organization, from insulin receptor signaling and intracellular stress pathways to tissue dysfunction and systemic inflammatory responses. They drew explicit distinctions between mechanistic and experimental evidence on one hand and human observational data on the other, framing causal claims accordingly. The central finding is that intracellular stress programs—including oxidative, endoplasmic reticulum, inflammatory, and fibrotic pathways—become maladaptive under conditions of chronic nutrient excess and persistent inflammation, creating self-reinforcing loops that drive endothelial dysfunction and atherogenesis. The authors propose an integrative framework identifying points of convergence between insulin resistance and inflammation that could guide future mechanistic research and translational hypothesis testing.
items.citation: Evgenii Gusev, Alexey Sarapultsev, Yulia A. Zhuravleva. Insulin Resistance and Inflammation. International Journal of Molecular Sciences. 2026

items.title: Artificial Intelligence for Exosomal Biomarker Discovery for Cardiovascular Diseases: Multi-Omics Integration, Reproducibility, and Translational Prospects
items.summary: This review examines the use of artificial intelligence to discover exosomal and extracellular vesicle biomarkers for cardiovascular diseases, addressing the challenge that these vesicle datasets are highly dimensional, heterogeneous, and plagued by pre-analytic variability and non-standardized workflows that limit reproducibility. The authors conducted a comprehensive comparative analysis of AI methodologies—including machine learning ensemble methods, deep learning, graph neural networks, and evolutionary neural networks—evaluating their capacity to integrate multi-omics profiles with clinical metadata for feature selection, disease subtyping, and interpretable model development. Ensemble approaches such as Random Forest and gradient boosting showed the most consistent classification performance across studies, while graph neural networks showed promise for pathway integration but require larger validation cohorts, and evolutionary neural networks faced interpretability barriers for clinical use. The review finds that current evidence for extracellular vesicle biomarkers in conditions such as acute myocardial infarction and heart failure remains largely exploratory, based on small cohorts with limited external validation, and falls short of the benchmarks needed for clinical implementation. The authors propose a practical framework specifying that translational readiness requires direct comparison with established standards like high-sensitivity troponin, multicenter validation using harmonized protocols, locked-in assay designs, and transparent AI reporting practices.
items.citation: Rasit Dinc, Nurittin Ardıç. Artificial Intelligence for Exosomal Biomarker Discovery for Cardiovascular Diseases: Multi-Omics Integration, Reproducibility, and Translational Prospects. Cells. 2026

items.title: Sepsis
items.citation: Mervyn Singer, Derek C Angus, Djillali Annane et al.. Sepsis. The Lancet. 2026

items.title: Behaviour-Conditioned Neural Processes for Adaptive Residential Short-Term Load Forecasting
items.summary: This paper investigates residential short-term load forecasting, a problem complicated by the heterogeneous and behaviorally diverse nature of household electricity consumption. The authors propose a behaviour-conditioned Attentive Neural Process framework that embeds inferred behavioural structure directly into the forecasting mechanism rather than treating it as an external grouping signal. The model uses a discrete latent variable to represent behavioural patterns, derived from clustering-based weak supervision during training but inferred purely from context at test time, alongside a continuous latent variable to capture shared functional uncertainty across diverse load profiles. Evaluated on a smart grid dataset with user-disjoint splits and variable context lengths, the proposed approach improves both point forecast accuracy and probabilistic calibration over a label-agnostic baseline, with the greatest gains occurring under limited context availability. The results demonstrate that incorporating inferred behavioural structure within a single probabilistic model enables more accurate and uncertainty-aware forecasting across heterogeneous households and forecast horizons.
items.citation: Ramin Soleimani, Andrea Visentin, Dirk Pesch. Behaviour-Conditioned Neural Processes for Adaptive Residential Short-Term Load Forecasting. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Euclid: Calibrating distances from surface brightness fluctuations with Early Release Observations of the Fornax cluster
items.summary: This paper presents the first application of the surface brightness fluctuations (SBF) distance measurement technique to data from the Euclid space mission, using pixel-to-pixel flux variations in galaxy images to derive redshift-independent distances. The authors applied the FAST-SBF code to galaxies in Euclid's Early Release Observations of the Fornax galaxy cluster, measuring stellar fluctuation amplitudes in the visible band despite the data reduction pipeline not being optimized for this purpose. A calibration of the absolute fluctuation magnitude as a function of galaxy color was established using Fornax galaxies, and then validated on galaxies in two additional observed fields, namely the Perseus cluster and dwarf satellite candidates around another galaxy. The derived distances show reasonable to good agreement with literature values, particularly when the pipeline's own quality indicators flag measurements as robust. The study also compared results against simple stellar population models, highlighting the additional potential of SBF analyses with Euclid for probing the stellar population properties of galaxies beyond distance measurement alone.
items.citation: R. Habas, M. Cantiello, G. Riccio et al.. Euclid: Calibrating distances from surface brightness fluctuations with Early Release Observations of the Fornax cluster. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Adaptive Fault Injection Planning for Multi-Layer Self-Healing AI Infrastructure
items.summary: This paper investigates how faults propagate across multiple layers of GPU-accelerator infrastructure used in hyperscale computing environments, identifying a critical gap in conventional testing approaches that evaluate each layer in isolation. The authors developed ADA-ST, an adaptive fault-injection methodology that constructs weighted fault-propagation graphs spanning hardware, firmware, management software, and orchestration layers, then uses iterative scenario selection to maximize coverage of cross-layer fault pathways. Drawing on a large corpus of real-world repair tickets from a production platform over multiple years, the study demonstrates that existing static test campaigns exercise only a small fraction of cross-layer fault-propagation paths, leaving the vast majority of the attack surface untested. ADA-ST achieves full edge coverage across all three studied platforms within a small number of iterations, and a knowledge-transfer mechanism called FLAM successfully ports propagation insights across hardware generations with high fidelity. Physical validation on the newest platform confirmed the existence of previously unexercised cross-layer vulnerabilities, including telemetry blind spots, detection gaps based on signal absence, multi-signal correlation failures, and unverified trust assumptions at a critical layer boundary.
items.citation: Saurabh Kulkarni, Yuxin Yang, Rohan Kulkarni et al.. Adaptive Fault Injection Planning for Multi-Layer Self-Healing AI Infrastructure. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Radiopurity material assays and radiation exposure projections for superconducting qubit measurements at SNOLAB
items.summary: This paper investigates the challenge that ionizing radiation poses to superconducting qubit coherence and quantum error correction, and prepares for the first underground operation of such devices at a dedicated cryogenic facility deep within a mine. The authors conducted an extensive radioassay campaign to characterize the radioactivity levels of materials used in their quantum device assembly. These measured radioactivity levels were then fed into detailed Monte Carlo simulations using Geant4 to estimate the rates and spectral characteristics of energy deposits from radiogenic sources within the facility. The simulations identify the dominant particle interaction types contributing to the projected radiation background. Additionally, the paper demonstrates how crystal dynamics simulations through a solid-state physics extension for Geant4 can help develop strategies to mitigate the effects of high-energy particle impacts on quantum devices.
items.citation: Y. Ahmed, B. Binoy, R. Bunker et al.. Radiopurity material assays and radiation exposure projections for superconducting qubit measurements at SNOLAB. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Quantum Fisher information of magnetic quantum phase transition on Kondo lattice
items.summary: This paper investigates how quantum information tools can characterize quantum phase transitions in strongly correlated electron systems, specifically strange metals and heavy fermion materials. The authors study multipartite entanglement across the quantum phase transition of a Kondo lattice model using the quantum Fisher information as their primary diagnostic. They demonstrate that the quantum Fisher information associated with spin components transverse to the magnetic order parameter serves as a marker for the destruction of heavy quasiparticles in the Kondo-destroyed magnetically ordered phase, with the underlying physics clarified through analysis of an antiferromagnetic Heisenberg model. The authors propose that these theoretical predictions could be tested experimentally via both unpolarized and polarized inelastic neutron scattering measurements in the ordered region of the heavy fermion phase diagram. The findings highlight how judiciously chosen operators can simultaneously witness multipartite entanglement in distinct sectors and illuminate the broader physics across different regions of a complex quantum phase diagram.
items.citation: Yuan Fang, Lei Chen, Mounica Mahankali et al.. Quantum Fisher information of magnetic quantum phase transition on Kondo lattice. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Eccentricity as a Magnifying Glass: Precision Population Inference Enabled by Eccentric Neutron Star--Black Hole Mergers
items.summary: This paper investigates how orbital eccentricity in neutron star–black hole binary mergers can improve our ability to infer the formation histories of compact binary systems. Because eccentric mergers yield substantially sharper measurements of intrinsic parameters such as component masses and spins compared to circular mergers, the authors examined how this precision enhancement propagates to population-level inference with current gravitational-wave observatories. They found that improved effective spin measurements allow confident identification of spin-orbit misaligned events at a notably high rate among eccentric detections, while enhanced mass measurements better constrain neutron star mass distributions and metallicity-dependent black hole mass distributions. The study also demonstrates that the population-level eccentricity distribution predicted for dynamically formed systems could be tested by the end of the fifth LIGO-Virgo-KAGRA observing run. Together, these results suggest that even a modest number of eccentric neutron star–black hole detections could meaningfully discriminate between isolated binary evolution and dynamical formation channels.
items.citation: Alberto Salvarese, Hsin-Yu Chen, Aaron Zimmerman et al.. Eccentricity as a Magnifying Glass: Precision Population Inference Enabled by Eccentric Neutron Star--Black Hole Mergers. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Rapid diagnostics innovations for urinary tract infections using molecular biology, artificial intelligence and antimicrobial resistance surveillance: a comprehensive review.
items.summary: This review examines the current landscape and emerging innovations in diagnostic approaches for urinary tract infections, a globally prevalent bacterial infection complicated by high recurrence, microbial diversity, biofilm formation, and rapidly evolving antimicrobial resistance. The authors surveyed molecular diagnostic technologies including nucleic acid amplification, biosensor-based platforms, microfluidic lab-on-chip systems, and omics-based approaches, evaluating how these overcome the limitations of traditional culture-based methods that suffer from slow turnaround times and insufficient characterization of resistance and virulence factors. A central focus is the integration of these molecular tools with artificial intelligence and machine learning models to enable rapid, sensitive, and pathogen-specific detection alongside antibiotic susceptibility testing. The review highlights the translational potential of these combined approaches for improving diagnostic precision, guiding targeted antimicrobial therapy, and supporting broader antimicrobial resistance surveillance and stewardship. Challenges related to medical ethics, regulatory frameworks, and clinical implementation are also addressed, with the authors concluding that next-generation AI-assisted diagnostics represent a transformative paradigm for managing antibiotic resistance in urinary tract infections.
items.citation: Goyal S, Sharma P, Aggarwal D et al.. Rapid diagnostics innovations for urinary tract infections using molecular biology, artificial intelligence and antimicrobial resistance surveillance: a comprehensive review.. Molecular biology reports. 2026

items.title: Novel Rapid Phenotypic Susceptibility Testing Techniques That Enhance Antimicrobial Stewardship and Clinical Decision-Making.
items.summary: This review paper examined emerging rapid phenotypic antimicrobial susceptibility testing technologies and their role in addressing the global challenge of antimicrobial resistance. The authors surveyed the fundamentals of phenotypic testing approaches, with particular attention to growth-based methods, and reviewed evidence on how these technologies affect clinical decision-making and antimicrobial stewardship programs. Rapid phenotypic testing delivers actionable susceptibility results substantially faster than conventional methods, which is especially critical in severe infections such as sepsis where delays in appropriate therapy worsen patient outcomes. The review also acknowledged implementation barriers, including variability in test performance, absence of standardized protocols, and cost-benefit concerns that complicate widespread adoption. Overall, the authors conclude that rapid phenotypic susceptibility testing represents an important advance in closing diagnostic gaps and enabling timely, evidence-based antibiotic therapy.
items.citation: Glover JS, Humphries R. Novel Rapid Phenotypic Susceptibility Testing Techniques That Enhance Antimicrobial Stewardship and Clinical Decision-Making.. Clinical chemistry. 2026

items.title: Rapid Detection of Pathogens and Resistance Genes in Positive Blood Cultures Using BioFire<sup>®</sup> FilmArray BCID2.
items.summary: This study evaluated the performance of a multiplex molecular diagnostic panel for detecting pathogens and antimicrobial resistance genes directly from positive blood cultures, comparing it to conventional culture-based methods including mass spectrometry and automated susceptibility testing. Fifty positive blood cultures from a university hospital were analyzed in parallel using both approaches, with resistance gene findings further confirmed by polymerase chain reaction. The panel demonstrated strong overall agreement with conventional methods, achieving complete concordance for certain key organisms such as *Escherichia coli* and *Pseudomonas aeruginosa*, while *Klebsiella pneumoniae* showed slightly lower but acceptable agreement. Resistance genes detected molecularly correlated well with phenotypic susceptibility results, and the panel identified a notably high proportion of polymicrobial infections. The authors conclude that the panel is a rapid and reliable complement to conventional diagnostics for bloodstream infections, though its interpretation requires clinical context, particularly when coagulase-negative staphylococci are detected.
items.citation: Çinar C, Çayci YT, Atakcan Z et al.. Rapid Detection of Pathogens and Resistance Genes in Positive Blood Cultures Using BioFire<sup>®</sup> FilmArray BCID2.. Polish journal of microbiology. 2026

items.title: When Faster Diagnosis Is Not Enough: Bringing Diagnostic Stewardship into Antimicrobial Stewardship.
items.citation: Mezzadri L, Bonfanti P. When Faster Diagnosis Is Not Enough: Bringing Diagnostic Stewardship into Antimicrobial Stewardship.. Le infezioni in medicina. 2026

items.title: Multiplex mismatch amplification mutation polymerase chain reaction assay for the rapid detection of antimicrobial-resistant determinants in Cutibacterium acnes.
items.summary: This paper addressed the growing clinical concern of antimicrobial resistance in Cutibacterium acnes, a bacterium central to the development of acne vulgaris. The researchers developed a multiplex mismatch amplification mutation assay PCR method designed to simultaneously detect multiple resistance determinants, including mutations in 23S rRNA, 16S rRNA, and GyrA, as well as acquired resistance genes, across a large collection of clinical isolates. The optimized protocol was validated against conventional susceptibility testing methods using several hundred clinical isolates. The assay achieved perfect sensitivity and specificity for identifying the major resistance factors, and the entire procedure could be completed in approximately one hour. The authors conclude that this rapid and accurate tool can support appropriate antimicrobial selection for acne treatment and help curb the emergence of resistant strains.
items.citation: Yoshihara K, Seyama S, Hayashi N et al.. Multiplex mismatch amplification mutation polymerase chain reaction assay for the rapid detection of antimicrobial-resistant determinants in Cutibacterium acnes.. Diagnostic microbiology and infectious disease. 2026

items.title: A review of bloodstream infections-pathogens, pathogenesis, diagnostic strategies, treatment methods-challenges and future aspects.
items.summary: This review examined bloodstream infections, which occur when pathogenic microorganisms enter the circulatory system and can progress to life-threatening conditions including sepsis, septic shock, and multi-organ failure. The authors conducted a comprehensive synthesis of peer-reviewed literature, clinical guidelines, and surveillance reports to evaluate pathogens, host-pathogen interactions, diagnostic approaches, antimicrobial resistance mechanisms, and emerging therapeutic innovations across bacterial, fungal, viral, and parasitic causes. Bacterial pathogens were found to predominate as the primary cause of bloodstream infections, with fungal infections led by Candida species, and the overall burden is compounded by rising antimicrobial resistance, diverse virulence mechanisms, and diagnostic delays. Advances in molecular diagnostics, metagenomic sequencing, biomarker-guided testing, and artificial intelligence-assisted analysis have meaningfully improved speed and accuracy of pathogen detection and clinical decision-making. The authors conclude that integrating next-generation diagnostics, genomic surveillance, antimicrobial stewardship, and precision medicine approaches holds substantial promise for enabling rapid, individualized treatment and ultimately reducing the high morbidity and mortality associated with these infections.
items.venue: European journal of clinical microbiology & infectious diseases : official publication of the European Society of Clinical Microbiology
items.citation: Kumar A, Kumar A, Tyagi A et al.. A review of bloodstream infections-pathogens, pathogenesis, diagnostic strategies, treatment methods-challenges and future aspects.. European journal of clinical microbiology & infectious diseases : official publication of the European Society of Clinical Microbiology. 2026

items.title: Executive Summary: Surviving Sepsis Campaign: International Guidelines for Management of Sepsis and Septic Shock 2026
items.summary: The 2026 Surviving Sepsis Campaign guidelines represent a comprehensive update to international guidance on the identification and management of sepsis and septic shock in adult patients, covering evidence published through mid-2025. The guidelines were developed using GRADE methodology, encompassing screening, antimicrobial therapy, hemodynamic management, respiratory support, adjunctive therapies, and long-term outcomes across a total of statements that include strong recommendations, conditional recommendations, good practice statements, and acknowledgments of insufficient evidence. Notable new recommendations include prehospital sepsis screening and antibiotic administration for patients with prolonged transport times, tailoring anaerobic antimicrobial coverage to individual risk factors, using selective digestive tract decontamination in low-resistance settings, and actively removing fluid after the acute resuscitation phase. Key changes from the 2021 guidelines include updated guidance favoring crystalloids alone over albumin for fluid resuscitation, supporting either invasive or noninvasive blood pressure monitoring, and consolidating antifungal recommendations to generally suggest against empiric antifungal therapy, while several existing recommendations—such as prolonged beta-lactam infusion and antimicrobial de-escalation—were upgraded in strength based on accumulating evidence.
items.citation: Hallie C. Prescott, Massimo Antonelli, Waleed Alhazzani et al.. Executive Summary: Surviving Sepsis Campaign: International Guidelines for Management of Sepsis and Septic Shock 2026. Critical Care Medicine. 2026

items.title: Gut microbiome–driven colorectal cancer via immune, metabolic, neural, and endocrine axes reprogramming
items.summary: This review examines the gut microbiome's role as a causal and mechanistic driver of colorectal cancer, moving beyond established genetic and environmental factors to explore how microbial communities reshape the tumor microenvironment. The authors synthesize molecular, ecological, and translational evidence showing how dysbiosis characterized by enrichment of specific pathobionts and depletion of protective short-chain-fatty-acid-producing bacteria reprograms immune, metabolic, neural, and endocrine networks to promote carcinogenesis. Key mechanisms identified include genotoxin-induced DNA damage from bacterial products such as colibactin and cytolethal distending toxin, epithelial barrier disruption, metabolic rewiring, and chronic inflammation that sustains immune suppression and tumor growth. The review conceptualizes colorectal cancer as a biofilm-structured ecosystem disease in which polymicrobial consortia coordinate barrier breakdown, immune evasion, and metabolic cooperation, with defined mutational signatures now directly linking specific microbial exposures to human cancer genomes. Based on this framework, the authors highlight emerging microbiota-targeted therapeutic strategies—including dietary modulation, bacteriophage therapy, engineered live biotherapeutics, and fecal microbiota transplantation—as avenues for precision prevention and treatment.
items.citation: Jhommara Bautista, Mónica Lamas‐Maceiras, Camila Hidalgo-Tinoco et al.. Gut microbiome–driven colorectal cancer via immune, metabolic, neural, and endocrine axes reprogramming. npj Biofilms and Microbiomes. 2026

items.title: Lipid metabolism in homeostasis and disease
items.summary: This review examines the role of lipid metabolism in maintaining cellular homeostasis and how its dysregulation contributes to a broad range of diseases. The authors synthesize the research history of lipid metabolism, covering lipid functions, transport, and signaling pathways, with particular emphasis on how lipid metabolites regulate immune cell proliferation, differentiation, and activation across innate and adaptive immune cell types including macrophages, T cells, B cells, and neutrophils. The review further explores multilevel regulatory mechanisms—genetic, epigenetic, posttranscriptional, and posttranslational—that govern lipid-driven immune responses. The authors discuss the implications of lipid metabolic dysregulation across diverse pathological contexts including autoimmune disease, cancer, neurodegeneration, cardiovascular disease, aging, and metabolic disorders. The review concludes by surveying therapeutic strategies targeting lipid metabolism, ongoing clinical trial progress, and emerging opportunities such as lipid-derived biomarkers and novel treatment approaches.
items.citation: Zhili Li, Wende Deng, Lanxuan Yang et al.. Lipid metabolism in homeostasis and disease. Signal Transduction and Targeted Therapy. 2026

items.title: Microbiota in cancer: current understandings and future perspectives
items.summary: This review examines the complex and multifaceted relationship between the microbiota and cancer, covering its roles in carcinogenesis, tumor progression, and prognosis. The authors trace the historical development of this research field and analyze the molecular mechanisms through which microbial communities influence oncogenic signaling pathways, immune responses, and tumor metabolism. The review addresses the dual nature of the microbiota as both a potential driver of tumor growth and a target for therapeutic intervention, including its capacity to modulate responses to immunotherapy, chemotherapy, and radiotherapy. Emerging anticancer strategies exploiting the microbiota—such as probiotics, antibiotics, and fecal microbiota transplantation—are discussed alongside approved treatments and ongoing clinical investigations. The authors conclude by advocating for personalized, microbiota-targeted approaches in oncology and propose a comprehensive framework to guide future research and clinical applications in this area.
items.citation: Yanxi Yao, Yifei Zhu, Keji Chen et al.. Microbiota in cancer: current understandings and future perspectives. Signal Transduction and Targeted Therapy. 2026

items.title: Graphene oxide as smart sustainable nanomaterial: a versatile multifunctional material with transformative potential in advanced materials science research
items.summary: This review paper examines graphene oxide, a two-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterial, as a multifunctional platform with broad applicability across energy, environmental, and healthcare domains. The authors survey the synthesis chemistry and property modification strategies that enable graphene oxide's distinctive characteristics, including tunable band gap, high surface area, structural flexibility, and adaptive surface chemistry spanning zero- to three-dimensional configurations. The review covers a wide range of applications, including photocatalytic degradation of pollutants and microplastics, carbon dioxide photoreduction, hydrogen production, battery technologies, and ultrasensitive molecular detection via surface-enhanced Raman scattering and gas sensing. The authors find that graphene oxide demonstrates notable standalone performance across these diverse fields, positioning it as a transformative nanomaterial with substantial real-world and industrial relevance. However, the review also identifies persistent challenges around scalability, long-term stability, and reproducibility, and proposes potential solutions to enable sustainable future deployment.
items.citation: S.N. Thakur, Ayush Badoni, Rupam Sharma et al.. Graphene oxide as smart sustainable nanomaterial: a versatile multifunctional material with transformative potential in advanced materials science research. npj Materials Sustainability. 2026

items.title: A comprehensive review of green synthesis methods and applications of nanoparticles derived from plant extracts and microorganisms
items.summary: This review examines the field of green synthesis, which uses biological organisms such as plants, bacteria, fungi, algae, and yeast as natural reducing and stabilizing agents to produce nanoparticles without relying on toxic chemicals or harsh reaction conditions. The authors surveyed literature from major scientific databases to critically assess advances in plant-mediated and microorganism-assisted nanoparticle fabrication, covering the mechanisms of particle formation, the role of biological constituents in determining size, morphology, and stability, and commonly used characterization techniques. The review evaluates the performance of green-synthesized nanoparticles across biomedical, agricultural, catalytic, and environmental applications, with particular attention to their photocatalytic and pollutant degradation capabilities in the context of environmental remediation. The authors identify persistent challenges including reproducibility, standardization, and scalability that currently limit translation from laboratory-scale studies to industrial practice. The review is intended to provide a structured critical foundation to guide future research aimed at making green nanotechnology practically viable.
items.citation: Shoaeb Mohammad Syed, Shivam Kulkarni, Mayur Patil et al.. A comprehensive review of green synthesis methods and applications of nanoparticles derived from plant extracts and microorganisms. Discover Green Chemistry. 2026

items.title: Rapid structural evolution of neutron-rich silicon isotopes toward N = 28
items.summary: This paper investigated the structural evolution of neutron-rich silicon isotopes as they approach the N = 28 shell closure, a region characterized by a transition from shell closure at N = 20 to strong collective behavior at N = 28. The researchers performed lifetime measurements of excited states in silicon-40 and conducted the first simultaneous lifetime and heavy-ion inelastic-scattering measurements in silicon-41. In silicon-40, the extracted lifetimes reveal moderate quadrupole collectivity at N = 26 along with signatures of triaxial nuclear shape. In silicon-41, two nearly degenerate low-energy states were found to have comparable electric quadrupole transition strengths from inelastic scattering, yet their lifetimes indicate that magnetic dipole transitions dominate. Taken together, these results suggest the nuclei evolve toward an oblate shape approaching N = 28, in agreement with large-scale shell-model theoretical predictions.
items.citation: G. L. Zimba, H. Iwasaki, B. A. Brown et al.. Rapid structural evolution of neutron-rich silicon isotopes toward N = 28. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Atomistic mechanism of corrosion-induced grain boundary migration in NiCr alloys in molten FLiNaK
items.summary: This paper investigated how grain boundaries influence the corrosion of nickel-chromium alloys exposed to molten fluoride salt, a poorly understood aspect of a persistent materials degradation problem. Using reactive molecular dynamics simulations, the researchers examined corrosion behavior across four distinct grain boundary types and corresponding bulk surfaces in molten FLiNaK. They found that surface crystallography governs the initial stages of dissolution, while grain boundary character controls where corrosion localizes and how it evolves over longer timescales. A key finding was the identification of a corrosion-driven grain boundary migration mechanism, in which fluorine localization, preferential chromium dissolution, and vacancy-mediated atomic mobility collectively drive grain boundary motion away from the dealloyed region. The coherent low-energy boundary studied was found to suppress these processes, suggesting that grain boundary engineering toward such special boundaries could improve corrosion resistance in nickel-chromium structural alloys.
items.citation: Sadia Khan, Hamdy Arkoub, Miaomiao Jin. Atomistic mechanism of corrosion-induced grain boundary migration in NiCr alloys in molten FLiNaK. arXiv. 2026

items.title: A Methodology for Auditable Trustworthiness Levels in AI Lifecycle Governance
items.summary: This paper addresses the challenge of determining whether an AI system remains sufficiently trustworthy throughout its operational lifecycle in a way that satisfies governance and auditability requirements. The authors developed a two-part methodology combining a formal framework for representing and learning trustworthiness levels with a practical governance procedure for documenting, monitoring, and reassessing those levels over time. The formal component models trustworthiness through context-sensitive, measurable dimensions and uses interpretable decision trees to produce explicit trustworthiness plateaus, readable level transitions, and two diagnostic tools—boundary margins and profile drift—that signal when a system's behavior is shifting. The governance procedure embeds these tools in a conformity-oriented workflow covering design-time labeling, post-deployment monitoring, and structured reassessment, with assigned human responsibilities and control gates at each stage. The approach is illustrated on synthetic lifecycle scenarios involving degradation, shocks, and system updates, and is presented not as a replacement for legal or expert judgment but as an evidential scaffold for documenting governance-relevant changes over time.
items.citation: Andrea Ferrario. A Methodology for Auditable Trustworthiness Levels in AI Lifecycle Governance. arXiv. 2026

items.title: CRAFT: Clustering Rubrics to Diagnose Weak LLM Capabilities and Generate Targeted Fine-Tuning Data
items.summary: This paper introduces CRAFT, a method designed to move beyond simply measuring where language models fail toward diagnosing why they fail and automatically generating training data to address those weaknesses. The approach treats each grading criterion in a rubric-based evaluation as a probe of a specific capability, extracts capability descriptions from prompt-rubric pairs, clusters them into a hierarchical tree, and identifies low-performing nodes at the most informative level of granularity. These identified weak capabilities then guide the generation of targeted supervised fine-tuning data. The method was compared against prompt-level clustering and untargeted random data generation across four open-source models and two professional domains, evaluated on held-out benchmarks disjoint from the diagnostic data. CRAFT achieved the strongest performance improvements in the finance domain for all tested models and in the legal domain for most models, demonstrating that diagnosing failures at the level of rubric criteria rather than broader categories or individual prompts produces both clearer diagnostic insight and measurably better fine-tuned models.
items.citation: Vipul Gupta, Zihao Wang, Razvan-Gabriel Dumitru et al.. CRAFT: Clustering Rubrics to Diagnose Weak LLM Capabilities and Generate Targeted Fine-Tuning Data. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Comparison of Energy System Optimization Software and Evaluation of Selected Frameworks
items.summary: This paper investigated how to select appropriate energy system optimization software for different use cases, a decision complicated by the diversity of energy system objectives and requirements. The researchers evaluated five software tools — REMix, MTRESS, COMANDO, OEMOF, and HOMER PRO — through an extensive review of literature, documentation, tutorials, and example models, and developed a structured set of comparison criteria to assess each tool. The analysis found that each tool has distinct strengths: one excels at investment path optimization, another handles atypical system components, a third is best suited for standard open-source optimization problems, and a fourth is recommended for rapid synthesis optimization accessible to users with limited programming experience. One of the five tools could not be fully evaluated due to insufficient available information. The study provides practical guidance for researchers and practitioners seeking to match energy system optimization challenges to the most appropriate software framework.
items.citation: Pedro Caixeta, David Gawron, Hüseyin K. Çakmak et al.. Comparison of Energy System Optimization Software and Evaluation of Selected Frameworks. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Quantum-classical crossover in fault-tolerant quantum dynamics simulation
items.summary: This paper investigates the point at which fault-tolerant quantum computers become practically superior to classical algorithms for simulating quantum many-body dynamics. The authors developed a scalable fault-tolerant framework that combines coherent observable estimation with a space-time-efficient implementation of non-Clifford rotations, designed to suppress residual logical errors that hamper existing partially fault-tolerant approaches. Benchmarking this framework against state-of-the-art tensor-network and variational Monte Carlo methods for mixed-field Ising dynamics, they identified a concrete quantum-classical crossover at modest system sizes, with fault-tolerant simulation dramatically outpacing classical approaches for both one- and two-dimensional models. The study finds that improvements to rotation-state injection and co-design of error correction with observable-estimation protocols together reduce both logical-error accumulation and sampling overhead, yielding substantial reductions in required qubit counts and runtimes as physical error rates improve. These results provide quantitative engineering targets for future fault-tolerant hardware and establish a concrete pathway toward practical quantum advantage in quantum dynamics simulation.
items.citation: Jinzhao Sun, Bozhen Zhou, Jue Xu et al.. Quantum-classical crossover in fault-tolerant quantum dynamics simulation. arXiv. 2026

items.title: A quantitative rapid test for urine creatinine <i>via</i> Fenton's reaction and a self-driven microfluidic device.
items.summary: This paper developed a rapid, quantitative point-of-care test for measuring urine creatinine, which is important both as a marker of kidney function and for normalizing other urinary biomarkers. The approach exploits creatinine's ability to inhibit hydroxyl radicals generated by Fenton's reaction, where these radicals would otherwise cleave an oligonucleotide linker connecting magnetic and polystyrene microparticles. Because creatinine scavenges the hydroxyl radicals, higher creatinine concentrations result in more intact linkers and thus greater microparticle binding, which is then quantified as the length of a visible polystyrene microparticle bar following magnetic separation on a self-driven microfluidic chip. The method achieved a clinically relevant limit of detection, demonstrated tolerance to common interferents across a wide pH range, and showed strong concordance with the standard clinical assay when validated on patient urine samples. These results indicate the platform offers an accurate, visually readable alternative for creatinine measurement suitable for clinical use.
items.citation: Hartanto H, Li J, Szeto CC et al.. A quantitative rapid test for urine creatinine <i>via</i> Fenton's reaction and a self-driven microfluidic device.. Lab on a chip. 2026

items.title: Fabrication of photoresist SU-8 pyrolyzed carbon electrodes and their integration in a microfluidic hematocrit detection system
items.summary: This paper addressed limitations in fabricating low-aspect-ratio planar carbon microelectrodes from pyrolyzed SU-8 photoresist, which typically suffer from adhesion failure, geometric distortion, and insufficient surface area under conventional two-step pyrolysis protocols. The researchers developed a three-step pyrolysis method to improve structural integrity and surface morphology while retaining planar geometries compatible with standard microfabrication. The resulting electrodes were integrated into a PDMS microfluidic device for hematocrit determination, where current responses from red blood cells in phosphate-buffered saline showed a linear correlation with cell concentration. Surface characterization revealed substantially greater roughness and thickness compared to platinum electrodes, yielding increased electroactive surface area that reduced charge-transfer and concentration overpotentials. The device achieved precision and accuracy for hematocrit measurement comparable to previously reported platinum-based systems, demonstrating that the approach can replace noble metal electrodes in diagnostic microdevices without sacrificing analytical performance.
items.citation: Lee HY, Rogers J, Kendrick C et al.. Fabrication of photoresist SU-8 pyrolyzed carbon electrodes and their integration in a microfluidic hematocrit detection system. 2026

items.title: Microfluidic and MEMS-Based Biosensing Platforms for Fungal Respiratory Infections in Immunocompromised Patients: Toward Rapid, Specific, and Minimally Invasive Diagnosis.
items.summary: This review examines the challenge of diagnosing invasive fungal respiratory infections in immunocompromised patients, where conventional diagnostic methods are hampered by nonspecific clinical presentations, limited sample access, and inadequate sensitivity or specificity. The paper surveys microfluidic and microelectromechanical systems-based biosensing platforms as emerging alternatives, covering technologies such as on-chip nucleic acid amplification, antigen immunodetection, CRISPR-enhanced assays, and host response profiling through transcriptomic, proteomic, and cytokine signatures. MEMS-based approaches are also discussed, including real-time analysis of exhaled volatile organic compounds, mechanical biosensing of fungal targets, and monitoring of device-associated biofilms. The review identifies persistent translational barriers including limited multicenter validation, biofouling, and absence of standardized biomarker panels, while pointing toward future integration of artificial intelligence-driven multianalyte interpretation and fully cartridge-based near-patient systems. The authors conclude that these combined innovations hold promise for shifting fungal diagnostics toward earlier, more precise, and patient-tailored interventions in vulnerable populations.
items.citation: Georgakopoulou VE, Pitiriga VC. Microfluidic and MEMS-Based Biosensing Platforms for Fungal Respiratory Infections in Immunocompromised Patients: Toward Rapid, Specific, and Minimally Invasive Diagnosis.. Biosensors. 2026

items.title: Quantum spin resonance in engineered proteins for multimodal sensing
items.summary: This paper investigates the quantum mechanical properties of engineered magneto-sensitive fluorescent proteins, specifically a protein called MagLOV, for use as multimodal biological sensors. Through directed evolution, the researchers modified these proteins to tune their responses to magnetic fields and radio frequencies, demonstrating optically detected magnetic resonance in living bacterial cells at room temperature with sensitivity sufficient for single-cell detection. The underlying mechanism involves radical pairs formed between the protein backbone and a bound flavin cofactor. By combining optically detected magnetic resonance with fluorescence magnetic-field effects, the researchers demonstrated a broad range of applications including magnetic resonance imaging using a genetically encoded probe, molecular microenvironment sensing, bio-imaging multiplexing, and lock-in detection strategies that overcome common biological imaging challenges such as light scattering and autofluorescence. Together, these findings establish a versatile suite of quantum sensing modalities built around the engineered quantum-mechanical properties of biological proteins.
items.citation: Gabriel Abrahams, Ana Štuhec, Vincent Spreng et al.. Quantum spin resonance in engineered proteins for multimodal sensing. Nature. 2026

items.title: Artificial Intelligence for Exosomal Biomarker Discovery for Cardiovascular Diseases: Multi-Omics Integration, Reproducibility, and Translational Prospects
items.summary: This review examined the use of artificial intelligence to discover exosomal and extracellular vesicle biomarkers for cardiovascular diseases, addressing key challenges in reproducibility and clinical translation. The authors conducted a comprehensive comparative analysis of machine learning, deep learning, and network-based approaches applied to multi-omics data derived from extracellular vesicles, which carry microRNAs, proteins, and lipids reflective of cardiovascular pathophysiology. Among the methods evaluated, ensemble approaches showed the most consistent classification performance, while graph neural networks emerged as promising for pathway integration, and evolutionary neural networks offered comparable discrimination but posed interpretability challenges. Current evidence for conditions such as acute myocardial infarction and heart failure remains largely exploratory, constrained by small cohorts and limited external validation, with few studies benchmarking exosomal panels against established clinical standards such as high-sensitivity troponin. The review proposes a practical framework—incorporating standardized isolation protocols, transparent AI reporting, and multicenter validation—to advance exosomal diagnostics from exploratory research toward clinical implementation.
items.citation: Rasit Dinc, Nurittin Ardıç. Artificial Intelligence for Exosomal Biomarker Discovery for Cardiovascular Diseases: Multi-Omics Integration, Reproducibility, and Translational Prospects. Cells. 2026

items.title: Commercial Translation of Electrochemical Biosensors: Supply Chain Strategy, Scale-Up Manufacturing, and Regulatory–Quality Considerations
items.summary: This review examines why electrochemical biosensors, despite their analytical maturity, rarely progress from laboratory demonstrations to commercial products. The authors investigated the translational gap by analyzing supply chain design, scale-up manufacturing strategies, and regulatory and quality frameworks across major markets including the United States, the European Union, and China. The review compares dominant manufacturing approaches such as roll-to-roll printing and semiconductor-derived microfabrication, evaluating how each aligns with different cost targets, product applications, and regulatory risk classifications. Case studies spanning disposable glucose strips, cartridge-based blood analyzers, and continuous monitoring systems illustrate how the same underlying electrochemical principles can encounter vastly different commercialization challenges depending on clinical context and system integration requirements. The authors argue that manufacturing and regulatory considerations should be treated as upstream optimization problems during research design rather than downstream obstacles, offering a system-level framework intended to accelerate the translation of electrochemical biosensors into market-ready diagnostic tools.
items.citation: Gao Zhou, Haibin Liu. Commercial Translation of Electrochemical Biosensors: Supply Chain Strategy, Scale-Up Manufacturing, and Regulatory–Quality Considerations. Biosensors. 2026

items.title: Therapeutic Potential of Extracellular Vesicles: From Biogenesis, Isolation and Molecular Characterization to Addressing Translational Gaps and Regulatory Barriers
items.summary: This review examines extracellular vesicles as mediators of intercellular communication, covering their biogenesis, molecular composition, and therapeutic relevance across human disease. The authors survey major EV classes, including exosomes, microvesicles, apoptotic bodies, and oncosomes, and discuss advances in isolation techniques, molecular characterization, and omics-based profiling. Particular attention is paid to EV roles in viral infection, inflammation, immune regulation, and cancer biology, as well as the clinical promise of mesenchymal stem cell-derived EVs in regenerative medicine and oncology, including modulation of EV signaling through photobiomodulation. The review identifies key translational obstacles such as standardization, scalability, and regulatory validation, noting that exosome-based therapeutics require harmonized quality controls and rigorous safety evaluation under existing regulatory frameworks. Overall, the authors argue that integrating mechanistic understanding with clinical application positions EVs as transformative tools for next-generation diagnostics and precision therapeutics.
items.citation: Dragan Primorac, Petar Brlek, Luka Bulić et al.. Therapeutic Potential of Extracellular Vesicles: From Biogenesis, Isolation and Molecular Characterization to Addressing Translational Gaps and Regulatory Barriers. International Journal of Molecular Sciences. 2026

items.title: Smart wearable and implantable biosensors for continuous health monitoring: materials, biocompatibility, and AI integration
items.summary: This review examines the current state of smart wearable and implantable biosensors designed for continuous, real-time monitoring of biophysical and biochemical signals in the context of personalized and preventive healthcare. The authors survey advances in flexible, stretchable, and biocompatible materials that enable long-term body integration, as well as multimodal sensing approaches that improve measurement robustness. The integration of artificial intelligence is discussed as a means of enhancing signal processing and generating predictive health insights. Despite these advances, the review identifies persistent challenges including motion artifacts, energy autonomy, data privacy, and the clinical interpretability of sensor outputs. The paper synthesizes progress across materials science, device architecture, and AI-assisted strategies to outline both the capabilities and remaining obstacles in the field.
items.citation: Thirumalaisamy Suryaprabha, Chunghyeon Choi, Yanfang Wu et al.. Smart wearable and implantable biosensors for continuous health monitoring: materials, biocompatibility, and AI integration. npj Flexible Electronics. 2026

items.title: Artificial Intelligence Drives Advances in Multi-Omics Analysis and Precision Medicine for Sepsis
items.summary: This paper reviews how artificial intelligence is transforming the study and clinical management of sepsis through integration with multi-omics technologies. The authors examine how machine learning and deep learning approaches can process the vast, high-dimensional datasets generated by genomics, transcriptomics, proteomics, and metabolomics to extract biologically meaningful patterns that conventional statistical methods cannot adequately handle. They describe how these AI-driven analyses have advanced early detection, molecular subtyping, prognosis prediction, and therapeutic target identification in sepsis. The review also identifies major obstacles to clinical translation, including data scarcity, cohort heterogeneity, interpretability limitations, computational demands, and ethical concerns, while proposing that standardized multicenter datasets, explainable AI frameworks, and interdisciplinary collaboration are necessary to overcome them. The authors conclude that the convergence of AI and multi-omics represents a shift toward precision medicine in sepsis, with the potential to move the field from descriptive molecular profiling toward predictive and mechanistically grounded clinical practice.
items.citation: Youxie Shen, Peidong Zhang, Jialiu Luo et al.. Artificial Intelligence Drives Advances in Multi-Omics Analysis and Precision Medicine for Sepsis. Biomedicines. 2026

items.title: A Dynamical Phase-Field Model for the Optical Properties of Ferroelectrics
items.summary: This paper addresses the challenge of predicting optical properties in ferroelectric materials, where the complex domain microstructure strongly influences the optical response but existing theoretical frameworks cannot capture this coupling. The authors developed a dynamical phase-field model that introduces an electronic polarization field to directly link the ferroelectric order parameter to the local optical response, enabling spatially resolved, temperature- and wavelength-dependent optical predictions in realistic microstructures. Applying the model to barium titanate thin films, they simulated the evolution of the local refractive index and electro-optic response under varying electric fields and temperatures, finding that domain walls dramatically enhance the local electro-optic coefficient far beyond bulk single-crystal values. The simulations quantitatively reproduced experimentally measured electro-optic coefficients in barium titanate on silicon films and captured behavior across multiple ferroelectric phase transitions, revealing how phase competition and coexistence govern the electro-optic response. The work establishes a general computational framework for predicting light-matter interactions in ferroelectric microstructures, offering a pathway for the rational design of ferroelectric photonic devices.
items.citation: Aiden Ross, Anya Frazer, Venkatraman Gopalan et al.. A Dynamical Phase-Field Model for the Optical Properties of Ferroelectrics. arXiv. 2026

items.title: CLIFE: Camera-LiDAR Fusion Framework for Edge-Deployable Roadside VRU Perception
items.summary: This paper addresses the challenge of detecting vulnerable road users at roadside intersections under difficult conditions such as occlusion, variable lighting, and adverse weather, while meeting strict edge-computing and latency requirements. The authors developed CLIFE, a camera-LiDAR fusion framework that performs targetless online calibration and lightweight late-fusion tracking entirely on a single embedded device without cloud offloading, achieving an algorithmic complexity that scales efficiently with the number of detected objects. The system was deployed across a set of signalized intersections and evaluated using synchronized multi-sensor data captured under diverse daytime, nighttime, and weather conditions. Results show that the fusion architecture meaningfully extends perceptual range and robustness beyond what either sensor achieves alone, while the late-fusion core achieves high frame rates on embedded hardware sufficient for real-time intersection monitoring. The authors conclude that CLIFE provides a practically deployable foundation for roadside safety applications, reducing both bandwidth demands and calibration overhead for agencies managing multi-intersection corridors.
items.citation: Tam Bang, Hoang H. Nguyen, Lei Cheng et al.. CLIFE: Camera-LiDAR Fusion Framework for Edge-Deployable Roadside VRU Perception. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Radiopurity material assays and radiation exposure projections for superconducting qubit measurements at SNOLAB
items.summary: This paper investigates the challenge that ionizing radiation poses to the coherence and reliable operation of superconducting qubits, which are fabricated on semiconductor substrates and vulnerable to cosmic rays and radiogenic backgrounds. To address this, the authors conducted an extensive material assay campaign to characterize the radioactivity levels of components intended for use in an underground superconducting qubit experiment at the CUTE facility at SNOLAB, which is shielded by two kilometers of rock overburden. The measured radioactivity data were fed into detailed Monte Carlo simulations using the Geant4 particle physics code to estimate the rates and spectral composition of energy deposits from radiogenic sources within the quantum device assembly. The study further identifies the dominant particle interaction types contributing to the projected background and discusses how crystal dynamics simulations using the G4CMP solid-state physics extension can help the broader community develop mitigation strategies against high-energy particle impacts on quantum devices. Together, these findings lay the groundwork for the first underground operation of superconducting qubits at SNOLAB and inform efforts to improve quantum coherence in shielded environments.
items.citation: Y. Ahmed, B. Binoy, R. Bunker et al.. Radiopurity material assays and radiation exposure projections for superconducting qubit measurements at SNOLAB. arXiv. 2026

items.title: A Kalman Filter-Assisted Data-Predictive SAR ADC With Reduced Switching Energy for Low-Power Applications
items.summary: This paper addresses the challenge of reducing energy consumption in successive approximation register analog-to-digital converters used in IoT and wearable health monitoring applications. The researchers integrated a Kalman filter into the SAR ADC architecture to predict the four most significant bits from prior conversion history, replacing the traditional sequential comparator-driven decisions for those bits with a recursive state estimation approach that enables simultaneous parallel capacitor switching. This strategy eliminates redundant digital-to-analog converter transitions and shortens the conversion cycle by four clock periods. The design, implemented in a standard CMOS process, supports dual-mode operation to maintain robustness when input signals behave erratically. The predictive mode achieved approximately half the total power consumption compared to conventional operation while maintaining signal quality metrics consistent with wireless sensor network requirements.
items.citation: Xiyuan Feng, Yuxiang Zhao, Jie Xiong et al.. A Kalman Filter-Assisted Data-Predictive SAR ADC With Reduced Switching Energy for Low-Power Applications. arXiv. 2026

items.title: A Methodology for Auditable Trustworthiness Levels in AI Lifecycle Governance
items.summary: This paper addresses the challenge of assessing and documenting whether an AI system remains sufficiently trustworthy across its operational lifecycle in a way that meets governance and auditability requirements. The authors propose a two-part methodology combining a formal framework for representing trustworthiness as interpretable rules over measurable dimensions, and a procedural workflow for embedding these assessments into ongoing governance activities including monitoring, reassessment, and reporting. The formal component uses decision trees as a proof-of-concept model to produce explicit trustworthiness levels, readable transition rules, and two diagnostic tools called boundary margins and profile drift that detect when a system's behavior is approaching or has crossed a governance-relevant threshold. The governance procedure assigns human responsibilities and control gates at key lifecycle stages, from design-time labeling through post-deployment monitoring, and is illustrated on synthetic lifecycle traces involving degradation, shocks, updates, heterogeneous monitoring schedules, and system comparisons. The methodology is positioned not as a replacement for legal or expert judgment but as an evidential infrastructure that supports transparent, contestable documentation of AI governance-relevant changes over time.
items.citation: Andrea Ferrario. A Methodology for Auditable Trustworthiness Levels in AI Lifecycle Governance. arXiv. 2026

items.title: CRAFT: Clustering Rubrics to Diagnose Weak LLM Capabilities and Generate Targeted Fine-Tuning Data
items.summary: This paper introduces CRAFT, a method designed to convert rubric-based evaluation datasets into model-specific diagnoses of capability weaknesses in large language models, going beyond simply identifying where a model fails to explaining why it fails. The approach extracts capability descriptions from prompt-rubric pairs, clusters them into a hierarchical capability tree, scores the target model at each node, and dynamically selects low-performing nodes to pinpoint failures at the clearest level of granularity. These identified weak capabilities then guide the generation of targeted supervised fine-tuning data. The method was compared against prompt-level clustering and untargeted random data generation across four open-source models, two professional domains, and a set of held-out benchmarks. CRAFT consistently achieved the strongest or near-strongest performance improvements, demonstrating that diagnosing weaknesses at the level of rubric criteria rather than prompts or broad categories produces both sharper diagnostic insight and measurably better fine-tuned models.
items.citation: Vipul Gupta, Zihao Wang, Razvan-Gabriel Dumitru et al.. CRAFT: Clustering Rubrics to Diagnose Weak LLM Capabilities and Generate Targeted Fine-Tuning Data. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Graphene oxide as smart sustainable nanomaterial: a versatile multifunctional material with transformative potential in advanced materials science research
items.summary: This review paper examines graphene oxide, a two-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterial, as a versatile and sustainable platform for addressing challenges across energy, environmental, and healthcare sectors. The authors survey the synthesis chemistry and property modification strategies that give graphene oxide its distinctive characteristics, including tunable band gap, high surface area, structural flexibility, and adaptable surface chemistry spanning zero- to three-dimensional nanostructural configurations. The review covers a broad range of applications, including photocatalytic degradation of pollutants and microplastics, adsorption of toxic substances and pathogens, carbon dioxide photoreduction, hydrogen production, battery technologies, and ultra-sensitive detection via surface-enhanced Raman scattering and gas sensing. The authors also address healthcare applications and highlight the material's promise for real-world and industrial deployment. However, they identify persistent challenges such as scalability limitations, long-term stability concerns, and reproducibility issues, and propose potential solutions while noting health considerations relevant to sustainable future use.
items.citation: S.N. Thakur, Ayush Badoni, Rupam Sharma et al.. Graphene oxide as smart sustainable nanomaterial: a versatile multifunctional material with transformative potential in advanced materials science research. npj Materials Sustainability. 2026

items.title: Aptamer-gated luminescence enhancement and red-shift in a MOF: a ratiometric and color-tonality visual biosensor for pathogenic E. coli detection
items.citation: Shilan Arif Fatah, Sameera Sh. Mohammed Ameen, Payam B. Hassan et al.. Aptamer-gated luminescence enhancement and red-shift in a MOF: a ratiometric and color-tonality visual biosensor for pathogenic E. coli detection. Microchimica Acta. 2026

items.title: Recent Advances in Microfluidic Chip Technology for Laboratory Medicine: Innovations and Artificial Intelligence Integration
items.summary: This review examines recent advances in microfluidic chip technology, commonly referred to as lab-on-a-chip systems, and their expanding role in laboratory medicine. The authors conducted a comprehensive review of innovations in chip design and fabrication, including developments in three-dimensional printing, modular architectures, and flexible substrates, alongside progress in biosensor integration for detecting circulating tumor cells, nucleic acids, and exosomes. A central focus is the convergence of microfluidics with artificial intelligence and machine learning, which has enabled so-called smart diagnostic platforms capable of self-optimization, automated analysis, and clinical decision support.
