The review highlights clinical applications spanning cancer diagnostics, infectious disease detection, point-of-care testing, personalized medicine, and emerging areas such as neuroscience diagnostics and microbiome profiling. The authors also address persistent challenges in manufacturing, clinical validation, and system integration, outlining future directions for translating these technologies into routine clinical and public health use.
items.citation: Hong Cai, Dongxia Wang, Yiqun Zhao et al.. Recent Advances in Microfluidic Chip Technology for Laboratory Medicine: Innovations and Artificial Intelligence Integration. Biosensors. 2026

items.title: Conducting Polymers for Electrochemical Sensing: From Materials and Metrology to Intelligent and Sustainable Biointerfaces
items.summary: This review examines the role of conducting polymers as central materials in electrochemical sensing and biosensing, covering both foundational systems and emerging device architectures. The authors trace the development of established conducting polymers through to nanostructured, wearable, and transient formats, analyzing fundamental charge-transport mechanisms, doping strategies, and the influence of morphology and biofunctionalization on sensor performance. A particular focus is placed on reliability limitations such as signal drift, dopant leaching, environmental degradation, and biofouling, alongside the absence of standardized metrology that prevents meaningful comparison across studies. To address this, the review proposes a framework for rigorous calibration, reference electrode design, and data reporting, and benchmarks performance metrics across major conducting polymer families and sensing modalities. The authors conclude by identifying future directions including organic mixed ionic-electronic conductors, biohybrid interfaces, artificial intelligence-assisted modeling, and sustainable transient electronics as promising avenues for the field.
items.citation: Giovanna Di Pasquale, Antonino Pollicino. Conducting Polymers for Electrochemical Sensing: From Materials and Metrology to Intelligent and Sustainable Biointerfaces. Sensors. 2026

items.title: Simultaneous Dual-Gene Detection of <i>Escherichia coli</i> O157:H7 Based on a CRISPR/Cas13-Mediated Biosensor.
items.summary: This study developed a biosensor for detecting *Escherichia coli* O157:H7, a foodborne pathogen of significant public health concern, by simultaneously targeting two key genetic markers that define its serotype. The researchers engineered a one-pot CRISPR/Cas13-based system exploiting the distinct collateral cleavage preferences of two different Cas13 proteins to create dual fluorescence channels, allowing simultaneous detection of both target genes within a single reaction tube. Recombinase polymerase amplification was integrated into the workflow to achieve high sensitivity, and the entire process from amplification to detection was consolidated to minimize contamination risk and equipment requirements. The biosensor was validated using a mouse infection model, with results from fecal samples showing agreement with standard PCR methods. The authors claim this dual-channel approach improves serotype discrimination and specificity compared to conventional CRISPR diagnostics, offering a rapid, simple, and field-deployable tool for food safety, clinical diagnostics, and infectious disease surveillance.
items.citation: He Y, Lin X, Zhang X et al.. Simultaneous Dual-Gene Detection of <i>Escherichia coli</i> O157:H7 Based on a CRISPR/Cas13-Mediated Biosensor.. JACS Au. 2026

items.title: Multiple cross displacement amplification coupled with a lateral flow biosensor for ultra-rapid and highly sensitive detection of <i>Escherichia coli</i> in neonatal sepsis.
items.summary: The study addressed the need for rapid, sensitive detection of *Escherichia coli* in neonatal sepsis, where conventional blood culture is slow and frequently insensitive. The researchers developed a multiple cross-displacement amplification assay coupled with a lateral flow biosensor targeting the *uidA* gene, using a ten-primer set optimized to run at a single temperature for forty minutes. The assay achieved femtogram-level sensitivity with no cross-reactivity against non-*E. coli* strains, and the complete workflow from nucleic acid extraction to visual result was finished within approximately one hour. Clinical validation using blood and cerebrospinal fluid specimens from neonates showed complete agreement with quantitative PCR and substantially higher positivity rates than conventional culture. The authors conclude that this approach offers a rapid, sensitive, and operationally simple tool suitable for point-of-care and resource-limited settings where early diagnosis of neonatal *E. coli* infection is critical.
items.citation: Xiao R, Zou P, Zhou J et al.. Multiple cross displacement amplification coupled with a lateral flow biosensor for ultra-rapid and highly sensitive detection of <i>Escherichia coli</i> in neonatal sepsis.. Microbiology spectrum. 2026

items.title: Target-to-signal conversion and spatial enrichment cascade boost CRISPR/Cas12a biosensing for trace-level pathogen detection.
items.summary: This study addressed the need for rapid and sensitive detection of *Salmonella typhimurium*, a foodborne pathogen of significant public health concern. The researchers developed a biosensor combining DNAzyme-catalyzed amplification, hybridization chain reaction, and CRISPR/Cas12a activity, wherein target recognition triggers a cascade that generates DNA nanowires bearing sequences activatable by Cas12a, which then cleaves the nanowires to restore a quenched fluorescent signal. This multi-stage signal enrichment strategy achieved detection across a broad linear range at very low bacterial concentrations, with an exceptionally low estimated detection limit. The biosensor demonstrated straightforward operation and rapid response while maintaining high sensitivity and specificity. The authors further propose that the platform is adaptable to other foodborne pathogens by swapping the recognition and primer sequences, suggesting broad applicability.
items.venue: Analytical methods : advancing methods and applications
items.citation: Liu Y, Yue X, Li B et al.. Target-to-signal conversion and spatial enrichment cascade boost CRISPR/Cas12a biosensing for trace-level pathogen detection.. Analytical methods : advancing methods and applications. 2026

items.title: Graphene foam biosensors for molecular diagnostics of antimicrobial resistance and emerging pathogen threats.
items.summary: This paper developed an electrochemical biosensor designed to rapidly detect antimicrobial resistance genes and emerging pathogen targets at the point of care. The platform uses disposable graphene foam electrodes functionalized with pyrene-modified peptide nucleic acid probes, which attach via π-π stacking to preserve the material's electronic properties and high surface area without requiring on-surface chemical reactions. A key advance was a heat-assisted functionalization protocol that reduced preparation time from hours to minutes while maintaining sensor performance, and the system achieved amplification-free and label-free detection of the colistin resistance gene with low detection limits for both synthetic DNA and bacterial plasmids. The biosensor clearly discriminated the target gene sequence from a non-complementary control, and retargeting to a different resistance gene was demonstrated simply by swapping the probe sequence. The platform showed consistent, reproducible performance across independently fabricated sensors, supporting its potential for scalable manufacturing of decentralized diagnostic tools against antimicrobial resistance and pandemic threats.
items.citation: Wang KY, Schulze H, Lozano Sanchez P et al.. Graphene foam biosensors for molecular diagnostics of antimicrobial resistance and emerging pathogen threats.. Biosensors & bioelectronics. 2026

items.title: Advanced biosensing strategies for high-risk foodborne pathogens: a comprehensive review of <i>Salmonella</i> and <i>Listeria monocytogenes</i>.
items.summary: This review examines advances in biosensor technologies specifically designed to detect two major foodborne pathogens, *Salmonella* spp. and *Listeria monocytogenes*, which together represent leading causes of foodborne illness and mortality globally. Rather than broadly surveying biosensing across many pathogens, the authors took a pathogen-specific approach, systematically mapping trends in bioreceptor development and transducer selection for these two organisms. The review also addresses, for the first time, how artificial intelligence tools can accelerate biosensor design, optimization, and decision-making in the context of detecting these particular pathogens. A central focus is bridging the gap between laboratory research and real-world application by evaluating which biosensing strategies hold genuine promise for scalability, portability, and routine deployment in food industry settings. The authors present the review as a practical roadmap intended to guide future research toward field-ready, industry-relevant detection solutions.
items.citation: Gumush SE, Al-Attar R, Sandbecker E et al.. Advanced biosensing strategies for high-risk foodborne pathogens: a comprehensive review of <i>Salmonella</i> and <i>Listeria monocytogenes</i>.. Food chemistry. Molecular sciences. 2026

items.title: Deep learning integrated plasmonic electrochemical sensing for fast and accurate pathogen detection.
items.summary: This paper investigated the integration of deep learning algorithms with plasmonic electrochemical biosensors for rapid and accurate detection of bacterial pathogens. The researchers developed a nanostructured biosensor using gold-nanoparticle graphene oxide hybrid nanocomposites to exploit localized surface plasmon resonance and enhance charge-transfer kinetics, targeting Escherichia coli, Salmonella typhimurium, and Staphylococcus aureus. A combined MobileNet-Transformer and Gated Recurrent Unit deep neural network pipeline was applied to process raw voltammetric signals, with the transformer component extracting features and the recurrent unit reducing temporal noise. The platform achieved ultra-low detection limits for all three pathogens from small sample volumes in under ten minutes, and the deep learning classifier outperformed baseline convolutional and recurrent neural network models in accuracy, area under the curve, and precision-recall performance. The authors claim this system offers a scalable, portable, and AI-assisted approach to electrochemical biosensing suitable for food safety, environmental monitoring, and point-of-care diagnostics at performance levels comparable to laboratory benchtop instruments.
items.citation: Mansour S, Negm N, Alhashmi AA et al.. Deep learning integrated plasmonic electrochemical sensing for fast and accurate pathogen detection.. Scientific reports. 2026

items.title: Gut microbiome–driven colorectal cancer via immune, metabolic, neural, and endocrine axes reprogramming
items.summary: This review paper examines the gut microbiome's role as a causal contributor to colorectal cancer, integrating molecular, ecological, and translational evidence to explain how microbial communities drive cancer initiation, progression, and treatment response. The authors synthesize findings showing that colorectal cancer-associated dysbiosis involves enrichment of specific pathobionts alongside depletion of protective short-chain-fatty-acid-producing bacteria, with these microbial shifts reprogramming immune, metabolic, neural, and endocrine networks within the tumor microenvironment. Key mechanisms include genotoxin-induced DNA damage from bacterial products such as colibactin and cytolethal distending toxin, epithelial barrier disruption, and chronic inflammation that sustains immune suppression and tumor growth, with defined mutational signatures now directly linking microbial exposures to human cancer genomes. The review conceptualizes colorectal cancer as a biofilm-structured, polymicrobial ecosystem disease in which microbial consortia coordinate barrier breakdown, immune evasion, and metabolic cooperation. Building on this framework, the authors highlight emerging microbiota-targeted therapeutic strategies—including dietary modulation, bacteriophage therapy, engineered live biotherapeutics, and fecal microbiota transplantation—and argue for repositioning the microbiome from a correlative feature to a tractable determinant of colorectal cancer pathogenesis and treatment.
items.citation: Jhommara Bautista, Mónica Lamas‐Maceiras, Camila Hidalgo-Tinoco et al.. Gut microbiome–driven colorectal cancer via immune, metabolic, neural, and endocrine axes reprogramming. npj Biofilms and Microbiomes. 2026

items.title: Precision Farming with Smart Sensors: Current State, Challenges and Future Outlook
items.summary: This review paper examines the role of smart sensor technologies in precision farming, focusing on their current state, challenges, and future prospects. The authors surveyed how smart sensors integrated with wireless sensor networks, the Internet of Things, artificial intelligence, robotics, drones, and cloud computing are being used to monitor soil conditions, plant growth, and environmental factors in real time. The integration of these technologies has substantially changed how agricultural data is collected, analyzed, and applied to optimize crop yields and conserve resources. However, the review identifies persistent obstacles including sensor calibration difficulties, data privacy concerns, interoperability issues, and barriers to widespread adoption among farmers. The authors conclude that addressing these challenges is essential to fully realizing the potential of smart sensor-driven precision farming for ensuring global food security and promoting sustainable agriculture.
items.citation: Bonface O. Manono, Boniface Mwami, Sylvester Mutavi et al.. Precision Farming with Smart Sensors: Current State, Challenges and Future Outlook. Sensors. 2026

items.title: Vision-Language Assistant for Emotional Reactions to Risky Driving
items.summary: This paper introduced a vision-language pipeline called Keep Yelling Assistant, designed to detect risky driving behaviors and generate emotionally expressive verbal responses tailored to driver preferences. The system combines a computer vision module using object detection to identify dangerous maneuvers such as sudden cut-ins and extract key driving metrics, with a language module that translates these observations into responses with user-selected emotional tones such as neutral, humorous, or analytical. Several state-of-the-art large language models were evaluated as the conversational backend, and the framework was tested on dashcam footage alongside a user study to assess emotional alignment and preference across different response personas. All tested language models received favorable ratings from participants, though preferences varied by persona, and one model pairing achieved the highest satisfaction score. The authors argue that integrating real-time perceptual data with emotionally adaptive dialogue represents a new direction for emotionally intelligent in-vehicle AI that could enhance safety, trust, and driver well-being.
items.citation: Harine Choi, Eun Hak Lee, Zhengzhong Tu. Vision-Language Assistant for Emotional Reactions to Risky Driving. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Quantum scanning synthetic optical holography
items.summary: This paper extends synthetic optical holography, a technique that enables quantitative phase imaging in scanning optical microscopy, into the quantum imaging domain. The authors combined synthetic optical holography with quantum imaging using undetected light, a method that exploits entangled photon pairs so that an object is probed at one wavelength while detection occurs at a different wavelength. By introducing a controlled synthetic phase carrier into a scanning implementation of this quantum imaging scheme, they reconstructed both amplitude and phase information from objects illuminated by mid-infrared photons while detecting only their visible-wavelength entangled partners. The approach was validated on binary, transparent, and biological samples, demonstrating complex-field imaging in a scanning quantum system. A key consequence of the method is that spatial resolution becomes decoupled from photon-pair spatial correlations, offering a pathway toward diffraction-limited, label-free mid-infrared phase imaging using visible-wavelength detectors.
items.citation: Josué R. León-Torres, Byron Caiza, Nadia Baumann et al.. Quantum scanning synthetic optical holography. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Adaptive Fault Injection Planning for Multi-Layer Self-Healing AI Infrastructure
items.summary: This paper investigates how faults propagate across multiple layers in GPU-accelerator infrastructure used by hyperscale operators, focusing on the gap between conventional single-layer fault testing and the complex cross-layer failure modes that occur in production systems. The authors developed ADA-ST, an adaptive fault-injection methodology built around a weighted fault-propagation graph that models interactions across hardware, firmware, management software, and orchestration layers simultaneously. Using repair ticket data from a production platform as an empirical foundation, they demonstrated that existing static test campaigns exercise only a small fraction of possible cross-layer fault-propagation paths, leaving the vast majority of the cross-layer attack surface untested. ADA-ST uses iterative, coverage-guided scenario selection to close this gap efficiently, achieving full edge coverage within a small number of iterations across three successive hardware generations, and a knowledge-transfer mechanism called FLAM was shown to carry propagation insights from one platform generation to the next with high fidelity. Physical validation on the newest platform confirmed the discovered propagation edges and revealed concrete cross-layer vulnerabilities including telemetry blind spots, absence-based detection failures, multi-signal correlation gaps, and unverified trust at a key layer boundary.
items.citation: Saurabh Kulkarni, Yuxin Yang, Rohan Kulkarni et al.. Adaptive Fault Injection Planning for Multi-Layer Self-Healing AI Infrastructure. arXiv. 2026

items.title: PRISA: Proactive Infrastructure LiDAR Framework for Intersection Safety Assessment
items.summary: This paper presents PRISA, a modular infrastructure-mounted LiDAR framework designed to monitor urban intersections and detect conflict risks in real time, with particular attention to vulnerable road users such as pedestrians and cyclists. The system combines a roadside sensing and perception layer with a plug-and-play risk assessment module that automatically generates site-specific training data from accumulated perception outputs, eliminating the need for manual annotation when training a trajectory prediction model. Risk is evaluated using two complementary surrogate safety metrics: Time-to-Collision for longitudinal vehicle conflicts and a novel Predicted Post-Encroachment Time measure for crossing and vulnerable-road-user interactions. The framework is privacy-preserving and robust to low-light conditions, runs at the edge on embedded hardware, and was validated both on a public benchmark dataset and through live deployment at a signalized intersection. Results demonstrate that the system achieves end-to-end latency well within practical real-time constraints across a multi-second predictive horizon, supporting its feasibility for proactive multi-agent safety monitoring.
items.citation: Tam Bang, Hussam Abubakr, Emiliano de la Garza Villarreal et al.. PRISA: Proactive Infrastructure LiDAR Framework for Intersection Safety Assessment. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Eccentricity as a Magnifying Glass: Precision Population Inference Enabled by Eccentric Neutron Star--Black Hole Mergers
items.summary: This paper investigates how orbital eccentricity in neutron star–black hole binary mergers can improve our ability to infer the astrophysical formation channels of these systems. Because eccentric mergers yield substantially sharper measurements of intrinsic parameters such as component masses and spins compared to circular mergers, the authors explore how these enhanced measurements propagate to population-level inference using gravitational-wave detectors. They find that better-constrained effective spin parameters allow spin-orbit misalignment to be confidently identified far more frequently in eccentric systems, enabling clearer discrimination between isolated binary evolution and dynamical formation channels. Improved mass measurements from eccentric events also tighten constraints on neutron star mass distributions and metallicity-dependent black hole mass distributions. The study concludes that by the end of the fifth LIGO-Virgo-KAGRA observing run, the population-level eccentricity distribution predicted for dynamically formed systems should be testable, making eccentricity a powerful tool for understanding compact binary formation history.
items.citation: Alberto Salvarese, Hsin-Yu Chen, Aaron Zimmerman et al.. Eccentricity as a Magnifying Glass: Precision Population Inference Enabled by Eccentric Neutron Star--Black Hole Mergers. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Impact of eccentricity and higher-modes on neutron star-black hole parameter estimation
items.summary: This paper investigated how orbital eccentricity and higher-order waveform modes affect the accuracy of parameter estimation for neutron star-black hole binary systems detected through gravitational waves. The authors conducted a systematic study using simulated gravitational-wave signals generated with an advanced eccentric waveform model, focusing on systems resembling the observed event GW200105_162426. They found that increasing eccentricity substantially improves the measurement precision of intrinsic parameters, with the effective spin and mass ratio becoming constrained far more tightly than in the quasi-circular case, and eccentricity itself recoverable with very small uncertainty at higher eccentricity values. However, extrinsic parameters such as luminosity distance and sky localization showed no significant improvement, indicating that eccentricity provides little additional information in those sectors, likely due to degeneracies with higher-order mode contributions. The results highlight the particular advantage of neutron star-black hole systems over binary black holes for eccentricity measurements, owing to their longer observable inspiral signals.
items.citation: Snehal Tibrewal, Aasim Jan, Aaron Zimmerman et al.. Impact of eccentricity and higher-modes on neutron star-black hole parameter estimation. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Etiology of severe acute respiratory infections among adults in northern Thailand using multiplex PCR: A post-COVID-19 surveillance study (2023-2024).
items.summary: This study investigated the causes of severe acute respiratory infections among hospitalized adults in northern Thailand during the post-COVID-19 pandemic period, a time when respiratory virus circulation patterns had been significantly altered. Researchers retrospectively analyzed respiratory samples from adult patients admitted to a regional hospital in Chiang Mai with SARI symptoms between late 2023 and early 2024, testing specimens using multiplex real-time PCR capable of detecting numerous respiratory pathogens. Nearly half of all SARI cases tested positive for at least one respiratory pathogen, with adenovirus and rhinovirus emerging as the most commonly detected viruses, and co-infections found in a notable proportion of patients. Seasonal patterns revealed distinct temporal clustering, with influenza A and rhinovirus peaking in January while adenovirus and enterovirus circulated throughout the study period. The authors conclude that broad molecular diagnostics and enhanced pathogen-specific surveillance are urgently needed to reduce reliance on empiric antibiotic treatment and to guide evidence-based clinical management in the post-pandemic era.
items.citation: Kham-Kjing N, Kawila R, Tariyo P et al.. Etiology of severe acute respiratory infections among adults in northern Thailand using multiplex PCR: A post-COVID-19 surveillance study (2023-2024).. PloS one. 2026

items.title: VisPan: real-time visualisation of multiplex amplicon-based sequencing panels for rapid syndromic surveillance and pathogen detection.
items.summary: This paper addresses the bioinformatics bottleneck in real-time pathogen detection using Oxford Nanopore sequencing by developing a visualization tool for multiplex amplicon-based sequencing panels. The authors adapted existing RAMPART software to enable real-time data visualization of multiplex PCR syndromic panels combined with Oxford Nanopore sequencing, allowing taxonomic assignment within minutes of raw data acquisition. The tool provides dynamic visual tracking of sequencing runs and amplicon coverage, supporting immediate diagnostic insights in clinical or field settings. Validation using complex clinical and environmental samples demonstrated reliable and accurate identification of all pathogens present, confirming the system's suitability for syndromic surveillance and outbreak management.
items.citation: Lechat P, Kwasiborski A, Vincent R et al.. VisPan: real-time visualisation of multiplex amplicon-based sequencing panels for rapid syndromic surveillance and pathogen detection.. Bioinformatics (Oxford, England). 2026

items.title: Performance evaluation of the BioFire respiratory panel 2.1 plus and exploratory analysis of Ct values for respiratory pathogen quantification.
items.summary: This study evaluated the diagnostic performance of the BioFire FilmArray RP2.1 plus multiplex PCR panel for detecting respiratory pathogens, while also exploring whether its cycle threshold values could serve as a semi-quantitative measure of pathogen load. Researchers retrospectively tested clinical specimens on the BioFire platform and compared results against established comparator assays, generating a large set of PCR results for qualitative and quantitative comparison. The BioFire panel showed excellent qualitative agreement with comparator methods, with most discordant results occurring in samples with high cycle threshold values, indicative of low pathogen load, and involving pathogens such as Bordetella pertussis and influenza viruses. Exploratory quantitative analyses revealed strong correlations between BioFire-derived cycle threshold values and those from reference assays, suggesting a reproducible cross-platform relationship. The authors conclude that while the panel performs well for qualitative detection, the semi-quantitative findings are preliminary and should be interpreted cautiously given the exploratory nature of the study and the inherent challenges of cross-platform comparisons.
items.venue: European journal of clinical microbiology & infectious diseases : official publication of the European Society of Clinical Microbiology
items.citation: Vitale A, Mahler Z, Loup V et al.. Performance evaluation of the BioFire respiratory panel 2.1 plus and exploratory analysis of Ct values for respiratory pathogen quantification.. European journal of clinical microbiology & infectious diseases : official publication of the European Society of Clinical Microbiology. 2026

items.title: Pneumococcal Detection and Bacterial Co-Detection in Children After COVID-19: A Two-Year Multiplex PCR Study.
items.summary: The study investigated pediatric pneumococcal respiratory infections and their bacterial co-detections during the two years immediately following the COVID-19 pandemic, a period when lifted non-pharmaceutical interventions were expected to alter pathogen circulation. Researchers retrospectively analyzed respiratory specimens from children aged up to eighteen years using a multiplex real-time PCR panel testing for seven bacterial pathogens at a tertiary pediatric hospital in Romania across 2022 and 2023. The findings revealed a substantial rise in pneumococcal positivity over the two-year period, alongside a significant increase in co-detection of Streptococcus pneumoniae with Haemophilus influenzae, while single pneumococcal detections declined proportionally. Community-acquired pneumonia diagnoses increased markedly in parallel, with boys and young urban children representing the predominant affected group. The authors conclude that this post-pandemic rebound in pneumococcal disease and shifting co-detection patterns supports the use of syndromic multiplex PCR testing to guide rapid pediatric diagnosis and antimicrobial stewardship.
items.citation: Stavăr-Matei L, Țocu L, Nechita A et al.. Pneumococcal Detection and Bacterial Co-Detection in Children After COVID-19: A Two-Year Multiplex PCR Study.. Biomedicines. 2026

items.title: Post-pandemic resurgence and co-detectiondynamics of respiratory pathogens revealed by syndromic multiplex PCR.
items.summary: This study investigated the distribution, co-detection patterns, and seasonal dynamics of respiratory pathogens in patients with suspected respiratory tract infections at a tertiary-care hospital in Türkiye during the post-pandemic period. Using the BioFire FilmArray syndromic multiplex PCR panel, researchers retrospectively analysed several hundred respiratory samples from patients over a one-year period to characterise pathogen frequencies, demographic associations, and temporal trends. At least one pathogen was detected in over half of samples, with Rhinovirus/Enterovirus being the most prevalent, followed by influenza A, adenovirus, SARS-CoV-2, RSV, and Mycoplasma pneumoniae. Co-detections occurred in approximately one-fifth of positive samples and were dominated by Rhinovirus/Enterovirus, while sequential sample analysis suggested that many apparent co-infections may reflect residual nucleic acid from prior rather than concurrent infections. The findings highlight marked seasonal variation in pathogen circulation, a strong correlation between RSV and influenza A activity, and the broader clinical and epidemiological utility of syndromic multiplex PCR for respiratory diagnostics in the post-pandemic era.
items.citation: Aygar IS. Post-pandemic resurgence and co-detectiondynamics of respiratory pathogens revealed by syndromic multiplex PCR.. BMC microbiology. 2026

items.title: Recent Advances in Microfluidic Chip Technology for Laboratory Medicine: Innovations and Artificial Intelligence Integration
items.summary: This review examines recent advances in microfluidic chip technology, commonly referred to as lab-on-a-chip systems, and their growing role in laboratory medicine and clinical diagnostics. The authors conducted a comprehensive review of innovations in chip design and fabrication, including developments in three-dimensional printing, modular architectures, and flexible substrates, alongside progress in biosensor integration for detecting circulating tumor cells, nucleic acids, and exosomes. A central focus is the convergence of microfluidics with artificial intelligence and machine learning, which has enabled the creation of smart diagnostic platforms capable of automated analysis, self-optimization, and clinical decision support across applications including cancer diagnostics, infectious disease detection, and point-of-care testing. The review also highlights emerging applications in neuroscience diagnostics and microbiome profiling as indicators of the technology's broad potential. The authors identify persistent challenges in manufacturing scalability, clinical validation, and system integration, and outline future directions for translating these platforms into routine clinical and public health practice.
items.citation: Hong Cai, Dongxia Wang, Yiqun Zhao et al.. Recent Advances in Microfluidic Chip Technology for Laboratory Medicine: Innovations and Artificial Intelligence Integration. Biosensors. 2026

items.title: Interplay of Microbiome, Oxidative Stress and Inflammation in Health and Disease
items.summary: This review paper examines the complex relationships among the human microbiome, oxidative stress, and inflammation in the context of both health and disease. The authors synthesized current evidence on how microbial metabolites and specific probiotic strains help maintain redox homeostasis through pathways including immune regulation, modulation of inflammatory processes, and influence over mitochondrial reactive oxygen species production and antioxidant signaling. Conversely, oxidative stress and inflammation can reciprocally alter microbial diversity and function, creating a self-reinforcing cycle that disrupts physiological homeostasis. The review highlights how dynamic changes in microbiome composition, shaped by various environmental and biological factors, are increasingly understood to be intertwined with oxidative and inflammatory processes in ways that contribute to the development of disease.
items.citation: Lourdes Herrera-Quintana, Pablo Iturbe-Sanz, Jorge Olivares-Arancibia et al.. Interplay of Microbiome, Oxidative Stress and Inflammation in Health and Disease. Antioxidants. 2026

items.title: Advanced Diagnostic Technologies and Molecular Biomarkers in Periodontitis: Systemic Health Implications and Translational Perspectives
items.summary: This review examined advances in diagnostic technologies and molecular biomarkers for periodontitis, a chronic inflammatory disease with known links to cardiometabolic and other systemic conditions where conventional clinical tools offer limited insight into underlying molecular activity. The authors conducted a narrative review of literature spanning roughly eight years, focusing on point-of-care biosensing platforms and biomarkers assessed in biological matrices including saliva, gingival crevicular fluid, blood, and dental plaque. Substantial progress was identified in multiplex optical and electrochemical platforms, including microfluidic systems and early intraoral wearable sensors capable of quantifying host-response proteins such as matrix metalloproteinase-8, cytokines, and chemokines. Omics-derived biomarkers including microRNAs, cell-free DNA, extracellular vesicle signals, proteomic profiles, and microbiome classifiers showed promise for diagnosis and disease monitoring while providing mechanistic connections to systemic inflammation. The authors concluded that these technologies currently hold value as adjuncts for risk stratification and longitudinal monitoring rather than replacements for conventional examination, and that broader clinical impact will require standardized disease definitions, harmonized sampling protocols, multicenter validation, and regulatory-grade evidence for diagnostics and artificial intelligence tools classified as medical devices.
items.citation: Sebastian Biesiadecki, Monika Janeczko, Joanna Kozak et al.. Advanced Diagnostic Technologies and Molecular Biomarkers in Periodontitis: Systemic Health Implications and Translational Perspectives. Journal of Clinical Medicine. 2026

items.title: STING controls glycolysis and histone lactylation to drive macrophage metabolic reprogramming in postoperative ileus
items.summary: This paper investigated the role of STING (stimulator of interferon genes) in regulating macrophage metabolism and inflammation in the context of postoperative ileus, a condition marked by dysregulated intestinal inflammation that impairs gastrointestinal motility. Using LPS-stimulated macrophages and STING knockout cells, the researchers examined how STING influences glycolysis and associated epigenetic changes. They found that STING promotes glycolysis by stabilizing the transcription factor HIF1α, which in turn drives histone lactylation at the hexokinase 2 gene locus to increase chromatin accessibility, and that IRF3 directly binds the HK2 promoter to further amplify HK2 expression and sustain glycolytic flux. This work delineates a self-reinforcing feedback loop in which STING orchestrates metabolic reprogramming through epigenetic modification, linking innate immune signaling to macrophage polarization. The findings suggest that targeting STING could represent a therapeutic strategy to modulate macrophage metabolism, reduce inflammation, and improve recovery following surgery.
items.citation: Kai Chen, Guofang Li, Ye Cheng et al.. STING controls glycolysis and histone lactylation to drive macrophage metabolic reprogramming in postoperative ileus. Communications Biology. 2026

items.title: A temporal and spatial atlas of adaptive immune responses in the lymph node following viral infection
items.summary: This paper investigated how adaptive immune cells are spatially organized within lymph nodes during viral infection, with particular focus on how T and B cell responses develop over time. The researchers developed a method called AIR-SPACE, which integrates high-resolution spatial transcriptomics with long-read sequencing of T and B cell receptors to simultaneously capture gene expression and immune receptor repertoires in their native tissue context. Applying this approach to mouse lymph nodes at multiple time points following Vaccinia virus infection, they mapped the evolving adaptive immune response across space and time. Key findings included the identification of heterogeneous activation niches defined by interferon-gamma production early in infection, the delineation of subanatomical germinal center structures with evidence that plasma cells exit through the dark zone, and the demonstration that B cell clones are shared across multiple germinal centers within a single lymph node, suggesting these structures form an interconnected and dynamic network.
items.venue: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences
items.citation: Shaowen Jiang, Jahanara Freedman, Madhav Mantri et al.. A temporal and spatial atlas of adaptive immune responses in the lymph node following viral infection. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences. 2026

items.title: Multidrug-resistant Pseudomonas aeruginosa infections: current status, challenges, and prospects of phage therapy
items.summary: This review examines the growing threat of multidrug-resistant Pseudomonas aeruginosa, a pathogen distinguished by its extensive virulence factors, biofilm-forming capacity, and high associated mortality, for which conventional antibiotic treatments are becoming increasingly ineffective. The authors synthesize recent advances in phage therapy as an alternative antimicrobial strategy, surveying clinical applications, therapeutic approaches, and emerging technological platforms directed at this pathogen. The review also analyzes the resistance mechanisms that arise during phage treatment and proposes counterstrategies to overcome them, including optimized phage-antibiotic combinations, phage genome engineering, and adaptive therapeutic frameworks. The authors argue that phage therapy, owing to its precise bactericidal activity and favorable safety profile, represents a compelling avenue for addressing antimicrobial resistance in this context and for advancing toward broader clinical translation.
items.citation: Bo Yan, Can Yan, Yafang Ding et al.. Multidrug-resistant Pseudomonas aeruginosa infections: current status, challenges, and prospects of phage therapy. Frontiers in Microbiology. 2026

items.title: Fast ungated five-dimensional cardiac MRI on a 1.5 T MR-linac for MRI-guided radiotherapy
items.summary: This paper addressed the challenge of characterizing complex cardiorespiratory motion for stereotactic arrhythmia radio-ablation of ventricular tachycardia, where current five-dimensional MRI approaches are too slow for clinical use. The researchers developed a fast, ungated five-dimensional MRI reconstruction method called CMR-MOTUS, based on a three-dimensional Cartesian acquisition that jointly optimizes a motion-corrected reference image and low-rank deformation vector fields to explicitly disentangle respiratory and cardiac motion. The method was validated using digital and physical cardiorespiratory phantoms and then evaluated in healthy volunteers, with motion consistency compared against conventional two-dimensional cine MRI. Results demonstrated accurate reconstruction in both phantom settings and strong agreement with cine MRI motion measurements in volunteers, with minimal cardiac and respiratory motion errors. Critically, the entire workflow required only one minute of acquisition and six minutes of reconstruction, suggesting the approach is feasible for integration into clinical MRI-guided radiotherapy workflows.
authors: C. A. T. van den Berg
items.citation: M. L. Terpstra, T. E. Olausson, M. M. N. Aubert et al.. Fast ungated five-dimensional cardiac MRI on a 1.5 T MR-linac for MRI-guided radiotherapy. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Dual-Security for Indoor OFDM-ISAC Systems via Temporal Artificial Noise
items.summary: This paper investigates the challenge of simultaneously securing both communication and sensing functions in indoor orthogonal frequency division multiplexing-based integrated sensing and communication systems, a problem largely unaddressed by prior work that tends to focus on only one security aspect at a time. The authors consider a scenario where a sensing user is authorized to sense but may eavesdrop on communications, while a communication user is authorized to communicate but may attempt unauthorized sensing, representing a particularly challenging case of authorized eavesdroppers with privileged information. To address this dual-security problem, the paper proposes a temporal artificial noise scheme designed to degrade unauthorized sensing by enlarging time-domain sensing errors while simultaneously reducing the signal-to-interference-plus-noise ratio seen by an eavesdropping sensing user through standard receiver processing in the frequency domain. Crucially, the scheme is designed to preserve the legitimate performance of both authorized users. Numerical results demonstrate that the proposed temporal artificial noise approach effectively provides dual protection for both sensing and communication security without compromising the performance of legitimate users.
items.citation: Yinchao Yang, Yathreb Bouazizi, Prabhat Raj Gautam et al.. Dual-Security for Indoor OFDM-ISAC Systems via Temporal Artificial Noise. arXiv. 2026

items.title: AEGIS: Assay-Aware Protocol Validation and Runtime Monitoring for Open-Source Liquid Handling Robots
items.summary: AEGIS is a two-layer validation and monitoring system designed to address the lack of error detection in low-cost, open-source liquid handling robots used in self-driving laboratories. The first layer combines a curated assay rule database with a large language model to perform pre-flight inspection of robot protocols, catching semantically invalid procedures—such as tip reuse between incompatible samples—before execution begins. The second layer uses computer vision, combining object detection with a dimensionality-reduction world model applied to pipette trajectory footage, to detect physical failures such as missing tips or partial dispenses during live robot operation. Testing on a benchmark of protocols across multiple assay families and live demonstration on a physical robot showed strong validation performance and reliable detection of planted failures, with a cost-reduction strategy that avoids calling expensive vision-language models on every action. The authors claim AEGIS is the first open-source system to unify protocol-level assay-aware validation with runtime visual monitoring for affordable liquid handlers, and they identify transparent liquids and small pipette volumes as principled limits of the current approach.
items.citation: Priyanka V. Setty, Arvind Ramanathan, Ian Foster et al.. AEGIS: Assay-Aware Protocol Validation and Runtime Monitoring for Open-Source Liquid Handling Robots. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Who Became Financially Vulnerable After COVID-19? A Population-Level Machine Learning Analysis Using MEPS Data
items.summary: This study investigated how the COVID-19 pandemic affected healthcare financial vulnerability in the United States by comparing nationally representative survey data from before and after the pandemic. The researchers defined financial vulnerability as out-of-pocket healthcare spending exceeding ten percent of family income, then applied a combination of interpretable logistic regression and machine learning models, including random forest and gradient boosting, to identify predictors and assess whether pre-pandemic models remained valid in the post-pandemic period. Poverty status, insurance coverage, and prescription drug spending emerged as the strongest predictors of financial burden, and subgroup analyses revealed persistent disparities with some evidence of heightened burden among vulnerable populations after the pandemic. Notably, models trained on pre-pandemic data showed only modest declines in predictive performance when applied to post-pandemic data, indicating that the core determinants of healthcare financial vulnerability were relatively stable across the two periods. The authors suggest their approach demonstrates the utility of combining statistical and machine learning methods for population health surveillance and may inform policy efforts aimed at reducing financial barriers to care.
items.citation: Alexey Kresin, Zien Cheng, Ammar Ahad et al.. Who Became Financially Vulnerable After COVID-19? A Population-Level Machine Learning Analysis Using MEPS Data. arXiv. 2026

items.title: SciDiagramEdit: Learning to Edit Scientific Diagrams from Paper Revisions
items.summary: This paper addresses the challenge of automating edits to scientific figures in research papers based on natural-language instructions, a task complicated by the dense, heterogeneous visual elements that compose such figures. The researchers created a benchmark called SciDiagramEdit by mining before-and-after figure pairs from version histories on arXiv, using authors' own revision intent as grounding for the editing instructions. The system operates on editable vector sources of figures, allowing users to inspect and co-edit individual primitives alongside the agent. To handle the diversity of editing instructions, they employed an agentic learning approach called skill evolution, where an agentic proposer iteratively refines the agent's skill specification from execution traces across multiple training epochs. Their results show that this approach progressively improves edit accuracy, demonstrating that natural paper revisions can serve as an effective training signal for instruction-driven figure editing.
items.citation: Yasheng Sun, Zezi Zeng, Yifan Yang et al.. SciDiagramEdit: Learning to Edit Scientific Diagrams from Paper Revisions. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Dynamic Entanglement Distribution for Multi-User and Multi-Protocol Quantum Networking
items.summary: This paper investigated how to dynamically distribute quantum entanglement across multiple users and protocols in a metropolitan-scale quantum network. The researchers built a network using a quantum reconfigurable optical add-drop multiplexer to route polarisation-entangled photon pairs from a broadband source to six users over deployed campus and metropolitan optical fibre. The system supported programmable full-mesh, partial-mesh, and sliced sub-network configurations, allowing flexible allocation of entanglement resources based on link conditions and service requirements. The network demonstrated stable six-user full-mesh operation over an extended period, and the same infrastructure was shown to support multiple quantum protocols, including a secure user onboarding protocol combined with network flooding over multiple paths. The authors conclude that this architecture provides a viable route toward reconfigurable, service-oriented quantum networking over existing fibre infrastructure.
items.citation: Rui Wang, Marcus J. Clark, Obada Alia et al.. Dynamic Entanglement Distribution for Multi-User and Multi-Protocol Quantum Networking. arXiv. 2026

items.title: On-site serological screening of Usutu virus in wild birds: Field evaluation of a Lateral flow Microarray ImmunoAssay using the WHO REASSURED framework.
items.summary: This study evaluated a field-deployable serological test for detecting Usutu virus and West Nile virus antibodies in wild birds at capture sites in the Netherlands, where both viruses are of growing concern. The researchers used a species-independent Lateral Flow Microarray ImmunoAssay capable of simultaneously detecting antibodies against both viruses, testing it on wild birds captured at ringing sites and comparing results against the gold-standard neutralization test. The assay demonstrated high sensitivity and specificity for Usutu virus detection, and successfully identified seroconversion in a Eurasian blackbird confirmed by the reference method. Performance was also assessed using the WHO REASSURED framework for point-of-care diagnostics, where the assay achieved a strong overall score. The findings support the feasibility of this rapid, equipment-light, multi-analyte format for on-site arbovirus surveillance in wild bird populations, including in resource-limited settings, pending further optimization and broader validation.
items.citation: Godarzi B, Holicki CM, Chandler FD et al.. On-site serological screening of Usutu virus in wild birds: Field evaluation of a Lateral flow Microarray ImmunoAssay using the WHO REASSURED framework.. Journal of virological methods. 2026

items.title: Dual-enhancement lateral flow immunoassay <i>via</i> gold-decorated ferritin nanoprobes and SpyTag/SpyCatcher site-directed conjugation.
items.summary: This study addressed the limited sensitivity of conventional lateral flow immunoassays, which arise from weak optical signals, randomly oriented antibodies, and inconsistent nanoprobe fabrication. The researchers developed a dual-enhancement approach combining gold-decorated ferritin nanocages with SpyTag/SpyCatcher chemistry, where engineered ferritin displaying SpyTag peptides served as a template for controlled gold nanocluster growth while retaining functional sites for subsequent antibody attachment. The gold deposition produced highly uniform core-shell nanostructures with amplified optical intensity, and the SpyCatcher-fused antibodies then formed spontaneous covalent isopeptide bonds with the ferritin surface, ensuring precise, site-directed orientation rather than random adsorption. Testing against human chorionic gonadotropin as a model analyte, the optimized assay achieved a detection limit dramatically lower than commercial gold nanoparticle-based strips, with strong performance and reliable spike-recovery rates in urine samples. The authors conclude that this synergistic strategy substantially enhances point-of-care diagnostic sensitivity and offers a broadly applicable platform for real-sample diagnostics.
items.venue: Analytical methods : advancing methods and applications
items.citation: Yuan X, Zhu X, Xu Y et al.. Dual-enhancement lateral flow immunoassay <i>via</i> gold-decorated ferritin nanoprobes and SpyTag/SpyCatcher site-directed conjugation.. Analytical methods : advancing methods and applications. 2026

items.title: Ag-rich Ag-Au nanoshells based lateral flow immunoassay for rapid and sensitive detection of bovine viral diarrhea virus.
items.citation: Ding Y, Zhang W, Huang Y et al.. Ag-rich Ag-Au nanoshells based lateral flow immunoassay for rapid and sensitive detection of bovine viral diarrhea virus.. Mikrochimica acta. 2026

items.title: First implementation of a polymer-polymer aqueous two-phase system to improve lateral-flow immunoassay sensitivity via dextranase integration on paper.
items.summary: This paper investigated whether a polymer-polymer aqueous two-phase system could be integrated with lateral-flow immunoassays to improve their sensitivity, addressing a longstanding limitation of low detection thresholds in point-of-care diagnostics. The researchers used a poly(ethylene glycol)-dextran system to preconcentrate protein targets into the dextran-rich phase, but overcame the previously prohibitive problem of high viscosity by incorporating the enzyme dextranase to degrade dextran and restore fluid flow on paper. Enzymatic and fluid imbibition studies were conducted to optimize conditions, and the approach was validated using the protein transferrin spiked into artificial saliva. The integrated system achieved a tenfold improvement in the limit of detection compared to a conventional lateral-flow immunoassay, and a simplified format with dextranase dehydrated directly onto the paper strip produced comparable sensitivity gains while reducing liquid handling steps. This work represents the first successful coupling of a polymer-polymer aqueous two-phase system with a lateral-flow immunoassay and opens the door to using this broader class of phase systems for point-of-care diagnostic enhancement.
items.venue: Analytical sciences : the international journal of the Japan Society for Analytical Chemistry
items.citation: Nicklen FD, Hadeed CH, Zhang F et al.. First implementation of a polymer-polymer aqueous two-phase system to improve lateral-flow immunoassay sensitivity via dextranase integration on paper.. Analytical sciences : the international journal of the Japan Society for Analytical Chemistry. 2026

items.title: Lateral flow immunoassay detection of soluble AXL for pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma diagnosis.
items.summary: This study addressed the critical need for early diagnostic tools for pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma, a cancer characterized by late-stage detection and poor survival due to a lack of reliable biomarkers. The researchers developed and optimized a quantitative colorimetric lateral flow immunoassay using gold nanoparticle conjugates to detect soluble AXL, a cleaved form of the AXL receptor tyrosine kinase, in plasma samples. Systematic optimization of antibody concentrations, buffer composition, and membrane type was performed to improve sensitivity and reproducibility. Clinical evaluation showed that the assay accurately discriminated patients with pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma from healthy controls, with results validated against conventional enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay. The authors claim this represents the first quantitative lateral flow immunoassay for soluble AXL detection in plasma and propose it as a promising point-of-care diagnostic tool that could facilitate earlier detection of this cancer.
items.citation: Vázquez-Bellón N, Rodríguez-Núñez M, Martínez-Bosch N et al.. Lateral flow immunoassay detection of soluble AXL for pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma diagnosis.. Talanta. 2026

items.title: Self-Contained Lateral-Flow Microfluidic Bead-Based Assay for Rapid Quantification of Early-Stage Kidney Biomarkers.
items.summary: This paper addressed the challenge of diagnosing kidney disease early, focusing on two emerging biomarkers, neutrophil gelatinase-associated lipocalin and cystatin C, whose quantification has previously required centralized laboratory infrastructure. The researchers developed the lateral-flow microfluidic bead-based assay, a self-contained device that integrates a microfluidic chip with paper-based components to run automated immunoassays rapidly using only a tiny volume of blood or urine. The system's modular architecture separates reagent storage and flow control from the binding and readout stages, providing design flexibility while enabling results within twenty minutes. Validated against standard enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay, the platform demonstrated comparable sensitivity with an extended dynamic range, and was successfully applied to neonatal mouse models of polycystic kidney disease, detecting biomarker elevations consistent with cyst progression. The authors propose this scalable platform as a tool for preclinical research and point-of-care kidney disease diagnostics outside of centralized laboratory settings.
items.citation: Porro G, Cristofori MS, Gagnieux C et al.. Self-Contained Lateral-Flow Microfluidic Bead-Based Assay for Rapid Quantification of Early-Stage Kidney Biomarkers.. Analytical chemistry. 2026

items.title: Recent Advances in Microfluidic Chip Technology for Laboratory Medicine: Innovations and Artificial Intelligence Integration
items.summary: This review examines recent innovations in microfluidic chip technology and their applications in laboratory medicine, focusing on advances in design, fabrication, biosensor integration, and artificial intelligence. The authors surveyed developments in chip manufacturing approaches including three-dimensional printing, modular architectures, and flexible substrates, which have improved device performance, sensitivity, and clinical utility while minimizing required sample volumes. Integration of advanced biosensors enables real-time detection of circulating tumor cells, nucleic acids, and exosomes, supporting applications spanning cancer diagnostics, infectious disease detection, point-of-care testing, and personalized medicine. The convergence of microfluidics with artificial intelligence and machine learning has produced smart diagnostic platforms capable of self-optimization, automated analysis, and clinical decision support, with emerging uses in neuroscience diagnostics and microbiome profiling further broadening the technology's scope. The review also addresses persistent challenges in manufacturing scalability, clinical validation, and system integration that must be overcome before next-generation microfluidic platforms can be widely adopted in routine clinical and public health settings.
items.citation: Hong Cai, Dongxia Wang, Yiqun Zhao et al.. Recent Advances in Microfluidic Chip Technology for Laboratory Medicine: Innovations and Artificial Intelligence Integration. Biosensors. 2026

items.title: Biosensing technologies for foodborne pathogen detection and healthcare: principles, emerging materials, and intelligent platforms
items.summary: This review examines advanced biosensing technologies for detecting priority foodborne pathogens, specifically Escherichia coli, Salmonella, and Listeria monocytogenes, which continue to threaten global public health. The authors survey electrochemical, optical, and microfluidic sensing platforms, analyzing how innovations in functional nanomaterials, aptamers, nanozymes, and surface engineering have improved sensor sensitivity, specificity, and performance in complex food matrices. A central theme is the integration of artificial intelligence and machine learning with these biosensors, enabling intelligent pattern recognition, real-time analytics, and high-throughput multiplexing that transform conventional detection systems into smart diagnostic tools. The review critically evaluates biosensor design principles, signal transduction mechanisms, and performance validation while identifying key challenges such as matrix interference, bioreceptor instability, scalability limitations, and the absence of regulatory standardization, along with mitigation strategies including ratiometric sensing, microfluidic pre-treatment, and explainable AI. Looking ahead, the authors advocate for self-powered biosensors, modular pathogen detection panels, blockchain integration, and standardized validation pipelines as pathways toward regulation-ready platforms for next-generation food safety monitoring.
items.citation: Purshottam J. Assudani, Balakrishnan . P., Anny Leema A et al.. Biosensing technologies for foodborne pathogen detection and healthcare: principles, emerging materials, and intelligent platforms. Microchimica Acta. 2026

items.title: Electrochemical Biosensors for Cancer Diagnosis and Prognosis Using Protein Biomarkers: Current Trends, Advances, and Clinical Translation Potential
items.summary: This review paper examines the current state of electrochemical biosensors designed to detect protein biomarkers in blood for cancer diagnosis and prognosis. The authors survey general strategies for electrochemical detection of serum proteins and catalog recent advances in liquid biopsy biosensors targeting cancer-related circulating proteins, noting that many approaches have achieved impressive analytical sensitivity. The review critically assesses the major obstacles hindering clinical translation, including matrix effects from complex biological samples, insufficient validation with patient-derived specimens, challenges in accounting for interpatient variability, and practical hurdles such as manufacturing scalability, cost, and long-term stability. Particular emphasis is placed on the analytical and clinical validation requirements that biosensors must meet to progress toward real-world diagnostic use. The authors argue that while the field has made substantial technical advances, bridging the gap between laboratory performance and clinical deployment remains a critical and underaddressed challenge.
items.citation: Michael E. J. Lopez Mujica, Elena E. Ferapontova. Electrochemical Biosensors for Cancer Diagnosis and Prognosis Using Protein Biomarkers: Current Trends, Advances, and Clinical Translation Potential. Sensors. 2026

items.title: Oxidative Stress in Health and Disease: Mechanisms and Therapeutic Perspectives
items.summary: This review examines the dual biological roles of reactive oxygen species, which act as essential signaling molecules at physiological concentrations but drive disease pathology when produced in excess or dysregulated. The authors synthesize current knowledge on the major types, sources, and physiological functions of reactive oxygen species alongside the molecular pathways through which oxidative stress causes cellular damage and signaling disruption. The review also critically evaluates contemporary and emerging methods for detecting and quantifying oxidative stress, spanning biochemical assays, fluorescent probes, biosensors, and advanced imaging approaches. The contribution of redox imbalance to major diseases including cancer, diabetes, cardiovascular conditions, neurodegeneration, and inflammatory disorders is examined. The authors conclude by outlining future directions in precision redox medicine, emphasizing targeted antioxidant-based diagnostic and therapeutic strategies informed by improved detection technologies and a deeper mechanistic understanding of redox biology.
items.citation: Shehwaz Anwar, Hajed Obaid A. Alharbi, Ali Yousif Babiker et al.. Oxidative Stress in Health and Disease: Mechanisms and Therapeutic Perspectives. International Journal of Molecular Sciences. 2026

items.title: Advanced Diagnostic Technologies and Molecular Biomarkers in Periodontitis: Systemic Health Implications and Translational Perspectives
items.summary: This review examined the current landscape of advanced diagnostic technologies and molecular biomarkers in periodontitis, with particular attention to their implications for systemic health. The authors conducted a narrative review of literature spanning nearly a decade, focusing on point-of-care biosensing platforms and molecular biomarkers derived from biological matrices including saliva, gingival crevicular fluid, blood, and dental plaque. The review found that multiplex optical and electrochemical platforms, including microfluidic systems and intraoral wearable sensors, have enabled quantification of host-response proteins such as matrix metalloproteinases and cytokines, while omics-derived markers including microRNAs, extracellular vesicle signals, and microbiome classifiers show promise for diagnosis and mechanistic links to systemic inflammation. Despite this progress, clinical translation remains constrained by study heterogeneity, spectrum bias, and inadequate external validation, meaning these technologies currently serve best as adjuncts for risk stratification and longitudinal monitoring rather than replacements for conventional periodontal examination. The authors conclude that realizing meaningful clinical and public-health impact will require standardized disease definitions, harmonized protocols, multicenter validation across comorbidity groups, and regulatory-grade evidence for diagnostics and artificial intelligence tools classified as medical devices.
items.citation: Sebastian Biesiadecki, Monika Janeczko, Joanna Kozak et al.. Advanced Diagnostic Technologies and Molecular Biomarkers in Periodontitis: Systemic Health Implications and Translational Perspectives. Journal of Clinical Medicine. 2026

items.title: Emerging liquid biopsy tools to analyse cancer biomarkers: electrochemical sensors and extracellular vesicle analysis
items.summary: This paper reviews emerging technologies for liquid biopsy in cancer diagnostics, focusing on electrochemical sensors and extracellular vesicles as complementary approaches to biomarker detection. The authors discuss the limitations of widely used methods such as ELISAs, including cost, storage constraints, and insufficient sensitivity for low-abundance biomarkers, and argue that electrochemical sensor platforms offer advantages in sensitivity, direct quantitation, and suitability for use outside centralised laboratories. The review also examines how extracellular vesicles, by encapsulating and protecting nucleic acid and protein cargo from degradation, may enable collective enrichment of multiple tumour-derived biomarkers from blood samples. The authors conclude that advances in these analytical technologies, combined with improved understanding of biomarker biology and clinical utility, could meaningfully improve cancer detection, characterisation, and monitoring while reducing invasiveness and cost.
items.citation: Anitha Devadoss, Michael Furness, N. Wu et al.. Emerging liquid biopsy tools to analyse cancer biomarkers: electrochemical sensors and extracellular vesicle analysis. Biochemical Society Transactions. 2026

items.title: MotionForesight: Re-purposing Video Models for Future 3D Scene-Flow Prediction
items.summary: This paper investigates how to predict the future three-dimensional motion of objects during human-object interactions using only passive monocular video observation. The authors developed MotionForesight, a system that takes a short video context as input and forecasts future 3D scene-flow trajectories for points on a manipulated object, without making assumptions about object properties or requiring auxiliary inputs such as language. The core insight is that pretrained video prediction models already contain rich priors about how objects move during interactions, and these priors can be redirected from pixel-space prediction toward geometric motion forecasting by replacing future RGB and depth information with learned mask latents and training a lightweight adapter. The system generates pseudo-ground-truth tracks from complete video clips, freezes the large pretrained video and tracking components, and trains only the adapter on a relatively small set of human interaction videos. The resulting model generalizes to diverse out-of-distribution objects, environments, and viewpoints, and outperforms substantially larger models trained on far more data, demonstrating that video priors can be efficiently repurposed for geometric forecasting relevant to embodied intelligence.
items.citation: Homanga Bharadhwaj, Yash Jangir. MotionForesight: Re-purposing Video Models for Future 3D Scene-Flow Prediction. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Physics-enhanced reinforcement learning for real-time optimal control of dynamical systems
items.summary: The paper investigates how to make reinforcement learning more practical for controlling high-dimensional, nonlinear dynamical systems, where standard approaches suffer from poor sample efficiency and the curse of dimensionality. The authors developed a framework called Physics-EnhAnced Reinforcement Learning (PEARL), which combines reinforcement learning with classical optimal control techniques by introducing an actor-adjoint algorithm that uses automatic differentiation and adjoint-based sensitivity computations to calculate policy gradients, with neural networks approximating future returns over short horizons. This design reduces the number of required environment interactions while avoiding the gradient instabilities that typically arise in long-horizon optimization. Testing on two parametric navigation problems in unsteady fluid flows demonstrated that PEARL outperforms state-of-the-art reinforcement learning algorithms, generalizes across multiple system configurations, and successfully scales to high-dimensional state and action spaces without needing dimensionality reduction or multi-agent decomposition.
items.citation: Matteo Tomasetto, Nicolò Botteghi, Gabriele Bruni et al.. Physics-enhanced reinforcement learning for real-time optimal control of dynamical systems. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Data-driven identification of multiscale self-similarity and asymptotic matching
items.summary: This paper addresses the identification of self-similarity in fluid mechanical systems, which is key to understanding scaling behaviour in turbulent flows. Building on an existing data-driven framework for single-scale self-similarity, the authors developed an algorithm capable of systematically identifying multiscale self-similarity from numerical data without prior knowledge of governing equations. The algorithm was applied to turbulent channel flow and both early- and late-stage homogeneous decaying turbulence, successfully recovering inner and outer similarity structures in each case. In the intermediate regions where inner and outer scales are both irrelevant, the algorithm independently recovered well-known scaling laws — the logarithmic law for turbulent channel flow and the classical energy spectrum power law for decaying turbulence — while also uncovering corrections to both laws linked to previously proposed alternative scaling theories. The study also revealed that early- and late-stage decaying turbulence share the same inner similarity structure but differ in their outer similarity expressions, reflecting their distinct dissipation law regimes.
items.citation: Kaixin Zhu, Nikos Bempedelis, Konstantinos Steiros. Data-driven identification of multiscale self-similarity and asymptotic matching. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Learning Standard Model structure from LHC data with Riemannian flow matching
items.summary: This paper investigates whether a single generative machine learning model can learn fundamental Standard Model physics directly from raw collider data, without being explicitly programmed with that knowledge. The authors developed ShellFlow, a transformer-based model using Riemannian conditional flow matching, which generates particle four-momenta constrained to their on-shell manifolds using only the on-shell condition and the invariant-mass formula as physics inputs. The model was trained on approximately one billion real proton-proton collision events from publicly available ATLAS Open Data and given no other physics information. From this single training run, the model successfully learned to reproduce particle kinematics, the positions of dilepton resonances including the J/ψ, Υ, and Z bosons, the leptonic Weinberg angle, and the W boson and top quark masses, as well as inter-particle correlations that were never part of the training objective. The results demonstrate that a substantial portion of Standard Model structure spanning five decades of invariant mass is directly learnable from recorded collision data alone.
items.citation: Midori Kato, Kevin A. Urquía-Calderón, Inar Timiryasov et al.. Learning Standard Model structure from LHC data with Riemannian flow matching. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Piecewise smooth stationary Euler flows with support in a neighborhood of a helix
items.summary: The paper constructs stationary solutions to the three-dimensional incompressible Euler equations that possess helical symmetry and are spatially supported near a helix. The approach reformulates the problem as a nonlinear overdetermined elliptic boundary value problem using a stream-function framework, requiring simultaneous prescription of Dirichlet and nonconstant Neumann boundary conditions. A central analytical challenge, addressed as a key step in the proof, is solving this genuinely anisotropic overdetermined elliptic problem. The resulting vortex structures are piecewise smooth and exhibit intrinsically anisotropic cross-sections whose leading-order shape is elliptic rather than radial, with a nontrivial third Fourier mode in the boundary geometry that reflects helical effects absent from earlier axisymmetric constructions.
items.citation: Daniel Peralta-Salas, Jie Wan. Piecewise smooth stationary Euler flows with support in a neighborhood of a helix. arXiv. 2026

items.title: A Kalman Filter-Assisted Data-Predictive SAR ADC With Reduced Switching Energy for Low-Power Applications
items.summary: This paper addresses the energy efficiency limitations of successive approximation register analog-to-digital converters used in low-power IoT and wearable health monitoring applications. The key bottleneck targeted is the high switching energy consumed by capacitive DAC transitions, particularly during the most significant bit decisions that dominate total power consumption. The proposed solution integrates a Kalman filter as a recursive state estimator that predicts the four most significant bits from prior conversion history before each cycle, allowing those capacitors to be switched simultaneously in parallel rather than sequentially. This approach eliminates redundant DAC transitions, reduces the conversion cycle length by four clock periods, and is complemented by an optimized switching scheme at the hardware level. The resulting design, implemented in a standard CMOS process, achieves approximately half the switching energy and cuts total power consumption by roughly half compared to conventional operation, while maintaining signal quality suitable for energy-constrained wireless sensor networks.
items.citation: Xiyuan Feng, Yuxiang Zhao, Jie Xiong et al.. A Kalman Filter-Assisted Data-Predictive SAR ADC With Reduced Switching Energy for Low-Power Applications. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Recent Advances in Microfluidic Chip Technology for Laboratory Medicine: Innovations and Artificial Intelligence Integration
items.summary: This review paper examines recent advances in microfluidic chip technology, commonly known as lab-on-a-chip systems, and their applications in laboratory medicine. The authors conducted a comprehensive review of innovations in chip design and fabrication, including developments such as three-dimensional printing, modular substrates, and integrated biosensors that enable real-time detection of circulating tumor cells, nucleic acids, and exosomes. A central focus is the convergence of microfluidics with artificial intelligence and machine learning, which has produced smart diagnostic platforms capable of self-optimization and clinical decision support across applications including cancer diagnostics, infectious disease detection, and point-of-care testing. The review finds that these combined technological advances have substantially improved device sensitivity, automation, and clinical applicability, while also extending the reach of microfluidic platforms into emerging areas such as neuroscience diagnostics and microbiome profiling. The authors also identify persistent challenges in manufacturing scalability, clinical validation, and system integration that must be addressed before next-generation microfluidic technologies can be routinely adopted in clinical and public health settings.
items.citation: Hong Cai, Dongxia Wang, Yiqun Zhao et al.. Recent Advances in Microfluidic Chip Technology for Laboratory Medicine: Innovations and Artificial Intelligence Integration. Biosensors. 2026

items.title: Brief Report: Association Between Pharmacologic Tenofovir Adherence Measures and Subsequent 24-Week Viral Load Outcomes for People With HIV in South Africa.
items.summary: This study investigated whether objective pharmacologic measures of antiretroviral therapy adherence could predict future HIV viral load outcomes among people with HIV in South Africa. Researchers compared three tenofovir concentration measures — a point-of-care urine antibody-based assay, quantitative urine tenofovir via liquid-chromatography-mass-spectrometry, and tenofovir-diphosphate in dried blood spots — and examined their associations with current and subsequent viral load at twenty-four weeks using logistic regression. Tenofovir-diphosphate concentrations in dried blood spots were significantly and negatively associated with viremia at twenty-four weeks, suggesting this measure can help predict future treatment failure, while neither urine-based measure showed a significant association with subsequent viral load. Undetectable point-of-care urine tenofovir results were associated with current viremia, lower CD4 counts, and likely recent antiretroviral therapy initiation rather than non-adherence in established patients. The findings suggest that dried blood spot tenofovir-diphosphate measurement is the most useful of the three adherence metrics for anticipating future viremia and guiding targeted interventions.
items.venue: Journal of acquired immune deficiency syndromes (1999)
items.citation: Bodley N, Govender K, Moodley P et al.. Brief Report: Association Between Pharmacologic Tenofovir Adherence Measures and Subsequent 24-Week Viral Load Outcomes for People With HIV in South Africa.. Journal of acquired immune deficiency syndromes (1999). 2026

items.title: Feasibility of integrating a point of care triage test into routine antiretroviral therapy monitoring in Mozambique: a qualitative evaluation
items.summary: This study explored the feasibility and acceptability of integrating a point-of-care triage test measuring interferon-gamma-induced protein 10 into routine antiretroviral therapy monitoring in Mozambique, where timely viral load testing remains constrained by laboratory delays and health system challenges. Using a qualitative approach nested within a clinical evaluation at two primary healthcare facilities, researchers conducted multiple rounds of interviews with people living with HIV on antiretroviral therapy, as well as one-off interviews with healthcare providers and programme stakeholders across health system levels. Participants broadly valued routine viral load monitoring but identified significant barriers to access, and the point-of-care triage test was found to be widely acceptable, with same-day results perceived to reduce patient anxiety, support treatment adherence, and enable more timely clinical decisions. Providers and stakeholders recognised the test's potential to improve monitoring efficiency by prioritising those needing confirmatory viral load testing, though concerns about test accuracy and the necessity of maintaining confirmatory testing were noted. The authors conclude that successful integration would require investment in training, workflow adaptation, and quality assurance, but that the test could meaningfully strengthen differentiated care approaches within existing antiretroviral therapy monitoring systems.
