items.citation: Myburgh H, Saura-Lázaro A, van den Bogaart E et al.. Feasibility of integrating a point of care triage test into routine antiretroviral therapy monitoring in Mozambique: a qualitative evaluation. 2026

items.title: Implementation bottlenecks of near point of care HIV viral load monitoring for children and young people in Tanzania: A Qualitative Study.
items.summary: This qualitative study examined the barriers and facilitators healthcare workers in Tanzania encountered when implementing near point-of-care HIV viral load monitoring for children and young people living with HIV. Using clinic observations and in-depth interviews with healthcare workers conducted at multiple time points, the researchers applied a structured thematic framework analysis to identify implementation determinants across intervention, provider, organisational, and broader contextual levels. The findings showed that near point-of-care testing improved result turnaround times and enabled same-day counselling, which motivated both staff and patients, and was supported by its compatibility with existing workflows, teamwork, and management commitment. However, significant bottlenecks included prolonged patient waiting times, shortages of physical infrastructure such as rooms and electricity, staff shortages, and delays caused by sample transport to nearby laboratories. The authors conclude that realising the full benefits of near point-of-care viral load monitoring requires targeted investment in staff training, infrastructure, sample handling processes, and alignment with clinical guidelines.
items.citation: Msoka P, Mtenga A, Maro R et al.. Implementation bottlenecks of near point of care HIV viral load monitoring for children and young people in Tanzania: A Qualitative Study.. PloS one. 2026

items.title: Baseline Data on Viral Suppression, Treatment Adherence and Mental Health in Peripartum Girls and Young Women With HIV in Zimbabwe.
items.summary: This study examined viral suppression, treatment adherence, drug exposure, and mental health among peripartum adolescent girls and young women living with HIV in Zimbabwe, a population known to have poorer viral control than older women. Participants aged fifteen to twenty-four years who were pregnant or up to twenty-four months postpartum and receiving tenofovir-based antiretroviral therapy were enrolled in a randomized controlled and observational cohort study, with point-of-care viral load testing and urine tenofovir assays conducted at baseline alongside mental health screening. The study found high rates of viral suppression at enrollment, which were strongly associated with detectable urine tenofovir levels and higher self-reported adherence, while food security, financial security, and being married were also positively linked to viral suppression. Mental health outcomes were generally favorable, with the majority of participants showing minimal or no depression and anxiety, though moderate HIV-related stigma was common. These baseline findings suggest that dolutegravir-based regimens are associated with strong virological outcomes in this peripartum population, and that objective adherence measures such as urine drug testing are particularly informative predictors of viral control.
items.venue: Journal of the International AIDS Society
items.citation: Chawana-Mutingwende TD, Ngara B, Mudadi LS et al.. Baseline Data on Viral Suppression, Treatment Adherence and Mental Health in Peripartum Girls and Young Women With HIV in Zimbabwe.. Journal of the International AIDS Society. 2026

items.title: Lipid metabolism in homeostasis and disease
items.summary: This review examines the role of lipid metabolism in maintaining cellular homeostasis and how its dysregulation contributes to a broad range of diseases. The authors surveyed the research history of lipid metabolism and focused particularly on how lipid functions, transport, and signaling pathways regulate immune cell behavior, including proliferation, differentiation, and activation in macrophages, T cells, B cells, and neutrophils. The review also explores multilevel regulatory mechanisms—spanning genetic, epigenetic, posttranscriptional, and posttranslational levels—that govern lipid metabolism in immune contexts. The authors discuss the pathological implications of lipid metabolic dysregulation across autoimmune diseases, cancer, neurodegeneration, cardiovascular disease, aging, and metabolic disorders. Finally, the review synthesizes current and emerging therapeutic strategies targeting lipid metabolism, surveying clinical trial progress and identifying lipid-derived biomarkers and novel treatment approaches as future priorities.
items.citation: Zhili Li, Wende Deng, Lanxuan Yang et al.. Lipid metabolism in homeostasis and disease. Signal Transduction and Targeted Therapy. 2026

items.title: Sepsis
items.citation: Mervyn Singer, Derek C Angus, Djillali Annane et al.. Sepsis. The Lancet. 2026

items.title: Exosomal bioactive molecules: molecular regulation, functional heterogeneity, and translational implications in disease diagnostics and therapeutics
items.summary: This review paper examines exosomes as nanoscale extracellular vesicles involved in intercellular communication, focusing on the molecular mechanisms that regulate their biogenesis, cargo loading, and functional heterogeneity. The authors synthesize current understanding of how molecular machinery, including endosomal sorting complexes, lipid domains, RNA-binding proteins, and post-translational modifications, governs the selective packaging of bioactive molecules such as proteins, lipids, and nucleic acids into exosomes. The review also surveys state-of-the-art technologies and omics platforms used to characterize exosomal content, and evaluates their clinical utility as liquid biopsy biomarkers across cancer, metabolic, and neurodegenerative diseases. Beyond diagnostics, the paper explores therapeutic applications of both native and engineered exosomes in drug delivery, wound healing, and neuroregeneration, including insights from ongoing clinical trials. The authors conclude by addressing persistent translational barriers such as isolation standardization and regulatory constraints, while proposing that emerging approaches including synthetic exosome mimetics and artificial intelligence-driven diagnostics will advance exosome-based precision medicine.
items.citation: Victor U. Chigozie. Exosomal bioactive molecules: molecular regulation, functional heterogeneity, and translational implications in disease diagnostics and therapeutics. Clinical and Experimental Medicine. 2026

items.title: Harnessing mucosal immunity for protective vaccines
items.citation: Dong-il Kwon, Sachin Bhagchandani, Stephen A. Ehrenzeller et al.. Harnessing mucosal immunity for protective vaccines. Nature reviews. Immunology. 2026

items.title: Droplet microfluidics for biomedical applications: emerging trends and future developments
items.summary: This review paper examines the field of droplet-based microfluidics, focusing on its application to biomedical contexts, particularly the synthesis of natural microspheres. The authors comprehensively survey the working principles underlying droplet microfluidic systems, including surface modification strategies for microdevices and the various fabrication methods used to produce microspheres. The review systematically outlines emerging biomedical applications of different types of naturally derived microspheres produced through these techniques. The authors also address current challenges limiting broader academic and industrial adoption of the technology. The paper aims to provide both a thorough mechanistic understanding of droplet-based microfluidics and a forward-looking perspective on future directions and novel applications in the field.
items.citation: Li Ma, Xiong Zhao, Junsheng Hou et al.. Droplet microfluidics for biomedical applications: emerging trends and future developments. Microsystems & Nanoengineering. 2026

items.title: FVAttn: Adaptive Sparse Attention with Runtime Load Balancing for Video Generation
items.summary: This paper addresses the computational bottleneck of self-attention in Video Diffusion Transformers, where processing long spatio-temporal sequences at high resolution becomes prohibitively expensive. The authors identify that training-free adaptive sparse attention, while effective at reducing computation, creates uneven per-head workloads across multiple GPUs under sequence parallelism, causing straggler problems that undermine efficiency gains. They developed a training-free sparse-attention system combining Top-p routing with a safety floor, video-aware block organization, and a runtime load balancing mechanism that migrates heavy attention heads between GPUs via peer-to-peer communication to equalize workloads. The system further exploits residual computational slack on non-critical GPUs by inserting additional high-value attention blocks, and hides scheduling overhead through computation-communication overlap. Evaluated on a step-distilled video generation model, the approach substantially reduces load imbalance and achieves large speedups in both attention computation and overall diffusion transformer inference while maintaining competitive video quality.
items.citation: Hao Liu, Chenghuan Huang, Ye Huang et al.. FVAttn: Adaptive Sparse Attention with Runtime Load Balancing for Video Generation. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Behaviour-Conditioned Neural Processes for Adaptive Residential Short-Term Load Forecasting
items.summary: This paper investigates residential short-term load forecasting, which is difficult due to the heterogeneity of household energy consumption and the influence of diverse behavioural routines. The authors propose a behaviour-conditioned Attentive Neural Process framework that embeds inferred behavioural structure directly into the forecasting mechanism rather than treating it as an external grouping signal, using a discrete latent variable to capture behavioural patterns and a continuous latent variable to represent shared functional uncertainty. Because ground-truth behavioural labels are unavailable, clustering-derived information provides weak supervision during training, while at test time the model infers behavioural class distributions solely from observed context. Evaluated on a real-world smart meter dataset with user-disjoint splits across variable context lengths and multi-step forecast horizons, the proposed model outperforms both a label-agnostic Neural Process baseline and deterministic fixed-window baselines, with the greatest gains occurring under limited context. The results demonstrate that embedding behavioural structure within the probabilistic forecasting mechanism supports accurate, uncertainty-aware load forecasting across heterogeneous households and conditions within a single model.
items.citation: Ramin Soleimani, Andrea Visentin, Dirk Pesch. Behaviour-Conditioned Neural Processes for Adaptive Residential Short-Term Load Forecasting. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Improving Improved Kernel PLS
items.summary: This paper addresses computational inefficiencies in the Improved Kernel Partial Least Squares algorithms, which are among the fastest existing methods for PLS calibration. The author targets two specific shared computational steps: the calculation of X rotations and Y loadings. For the X rotations, a direct evaluation strategy replaces sequential term-by-term accumulation, enabling better parallelization on modern hardware without changing the number of multiplications. For the Y loadings, the author identifies previously unreported mathematical equivalences showing that each loading can be derived from quantities already available within the same iteration, reducing the computational cost from a scaling dependent on the number of predictor variables to one dependent only on the number of response variables under common conditions. Both modifications are proven to produce numerically identical results to the original algorithms, and benchmarks on CPU and GPU hardware demonstrate substantial speedups for the isolated steps and meaningful overall acceleration for complete model fits.
items.citation: Ole-Christian Galbo Engstrøm. Improving Improved Kernel PLS. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Subgrain-resolved Analysis of Degradation in Cu Metallization via Scanning 3DXRD and Thermomechanical Modeling
items.summary: This study investigated degradation mechanisms in copper metallization layers used in power semiconductor devices, where short-circuit events cause rapid heating and thermomechanical stresses that can compromise device reliability. The researchers combined scanning three-dimensional X-ray diffraction experiments with thermomechanical crystal plasticity simulations to track grain-resolved plastic deformation during rapid cyclic loading. This integrated approach enabled identification of potential degradation hotspots at the subgrain level by capturing the evolution of microstructural deformation. The findings provide mechanistic insight into how microstructure governs the formation of degradation hotspots, establishing a foundation for microstructure-informed, physics-based reliability modeling of copper metallization in power devices.
items.citation: Nikhil Prabhu, Laura Neumann, Michael Reisinger et al.. Subgrain-resolved Analysis of Degradation in Cu Metallization via Scanning 3DXRD and Thermomechanical Modeling. arXiv. 2026

items.title: App-Based Performance Characterization of Cellular and Wi-Fi Networks in Dense Stadium Deployments
items.summary: This paper studied wireless network performance in one of the most demanding real-world environments for mobile connectivity: a major college football stadium filled with tens of thousands of simultaneous users. The researchers deployed commercial smartphones to run realistic application workloads—web browsing, messaging, and social media uploads—across a multi-tier infrastructure combining enterprise Wi-Fi with high-density cellular networks from multiple carriers operating both Non-Standalone and Standalone 5G configurations. They found that while cellular networks perform well in an empty stadium, game-day crowd loads create a severe uplink bottleneck, with anchor-band congestion under Non-Standalone deployments causing catastrophic latency for web browsing and upload failure rates approaching half of all attempts. Certain low-band frequency channels maintained signal quality but exhibited extremely high block error rates during active use, leading to substantial page-load failures. In contrast, the dense distributed Wi-Fi infrastructure matched the best cellular downlink performance while proving far more resilient for uplink and latency under full crowd conditions, leading the authors to conclude that localized Wi-Fi densification is essential for managing extreme stadium traffic.
items.citation: Hardani Ismu Nabil, Muhammad Iqbal Rochman, S. M. Haider Ali Shuvo et al.. App-Based Performance Characterization of Cellular and Wi-Fi Networks in Dense Stadium Deployments. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Equilibrium analysis in a multi-agent reinsurance chain
items.summary: This paper examines how multiple insurers and reinsurers interact strategically within a layered reinsurance chain, a setting that reflects real-world risk transfer arrangements but has received limited formal game-theoretic treatment. The authors model the system as a stochastic differential game, using Stackelberg games to capture the hierarchical buyer-seller relationship at each layer and a non-zero-sum game to represent competition among insurers, with all parties permitted to invest in both risk-free and risky assets. The analysis is conducted separately for proportional reinsurance and excess-of-loss reinsurance contracts to account for structural differences between contract types. Applying dynamic programming under the mean-variance criterion, the authors derive closed-form equilibrium strategies for investment and reinsurance decisions by solving extended Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman systems. A key finding is that heightened competition in the insurance market depresses the safety loadings charged across all layers of the reinsurance chain, linking market structure directly to reinsurance pricing outcomes.
items.citation: Kaizheng Wang, Wei Liu, Zhuo Jin et al.. Equilibrium analysis in a multi-agent reinsurance chain. arXiv. 2026

items.title: 'It's Primarily Around Their Viral Load': Public Health Decision-Making and HIV Risk Assemblages in Ontario, Canada.
items.summary: This study examined how public health practitioners in Ontario, Canada determine when the behaviors of people living with HIV constitute "significant" risk warranting official intervention, including enforceable orders. Drawing on assemblage theory, the researchers conducted semi-structured interviews with public health personnel and analyzed the data using reflexive thematic analysis. They found that risk assessments were shaped by an interplay of factors including viral load, continuity in HIV care, co-infection with other sexually transmissible infections, and whether individuals complied with public health directives. The Undetectable = Untransmittable principle had begun to reshape how risk was understood, effectively neutralizing concern when viral suppression was achieved, but its adoption was uneven across public health units, resulting in inconsistent and sometimes coercive responses. The authors argue that conceptualizing HIV risk as relationally produced rather than individually determined challenges punitive approaches, and call for broader adoption of equity-based practices alongside the Undetectable = Untransmittable framework.
items.citation: Daroya E, French M, Hastings C et al.. 'It's Primarily Around Their Viral Load': Public Health Decision-Making and HIV Risk Assemblages in Ontario, Canada.. Sociology of health & illness. 2026

items.title: Durable viral suppression in the dolutegravir era: a data-enabled, risk-stratified model for integrated HIV care in Uganda.
items.summary: This paper examines why viral non-suppression, advanced HIV disease, and multimorbidity persist in Uganda despite near-universal adoption of dolutegravir-based antiretroviral therapy, concluding that gaps in service delivery rather than regimen limitations are the primary driver. The authors analyzed a large routine-care cohort from The AIDS Support Organization spanning a decade, alongside a regional systematic review and meta-analysis covering East Africa, finding that viral non-suppression clusters in predictable social and clinical risk groups. Drawing on these data sources, they developed a risk-stratified, data-enabled care model that uses routine electronic health record signals to trigger rapid follow-up for unsuppressed clients and deliver a time-limited adherence and socioeconomic support bundle. The model also proposes integrating tuberculosis and non-communicable disease management within existing HIV care platforms, guided by a streamlined dashboard tracking cascade timeliness and high-risk package delivery. The authors argue that this pragmatic framework can translate antiretroviral scale-up into durable viral suppression, fewer advanced HIV disease complications, and accelerated progress toward global treatment targets.
items.citation: Namaganda MM, Nabende JN, Kateete DP et al.. Durable viral suppression in the dolutegravir era: a data-enabled, risk-stratified model for integrated HIV care in Uganda.. Frontiers in public health. 2026

items.title: Comparative evaluation of a multi-antigen malaria rapid diagnostic test against qPCR and microscopy in a co-endemic area of Southern Ethiopia.
items.summary: This study evaluated the diagnostic performance of a multi-antigen rapid diagnostic test for malaria in a region of southern Ethiopia where both *Plasmodium falciparum* and *Plasmodium vivax* co-circulate, and where conventional HRP2-based tests are compromised by low parasite densities and gene deletions. Febrile patients attending health facilities during peak transmission season were tested using the new Bioline Malaria multi-Ag RDT, which detects HRP2 and dual pLDH markers, alongside expert microscopy and quantitative PCR as reference standards. Malaria prevalence was highest when assessed by qPCR, which also identified the greatest number of species-specific and mixed infections, while the multi-antigen RDT showed high specificity and strong agreement with microscopy but reduced sensitivity when compared against qPCR. This gap in performance against the molecular reference indicates that the RDT missed a meaningful proportion of submicroscopic, low-density infections. The authors conclude that while the multi-antigen RDT is suitable for routine clinical diagnosis, more sensitive tools are needed for comprehensive malaria surveillance and control in co-endemic settings.
items.citation: Bogale A, Mitiku A, Tefera S et al.. Comparative evaluation of a multi-antigen malaria rapid diagnostic test against qPCR and microscopy in a co-endemic area of Southern Ethiopia.. Scientific reports. 2026

items.title: Characterization of arbovirus infections in patients within Haiti by screening discarded malaria rapid diagnostic test cassettes.
items.summary: This study investigated circulating arboviruses in Haiti during a period when traditional surveillance efforts were disrupted by security concerns, using an unconventional sample source. Researchers extracted nucleic acids from discarded malaria rapid diagnostic test cassettes collected from febrile patients across two Haitian departments over several years, then applied a series of real-time PCR assays and next-generation sequencing to identify viral pathogens. Testing revealed dengue virus serotype three as the sole arbovirus detected, with all positive samples originating from collections made in a single year, and phylogenetic analysis showed close genetic similarity to strains circulating elsewhere in the Caribbean during the same period. No alphaviruses or Oropouche virus were detected in the subset of samples tested for those pathogens. The findings validate discarded malaria rapid diagnostic tests as a practical and informative surveillance tool in resource-limited or politically unstable settings, capable of supporting both detection and molecular characterization of arboviral threats.
items.citation: Matulis GA, Smith HP, Hall G et al.. Characterization of arbovirus infections in patients within Haiti by screening discarded malaria rapid diagnostic test cassettes.. PLoS neglected tropical diseases. 2026

items.title: Microbiota in cancer: current understandings and future perspectives
items.summary: This review examines the complex and multifaceted relationship between the microbiota and cancer, encompassing roles in carcinogenesis, disease progression, and prognosis. The authors trace the historical development of this research field and analyze the molecular mechanisms through which microbiota influence oncogenic signaling pathways, immune responses, and tumor metabolism. The review addresses the dual nature of microbial communities in cancer, as they can both promote tumor growth and serve as targets for therapeutic intervention, including their capacity to modulate responses to immunotherapy, chemotherapy, and radiotherapy. Emerging anticancer strategies that target the microbiota—such as probiotics, antibiotics, and fecal microbiota transplantation—are surveyed alongside approved drugs and ongoing clinical trials. The authors conclude by emphasizing the need for personalized, microbiota-targeted approaches to advance oncological research and clinical application.
items.citation: Yanxi Yao, Yifei Zhu, Keji Chen et al.. Microbiota in cancer: current understandings and future perspectives. Signal Transduction and Targeted Therapy. 2026

items.title: ZigBee Based Low Latency IoT and AI Integrated Framework for Real Time Telehealth Monitoring
items.summary: This paper presents an IoT and AI-integrated wireless health monitoring framework designed for elderly and chronically ill patients in care settings. The system uses ZigBee protocol to transmit physiological data—including heart rate, body temperature, and oxygen levels—from wearable sensors to a cloud-based processing unit with low power consumption and minimal latency. Multiple machine learning algorithms, including random forest, support vector machine, and logistic regression, were applied to detect health anomalies, with the random forest model achieving the highest reported performance. Testing in a real-world care facility demonstrated that the system operates with very low transmission delay and power draw, making it suitable for continuous long-term deployment. The authors claim their framework addresses key shortcomings of existing telehealth systems—such as high latency and false alarms—while offering a scalable, modular architecture that can accommodate additional sensor types as patient needs evolve.
items.citation: B. V. Ravi Kumar, Mohammad Shahnawaz Shaikh. ZigBee Based Low Latency IoT and AI Integrated Framework for Real Time Telehealth Monitoring. Emerging Science Journal. 2026

items.title: Sepsis
items.citation: Mervyn Singer, Derek C Angus, Djillali Annane et al.. Sepsis. The Lancet. 2026

items.title: Recent Advances in Microfluidic Chip Technology for Laboratory Medicine: Innovations and Artificial Intelligence Integration
items.summary: This review examines recent developments in microfluidic chip technology, commonly referred to as lab-on-a-chip systems, and their growing role in laboratory medicine. The authors conducted a comprehensive survey of innovations in chip design and fabrication, including advances such as three-dimensional printing and flexible modular substrates, alongside progress in biosensor integration for detecting circulating tumor cells, nucleic acids, and exosomes. A central focus is the convergence of microfluidics with artificial intelligence and machine learning, which has enabled the creation of smart diagnostic platforms capable of automated analysis, self-optimization, and clinical decision support across applications ranging from cancer diagnostics to point-of-care testing. The review also covers emerging uses in neuroscience diagnostics and microbiome profiling, illustrating the breadth of potential clinical impact. The authors additionally address persistent challenges in manufacturing scalability, clinical validation, and system integration, while outlining future directions for translating these technologies into routine clinical and public health practice.
items.citation: Hong Cai, Dongxia Wang, Yiqun Zhao et al.. Recent Advances in Microfluidic Chip Technology for Laboratory Medicine: Innovations and Artificial Intelligence Integration. Biosensors. 2026

items.title: Cancer workforce—a global crisis: a Lancet Oncology Commission
items.citation: Hedvig Hricak, Zachary J. Ward, Fabio Ynoe Moraes et al.. Cancer workforce—a global crisis: a Lancet Oncology Commission. The Lancet Oncology. 2026

items.title: Safety of a large language model-based clinical decision support system in African primary healthcare
items.summary: This study evaluated the safety of a large language model-based clinical decision support system embedded in electronic medical records across primary care clinics in Kenya. Trained physicians retrospectively reviewed over a thousand patient encounter records generated during a several-month deployment period. The system demonstrated a low rate of hallucinations and nearly universal alignment of its clinical management guidance with local guidelines. However, safety concerns emerged because clinicians disproportionately retained harmful recommendations from the model in their final documentation while failing to adopt beneficial suggestions that could have mitigated pre-existing risks identified in their own notes. The authors conclude that while the tool shows promise for quality improvement in African primary healthcare, the asymmetric uptake of harmful versus helpful outputs highlights the urgent need for usability improvements, locally tailored safeguards, and prospective trials to establish patient-level benefit.
items.citation: Ambrose Agweyu, Paul Mwaniki, Wilkister Musau et al.. Safety of a large language model-based clinical decision support system in African primary healthcare. Nature Health. 2026

items.title: Rapid structural evolution of neutron-rich silicon isotopes toward N = 28
items.summary: This study investigated the structural evolution of neutron-rich silicon isotopes as they approach the neutron number N=28, a region characterized by a transition from shell closure at N=20 to pronounced quadrupole collectivity. The researchers performed lifetime measurements of excited states in one isotope and combined these with heavy-ion inelastic-scattering measurements in a neighboring odd-mass isotope, the latter representing the first simultaneous measurement of its kind for that nucleus. In the N=26 isotope, the extracted lifetimes revealed moderate quadrupole collectivity alongside signatures of triaxial nuclear shape, while in the odd-mass isotope two nearly degenerate low-energy states were found to have comparable electromagnetic transition strengths from inelastic scattering but lifetimes dominated by magnetic dipole decays. Taken together, the results point toward an evolution to an oblate nuclear shape as neutron number increases toward N=28, in agreement with large-scale shell-model calculations.
items.citation: G. L. Zimba, H. Iwasaki, B. A. Brown et al.. Rapid structural evolution of neutron-rich silicon isotopes toward N = 28. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Behaviour-Conditioned Neural Processes for Adaptive Residential Short-Term Load Forecasting
items.summary: This paper investigates residential short-term load forecasting, a problem complicated by the heterogeneity and behavioural variability of household electricity consumption. The authors propose a behaviour-conditioned Attentive Neural Process framework that embeds inferred behavioural structure directly into the forecasting model rather than using it only as an external grouping signal. The approach employs a discrete latent variable to capture behavioural patterns, derived through weak supervision from clustering during training and inferred purely from context at test time, alongside a continuous latent variable representing shared functional uncertainty across diverse profiles. Evaluated on a smart grid dataset with user-disjoint splits and variable context lengths, the proposed model improves both point forecast accuracy and probabilistic calibration relative to a label-agnostic baseline, with the largest gains observed when contextual information is limited. The results support the feasibility of a single unified model for uncertainty-aware load forecasting across heterogeneous households and forecast horizons.
items.citation: Ramin Soleimani, Andrea Visentin, Dirk Pesch. Behaviour-Conditioned Neural Processes for Adaptive Residential Short-Term Load Forecasting. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Euclid: Calibrating distances from surface brightness fluctuations with Early Release Observations of the Fornax cluster
items.summary: This paper investigates the use of surface brightness fluctuations (SBF) — pixel-to-pixel flux variations in galaxy images caused by the statistical distribution of stars — as a method for measuring redshift-independent distances using data from the Euclid space mission. The authors applied the FAST-SBF code to Euclid Early Release Observations of galaxies in the Fornax cluster, measuring stellar fluctuation amplitudes in the visible band despite the data reduction pipeline not being optimized for this technique. They calibrated the absolute fluctuation magnitude as a function of galaxy color using a sample of Fornax galaxies, then validated this calibration against galaxies in two additional observed fields, the Perseus cluster and dwarf satellite candidates around a nearby spiral galaxy. The resulting distances show reasonable agreement with literature values, with particularly good agreement when the analysis flagged measurements as robust. The study also compares the observational results with simple stellar population models, highlighting the potential for Euclid SBF measurements to simultaneously constrain the stellar populations of early-type galaxies across large imaging surveys.
items.citation: R. Habas, M. Cantiello, G. Riccio et al.. Euclid: Calibrating distances from surface brightness fluctuations with Early Release Observations of the Fornax cluster. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Adaptive Fault Injection Planning for Multi-Layer Self-Healing AI Infrastructure
items.summary: This paper investigates how faults propagate across multiple layers in GPU-accelerated infrastructure used by hyperscale operators, arguing that conventional fault-injection testing—which examines each layer in isolation—leaves the vast majority of cross-layer failure paths untested. The authors developed ADA-ST, an adaptive fault-injection methodology that constructs weighted fault-propagation graphs across hardware, firmware, management software, and orchestration layers, then iteratively selects test scenarios to maximize coverage of cross-layer propagation edges. Drawing on a large corpus of real-world repair tickets from a production platform as an empirical foundation, they demonstrated that existing static test campaigns cover only a small fraction of modeled propagation edges, while ADA-ST achieves full edge coverage within a small number of iterations across three successive hardware generations. They also introduced the Fault-Layer Abstraction Mapping technique to transfer propagation knowledge between hardware generations, achieving near-complete fidelity across platform transitions. Physical validation on the newest platform confirmed all tested propagation edges and revealed previously unexercised vulnerabilities including telemetry blind spots, detection gaps based on signal absence, multi-signal correlation failures, and trust-without-verification weaknesses at layer boundaries.
items.citation: Saurabh Kulkarni, Yuxin Yang, Rohan Kulkarni et al.. Adaptive Fault Injection Planning for Multi-Layer Self-Healing AI Infrastructure. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Radiopurity material assays and radiation exposure projections for superconducting qubit measurements at SNOLAB
items.summary: This paper investigates how ionizing radiation from cosmic rays and radioactive materials threatens the coherence and reliable operation of superconducting qubit devices, and explores a deep underground facility as a means of mitigating these effects. The authors conducted an extensive radioassay campaign of materials intended for use in a superconducting qubit assembly to be operated at the CUTE facility at SNOLAB, which is shielded by approximately two kilometers of rock overburden. These measured radioactivity levels were then fed into detailed Monte Carlo simulations using Geant4 to estimate the rates and spectral characteristics of energy deposits from radiogenic sources in the underground environment. The study identifies the dominant particle interaction types contributing to the projected radiation background and demonstrates how crystal dynamics simulations via the G4CMP solid-state physics extension can help characterize the physical effects of high-energy particle impacts on quantum devices. The work represents preparation for the first underground operation of superconducting qubits at SNOLAB and aims to inform broader community strategies for mitigating radiation-induced decoherence in quantum computing hardware.
items.citation: Y. Ahmed, B. Binoy, R. Bunker et al.. Radiopurity material assays and radiation exposure projections for superconducting qubit measurements at SNOLAB. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Quantum Fisher information of magnetic quantum phase transition on Kondo lattice
items.summary: This paper investigates multipartite entanglement across the quantum phase transition of a Kondo lattice model, a system relevant to understanding strange metals and heavy fermion physics. The authors employ quantum Fisher information as a quantitative tool to characterize entanglement, focusing on spin components transverse to the magnetic order parameter. They find that this quantity serves as a diagnostic for the destruction of heavy quasiparticles in the Kondo-destroyed magnetically ordered phase, with the underlying physics clarified through analysis of an antiferromagnetic Heisenberg model. The authors propose that these theoretical results can be tested experimentally via both unpolarized and polarized inelastic neutron scattering measurements in the ordered region of the heavy fermion phase diagram. Their work demonstrates that careful choice of operators in quantum Fisher information calculations can both witness multipartite entanglement in distinct sectors and illuminate the broader physics across different regions of a quantum phase diagram.
items.citation: Yuan Fang, Lei Chen, Mounica Mahankali et al.. Quantum Fisher information of magnetic quantum phase transition on Kondo lattice. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Emerging blood-based diagnostic strategies for tuberculosis.
items.summary: This review examines blood-based diagnostic approaches for tuberculosis, a disease caused by *Mycobacterium tuberculosis* that continues to pose a major global health burden. The authors focus on serological methods, which have gained renewed interest due to the ongoing identification of novel biomarkers and their advantages of simplicity, rapid turnaround, and cost-effectiveness compared to conventional sputum culture and molecular assays. The review synthesizes current knowledge on biomarkers employed in TB serodiagnosis, with particular emphasis on their utility for diagnosing extrapulmonary tuberculosis and for patients who yield negative results from standard diagnostic tools. The authors also discuss how advanced diagnostic technologies can be integrated with these serological strategies to enhance performance across varied clinical contexts. Overall, the review argues that emerging blood-based approaches hold meaningful potential to improve tuberculosis diagnosis in settings where existing methods fall short.
items.citation: Yue C, Geng X, Sun X et al.. Emerging blood-based diagnostic strategies for tuberculosis.. Clinical chemistry and laboratory medicine. 2026

items.title: Diagnostic accuracy, operational feasibility, and cost considerations of Truenat MTB Plus and Xpert MTB/RIF Ultra for pulmonary tuberculosis.
items.summary: This study compared two WHO-endorsed nucleic acid amplification tests — Truenat MTB Plus and Xpert MTB/RIF Ultra — for detecting pulmonary tuberculosis and rifampicin resistance among presumptive TB patients in India, while also evaluating their operational feasibility and costs. Using mycobacterial culture and phenotypic drug susceptibility testing as reference standards, investigators enrolled presumptive pulmonary TB patients at a dedicated tuberculosis centre and tested paired sputum specimens on both platforms. Xpert MTB/RIF Ultra demonstrated higher sensitivity and specificity for TB detection compared to Truenat MTB Plus, and also produced substantially fewer invalid and indeterminate results, particularly for rifampicin resistance testing. Truenat, however, was considerably less expensive per test, offering a meaningful cost advantage for decentralized settings despite its higher rate of results requiring repeat testing. The authors conclude that a tiered diagnostic approach using both platforms according to setting and capacity could optimize molecular testing access and advance TB elimination goals.
items.citation: Sidiq Z, Anand A, Dwivedi KK et al.. Diagnostic accuracy, operational feasibility, and cost considerations of Truenat MTB Plus and Xpert MTB/RIF Ultra for pulmonary tuberculosis.. PloS one. 2026

items.title: Diagnostic Accuracy of Molecular Testing on Saliva and Oral Swabs for Pulmonary Tuberculosis.
items.summary: This study evaluated whether molecular testing on saliva and oral swabs could serve as accurate, non-sputum alternatives for diagnosing pulmonary tuberculosis, addressing a critical gap in TB detection. Researchers conducted a nested case-control study within a prospective cohort of adults and children being evaluated for pulmonary TB at primary care centers in Colombia, using sputum liquid mycobacterial culture as the reference standard and applying Xpert MTB/RIF Ultra to paired saliva and nylon-flocked oral swab samples. Saliva demonstrated high sensitivity and specificity for culture-confirmed TB, exceeding the World Health Organization's target threshold for a low-complexity, non-sputum diagnostic test, while oral swabs showed somewhat lower sensitivity but similarly high specificity. Saliva sensitivity was significantly greater than that of swabs, though the two specimen types did not differ significantly in specificity. The vast majority of participants found both collection methods acceptable, supporting the feasibility of these non-sputum approaches for TB diagnosis.
items.venue: Clinical infectious diseases : an official publication of the Infectious Diseases Society of America
items.citation: Vargas DA, Fuertes-Bucheli JF, Sanchez-Hidalgo A et al.. Diagnostic Accuracy of Molecular Testing on Saliva and Oral Swabs for Pulmonary Tuberculosis.. Clinical infectious diseases : an official publication of the Infectious Diseases Society of America. 2026

items.title: Comparative Diagnostic Accuracy of GeneXpert MTB/RIF and TB-LAMP for Tuberculosis Detection Among Presumptive Tuberculosis Cases in Bayelsa State, Nigeria
items.summary: This study compared the diagnostic accuracy of two rapid nucleic acid amplification tests, GeneXpert MTB/RIF and TB-LAMP, against the gold standard of solid mycobacterial culture for detecting tuberculosis among presumptive cases in Bayelsa State, Nigeria. A cross-sectional design was used, collecting sputum samples from nearly four hundred participants across multiple health facilities and evaluating each sample with all three methods in parallel. GeneXpert showed strong concordance with culture, achieving high specificity and positive predictive value while also providing rifampicin resistance information, making it well suited as a confirmatory tool for suspected drug-resistant disease. TB-LAMP demonstrated slightly higher sensitivity but lower specificity than GeneXpert, and a small proportion of its positive results were attributed to non-tuberculous mycobacteria, highlighting a false-positive risk. The authors conclude that a sequential algorithm using TB-LAMP as an initial screen followed by GeneXpert for confirmation could improve case detection while making efficient use of limited resources in high-burden settings.
items.citation: UMUWE P, Wariso KT, ILOZUMBA J et al.. Comparative Diagnostic Accuracy of GeneXpert MTB/RIF and TB-LAMP for Tuberculosis Detection Among Presumptive Tuberculosis Cases in Bayelsa State, Nigeria. 2026

items.title: Field-ready portable rapid nucleic acid test for tuberculosis detection and drug-resistance profiling in resource-limited settings
items.summary: This paper developed a portable, field-ready diagnostic platform for tuberculosis that integrates nucleic acid amplification with artificial intelligence and smartphone-based readout for use in resource-limited settings. The system employs loop-mediated isothermal amplification targeting dual Mycobacterium tuberculosis genetic markers, and the same device architecture—without any modification—also enables drug-resistance profiling by detecting mutations associated with rifampicin and isoniazid resistance through an allele-specific approach. Clinical evaluation across a set of patient samples demonstrated high sensitivity and specificity, and real-world deployment in a primary healthcare setting operated by minimally trained personnel achieved sensitivity and perfect specificity benchmarked against GeneXpert. The platform thus unifies primary TB screening and multidrug-resistance detection within a single compact framework, aiming to bring reliable molecular diagnostics to decentralized, high-burden environments where conventional laboratory infrastructure is unavailable.
items.citation: Nag S, Banerjee S, Banerjee S et al.. Field-ready portable rapid nucleic acid test for tuberculosis detection and drug-resistance profiling in resource-limited settings. 2026

items.title: Diagnostic accuracy of GeneReady MTB assay on sputum for detecting pulmonary tuberculosis: a prospective multicenter study in China.
items.summary: This prospective multicenter study evaluated the diagnostic performance of a novel molecular assay, GeneReady MTB, for detecting pulmonary tuberculosis from sputum samples collected at three hospitals in China. Participants with symptoms suggestive of active pulmonary tuberculosis underwent testing with GeneReady alongside smear microscopy, mycobacterial culture, and the established Xpert MTB/RIF assay, with results benchmarked against a microbiological reference standard. GeneReady demonstrated high sensitivity and specificity, with performance stratified by bacterial load showing better sensitivity in smear-positive than smear-negative patients. Agreement between GeneReady and Xpert MTB/RIF was strong for both positive and negative results. The authors conclude that GeneReady offers diagnostic accuracy comparable to Xpert MTB/RIF while providing advantages of lower cost, faster turnaround, and simpler operation, supporting its potential as an initial diagnostic tool in resource-limited settings, though further evaluation across diverse patient populations is recommended.
items.citation: Shang Y, Tang Z, Deng Y et al.. Diagnostic accuracy of GeneReady MTB assay on sputum for detecting pulmonary tuberculosis: a prospective multicenter study in China.. Journal of clinical microbiology. 2026

items.title: Lipid metabolism in homeostasis and disease
items.summary: This review examines the role of lipid metabolism in maintaining cellular homeostasis and how its dysregulation contributes to a broad spectrum of diseases. The authors synthesize the research history of lipid metabolism and place particular emphasis on how immune cells, including macrophages, T cells, B cells, and neutrophils, depend on lipid metabolic processes for their proliferation, differentiation, and activation in both innate and adaptive immunity. The review covers lipid transport, signaling pathways, and multilevel regulatory mechanisms spanning genetic, epigenetic, posttranscriptional, and posttranslational control. These insights are connected to disease contexts such as cancer, cardiovascular disease, autoimmune conditions, neurodegeneration, and metabolic disorders. The authors conclude by surveying therapeutic strategies targeting lipid metabolism, ongoing clinical trial progress, and emerging directions including lipid-derived biomarkers and novel treatment approaches.
items.citation: Zhili Li, Wende Deng, Lanxuan Yang et al.. Lipid metabolism in homeostasis and disease. Signal Transduction and Targeted Therapy. 2026

items.title: Insulin Resistance and Inflammation
items.summary: This paper examines the bidirectional relationship between insulin resistance and inflammation, with a particular focus on how each condition drives and amplifies the other to produce cardiometabolic and vascular disease. The authors conducted a conceptual narrative review, synthesizing evidence across multiple levels of analysis spanning insulin receptor signaling, intracellular stress pathways including oxidative, endoplasmic reticulum, inflammatory, and fibrotic mechanisms, tissue dysfunction, and systemic inflammatory responses. They deliberately separated mechanistic and experimental evidence from human observational and interventional data to clarify the basis for causal versus associative claims. The central finding is a proposed integrative framework in which stress-response pathways become maladaptive under conditions of chronic nutrient excess and persistent inflammation, creating self-reinforcing feedback loops between insulin resistance and inflammation that accelerate endothelial dysfunction and atherogenesis. The authors argue this framework identifies key points of convergence that can inform mechanistic research priorities and guide translational hypothesis testing in cardiometabolic disease.
items.citation: Evgenii Gusev, Alexey Sarapultsev, Yulia A. Zhuravleva. Insulin Resistance and Inflammation. International Journal of Molecular Sciences. 2026

items.title: Sepsis
items.citation: Mervyn Singer, Derek C Angus, Djillali Annane et al.. Sepsis. The Lancet. 2026

items.title: Implementation of the NHS England Lung Cancer Screening Programme over 5 years
items.summary: The paper reports on the implementation and outcomes of the NHS England Lung Cancer Screening Programme, which uses low-dose computed tomography to detect lung cancer early in high-risk individuals aged 55 to 74 who have a smoking history. The programme employs multivariable risk models to determine eligibility and delivers screening through a regionally federated clinical infrastructure guided by national strategic and economic frameworks. Over five years, more than two million people were invited, resulting in thousands of lung cancer diagnoses, with nearly two-thirds detected at the earliest tumour stage — a marked shift toward earlier-stage diagnosis compared to prior patterns, especially in socioeconomically deprived areas. The authors argue that the programme demonstrates both the feasibility and scalability of large-scale screening through centralized protocols and effective project management, while acknowledging that inequalities in participation remain to be addressed. The findings are presented as a practical model to support the broader adoption of lung cancer screening across the UK and internationally.
items.citation: Richard W. Lee, Arjun Nair, Haval Balata et al.. Implementation of the NHS England Lung Cancer Screening Programme over 5 years. Nature Medicine. 2026

items.title: Therapeutic Potential of Extracellular Vesicles: From Biogenesis, Isolation and Molecular Characterization to Addressing Translational Gaps and Regulatory Barriers
items.summary: This review examines extracellular vesicles as mediators of intercellular communication, covering their biogenesis, molecular composition, and therapeutic promise across human diseases. The authors survey the major EV classes, including exosomes, microvesicles, apoptotic bodies, and oncosomes, and discuss advances in their isolation, characterization, and omics-based profiling. Particular attention is given to EV involvement in viral infection, inflammation, immune regulation, and cancer biology, as well as the clinical potential of mesenchymal stem cell-derived EVs in regenerative medicine and oncology. The review also highlights photobiomodulation as an emerging means of therapeutically modulating EV signaling. Finally, the authors address translational obstacles surrounding standardization, scalability, and regulatory validation under existing oversight frameworks, arguing that harmonized quality controls are essential to realizing EVs as next-generation diagnostic and therapeutic tools in precision medicine.
items.citation: Dragan Primorac, Petar Brlek, Luka Bulić et al.. Therapeutic Potential of Extracellular Vesicles: From Biogenesis, Isolation and Molecular Characterization to Addressing Translational Gaps and Regulatory Barriers. International Journal of Molecular Sciences. 2026

items.title: Will AI Replace Physicians in the Near Future? AI Adoption Barriers in Medicine
items.summary: This narrative review examined whether current artificial intelligence systems, including convolutional neural networks for medical imaging and large language models for clinical language tasks, are capable of replacing physicians in the near future. The authors systematically evaluated the scientific literature on AI performance across medical imaging interpretation, clinical knowledge assessment, patient communication, and generalization capabilities, while also considering regulatory and legal frameworks. They found that AI demonstrates strong accuracy and reproducibility in narrowly defined tasks such as image analysis, triage support, and documentation, thereby reducing variability and improving workflow efficiency. However, significant barriers to physician replacement persist, including poor generalization beyond training data distributions, inability to conduct physical examinations or procedural interventions, susceptibility to hallucinations and overconfidence in language models, and unresolved questions of legal liability and accountability. The authors conclude that AI will augment rather than replace physicians, with the most plausible near-term trajectory involving supervised automation of well-defined tasks while clinical judgment, physical examination, and ethical accountability remain physician-dependent.
items.citation: Rafał Obuchowicz, Adam Piórkowski, Karolina Nurzyńska et al.. Will AI Replace Physicians in the Near Future? AI Adoption Barriers in Medicine. Diagnostics. 2026

items.title: Rapid structural evolution of neutron-rich silicon isotopes toward N = 28
items.summary: This paper investigates the structural evolution of neutron-rich silicon isotopes as they approach the neutron number N = 28, a region where competing nuclear shapes and shell effects create complex behavior. The researchers performed lifetime measurements of excited states in one isotope and, for another, conducted the first simultaneous lifetime and heavy-ion inelastic-scattering measurements. In the N = 26 isotope, the extracted transition strengths reveal moderate quadrupole collectivity alongside signatures of triaxial nuclear shape, while in the N = 27 isotope, two closely spaced low-energy states show comparable electromagnetic transition strengths from scattering but are dominated by magnetic dipole decays according to the lifetime data. Together, these results point toward an evolution to oblate nuclear deformation approaching N = 28, in agreement with large-scale shell-model calculations.
items.citation: G. L. Zimba, H. Iwasaki, B. A. Brown et al.. Rapid structural evolution of neutron-rich silicon isotopes toward N = 28. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Behaviour-Conditioned Neural Processes for Adaptive Residential Short-Term Load Forecasting
items.summary: This paper investigates residential short-term load forecasting, which is complicated by the heterogeneous and behaviorally variable nature of household energy consumption. The authors propose a behaviour-conditioned Attentive Neural Process framework that embeds inferred behavioural structure directly into the forecasting model rather than using it only as an external grouping signal. A discrete latent variable captures behavioural patterns derived from clustering-based weak supervision during training, while a continuous latent variable accounts for shared functional uncertainty across diverse load profiles, with test-time conditioning relying solely on context-inferred class distributions. Experiments on a smart grid dataset with user-disjoint splits and variable context lengths show that the proposed model improves probabilistic and point forecast accuracy over a label-agnostic baseline, with the greatest gains observed when context is limited. The results demonstrate that behavioural conditioning within a Neural Process framework supports uncertainty-aware, single-model forecasting across heterogeneous households and forecast horizons.
items.citation: Ramin Soleimani, Andrea Visentin, Dirk Pesch. Behaviour-Conditioned Neural Processes for Adaptive Residential Short-Term Load Forecasting. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Euclid: Calibrating distances from surface brightness fluctuations with Early Release Observations of the Fornax cluster
items.summary: This paper presents the first application of the surface brightness fluctuations (SBF) distance measurement technique to data from the Euclid space mission, using early release observations of the Fornax galaxy cluster. SBF exploits pixel-to-pixel flux variations in galaxy images caused by the statistical distribution of stars, offering a redshift-independent distance indicator from photometric data alone. The authors used the FAST-SBF code to measure stellar fluctuation amplitudes in a sample of Fornax galaxies, calibrating the absolute fluctuation magnitude as a function of galaxy colour, then validated this calibration against galaxies in two additional fields covering the Perseus cluster and dwarf satellite candidates around another galaxy. Despite the Euclid data reduction pipeline not being optimized for SBF measurements, the results show reasonable to good agreement with published distances in the literature. The study also compares the measurements against stellar population models, highlighting the potential of Euclid SBF observations to probe the underlying stellar populations of galaxies in addition to measuring distances.
items.citation: R. Habas, M. Cantiello, G. Riccio et al.. Euclid: Calibrating distances from surface brightness fluctuations with Early Release Observations of the Fornax cluster. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Adaptive Fault Injection Planning for Multi-Layer Self-Healing AI Infrastructure
items.summary: This paper investigates the challenge of testing self-healing infrastructure in GPU-accelerator platforms, where faults can propagate across multiple layers—hardware, firmware, management software, and orchestration—in ways that evade detection or trigger conflicting repairs. The authors developed ADA-ST, an adaptive fault-injection methodology that constructs weighted fault-propagation graphs across these layers and uses iterative, coverage-guided scenario selection to prioritize cross-layer test cases rather than testing each layer in isolation. Drawing on a large corpus of real-world repair tickets from a production platform as an empirical foundation, they demonstrated that conventional static test campaigns exercise only a small fraction of the possible cross-layer fault-propagation pathways. ADA-ST achieves full edge coverage within a small number of iterations across three successive hardware generations, and a knowledge-transfer mechanism called FLAM propagates fault-propagation models across hardware generations with high fidelity. Physical validation on the newest platform confirmed cross-layer vulnerabilities including telemetry blind spots, absence-based detection gaps, multi-signal correlation failures, and unverified trust propagation between infrastructure layers.
items.citation: Saurabh Kulkarni, Yuxin Yang, Rohan Kulkarni et al.. Adaptive Fault Injection Planning for Multi-Layer Self-Healing AI Infrastructure. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Radiopurity material assays and radiation exposure projections for superconducting qubit measurements at SNOLAB
items.summary: This paper addresses the challenge that ionizing radiation from cosmic rays and radioactive decay poses to the coherence and reliable operation of superconducting qubit quantum devices. The researchers conducted an extensive material assay program to characterize the radioactivity levels of components intended for use in a superconducting qubit assembly to be operated at a deep underground facility shielded by substantial rock overburden. These radioactivity measurements were fed into detailed Monte Carlo simulations using the Geant4 particle physics tracking code to estimate the expected rates and spectral characteristics of energy deposits from radiogenic sources in that ultra-low-background environment. The study further employed crystal dynamics simulations via the G4CMP solid-state physics extension to model how high-energy particle impacts propagate in semiconductor substrates. The findings provide a characterization of the dominant background sources and particle interaction types expected underground, informing strategies to mitigate radiation-induced decoherence in superconducting quantum devices.
items.citation: Y. Ahmed, B. Binoy, R. Bunker et al.. Radiopurity material assays and radiation exposure projections for superconducting qubit measurements at SNOLAB. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Quantum Fisher information of magnetic quantum phase transition on Kondo lattice
items.summary: This paper investigates quantum phase transitions in a Kondo lattice model, which serves as a paradigm for understanding strange metals and heavy fermion systems. The authors apply quantum Fisher information as a tool to characterize multipartite entanglement across the transition between a heavy Fermi liquid phase and a Kondo-destroyed magnetically ordered phase. They find that quantum Fisher information associated with spin components transverse to the magnetic order parameter specifically captures the destruction of heavy quasiparticles, a phenomenon they illuminate by analyzing the antiferromagnetic Heisenberg model. The study proposes that these theoretical predictions can be tested experimentally through both unpolarized and polarized inelastic neutron scattering measurements in the ordered region of the heavy fermion phase diagram. The results demonstrate that carefully chosen operators can simultaneously serve as witnesses of multipartite entanglement in distinct sectors and provide physical insight into the behavior of strongly correlated quantum systems across different parts of their phase diagrams.
items.citation: Yuan Fang, Lei Chen, Mounica Mahankali et al.. Quantum Fisher information of magnetic quantum phase transition on Kondo lattice. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Point-of-care screening for curable sexually transmitted infections in antenatal care in Zimbabwe: a costing study.
items.summary: This study examined the costs of integrating point-of-care screening for three curable sexually transmitted infections into antenatal care in Harare, Zimbabwe, compared with standard syndromic management. Researchers implemented screening at two primary healthcare clinics and collected economic cost data through staff interviews, time-and-motion studies, and review of study records, subsequently modelling implementation in a low-volume routine care setting over a twelve-month period. A notably high composite prevalence of the three infections was identified among pregnant women screened. The largest cost drivers were test kits and personnel, which together accounted for the majority of total intervention costs. The authors conclude that the incremental cost of point-of-care antenatal STI screening over syndromic management is substantial and primarily driven by test kit costs, highlighting the need for more affordable point-of-care diagnostics to make such screening viable in low-resource settings.
items.citation: Martin K, Bath D, Nyamwanza R et al.. Point-of-care screening for curable sexually transmitted infections in antenatal care in Zimbabwe: a costing study.. Sexually transmitted infections. 2026

items.title: Cost-effectiveness of a decentralised molecular point-of-care testing programme for sexually transmitted infections in remote primary care health services in Australia.
items.summary: This paper examined the cost-effectiveness of a decentralised molecular point-of-care testing programme for sexually transmitted infections in remote Australian First Nations communities, where the burden of chlamydia, gonorrhoea, and trichomonas is disproportionately high among young adults. The researchers developed a decision analytic model comparing costs and health outcomes of point-of-care testing versus standard laboratory-based testing over a ten-year horizon, using a government payer perspective with cost per quality-adjusted life year as the primary outcome. The model predicted that point-of-care testing would substantially reduce rates of pelvic inflammatory disease and preterm or low birthweight births among those infected, reflecting faster treatment facilitated by same-visit results. The incremental cost-effectiveness ratio fell well below the nominated willingness-to-pay threshold, indicating the programme represents good value for money, with results most sensitive to infection prevalence and the probability of receiving treatment within two days. The authors conclude that sustaining and scaling molecular point-of-care testing in remote primary care settings is cost-effective compared with standard care and is likely to improve reproductive health outcomes for this population.
items.citation: Watts CG, Causer LM, Matthews SJ et al.. Cost-effectiveness of a decentralised molecular point-of-care testing programme for sexually transmitted infections in remote primary care health services in Australia.. Sexually transmitted infections. 2026

items.title: Use of point-of-care nucleic acid amplification testing for chlamydia and gonorrhea in community outreach settings in Dallas, Texas, USA.
items.summary: This study examined the implementation of rapid point-of-care nucleic acid amplification testing for chlamydia and gonorrhea in non-clinical, community outreach settings in an urban environment. Researchers conducted a program evaluation and retrospective data review of individuals who received sexually transmitted infection testing, using chi-squared and Fisher's exact tests to explore whether demographic characteristics or symptom presence were associated with positive test results. The testing platform delivered results within thirty minutes, and all individuals who tested positive were referred for treatment within one day of diagnosis. No statistically significant associations were found between test positivity and demographic or symptom variables, though the small sample size limited these analyses. The authors conclude that point-of-care nucleic acid amplification testing can enable rapid diagnosis and prompt treatment referral in outreach settings, while calling for further research into scalability, cost, and treatment completion rates.
items.citation: Blanchard HL, Gonzalez E, Chow J. Use of point-of-care nucleic acid amplification testing for chlamydia and gonorrhea in community outreach settings in Dallas, Texas, USA.. Sexual health. 2026

items.title: From syndromic management to diagnostic-led management: a report from an expert meeting on introducing sexually transmitted infection testing in low-resource settings.
items.summary: This paper examined the transition from syndromic to diagnostic-led management of sexually transmitted infections in low-resource settings, where syndromic approaches persist despite their limitations of overtreatment, undertreatment, and poor surveillance. An expert meeting convened in Nairobi brought together specialists across STI diagnostics, policy, regulation, and clinical practice to identify priorities for introducing and scaling up point-of-care testing, with a focus on sub-Saharan Africa. Participants analyzed barriers and opportunities at four levels — individual client, health service delivery, national, and global — and generated recommendations spanning improved patient knowledge, optimized clinical workflows, testing outside traditional facilities, updated treatment guidelines, strengthened laboratory quality assurance, harmonized regulatory requirements, and expanded manufacturing capacity. The group concluded that effective scale-up of point-of-care STI diagnostics will require coordinated multilevel action to ensure equitable access, reduce antimicrobial resistance, and improve STI control outcomes.
items.citation: Manguro GO, Ray J, Schocken C et al.. From syndromic management to diagnostic-led management: a report from an expert meeting on introducing sexually transmitted infection testing in low-resource settings.. Sexually transmitted infections. 2026

items.title: The International Guideline for the Definition, Classification, Diagnosis and Management of Urticaria
items.summary: This paper presents an updated international clinical guideline for urticaria, a mast cell-driven disease characterized by wheals, angioedema, or both. The guideline was developed using Cochrane methodology and GRADE evidence-grading principles, with input from a large international consensus conference involving delegates from numerous national and international societies across dozens of countries. The document addresses the definition and classification of urticaria, distinguishing between acute and chronic forms, with the latter further divided into chronic spontaneous and chronic inducible subtypes. It provides evidence-based and expert-guided recommendations for diagnosis and management across urticaria subtypes, noting that while chronic urticaria substantially impairs quality of life and daily functioning, novel therapeutic options are now available. The guideline was formally acknowledged by the European Union of Medical Specialists.
items.citation: T. Zuberbier, Z. Abdul Hameed Ansari, A. H. Abdul Latiff et al.. The International Guideline for the Definition, Classification, Diagnosis and Management of Urticaria. Allergy. 2026

items.title: An epidemiological approach to sexually transmitted diseases – with special reference to contact tracing and screening
items.summary: This thesis investigated epidemiological methods for controlling sexually transmitted diseases, with particular focus on identifying asymptomatic individuals through contact tracing and screening. The research evaluated contact tracing routines, compared characteristics of chlamydia-infected women from different clinical settings and their partners, and examined whether contact tracing data could inform mathematical models of partner choice and disease spread. Key findings included that contact tracing effectively controlled a gonorrhoea outbreak, and that integrating contact tracing into long-term HIV care identified many previously unknown cases. Screening for chlamydia in family planning clinics revealed meaningful prevalence, with infection risk associated with younger age, shorter relationship duration, lack of condom use, and no prior genital infection history, while strengthening follow-up measures and legal inclusion reduced reinfection rates. The study also found that partner choice was not well described by a random mixing model, as steady partner selection was relatively restricted while casual partner selection was more random, and differences between screening-identified and clinic-identified chlamydia cases were insufficient to justify varying approaches to partner management.
items.citation: Kristina Ramstedt. An epidemiological approach to sexually transmitted diseases – with special reference to contact tracing and screening. Acta Dermato Venereologica. 2026

items.title: Review of selected mosquito-borne diseases: arboviruses (dengue, chikungunya, Zika, West Nile, Japanese encephalitis, yellow fever) and parasitic diseases (malaria, lymphatic Filariasis)
items.summary: This review paper examines a broad range of mosquito-borne diseases caused by both arboviruses and parasites, transmitted through *Aedes*, *Anopheles*, and *Culex* mosquitoes. The authors synthesize the clinical features, global distribution patterns, and transmission dynamics of diseases including dengue, chikungunya, Zika, West Nile virus, Japanese encephalitis, yellow fever, malaria, and lymphatic filariasis. The review highlights how environmental factors such as temperature and humidity, combined with urbanization and climate change, are driving geographic expansion of these diseases beyond their traditional tropical ranges. Current and emerging control strategies are evaluated, including vector management, newly developed vaccines, chemoprophylaxis, and novel biotechnological interventions. The authors conclude that persistent challenges such as insecticide resistance and substantial socioeconomic burdens underscore the urgent need for integrated, multi-pronged approaches to disease control.
items.citation: Yu-Lou Wei, Zhaoyu Wu, Runle Li et al.. Review of selected mosquito-borne diseases: arboviruses (dengue, chikungunya, Zika, West Nile, Japanese encephalitis, yellow fever) and parasitic diseases (malaria, lymphatic Filariasis). Frontiers in Public Health. 2026

items.title: Harnessing mucosal immunity for protective vaccines
items.citation: Dong-il Kwon, Sachin Bhagchandani, Stephen A. Ehrenzeller et al.. Harnessing mucosal immunity for protective vaccines. Nature reviews. Immunology. 2026

items.title: Integrative Proteomics Reveal Neuroimmune and Dopaminergic Alterations Across the Nociceptive Neuraxis in Neuropathic Pain
items.summary: This study investigated the molecular changes occurring across the full pain-processing neuraxis in neuropathic pain, spanning peripheral nerve, spinal cord, and a cortical region involved in affective and cognitive pain processing. Using a rat model of chronic constriction injury combined with high-resolution mass spectrometry-based quantitative proteomics and immunofluorescence validation, the researchers profiled protein expression changes in the sciatic nerve, spinal cord, and orbitofrontal cortex. Across all three regions, neuroinflammatory pathways were prominently dysregulated, with S100 proteins consistently elevated alongside markers of astrocyte and microglial activation, indicating a pervasive and region-spanning neuroimmune response. Notably, the orbitofrontal cortex showed pronounced upregulation of dopamine-regulating proteins, implicating reward-related analgesic circuitry in the affective dimension of chronic pain. The authors propose that neuropathic pain is sustained by an interplay between glial-driven neuroinflammation and dopaminergic neuromodulatory adaptations, and they highlight S100 proteins and dopamine-related targets as candidate therapeutic entry points for more comprehensive pain management.
items.citation: Shreyasi Majumdar, Santosh Kumar Prajapati, Aishwarya Dande et al.. Integrative Proteomics Reveal Neuroimmune and Dopaminergic Alterations Across the Nociceptive Neuraxis in Neuropathic Pain. Cells. 2026

items.title: A Novel Index Measure of Housing-Related Risk as a Predictor of Overdose Among Young People Who Inject Drugs and Injection Networks
items.summary: This study examined the relationship between housing instability and drug overdose risk among young people who inject drugs, arguing that existing research has inadequately captured housing instability by treating it as a single-dimensional construct. The researchers developed a multi-dimensional housing instability risk index composed of five dichotomous variables and applied it to baseline data from a network-based longitudinal study of young people who inject drugs in metropolitan Chicago. Using negative binomial regression and controlling for multiple covariates, they found a significant positive association between the housing instability risk score and lifetime overdose count. The findings support the value of a holistic, multi-dimensional approach to measuring housing instability and suggest that interventions addressing overdose risk should account for the complex and varied ways in which housing instability manifests.
items.citation: Kathleen Kristensen, Leslie D. Williams, Charlie Kaplan et al.. A Novel Index Measure of Housing-Related Risk as a Predictor of Overdose Among Young People Who Inject Drugs and Injection Networks. Journal of Urban Health. 2026

items.title: Behaviour-Conditioned Neural Processes for Adaptive Residential Short-Term Load Forecasting
items.summary: This paper investigates residential short-term load forecasting, which is difficult because household electricity demand varies widely across users and over time due to differing behavioural routines. The authors propose a behaviour-conditioned Attentive Neural Process framework that embeds inferred behavioural structure directly into the forecasting model, representing it as a discrete latent variable alongside a continuous latent variable for shared functional uncertainty across diverse profiles. To avoid reliance on ground-truth behavioural labels, the model uses clustering-derived weak supervision during training while inferring behavioural class distributions purely from context at test time. Experiments on a smart grid dataset with user-disjoint splits and variable forecasting conditions show that the proposed approach improves both point-forecast and probabilistic accuracy over a label-agnostic baseline, with the greatest gains occurring when contextual information is limited. The results support the viability of a single unified model for uncertainty-aware forecasting across heterogeneous households, forecast horizons, and context lengths.
items.citation: Ramin Soleimani, Andrea Visentin, Dirk Pesch. Behaviour-Conditioned Neural Processes for Adaptive Residential Short-Term Load Forecasting. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Euclid: Calibrating distances from surface brightness fluctuations with Early Release Observations of the Fornax cluster
items.summary: This paper presents the first application of surface brightness fluctuations (SBF) to data from the Euclid space mission, using pixel-to-pixel flux variations in galaxy images as a redshift-independent distance indicator. The authors applied the FAST-SBF code to measure stellar fluctuation amplitudes in the visible band for a sample of galaxies in the Fornax cluster from Euclid's Early Release Observations, despite the data reduction pipeline not being optimized for this technique. They calibrated the absolute fluctuation magnitude as a function of galaxy colour using Fornax galaxies, then validated this calibration against galaxies in two additional fields: the Perseus cluster and dwarf satellite candidates around a nearby spiral galaxy. The resulting distances show reasonable overall agreement with literature values, and strong agreement in cases where the measurements were flagged as robust. The authors also compare their results with simple stellar population models, highlighting the additional potential of Euclid SBF measurements to probe the stellar population properties of galaxies beyond pure distance estimation.
items.citation: R. Habas, M. Cantiello, G. Riccio et al.. Euclid: Calibrating distances from surface brightness fluctuations with Early Release Observations of the Fornax cluster. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Adaptive Fault Injection Planning for Multi-Layer Self-Healing AI Infrastructure
items.summary: This paper investigates how faults propagate across multiple layers in GPU-accelerator infrastructure used by hyperscale operators, focusing on the limitations of conventional testing approaches that evaluate each layer in isolation. The authors developed ADA-ST, an adaptive fault-injection methodology that constructs weighted fault-propagation graphs spanning hardware, firmware, management software, and orchestration layers, then uses iterative scenario selection to maximize coverage of cross-layer fault pathways. Drawing on a large production dataset of repair tickets accumulated over several years, they demonstrated that existing static test campaigns leave the vast majority of cross-layer fault-propagation edges unexercised. ADA-ST achieved full edge coverage within a small number of iterations across three successive hardware platforms, and a knowledge-transfer mechanism called FLAM successfully carried propagation insights from older to newer platforms with near-complete fidelity.
