Physical validation on the newest platform confirmed the identified cross-layer vulnerabilities, which included telemetry blind spots, absence-based detection failures, multi-signal correlation gaps, and trust-without-verification propagation between management layers.
items.citation: Saurabh Kulkarni, Yuxin Yang, Rohan Kulkarni et al.. Adaptive Fault Injection Planning for Multi-Layer Self-Healing AI Infrastructure. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Radiopurity material assays and radiation exposure projections for superconducting qubit measurements at SNOLAB
items.summary: This paper investigates the challenge posed by ionizing radiation to superconducting qubit performance and explores the use of a deep underground laboratory to mitigate these effects. The authors conducted an extensive material assay program to characterize the radioactivity of components intended for use in the first underground operation of superconducting qubits at a shielded underground facility, where roughly two kilometers of rock overburden dramatically reduces cosmic ray flux. These assay results were fed into Monte Carlo simulations using the Geant4 particle physics tracking code to estimate the rates and spectral composition of energy deposits from radiogenic sources expected in the experimental assembly. The simulations further identified the dominant particle interaction types contributing to the projected background. Additionally, the authors demonstrate how crystal dynamics simulations using a solid-state physics extension for Geant4 can help guide broader community efforts to develop strategies for mitigating the effects of high-energy particle impacts on quantum coherence.
items.citation: Y. Ahmed, B. Binoy, R. Bunker et al.. Radiopurity material assays and radiation exposure projections for superconducting qubit measurements at SNOLAB. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Quantum Fisher information of magnetic quantum phase transition on Kondo lattice
items.summary: This paper investigates multipartite entanglement across the quantum phase transition of a Kondo lattice model, a system relevant to understanding strange metals and heavy fermion physics. The authors employ quantum Fisher information as a diagnostic tool, examining how it behaves for spin components oriented transverse to the magnetic order parameter. They find that the quantum Fisher information in these transverse channels serves as a witness for the destruction of heavy quasiparticles in the Kondo-destroyed magnetically ordered phase, with the underlying physics clarified through analysis of the antiferromagnetic Heisenberg model. The authors propose that these theoretical predictions could be tested experimentally using both unpolarized and polarized inelastic neutron scattering in the ordered region of the heavy fermion phase diagram. The work demonstrates that carefully chosen operators can simultaneously witness multipartite entanglement in distinct sectors and illuminate the broader physics across different regions of a quantum phase diagram.
items.citation: Yuan Fang, Lei Chen, Mounica Mahankali et al.. Quantum Fisher information of magnetic quantum phase transition on Kondo lattice. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Eccentricity as a Magnifying Glass: Precision Population Inference Enabled by Eccentric Neutron Star--Black Hole Mergers
items.summary: This paper investigates how orbital eccentricity in neutron star–black hole (NSBH) binary mergers can improve our ability to distinguish between different astrophysical formation channels for compact binaries. Because eccentric mergers yield sharper measurements of intrinsic parameters such as component masses and spins compared to circular mergers, the authors explore the downstream benefits for population-level inference using gravitational-wave detectors. They find that enhanced measurements of the effective spin parameter allow spin-orbit misaligned systems to be confidently identified at a much higher rate during the next major observing run, and that neutron star and black hole mass distributions can be recovered with substantially greater fidelity. The study also demonstrates that the characteristic eccentricity distribution predicted for dynamically formed binaries could be statistically tested by the end of that observing run. Overall, the work establishes eccentricity as a powerful diagnostic tool that can help resolve the formation histories of NSBH systems, particularly in light of evidence for measurable eccentricity in at least one observed gravitational-wave event.
items.citation: Alberto Salvarese, Hsin-Yu Chen, Aaron Zimmerman et al.. Eccentricity as a Magnifying Glass: Precision Population Inference Enabled by Eccentric Neutron Star--Black Hole Mergers. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Association between menstrual-related disorders and sexually transmitted infections: A nationwide cross-sectional study in Japan.
items.summary: This nationwide cross-sectional study investigated whether menstrual-related disorders, specifically endometriosis and dysmenorrhea, are associated with sexually transmitted infections among young women in Japan. Using a large insurance claims database, researchers compared the prevalence of five sexually transmitted infections between women with and without menstrual-related disorders, and also examined whether hormonal therapy use modified any observed associations. Women with menstrual-related disorders showed substantially higher prevalence of all five sexually transmitted infections compared to those without such disorders, with endometriosis showing the strongest association, particularly for genital chlamydia infection. The association was consistent across both endometriosis and dysmenorrhea subgroups, while hormonal therapy use had minimal influence on sexually transmitted infection prevalence within the affected group. The authors conclude that sexually transmitted infection screening and reproductive health education should be integrated into routine gynecological care for women with these menstrual conditions, while also cautioning that healthcare-seeking behavior and diagnostic patterns in claims data may partly explain the observed associations.
items.citation: Yoshihara T, Owada S, Arita H et al.. Association between menstrual-related disorders and sexually transmitted infections: A nationwide cross-sectional study in Japan.. PloS one. 2026

query: CRISPR based diagnostics infectious disease detection

items.title: CRISPR-Cas and Infectious Diseases: A Decade of Translational Advances in Molecular Biotechnology.
items.summary: This review examines the progress made over roughly a decade in applying CRISPR-Cas systems to the diagnosis, treatment, and prevention of infectious diseases. The authors survey key diagnostic platforms that enable rapid and sensitive pathogen detection, as well as therapeutic strategies that use CRISPR-Cas9 to target viral genomes and address antimicrobial resistance, and vaccine approaches involving live-attenuated and personalized neoantigen constructs. Their assessment finds that CRISPR-based diagnostics have advanced furthest toward clinical evaluation, having moved from proof-of-concept to preclinical use, while therapeutic and personalized vaccine applications remain at earlier, more exploratory stages due to delivery and safety constraints. The review uniquely integrates all three application areas within a single infectious disease framework and critically evaluates their relative maturity alongside technical, regulatory, and ethical barriers. The authors conclude that realizing the transformative potential of CRISPR-Cas for infectious disease management will require resolving challenges in delivery efficiency, off-target effects, and ethical regulation to enable safe and equitable clinical translation.
items.citation: Mayuri K, Saravanan KM, Somala CS et al.. CRISPR-Cas and Infectious Diseases: A Decade of Translational Advances in Molecular Biotechnology.. Biochemical genetics. 2026

items.title: Trends of nucleic acid - based point-of-care diagnostics for infectious diseases.
items.summary: This review examines the current state and emerging trends of nucleic acid-based point-of-care diagnostics for infectious diseases, motivated by the global need for rapid, decentralized testing outside conventional laboratory settings. The authors surveyed the full diagnostic workflow by organizing the field into three sequential operational steps: nucleic acid extraction, amplification, and detection. For extraction, they assessed approaches such as magnetic bead-based, paper-based, and integrated microfluidic methods with an emphasis on simplicity and equipment-free operation. Amplification strategies reviewed include both thermal cycling methods like PCR and isothermal alternatives such as LAMP, RPA, and NASBA, all adapted for portable and low-power conditions. On the detection side, the review covers platforms ranging from colorimetric and fluorescent lateral flow assays to advanced biosensors incorporating CRISPR/Cas systems and toehold switch mechanisms, which together offer highly sensitive and programmable nucleic acid recognition.
items.citation: Hatem H, Mysara M, Ramadan R. Trends of nucleic acid - based point-of-care diagnostics for infectious diseases.. Journal of biological engineering. 2026

items.title: Improving the Precision of Etiological Diagnosis in Bacterial Infections Using Molecular Technologies: A Comparative Analysis of Platforms, AI Integration, and Point-of-Care Deployment.
items.summary: This review examined the landscape of molecular diagnostic technologies for bacterial infections, with a focus on improving the speed and accuracy of etiological diagnosis amid growing antimicrobial resistance. The authors conducted a structured comparative analysis of major platforms including PCR and its variants, isothermal amplification, next-generation sequencing, CRISPR-based diagnostics, and digital PCR, evaluating each against key analytical parameters such as sensitivity, specificity, limit of detection, turnaround time, and multiplexing capacity. The review also critically assessed the integration of artificial intelligence and machine learning into diagnostic workflows for resistance prediction and clinical decision support. Additionally, the translational potential of these technologies for point-of-care deployment was discussed alongside considerations of clinical validation and real-world operational constraints. The authors conclude that advancing culture-independent bacteriological diagnostics requires balancing analytical performance with clinical interpretability, and they outline key challenges and future directions for the field.
items.citation: Cotelici AM, Bălășoiu AT, Boldeanu MV et al.. Improving the Precision of Etiological Diagnosis in Bacterial Infections Using Molecular Technologies: A Comparative Analysis of Platforms, AI Integration, and Point-of-Care Deployment.. International journal of molecular sciences. 2026

items.title: Synthetic Biology-Enabled Biosensing Platforms for Point-of-Care In Vitro Diagnostics: Programmable Modules, Clinical Applications, and Translational Challenges.
items.summary: This review examines how synthetic biology is transforming point-of-care in vitro diagnostics by enabling programmable, modular biosensing systems that overcome the limitations of conventional fixed-chemistry assays requiring centralized instrumentation. The authors organize synthetic biology-based diagnostic platforms into four functional layers covering molecular recognition, signal transduction and amplification, output generation, and system integration, then discuss four major enabling technologies: cell-free protein synthesis systems, aptamer and riboswitch sensors, CRISPR-Cas diagnostic platforms, and microfluidic integration. Clinical applications spanning infectious disease detection, cancer biomarker analysis, and drug metabolism and toxicity screening are surveyed from recent literature. The review also addresses practical translational challenges beyond analytical sensitivity, including matrix tolerance, workflow complexity, manufacturability, quantitative capability, and regulatory readiness. Future directions identified include artificial intelligence-assisted biosensor design, multimodal readouts, interoperable platform architectures, and rigorous real-world clinical validation.
items.citation: Bao C, Zhang H, Jiang L et al.. Synthetic Biology-Enabled Biosensing Platforms for Point-of-Care In Vitro Diagnostics: Programmable Modules, Clinical Applications, and Translational Challenges.. Biosensors. 2026

items.title: CRISPR/Cas12a2-Mediated Ultrasensitive Assay for Rapid Detection of H1N1 Influenza Virus RNA.
items.summary: This study developed a diagnostic platform for detecting H1N1 influenza virus RNA using the CRISPR-Cas12a2 system, specifically leveraging the SuCas12a2 enzyme's dual RNA cleavage and collateral single-stranded DNA degradation activities to generate a fluorescence signal. The assay targeted the conserved M gene of influenza A H1N1, combining optimized guide RNA design with reverse transcription PCR to achieve highly sensitive detection. Clinical validation using nasopharyngeal swab samples demonstrated strong concordance with real-time quantitative PCR while identifying additional positive cases that qPCR missed, suggesting the platform may offer superior sensitivity. The method achieved a detection limit of five RNA copies per microliter within ninety minutes and showed complete specificity against a panel of other respiratory pathogens. The authors conclude that CRISPR-Cas12a2 represents a robust and rapid molecular diagnostic alternative well suited to outbreak response needs.
items.citation: Wen C, Min X, Yu H et al.. CRISPR/Cas12a2-Mediated Ultrasensitive Assay for Rapid Detection of H1N1 Influenza Virus RNA.. ACS omega. 2026

items.title: Tackling cuproptosis: from metabolic rewiring to therapeutic exploitation in cancer
items.summary: This review examines cuproptosis, a recently characterized form of regulated cell death driven by copper overload that causes mitochondrial dysfunction through aggregation of lipoylated tricarboxylic acid cycle enzymes and destabilization of iron-sulfur cluster proteins. The paper synthesizes current mechanistic understanding of cuproptosis, including its regulation by copper homeostasis, metabolic reprogramming, and signaling pathways such as p53, HIF-1α, Wnt/β-catenin, and AKT. The review also explores how copper shapes antitumor immunity within the tumor microenvironment, highlighting implications for cancer immunotherapy. Therapeutic approaches employing copper ionophores and nanomedicine platforms are discussed as strategies to selectively induce cuproptosis across multiple cancer types, with preclinical evidence supporting particular relevance in malignancies characterized by copper dysregulation or mitochondrial metabolic adaptation. The authors conclude that successful clinical translation will require biomarker-driven patient stratification and optimized drug delivery systems, framing cuproptosis as a promising avenue in precision oncology.
items.citation: Qian Hao, Yu Gan, Xiang Zhou. Tackling cuproptosis: from metabolic rewiring to therapeutic exploitation in cancer. Cellular and Molecular Immunology. 2026

items.title: Therapeutic Potential of Extracellular Vesicles: From Biogenesis, Isolation and Molecular Characterization to Addressing Translational Gaps and Regulatory Barriers
items.summary: This review examines extracellular vesicles as mediators of intercellular communication, covering their biogenesis, molecular composition, and therapeutic potential across human disease. The authors survey the major classes of extracellular vesicles and recent methodological advances in their isolation, characterization, and omics-based profiling, with particular attention to their roles in viral infection, inflammation, immune regulation, and cancer biology. The review highlights emerging clinical applications of mesenchymal stem cell-derived extracellular vesicles in regenerative medicine and oncology, and discusses photobiomodulation as a means of therapeutically modulating extracellular vesicle signaling. Key translational obstacles are addressed, including the need for standardization, scalability, and harmonized quality controls required to meet regulatory oversight from bodies such as the FDA and EMA. The authors conclude that integrating mechanistic understanding with clinical application positions extracellular vesicles as transformative next-generation tools in precision diagnostics and therapeutics.
items.citation: Dragan Primorac, Petar Brlek, Luka Bulić et al.. Therapeutic Potential of Extracellular Vesicles: From Biogenesis, Isolation and Molecular Characterization to Addressing Translational Gaps and Regulatory Barriers. International Journal of Molecular Sciences. 2026

items.title: Recent Advances in Microfluidic Chip Technology for Laboratory Medicine: Innovations and Artificial Intelligence Integration
items.summary: This review examines recent advances in microfluidic chip technology and their applications in laboratory medicine, with particular focus on design innovations and artificial intelligence integration. The authors surveyed developments in fabrication methods such as three-dimensional printing and modular flexible substrates, as well as the incorporation of biosensors capable of detecting circulating tumor cells, nucleic acids, and exosomes in real time. They found that combining these miniaturized platforms with artificial intelligence and machine learning has produced smart diagnostic systems capable of self-optimization, automated analysis, and clinical decision support across areas including cancer diagnostics, infectious disease detection, and point-of-care testing. The review also identifies ongoing challenges in manufacturing scalability, clinical validation, and system integration that must be addressed before widespread adoption. Emerging applications in neuroscience diagnostics and microbiome profiling are highlighted as directions that further illustrate the broad translational potential of the technology.
items.citation: Hong Cai, Dongxia Wang, Yiqun Zhao et al.. Recent Advances in Microfluidic Chip Technology for Laboratory Medicine: Innovations and Artificial Intelligence Integration. Biosensors. 2026

items.title: Cell death in cancer
items.summary: This review examines the expanding landscape of cell death programs and their roles in cancer biology, from tumor development and progression through treatment response. The authors synthesize current understanding of multiple cell death modalities, including apoptosis, necroptosis, pyroptosis, and ferroptosis, within a unified framework relevant to oncology. While apoptosis remains the dominant mechanism triggered by conventional radiation and chemotherapy, non-apoptotic programs are recognized as drivers of inflammatory responses that shape interactions between tumor cells, stromal tissue, and immune components, with direct consequences for immunotherapy efficacy. Ferroptosis is highlighted as a distinct modality driven by iron-dependent lipid peroxidation rather than a canonical induction signal, arising instead from dysregulation of lipid, iron, and redox metabolism. The review also addresses how cancer cells evade these death programs and how such evasion contributes to immune suppression and therapeutic resistance, framing restoration of cell death as a compelling therapeutic strategy.
items.citation: Marcus Conrad, Andreas Strasser, Philipp J. Jost et al.. Cell death in cancer. Cell. 2026

items.title: Biosensing technologies for foodborne pathogen detection and healthcare: principles, emerging materials, and intelligent platforms
items.summary: This review examines the current state and future directions of biosensing technologies for detecting high-priority foodborne pathogens, including Escherichia coli, Salmonella, and Listeria monocytogenes, which remain persistent threats to global public health. The authors survey electrochemical, optical, and microfluidic sensing platforms, analyzing how advances in functional nanomaterials, aptamers, nanozymes, and surface engineering strategies have improved sensor sensitivity, specificity, and performance in complex food matrices. A central focus is the integration of artificial intelligence and machine learning with these biosensors, enabling intelligent pattern recognition, real-time analytics, and high-throughput multiplexed detection that transforms conventional systems into smart diagnostic devices. The review critically identifies key challenges facing the field, such as matrix interference, bioreceptor instability, scalability limitations, and the absence of regulatory standardization, and proposes mitigation strategies including ratiometric sensing, microfluidic pre-treatment, and explainable AI frameworks. Looking forward, the authors advocate for self-powered biosensors, modular pathogen detection panels, blockchain-integrated data management, and standardized validation pipelines as pathways toward deployable, regulation-ready food safety monitoring platforms.
items.citation: Purshottam J. Assudani, Balakrishnan . P., Anny Leema A et al.. Biosensing technologies for foodborne pathogen detection and healthcare: principles, emerging materials, and intelligent platforms. Microchimica Acta. 2026

items.title: Comprehensive Review on Candidemia: Epidemiology, Diagnosis, Treatment, and Future Directions
items.summary: This review examines candidemia, a serious bloodstream infection caused by Candida species that represents a major source of hospital-acquired infections with high mortality and significant healthcare costs. The authors synthesized current knowledge spanning epidemiology, species distribution, diagnostic approaches, and therapeutic strategies. The review identifies diagnostic delay stemming from the limitations of conventional detection methods as a central challenge, and evaluates emerging technologies alongside susceptibility-guided antifungal therapy as means to improve patient outcomes. Therapeutic considerations addressed include empiric and targeted treatment regimens, management of mixed infections, host-drug interactions, and the growing problem of antifungal resistance, with attention given to combination therapy and novel agents in clinical development. Looking ahead, the review highlights artificial intelligence-assisted diagnostics, vaccine-based prophylaxis, and synergistic treatment strategies as promising directions for improving candidemia management.
items.citation: Haojie Dong, Minghui Song, Jingfang Sun et al.. Comprehensive Review on Candidemia: Epidemiology, Diagnosis, Treatment, and Future Directions. iFungi. 2026

items.title: Knowing the Self, Understanding the World: A Dual-Cognition Benchmark for UAV Spatio-temporal Reasoning with MLLMs
items.summary: This paper addresses the gap in evaluating multimodal large language models for unmanned aerial vehicle applications, specifically their ability to reason simultaneously about the UAV's own state and the surrounding environment across multiple viewpoints and time scales. The authors introduce UAV-DualCog, a benchmark built on a dual-cognition framework that encompasses both image and video tasks requiring spatial and temporal grounding rather than simple discrete answer prediction. The benchmark is constructed through an automated pipeline leveraging scene-level semantic point clouds, producing a scalable dataset spanning diverse scenes with numerous landmarks and thousands of question-answer samples. Evaluation of current multimodal large language models reveals substantial performance gaps, with self-state reasoning, viewpoint transformation, precise spatial grounding, and temporal interval localization identified as persistent bottlenecks, while human participants find the tasks tractable. The authors also release a training split from disjoint scenes and demonstrate through lightweight optimization experiments that it offers useful structured supervision, positioning the resource as both an evaluation tool and a data resource for developing more capable UAV agents.
items.citation: Like Liu, Zhengzheng Xu, Haitao He et al.. Knowing the Self, Understanding the World: A Dual-Cognition Benchmark for UAV Spatio-temporal Reasoning with MLLMs. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Handroid: Bridging Dexterous Hand and Humanoid
items.summary: This paper introduces Handroid, a desktop-scale robot platform designed to bridge the gap between dexterous robotic hands and humanoid robots by combining both capabilities within a single reconfigurable system. The platform uses a shared set of electromechanical modules that can be physically reconfigured into either an anthropomorphic dexterous hand or a small humanoid with a head, arms, and legs, without requiring separate hardware. The authors developed a unified control and learning framework supporting teleoperation, dexterous grasping, in-hand manipulation, reinforcement-learning-based locomotion, and motion authoring. The system was validated through real-world experiments including dexterous manipulation tasks and a long-horizon demonstration involving embodiment reconfiguration, locomotion, docking, and pick-and-place operations. The work positions Handroid as a compact, reproducible platform intended to advance research in morphology-reconfigurable robotics and cross-embodiment robot learning.
items.citation: Ruogu Li, Chenyang Ma, Sikai Li et al.. Handroid: Bridging Dexterous Hand and Humanoid. arXiv. 2026

items.title: A Blueprint for Equilibrium-Based Differentiable Continuous-Variable Thermodynamic Computing
items.summary: This paper introduces a blueprint for a thermodynamic computing framework designed to reduce the energy consumption and latency associated with machine-learning workloads. The approach leverages stochastic analog physical processes governed by Langevin dynamics with tunable energy potentials, which allow hardware to natively generate and sample from parameterized energy-based models. Using the framework of probabilistic graphical models, the authors show how popular machine learning model classes can be constructed and trained on top of these hardware-native primitives. Runtime and energy consumption are analyzed through both theoretical arguments and numerical studies, and a preliminary experimental realization is demonstrated using stochastic analog superconducting circuits driven by thermal noise. Together, these contributions outline a roadmap toward energy-efficient thermodynamic hardware platforms for probabilistic machine learning.
items.citation: Owen Lockwood, Jérémy Béjanin, Joost Bus et al.. A Blueprint for Equilibrium-Based Differentiable Continuous-Variable Thermodynamic Computing. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Vision-Language Assistant for Emotional Reactions to Risky Driving
items.summary: This paper investigated how vision-language systems can incorporate emotional expressiveness into driver assistance by detecting risky driving behaviors and generating emotionally tailored verbal responses. The researchers developed a two-module pipeline called Keep Yelling Assistant, in which a computer vision module using object detection identifies dangerous maneuvers such as sudden cut-ins and extracts key driving metrics, while a language module converts this structured information into responses calibrated to user-defined emotional tones such as neutral, humorous, or analytical. Several leading large language models were evaluated as the language backend, and the system was tested on dashcam footage alongside a user study assessing participant preferences and emotional alignment. Results showed that all tested language models received favorable ratings from participants, though preferences differed across emotional personas, and one particular vision-language model combination achieved the highest satisfaction score. The authors conclude that integrating real-time perceptual data with emotionally adaptive dialogue represents a novel direction for in-vehicle artificial intelligence aimed at improving safety, trust, and driver well-being.
items.citation: Harine Choi, Eun Hak Lee, Zhengzhong Tu. Vision-Language Assistant for Emotional Reactions to Risky Driving. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Cluster-Aware Matching via Laplacian Optimal Transport
items.summary: This paper addresses the problem of matching point clouds that have an underlying cluster structure, where region-to-region alignment is more meaningful than point-to-point correspondence. The authors propose Laplacian Optimal Transport (LapOT), which regularizes the classical optimal transport problem with quadratic Laplacian terms derived from similarity graphs of the point clouds, thereby encouraging the resulting coupling to respect the cluster structure of both sets simultaneously. They also introduce Refined Simultaneous Clustering, a method that uses the cluster-aware coupling from LapOT to generate consistent partitions across point sets, overcoming the instability associated with clustering each point set independently. Through theoretical analysis and empirical experiments, the authors demonstrate that LapOT produces alignments that are more consistent, stable, and interpretable than standard optimal transport approaches when the data possess inherent cluster structure.
items.citation: Gabriel Samberg, YoonHaeng Hur, Yuehaw Khoo et al.. Cluster-Aware Matching via Laplacian Optimal Transport. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Physics-enhanced reinforcement learning for real-time optimal control of dynamical systems
items.summary: This paper addresses the challenge of applying reinforcement learning to high-dimensional, nonlinear dynamical systems, where standard methods suffer from poor sample efficiency due to the curse of dimensionality in exploration-exploitation. The authors developed a framework called Physics-EnhAnced Reinforcement Learning (PEARL), which integrates traditional optimal control theory with reinforcement learning through an actor-adjoint algorithm that uses automatic differentiation and adjoint-based sensitivity analysis to compute policy gradients over short time horizons while approximating long-term returns with neural networks. This hybrid approach exploits the differentiability of physical system dynamics to guide policy learning, reducing the number of required environment interactions and mitigating gradient instability over long horizons. The framework was evaluated on two parametric navigation problems in unsteady fluid flows, demonstrating superior performance compared to state-of-the-art reinforcement learning algorithms. PEARL was shown to be sample efficient, to generalize across multiple parametric scenarios, and to scale to high-dimensional state and action spaces without requiring dimensionality reduction or multi-agent decomposition.
items.citation: Matteo Tomasetto, Nicolò Botteghi, Gabriele Bruni et al.. Physics-enhanced reinforcement learning for real-time optimal control of dynamical systems. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Biochemical and Biotechnological Approaches to Disease Diagnosis and Therapeutics: A Comprehensive Review.
items.summary: This review examines how advances in biochemical and biotechnological sciences have reshaped disease diagnosis and treatment across oncological, infectious, metabolic, and degenerative conditions. The authors synthesized key innovations spanning biochemical diagnostics and biotechnology-driven therapeutics, considering their relevance to precision medicine and clinical practice. The review finds that recent developments have meaningfully improved early disease detection, molecular characterization, and targeted therapeutic strategies, while also enabling minimally invasive monitoring and more personalized clinical decision-making. The authors conclude that these innovations are central to the ongoing progress of precision medicine, but stress that continued validation, optimization, and integration into healthcare systems will be necessary to sustain improvements in patient outcomes.
items.citation: Kadwe P, Jena L, Jena D et al.. Biochemical and Biotechnological Approaches to Disease Diagnosis and Therapeutics: A Comprehensive Review.. Cureus. 2026

items.title: Noninvasive SARS-CoV-2 detection using a low-cost electronic nose.
items.summary: The study investigated whether a low-cost, noninvasive electronic nose could accurately detect SARS-CoV-2 infection as an alternative to conventional diagnostic methods that are often uncomfortable, slow, or less accurate in early infection. The device used an array of commercially available metal-oxide gas sensors to detect and analyze volatile organic compounds in saliva and exhaled breath, which shift in composition in response to viral infection. Several machine learning algorithms were applied to classify samples as infected or non-infected, with the best-performing classifier achieving area-under-the-curve values approaching approximately nine-tenths for both sample types in a real-world hospital cohort. The findings demonstrate proof-of-concept feasibility for using this affordable sensor-based platform at the point of care, with exhaled breath performing marginally better than saliva as a diagnostic medium.
items.venue: The Brazilian journal of infectious diseases : an official publication of the Brazilian Society of Infectious Diseases
items.citation: Fialkovitz G, Sousa PL, Hidifira AM et al.. Noninvasive SARS-CoV-2 detection using a low-cost electronic nose.. The Brazilian journal of infectious diseases : an official publication of the Brazilian Society of Infectious Diseases. 2026

items.title: Multiniche mycobiome profiling identifies distinctive fungal dysbiosis in common variable immunodeficiency.
items.summary: This study investigated the fungal microbiome (mycobiome) across multiple body sites in adults with common variable immunodeficiency (CVID), a condition known for bacterial dysbiosis but poorly characterized in terms of fungal communities. Using ITS1 amplicon sequencing of saliva, sputum, and stool samples from CVID patients with and without immune dysregulation alongside healthy controls, the researchers performed diversity analyses, differential abundance testing, and machine learning-based biomarker identification. They found that CVID was consistently associated with reduced fungal richness and evenness across all sampled niches, along with a reproducible enrichment of opportunistic yeasts, particularly *Candida albicans*, at the expense of commensal and environmental fungi. Notably, this fungal dysbiosis did not distinguish between CVID patients with immune dysregulation and those with infectious-only manifestations, suggesting it is a shared feature of the condition rather than a marker of disease severity. Machine learning models built on fungal profiles showed strong discriminatory performance in separating CVID patients from healthy controls, supporting the potential of mycobiome signatures as non-invasive diagnostic biomarkers.
items.citation: Cabanero-Navalon MD, Carda-Diéguez M, Mira A et al.. Multiniche mycobiome profiling identifies distinctive fungal dysbiosis in common variable immunodeficiency.. Frontiers in immunology. 2026

items.title: Salivary exosomal <i>Mycobacterium tuberculosis</i> DNA enables sensitive detection of paucibacillary tuberculosis: a molecular diagnosis study.
items.summary: This study investigated whether tuberculosis could be diagnosed using *Mycobacterium tuberculosis* DNA extracted from salivary exosomes, offering a non-sputum-based alternative for detecting paucibacillary disease. The researchers developed an exosomal nucleic acid (ExoNA) diagnostic method that integrates salivary exosome enrichment, nucleic acid extraction, and dual-target quantitative PCR melting curve analysis, then evaluated its performance in a pilot clinical study of adults with suspected pulmonary tuberculosis. Salivary exosomal DNA was found to be the predominant detectable form of *M. tuberculosis* DNA in saliva, and the method reduced turnaround time substantially while achieving a low limit of detection. The assay demonstrated moderate sensitivity and specificity against microbiological reference standards, and these diagnostic performance metrics were maintained in the subset of patients with negative smear microscopy results, indicating utility in paucibacillary cases. The authors conclude that this non-invasive, rapid salivary diagnostic shows promise but requires multicenter validation in more diverse populations before clinical implementation.
items.venue: Frontiers in cellular and infection microbiology
items.citation: Ma J, Zheng X, He Y et al.. Salivary exosomal <i>Mycobacterium tuberculosis</i> DNA enables sensitive detection of paucibacillary tuberculosis: a molecular diagnosis study.. Frontiers in cellular and infection microbiology. 2026

items.title: Gut microbiome–driven colorectal cancer via immune, metabolic, neural, and endocrine axes reprogramming
items.summary: This review examines the gut microbiome's causal role in colorectal cancer development and treatment response, synthesizing molecular, ecological, and translational evidence across immune, metabolic, neural, and endocrine axes. The authors analyze how dysbiotic microbial communities — characterized by enrichment of pathobionts such as Fusobacterium nucleatum, colibactin-producing Escherichia coli, and enterotoxigenic Bacteroides fragilis, alongside depletion of short-chain-fatty-acid-producing commensals — drive carcinogenesis through genotoxin-mediated DNA damage, epithelial barrier disruption, chronic inflammation, and immune suppression. Bacterial metabolites including colibactin, cytolethal distending toxin, and indolimines are shown to leave defined mutational signatures in human cancer genomes, directly linking microbial exposures to oncogenic transformation. The review conceptualizes colorectal cancer as a biofilm-structured, polymicrobial ecosystem disease in which microbial consortia cooperatively coordinate barrier breakdown, metabolic rewiring, and immune evasion within the tumor microenvironment. Building on this framework, the authors highlight emerging microbiota-targeted therapeutic strategies — including dietary modulation, bacteriophage therapy, engineered live biotherapeutics, and fecal microbiota transplantation — aimed at repositioning the microbiome as an actionable target for precision prevention and treatment.
items.citation: Jhommara Bautista, Mónica Lamas‐Maceiras, Camila Hidalgo-Tinoco et al.. Gut microbiome–driven colorectal cancer via immune, metabolic, neural, and endocrine axes reprogramming. npj Biofilms and Microbiomes. 2026

items.title: Therapeutic Potential of Extracellular Vesicles: From Biogenesis, Isolation and Molecular Characterization to Addressing Translational Gaps and Regulatory Barriers
items.summary: This review examines extracellular vesicles as mediators of intercellular communication, covering their biogenesis, molecular composition, and therapeutic relevance across human disease. The authors survey the major classes of extracellular vesicles and recent methodological advances in their isolation, molecular characterization, and omics-based profiling, with particular attention to their roles in viral infection, inflammation, immune regulation, and cancer biology. The review highlights emerging clinical applications of mesenchymal stem cell-derived extracellular vesicles in regenerative medicine and oncology, and also discusses photobiomodulation as a means of therapeutically modulating extracellular vesicle signaling. A central theme is the translational gap between laboratory discovery and clinical use, encompassing challenges of standardization, scalability, and regulatory validation under existing frameworks such as those governing exosome-based therapeutics. The authors conclude that integrating mechanistic understanding with harmonized quality controls positions extracellular vesicles as transformative tools for next-generation diagnostics and precision therapeutics.
items.citation: Dragan Primorac, Petar Brlek, Luka Bulić et al.. Therapeutic Potential of Extracellular Vesicles: From Biogenesis, Isolation and Molecular Characterization to Addressing Translational Gaps and Regulatory Barriers. International Journal of Molecular Sciences. 2026

items.title: Smart wearable and implantable biosensors for continuous health monitoring: materials, biocompatibility, and AI integration
items.summary: This review examines smart wearable and implantable biosensors designed for continuous, real-time monitoring of biophysical and biochemical signals in the context of personalized and preventive healthcare. The paper surveys advances in flexible, stretchable, and biocompatible materials that enable long-term comfort and integration with the body, as well as multimodal and multi-analyte sensing approaches that improve measurement robustness. It also covers how artificial intelligence is being applied to enhance signal processing and generate predictive health insights from sensor data. The review identifies persistent challenges including motion artifacts, energy autonomy, data privacy, and the clinical interpretability of AI-derived outputs. Overall, the paper synthesizes current progress across materials, device architectures, and AI-assisted strategies to outline the state of the field and its remaining obstacles.
items.citation: Thirumalaisamy Suryaprabha, Chunghyeon Choi, Yanfang Wu et al.. Smart wearable and implantable biosensors for continuous health monitoring: materials, biocompatibility, and AI integration. npj Flexible Electronics. 2026

items.title: Recent Advances in Microfluidic Chip Technology for Laboratory Medicine: Innovations and Artificial Intelligence Integration
items.summary: This review examines recent advances in microfluidic chip technology, commonly known as lab-on-a-chip systems, and their expanding role in laboratory medicine and clinical diagnostics. The authors surveyed innovations in chip design and fabrication approaches, including three-dimensional printing and flexible modular substrates, alongside the integration of advanced biosensors capable of detecting circulating tumor cells, nucleic acids, and exosomes in real time. A central focus is the convergence of microfluidics with artificial intelligence and machine learning, which has enabled the development of smart diagnostic platforms that support automated analysis, self-optimization, and clinical decision support across applications ranging from cancer diagnostics and infectious disease detection to point-of-care testing and personalized medicine. The review also highlights emerging uses in neuroscience diagnostics and microbiome profiling, illustrating the breadth of the technology's potential. The authors discuss persistent challenges in manufacturing scalability, clinical validation, and system integration, and outline future directions for translating these next-generation platforms into routine clinical and public health practice.
items.citation: Hong Cai, Dongxia Wang, Yiqun Zhao et al.. Recent Advances in Microfluidic Chip Technology for Laboratory Medicine: Innovations and Artificial Intelligence Integration. Biosensors. 2026

items.title: Clinical Applications of Extracellular Vesicles: Promises and Pitfalls
items.summary: This narrative review examines the clinical potential of extracellular vesicles, membrane-bound nanoparticles released by virtually all cell types that mediate intercellular communication by transferring proteins, lipids, and nucleic acids. The authors surveyed the emerging roles of these vesicles across a broad range of medical specialties, including oncology, neurology, cardiology, dermatology, and reproductive medicine, among others, drawing on current classification frameworks such as the MISEV2023 guidelines. Across these disciplines, extracellular vesicles show promise both as diagnostic biomarkers for detecting physiological and pathological states and as vehicles for targeted drug delivery and regenerative therapies, with exosomes in particular attracting attention as nanocarriers for precision medicine. However, the review identifies substantial obstacles to clinical translation, including the lack of standardized isolation and characterization protocols, challenges in defining appropriate dosing, difficulties in scaling up production under good manufacturing practice conditions, and the need for regulatory approval. The authors conclude that interdisciplinary collaboration and rigorous clinical testing are essential to realize the transformative potential of extracellular vesicles in personalized healthcare.
items.citation: Dragan Primorac, Petar Brlek, Luka Bulić et al.. Clinical Applications of Extracellular Vesicles: Promises and Pitfalls. International Journal of Molecular Sciences. 2026

items.title: Gastroduodenal Disorders
items.summary: This paper presents a classification framework for gastroduodenal disorders, focusing on symptoms attributable to the gastroduodenal region. The authors organize these conditions into five major categories encompassing functional dyspepsia, nausea and vomiting disorders, belching disorders, inability to belch syndrome, and rumination syndrome, each with defined clinical characteristics and, in several cases, distinct subcategories. Notable features of the classification include the recognition of cannabinoid hyperemesis syndrome and inability to belch syndrome as discrete diagnostic entities, the latter being described as a new category. The framework distinguishes conditions by their specific symptom patterns, such as meal-related versus non-meal-related epigastric symptoms in functional dyspepsia, and by the anatomical origin of gas in belching disorders. Overall, the paper provides a structured diagnostic taxonomy intended to guide the identification and differentiation of functional gastroduodenal conditions.
items.citation: H. Tornblom, Florencia Carbone, William L. Hasler et al.. Gastroduodenal Disorders. Gastroenterology. 2026

items.title: Knowing the Self, Understanding the World: A Dual-Cognition Benchmark for UAV Spatio-temporal Reasoning with MLLMs
items.summary: This paper investigates the capabilities of multimodal large language models in unmanned aerial vehicle scenarios, specifically targeting what the authors call "dual cognition" — the simultaneous ability to reason about the UAV's own state and the surrounding environment across multiple viewpoints and time. To study this, the authors constructed UAV-DualCog, a benchmark built from scene-level semantic point clouds through an automated pipeline, encompassing both image and video tasks that require spatial and temporal grounding rather than simple discrete answer selection. Evaluations of current multimodal large language models on this benchmark reveal substantial shortcomings, with self-state reasoning, viewpoint transformation, precise spatial grounding, and temporal interval localization identified as persistent failure points. Human baseline testing confirmed the benchmark is interpretable to people, establishing that the difficulty is specific to existing models rather than an artifact of ambiguous task design. The authors also introduce a training split from disjoint scenes and demonstrate through a lightweight optimization probe that this data provides useful structured supervision, positioning the benchmark as both an evaluation tool and a training resource for advancing aerial agent capabilities.
items.citation: Like Liu, Zhengzheng Xu, Haitao He et al.. Knowing the Self, Understanding the World: A Dual-Cognition Benchmark for UAV Spatio-temporal Reasoning with MLLMs. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Handroid: Bridging Dexterous Hand and Humanoid
items.summary: The paper introduces Handroid, a desktop-scale robot platform designed to unify the capabilities of dexterous hands and humanoid robots within a single reconfigurable system. The researchers built a dual-embodiment platform that reuses the same electromechanical body to function either as an anthropomorphic dexterous hand or as a small humanoid with a head, arms, and legs, all within a compact and lightweight form factor. A unified control and learning framework supports a wide range of capabilities including teleoperation, dexterous grasping, in-hand manipulation, reinforcement-learning-based locomotion, and motion authoring. The platform was validated through real-world experiments spanning dexterous manipulation, gait generation, and a long-horizon task that required the robot to reconfigure between embodiments, navigate, dock, and perform pick-and-place operations. The authors position Handroid as a reproducible and compact platform for studying morphology-reconfigurable robotics and cross-embodiment learning.
items.citation: Ruogu Li, Chenyang Ma, Sikai Li et al.. Handroid: Bridging Dexterous Hand and Humanoid. arXiv. 2026

items.title: A Blueprint for Equilibrium-Based Differentiable Continuous-Variable Thermodynamic Computing
items.summary: This paper proposes a framework for thermodynamic computing designed to reduce the energy consumption and latency associated with machine-learning workloads. The approach centers on analog physical hardware in which stochastic processes governed by Langevin dynamics with tunable energy potentials are used to generate and sample from parameterized energy-based models. Using the formalism of probabilistic graphical models, the authors show how popular machine-learning model classes can be constructed and trained directly on these hardware-native representations. They analyze the theoretical runtime and energy efficiency of various models within this thermodynamic paradigm and support their findings with numerical studies. As an experimental proof of concept, they demonstrate stochastic analog superconducting circuits driven by thermal noise as a candidate physical implementation of the proposed stack.
items.citation: Owen Lockwood, Jérémy Béjanin, Joost Bus et al.. A Blueprint for Equilibrium-Based Differentiable Continuous-Variable Thermodynamic Computing. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Vision-Language Assistant for Emotional Reactions to Risky Driving
items.summary: This study investigated whether a vision-language system could detect risky driving behaviors and respond to them in emotionally expressive, user-tailored ways to improve driver awareness and comfort. The researchers developed a pipeline called Keep Yelling Assistant, which combines a computer vision module using object detection models to identify dangerous maneuvers such as sudden cut-ins and extract key driving metrics, with a language module that converts those metrics into verbal reactions tuned to user-selected emotional tones such as neutral, humorous, or analytical. The results showed that all tested language models received favorable ratings from participants, though preferences varied across different persona styles, with one model and detector pairing achieving the highest overall satisfaction score. The authors conclude that combining real-time driving perception with emotionally adaptive dialogue represents a new direction for emotionally intelligent in-vehicle AI with implications for safety, trust, and driver well-being.
items.citation: Harine Choi, Eun Hak Lee, Zhengzhong Tu. Vision-Language Assistant for Emotional Reactions to Risky Driving. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Cluster-Aware Matching via Laplacian Optimal Transport
items.summary: This paper addresses the problem of matching point clouds that have an intrinsic cluster structure, where robust region-to-region alignment is preferred over precise point-to-point correspondence. The authors propose Laplacian Optimal Transport, which regularizes the classical optimal transport problem with quadratic Laplacian terms derived from similarity graphs of the point clouds, encouraging the resulting coupling to respect the cluster organization of both sets. Through theoretical analysis and empirical experiments, the authors demonstrate that their approach yields more stable, consistent, and interpretable alignments between structured point clouds compared to existing methods.

items.title: Physics-enhanced reinforcement learning for real-time optimal control of dynamical systems
items.summary: This paper investigates how to make reinforcement learning more practical for controlling high-dimensional, nonlinear dynamical systems, where standard approaches struggle due to poor sample efficiency and the curse of dimensionality. The authors developed a framework called Physics-EnhAnced Reinforcement Learning (PEARL), which combines reinforcement learning with classical optimal control techniques by introducing an actor-adjoint algorithm that exploits the differentiability of the system dynamics. Testing on two parametric navigation problems in unsteady fluid flows, the authors demonstrate that PEARL outperforms state-of-the-art reinforcement learning algorithms, generalizes across multiple parametric scenarios, and scales to high-dimensional state and action spaces without needing dimensionality reduction or multi-agent decomposition.

items.venue: Animals : an open access journal from MDPI

items.title: Trends of nucleic acid - based point-of-care diagnostics for infectious diseases.
items.summary: This review paper examined the current landscape of nucleic acid-based point-of-care diagnostics for infectious diseases, motivated by the global need for decentralized, rapid, and accessible testing. The authors organized their comprehensive overview around the three core operational steps of such diagnostics: nucleic acid extraction, amplification, and detection. For extraction, they evaluated approaches including magnetic bead-based, paper-based, and integrated microfluidic methods, emphasizing their suitability for simplified and equipment-free deployment. They surveyed key amplification strategies, including both thermal cycling and isothermal methods, that have been adapted for portable and low-power conditions. On the detection side, the review covered a spectrum from conventional colorimetric and fluorescent assays to advanced biosensor platforms exploiting CRISPR/Cas systems and toehold switch principles, highlighting the trajectory toward increasingly sensitive and programmable nucleic acid recognition at the point of care.
items.citation: Hatem H, Mysara M, Ramadan R. Trends of nucleic acid - based point-of-care diagnostics for infectious diseases.. Journal of biological engineering. 2026

query: group A streptococcus molecular diagnostic test

items.title: Performance of the Xpert Xpress Strep A for detection of <i>Streptococcus pyogenes</i> from pediatric pleural fluids.
items.summary: This study evaluated the diagnostic performance of the Xpert Xpress Strep A, a quantitative real-time PCR assay originally designed for throat swabs, for detecting *Streptococcus pyogenes* from pediatric pleural fluids, where conventional culture is known to have poor sensitivity. Researchers compared the assay against bacterial culture and a composite reference standard comprising positive culture combined with a laboratory-developed PCR targeting a different gene, using a convenience sample of pediatric pleural fluid specimens collected over approximately two years. Xpert detected all confirmed positive specimens, achieving perfect agreement with the composite reference standard and the laboratory-developed PCR, while bacterial culture alone identified only a minority of positive cases. The markedly superior sensitivity of Xpert over culture is particularly clinically relevant because many patients receive antibiotics before sample collection, reducing microbial viability. The authors conclude that Xpert Xpress Strep A offers rapid, highly sensitive detection of Group A *Streptococcus* from pleural fluid, supporting both timely clinical management and public health surveillance of invasive disease.
items.citation: Amaral G, Gadkar VJ, Almodahka A et al.. Performance of the Xpert Xpress Strep A for detection of <i>Streptococcus pyogenes</i> from pediatric pleural fluids.. Microbiology spectrum. 2026

items.title: Microbiota in cancer: current understandings and future perspectives
items.summary: This review examines the relationship between the microbiota and cancer, covering how microbial communities influence carcinogenesis, tumor progression, and patient prognosis. The authors trace the historical development of this research field and analyze the molecular mechanisms by which the microbiota modulates oncogenic pathways, immune responses, and tumor metabolism. The review addresses the dual nature of microbial communities in cancer, recognizing that the microbiota can both promote tumor growth and serve as a target for therapeutic intervention. Particular attention is given to how the microbiota influences responses to immunotherapy, chemotherapy, and radiotherapy, as well as emerging anticancer strategies such as probiotics, antibiotics, and fecal microbiota transplantation. The authors conclude by emphasizing the need for personalized, microbiota-targeted approaches in oncology and propose a comprehensive framework to guide future research and clinical applications in this field.
items.citation: Yanxi Yao, Yifei Zhu, Keji Chen et al.. Microbiota in cancer: current understandings and future perspectives. Signal Transduction and Targeted Therapy. 2026

items.title: Graphene oxide as smart sustainable nanomaterial: a versatile multifunctional material with transformative potential in advanced materials science research
items.summary: This review examines graphene oxide, a two-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterial, as a multifunctional platform capable of addressing challenges across energy, environmental, and healthcare domains. The paper surveys the synthesis chemistry and property modification strategies that underpin graphene oxide's tunable characteristics, including its adjustable band gap, high surface area, structural flexibility, and capacity for surface functionalization across dimensional configurations ranging from zero-dimensional to three-dimensional architectures. The review covers a broad range of applications, including photocatalytic degradation of pollutants and microplastics, adsorption of toxic substances, carbon dioxide photoreduction, hydrogen production, battery technologies, and ultra-sensitive detection via surface-enhanced Raman scattering and gas sensing. Healthcare applications are also discussed alongside the material's promise as a sustainable industrial nanomaterial. The paper acknowledges significant practical obstacles facing graphene oxide, including scalability limitations, long-term stability concerns, and reproducibility issues, and proposes possible solutions to support its transition toward real-world deployment.
items.citation: S.N. Thakur, Ayush Badoni, Rupam Sharma et al.. Graphene oxide as smart sustainable nanomaterial: a versatile multifunctional material with transformative potential in advanced materials science research. npj Materials Sustainability. 2026

items.title: Gut Microbiota-Derived Short-Chain Fatty Acids in Inflammatory Bowel Disease: Mechanistic Insights into Gut Inflammation, Barrier Function, and Therapeutic Potential
items.summary: This review examined the relationship between gut microbiota-derived short-chain fatty acids (SCFAs) and inflammatory bowel disease, which encompasses Crohn's disease and ulcerative colitis. The authors synthesized existing research on how SCFAs, primarily acetate, propionate, and butyrate, are produced through microbial fermentation of dietary fiber and influence gut physiology. Butyrate was highlighted as particularly important, functioning as an energy source for colonocytes while also reinforcing epithelial barrier integrity, suppressing pro-inflammatory cytokines, and modulating both local and systemic immune responses. Despite these promising biological roles, the review noted that clinical trials investigating SCFA administration have produced variable and inconsistent outcomes, attributed to differences in study design and patient populations. The authors critically assessed the therapeutic potential of all three major SCFAs in IBD management, situating their effects within the broader context of intestinal barrier function, immune modulation, and microbiota composition.
items.citation: Roberta Ottria, Susan Mirmajidi, Pierangela Ciuffreda. Gut Microbiota-Derived Short-Chain Fatty Acids in Inflammatory Bowel Disease: Mechanistic Insights into Gut Inflammation, Barrier Function, and Therapeutic Potential. International Journal of Molecular Sciences. 2026

items.title: Sepsis
items.citation: Mervyn Singer, Derek C Angus, Djillali Annane et al.. Sepsis. The Lancet. 2026

items.title: CRISPR–Cas technologies in neurodegenerative disorders: mechanistic insights, therapeutic potential, and translational challenges
items.summary: This review examines the application of CRISPR-Cas genome-editing technologies to neurodegenerative diseases including Alzheimer's, Parkinson's, Huntington's, and amyotrophic lateral sclerosis. The authors critically evaluate preclinical and early translational evidence showing that various CRISPR platforms can correct pathogenic mutations, suppress toxic gene expression, and restore neuronal function, with advanced modalities such as base editing, prime editing, and RNA-targeting systems offering improved precision in post-mitotic neurons. The review also highlights how AI-assisted guide RNA design, machine-learning prediction of off-target effects, and CRISPR-based diagnostics are enhancing therapeutic safety and monitoring, while patient-derived brain organoids provide human-relevant models for validation. Despite this progress, the authors identify substantial remaining obstacles including delivery across the blood-brain barrier, immune responses, long-term safety, and ethical and regulatory hurdles. They conclude that CRISPR-Cas technologies hold genuine disease-modifying potential for neurodegeneration, contingent on continued advances in delivery systems, artificial intelligence integration, and regulatory frameworks.
items.citation: Raya Kh. Yashooa, Ari Q. Nabi, Shukur Wasman Smail et al.. CRISPR–Cas technologies in neurodegenerative disorders: mechanistic insights, therapeutic potential, and translational challenges. Frontiers in Neurology. 2026

items.title: Interplay of Microbiome, Oxidative Stress and Inflammation in Health and Disease
items.summary: This review examines the complex relationships among the human microbiome, oxidative stress, and inflammation in the context of both health and disease. The authors synthesize existing evidence on how microbial metabolites and specific probiotic strains help maintain redox homeostasis through mechanisms including immune regulation, modulation of inflammatory processes, and influence over mitochondrial reactive oxygen species production and antioxidant signaling. Conversely, oxidative stress and inflammation can reciprocally disrupt the microbiome by altering its diversity and function, potentially creating a self-reinforcing cycle that undermines homeostasis. The review concludes that this bidirectional interplay is increasingly recognized as relevant to a range of physiological and pathological states, though the underlying relationships remain incompletely understood.
items.citation: Lourdes Herrera-Quintana, Pablo Iturbe-Sanz, Jorge Olivares-Arancibia et al.. Interplay of Microbiome, Oxidative Stress and Inflammation in Health and Disease. Antioxidants. 2026

items.title: Nonequilibrium thermodynamics of feedback-control: a phase-space perspective
items.summary: This paper examines the nonequilibrium thermodynamics of Maxwell demon-type feedback control systems, focusing on cases where the demon does not have access to the system's complete phase space, a common limitation in real-world applications. The author derives generalized fluctuation relations applicable to these singular feedback-controlled systems, introducing a central concept termed "unavailable information," which represents the portion of measurement-acquired information that cannot be converted into useful work. To illustrate the framework, a Szilard engine with finite measurement resolution is analyzed, revealing that in the high-resolution limit the information acquired by the demon can diverge while the extractable work remains bounded. This apparent paradox is resolved by showing that an equally divergent quantity of information becomes unavailable, preserving thermodynamic consistency. The work thus provides a phase-space perspective that generalizes the standard information-thermodynamics relationship to physically realistic measurement scenarios.
items.citation: José A. Almanza-Marrero. Nonequilibrium thermodynamics of feedback-control: a phase-space perspective. arXiv. 2026

items.title: PagedWeight: Efficient MoE LLM Serving with Dynamic Quality-Aware Weight Quantization
items.summary: This paper addresses the memory tension that arises in Mixture-of-Experts large language model serving when GPU memory must be shared between model weights and a growing KV cache. The authors propose PagedWeight, a runtime management system that dynamically quantizes expert weights and balances their numerical precision against KV cache demands in a quality-aware manner. The approach explicitly navigates the tradeoff among task accuracy, memory consumption, and throughput or latency, adapting quantization decisions on the fly rather than applying a fixed static scheme. Evaluated across memory-sensitive serving scenarios, PagedWeight achieves accuracy equivalent to full-precision models while delivering substantial GPU memory savings and throughput gains, and outperforms existing quantization baselines in output quality at comparable memory budgets with minimal throughput penalty.
items.citation: Yuchen Yang, Yifan Zhao, Anisha Dasgupta et al.. PagedWeight: Efficient MoE LLM Serving with Dynamic Quality-Aware Weight Quantization. arXiv. 2026

items.title: A Dynamical Phase-Field Model for the Optical Properties of Ferroelectrics
items.summary: This paper addresses the challenge of predicting optical properties in ferroelectric materials, where the complex domain structure is intimately linked to optical behavior but has lacked adequate theoretical treatment. The authors developed a dynamical phase-field model that couples the ferroelectric order parameter to a local electronic polarization field, enabling spatially resolved, temperature- and wavelength-dependent optical predictions in realistic ferroelectric microstructures. Applying this framework to barium titanate thin films, they simulated how the ferroelectric domain structure influences the local refractive index and electro-optic response under varying electric fields and temperatures. They found that domain walls dramatically enhance the local electro-optic response to values several times larger than the bulk single-crystal value, and their simulations quantitatively reproduced experimentally measured electro-optic coefficients as well as temperature-dependent behavior across multiple phase transitions, revealing how phase competition and coexistence govern the optical response. The work establishes a general computational approach for designing ferroelectric materials for photonic applications.
items.citation: Aiden Ross, Anya Frazer, Venkatraman Gopalan et al.. A Dynamical Phase-Field Model for the Optical Properties of Ferroelectrics. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Dressed States Call for Logarithmic Asymptotic Symmetries
items.summary: The paper investigates what spacetime symmetry group underlies the quantum description of particles dressed with clouds of infrared gauge bosons, following the spirit of Wigner's classification of elementary particles as irreducible unitary representations of symmetry groups. The authors demonstrate that standard asymptotic symmetries, such as the Bondi-Metzner-Sachs group in the gravitational case, must be supplemented by previously identified logarithmic symmetries to account for dressed states. A key result is that the Hilbert space of a dressed particle factorizes as a tensor product of the space of bare one-particle states with a Hilbert space of soft gauge bosons. This factorization is shown to be impossible without the logarithmic transformations, which are responsible for generating a Heisenberg central extension that is essential to the structure of the soft sector.
items.citation: Jorrit Bosma, Jeremy A. Mann, Charles Marteau et al.. Dressed States Call for Logarithmic Asymptotic Symmetries. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Physics-enhanced reinforcement learning for real-time optimal control of dynamical systems
items.summary: This paper addresses the challenge of applying reinforcement learning to high-dimensional, parametric dynamical systems, where standard approaches suffer from poor sample efficiency and difficulty scaling to large state and action spaces. The authors developed a framework called Physics-EnhAnced Reinforcement Learning (PEARL), which combines reinforcement learning with classical optimal control theory by introducing an actor-adjoint algorithm that uses automatic differentiation to compute policy gradients and adjoint-based sensitivity analysis to approximate future returns via neural networks. This hybrid approach reduces the number of required environment interactions by leveraging the differentiable physics of the system, while also mitigating instabilities that arise from propagating gradients over long time horizons. The framework was evaluated on two parametric navigation problems in unsteady fluid flows, where it outperformed state-of-the-art reinforcement learning algorithms in terms of both sample efficiency and control performance. The results demonstrate that PEARL generalizes across multiple flow scenarios and scales to high-dimensional state and action spaces without relying on dimensionality reduction or multi-agent decomposition strategies.
items.citation: Matteo Tomasetto, Nicolò Botteghi, Gabriele Bruni et al.. Physics-enhanced reinforcement learning for real-time optimal control of dynamical systems. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Quantum scanning synthetic optical holography
items.citation: Josué R. León-Torres, Byron Caiza, Nadia Baumann et al.. Quantum scanning synthetic optical holography. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Innovative epitope-based ELISA assay for detecting Group B Streptococcus carriage in pregnant women.
items.summary: The paper studied whether synthetic peptide epitopes derived from Group B Streptococcus proteins could serve as the basis for a novel ELISA-based diagnostic test to detect GBS carriage in pregnant women, offering an alternative to the current culture-based gold standard. The researchers selected three epitopes from the bacterial proteins GroEL, enolase, and EF-Tu, and tested them individually and in combination against serum samples from GBS-positive and GBS-negative pregnant women, measuring IgG, IgM, and IgA antibody responses. All three epitopes, both alone and in mixtures, significantly distinguished between the two groups, with the enolase-derived and EF-Tu-derived peptides performing best individually due to their lower required concentrations and higher absorbance values in positive samples. Combining the epitopes in various mixtures further improved immunoreactivity beyond what any single epitope achieved alone. The authors conclude that this epitope-based ELISA represents a promising and potentially pioneering alternative to culture methods for GBS immunodiagnosis in pregnancy.
items.citation: Brzychczy-Włoch M, Dobrut A, Drożdż K et al.. Innovative epitope-based ELISA assay for detecting Group B Streptococcus carriage in pregnant women.. Applied microbiology and biotechnology. 2026

items.title: Bridging the diagnostic gap: the role of culture methods in the era of syndromic panels for central nervous system infections.
items.summary: This study evaluated the diagnostic performance of a syndromic molecular panel for central nervous system infections by comparing it against conventional cerebrospinal fluid culture at a national reference center over three years. Using a large dataset of paired molecular and culture results, the researchers calculated measures of concordance, sensitivity, specificity, and predictive values for the panel both across all pathogens and when restricted to only the bacterial targets included in the panel. The syndromic panel demonstrated high specificity and an excellent negative predictive value when analysis was confined to its covered bacterial targets, but overall sensitivity was substantially lower when all clinically relevant organisms were considered. Conventional culture identified important pathogens absent from the panel's target list, including common staphylococci, streptococci, and rare environmental organisms, underscoring a meaningful diagnostic gap. The authors conclude that syndromic molecular panels cannot serve as standalone diagnostics and must be systematically combined with conventional culture to ensure comprehensive pathogen detection and guide appropriate antimicrobial therapy.
items.venue: Frontiers in cellular and infection microbiology
items.citation: Sberna G, D'Arezzo S, Valli MB et al.. Bridging the diagnostic gap: the role of culture methods in the era of syndromic panels for central nervous system infections.. Frontiers in cellular and infection microbiology. 2026

items.title: Performance of the LIAISON PLEX gram-positive blood culture assay for identifying bacterial pathogens and resistance genes in blood cultures.
items.summary: This study evaluated the diagnostic performance of the LIAISON PLEX Gram-Positive Blood Culture Assay, a multiplexed molecular test designed to rapidly identify gram-positive bacterial pathogens and antibiotic resistance markers directly from positive blood culture bottles. The assay was assessed across multiple sites using a combination of prospective clinical samples, pre-selected samples, and contrived specimens, with analytical testing also examining inclusivity, cross-reactivity, interference, and compatibility with blood culture bottles from multiple manufacturers. The assay demonstrated very high overall sensitivity and specificity for detecting its panel of gram-positive bacterial targets and resistance markers, with the vast majority of tested strains successfully identified in inclusivity testing and only minimal cross-reactivity observed among off-panel species. Performance was maintained in the context of polymicrobial infections and in the presence of potential interfering substances. The authors conclude that the assay offers fast, accurate, and reliable detection of clinically significant gram-positive organisms and their resistance genes, with implications for antibiotic stewardship and patient management in bloodstream infections.
items.citation: Chow S-K, Stevig C, Granato P et al.. Performance of the LIAISON PLEX gram-positive blood culture assay for identifying bacterial pathogens and resistance genes in blood cultures.. Journal of clinical microbiology. 2026

items.title: Validation of an in-house developed rapid assay for Group B Streptococcus detection in pregnancy.
items.summary: This study addressed the need for rapid, accurate detection of Group B Streptococcus colonization in pregnant women, a key risk factor for neonatal infections and preterm stillbirth. The researchers developed an in-house assay combining CRISPR/Cas12a with recombinase polymerase amplification, an isothermal amplification method, and validated it against a commercial quantitative real-time PCR assay using vaginal-rectal swabs from pregnant women. The assay demonstrated high specificity by accurately distinguishing GBS from other reproductive tract pathogens, and results could be read either visually via colloidal gold test strips or with a fluorescence reader within one hour. Clinical evaluation on a large cohort of swabs showed strong concordance with the conventional PCR method. The authors conclude that this platform offers high sensitivity, specificity, and accuracy with a rapid turnaround, suggesting meaningful potential for clinical deployment in prenatal GBS screening.
items.venue: International journal of medical microbiology : IJMM
items.citation: Hu S, Xie J, Liu L et al.. Validation of an in-house developed rapid assay for Group B Streptococcus detection in pregnancy.. International journal of medical microbiology : IJMM. 2026

items.title: Utility and clinical relevance of multiplex PCR panels for diagnosis of central nervous system infections.
items.summary: This study evaluated the real-world diagnostic performance and clinical utility of multiplex polymerase chain reaction panels designed to detect pathogens causing meningitis and encephalitis directly from cerebrospinal fluid samples. Researchers retrospectively reviewed samples tested over a five-year period at a tertiary care hospital, examining test results, pathogen detection rates, co-detections, and whether positive findings led to documented changes in diagnosis or treatment. Viral pathogens dominated detections, with bacterial agents also identified, and co-detections occurred in a notable proportion of positive cases. Despite the panels' capacity for rapid, broad pathogen identification, fewer than half of positive results were associated with a clinically relevant action, repeat testing after an initial negative result yielded little additional diagnostic value, and some tests were ordered for conditions the panels do not cover. The authors conclude that while these panels have clear diagnostic potential, suboptimal test utilisation and limited clinician awareness of panel contents constrain their clinical impact, underscoring the need for clearer testing criteria and improved education.
items.venue: The Indian journal of medical research
items.citation: Gürler M, Karaoğlu Ç, Yurtcu EE et al.. Utility and clinical relevance of multiplex PCR panels for diagnosis of central nervous system infections.. The Indian journal of medical research. 2026

query: point-of-care nucleic acid amplification test infectious disease

items.title: Recent Advances in Microfluidic Chip Technology for Laboratory Medicine: Innovations and Artificial Intelligence Integration
items.summary: This review paper examines recent advances in microfluidic chip technology, commonly referred to as lab-on-a-chip systems, and their transformative impact on laboratory medicine. The authors conducted a comprehensive review of innovations in chip design and fabrication, including developments such as three-dimensional printing, modular architectures, and flexible substrates, alongside progress in biosensor integration for detecting circulating tumor cells, nucleic acids, and exosomes. A central focus is the convergence of microfluidics with artificial intelligence and machine learning, which has enabled the development of smart diagnostic platforms capable of self-optimization, automated analysis, and clinical decision support across applications ranging from cancer diagnostics and infectious disease detection to point-of-care testing and personalized medicine. The review also highlights emerging uses in neuroscience diagnostics and microbiome profiling, underscoring the broad clinical potential of these technologies.
