Alongside these advances, the authors address ongoing challenges in manufacturing scalability, clinical validation, and system integration that must be resolved before next-generation microfluidic platforms can be routinely adopted in clinical and public health settings.
items.citation: Hong Cai, Dongxia Wang, Yiqun Zhao et al.. Recent Advances in Microfluidic Chip Technology for Laboratory Medicine: Innovations and Artificial Intelligence Integration. Biosensors. 2026

items.title: Trends of nucleic acid - based point-of-care diagnostics for infectious diseases.
items.summary: This review paper examines the landscape of nucleic acid-based point-of-care diagnostics for infectious diseases, motivated by the global need for decentralized, rapid, and accessible testing. The authors surveyed the full workflow of point-of-care nucleic acid testing by organizing their analysis around three operational stages: extraction, amplification, and detection. For extraction, they assessed approaches including magnetic bead-based, paper-based, and integrated microfluidic methods with emphasis on simplicity and equipment-free operation. Amplification strategies reviewed included both thermal cycling methods such as polymerase chain reaction and isothermal alternatives such as loop-mediated isothermal amplification and recombinase polymerase amplification, all adapted for portable, low-power conditions. On the detection side, the review spans colorimetric and fluorescent lateral flow assays through to advanced biosensor platforms exploiting CRISPR/Cas systems and toehold switch mechanisms, which offer highly sensitive and programmable nucleic acid recognition suitable for point-of-care deployment.
items.citation: Hatem H, Mysara M, Ramadan R. Trends of nucleic acid - based point-of-care diagnostics for infectious diseases.. Journal of biological engineering. 2026

items.title: A Novel Voltammetric Point-of-Care Device for Rapid and Accurate Nucleic Acid and Viral Pathogen Detection.
items.summary: This paper developed and evaluated a novel electrochemical nucleic acid amplification test that combines isothermal amplification with real-time voltammetric detection for infectious disease diagnostics. The approach detects proton release generated during amplification by redox probing of a pH-sensing compound, addressing limitations of traditional lateral flow assays and optical detection methods. The system was tested against multiple respiratory pathogens including SARS-CoV-2, influenza A and B viruses, and RSV A using RNA purified from clinical samples, achieving high concordance with RT-qPCR. A proof-of-concept extraction-free workflow was also demonstrated using equine nasal swab samples for a veterinary pathogen, with all samples correctly classified. The authors conclude that real-time voltammetric LAMP is feasible across several sample types and pathogens, though broader validation in additional matrices and prospective cohorts is still needed.
items.citation: Munita R, Lyttleton R, Danefur E et al.. A Novel Voltammetric Point-of-Care Device for Rapid and Accurate Nucleic Acid and Viral Pathogen Detection.. ACS omega. 2026

items.title: Improving the Precision of Etiological Diagnosis in Bacterial Infections Using Molecular Technologies: A Comparative Analysis of Platforms, AI Integration, and Point-of-Care Deployment.
items.summary: This review examined the landscape of molecular diagnostic technologies for bacterial infections, with particular emphasis on their role in addressing the growing challenge of antimicrobial resistance. The authors conducted a comparative analysis of major platforms including various forms of PCR, isothermal amplification, next-generation sequencing, CRISPR-based diagnostics, and digital PCR, evaluating each against key performance parameters such as sensitivity, specificity, detection limits, turnaround time, and multiplexing capability. The integration of artificial intelligence and machine learning into diagnostic workflows for resistance prediction and clinical decision support was also critically assessed, alongside the translational potential of these technologies for point-of-care deployment. The review concludes that while molecular approaches offer substantial improvements over conventional culture-based methods in speed and sensitivity, balancing analytical performance with clinical interpretability remains a central challenge. The authors identify key obstacles and future directions for advancing culture-independent bacteriological diagnostics in real-world clinical settings.
items.citation: Cotelici AM, Bălășoiu AT, Boldeanu MV et al.. Improving the Precision of Etiological Diagnosis in Bacterial Infections Using Molecular Technologies: A Comparative Analysis of Platforms, AI Integration, and Point-of-Care Deployment.. International journal of molecular sciences. 2026

items.title: A nested PCR-based point-of-care multiplex test for detection of bacterial pathogens in cerebrospinal fluids.
items.summary: This study developed and evaluated a multiplex one-tube nested real-time PCR assay integrated with a point-of-care testing instrument for detecting three bacterial pathogens responsible for cerebrospinal fluid infections. The researchers assessed the system's sensitivity using recombinant plasmids and its specificity against common CSF-associated pathogens, then validated performance using both simulated and clinical CSF samples in comparison with conventional real-time PCR following manual nucleic acid extraction. The integrated point-of-care approach achieved very low detection limits for all three target pathogens while reducing total testing time to approximately two hours and enabling a fully automated sample-to-result workflow without the need for culture. Testing on clinical samples demonstrated that the new method identified additional positive cases that conventional real-time PCR had missed, indicating superior sensitivity. The authors conclude that this platform offers a rapid, highly sensitive, and operationally streamlined approach for diagnosing bacterial meningitis-associated pathogens directly from cerebrospinal fluid.
items.venue: Frontiers in cellular and infection microbiology
items.citation: Wu J, Zhao Z, Pang P et al.. A nested PCR-based point-of-care multiplex test for detection of bacterial pathogens in cerebrospinal fluids.. Frontiers in cellular and infection microbiology. 2026

items.title: Isothermal nucleic acid amplification techniques: A comprehensive overview on their principle & applications as alternative to PCR.
items.summary: This review paper examined isothermal nucleic acid amplification methods as alternatives to conventional PCR-based diagnostics. The authors surveyed the principles, detection strategies, and applications of the most widely used techniques, including loop-mediated isothermal amplification, helicase-dependent amplification, rolling circle amplification, strand displacement amplification, nucleic acid sequence-based amplification, and recombinase polymerase amplification. Because these methods operate at a constant temperature and do not require thermal cycling equipment, they are particularly suited to resource-limited settings where rapid, affordable, and accessible molecular testing is needed. The review addresses a gap in the existing literature by consolidating information on reaction mechanisms, product detection approaches, and clinical diagnostic applications into a single comprehensive resource. The authors conclude that isothermal amplification methods hold strong potential for expanding genomic testing access across research and clinical contexts, and they outline future perspectives for the field.
items.citation: Amare Amdiyee A, Sisay Tessema T. Isothermal nucleic acid amplification techniques: A comprehensive overview on their principle & applications as alternative to PCR.. Practical laboratory medicine. 2026

items.title: A Multiplex One-Tube Nested Real-Time PCR Assay for the Point-of-Care Testing of Infectious Meningitis.
items.summary: This paper describes the development and evaluation of a multiplex one-tube nested real-time PCR assay designed for rapid point-of-care detection of three key meningitis pathogens in cerebrospinal fluid. The assay was integrated with a portable, fully automated nucleic acid testing platform and used a closed-system nested amplification approach to reduce contamination risk and operational complexity. Analytical testing demonstrated very low detection limits, no cross-reactivity against a broad panel of non-target microorganisms, and maintained sensitivity in simulated clinical samples. When evaluated against clinical cerebrospinal fluid samples, the assay showed near-perfect agreement with conventional quantitative PCR while also detecting additional cases of one fungal pathogen, suggesting superior sensitivity in some instances. The complete workflow produced results in under two hours, indicating the approach offers a practical, rapid, and sensitive alternative for diagnosing infectious meningitis in clinical settings, though the authors note that validation in larger patient cohorts remains necessary.
items.citation: Zhang D, Wang J, Zhao Z et al.. A Multiplex One-Tube Nested Real-Time PCR Assay for the Point-of-Care Testing of Infectious Meningitis.. Pathogens (Basel, Switzerland). 2026

items.title: Data-Centric Foundation Models in Computational Healthcare: A Survey
items.summary: This survey examines the intersection of foundation models and computational healthcare through a data-centric lens, focusing on how data quality, scale, and characterization shape AI performance in clinical settings. The authors investigate a broad range of data-centric approaches spanning the full pipeline from model pre-training through inference, addressing longstanding healthcare challenges such as data scarcity, annotation burden, patient privacy, and ethical constraints. The survey covers key considerations in AI security, evaluation, and alignment with human values as they apply to healthcare foundation models. The authors conclude with an optimistic outlook on how foundation model-based analytics can improve patient outcomes and streamline clinical workflows, and they maintain a living repository of relevant models and datasets to support the research community.
items.citation: Yunkun Zhang, Jin Gao, Zheling Tan et al.. Data-Centric Foundation Models in Computational Healthcare: A Survey. ACM Computing Surveys. 2026

items.title: Biosensing technologies for foodborne pathogen detection and healthcare: principles, emerging materials, and intelligent platforms
items.summary: This review examines the current landscape of biosensing technologies designed to detect priority foodborne pathogens, with a focus on electrochemical, optical, and microfluidic detection modalities applicable to both food safety and clinical settings. The authors survey how advances in functional nanomaterials, aptamers, nanozymes, and surface engineering strategies have enhanced sensor sensitivity and specificity in complex food matrices. A central theme is the integration of artificial intelligence and machine learning into these platforms, enabling real-time analytics, intelligent pattern recognition, and high-throughput multiplexed detection that transforms conventional sensors into smart diagnostic devices. The review critically assesses persistent challenges including signal interference from food matrices, instability of biorecognition elements, manufacturing scalability, and the absence of regulatory standardization, while proposing mitigation approaches such as ratiometric sensing, microfluidic pre-treatment, and explainable AI frameworks. Looking ahead, the authors advocate for self-powered biosensors, modular pathogen panel hubs, blockchain-integrated data management, and standardized validation pipelines as pathways toward deployable, regulation-ready platforms for next-generation food safety monitoring.
items.citation: Purshottam J. Assudani, Balakrishnan . P., Anny Leema A et al.. Biosensing technologies for foodborne pathogen detection and healthcare: principles, emerging materials, and intelligent platforms. Microchimica Acta. 2026

items.title: Dairy cows infected with influenza A(H5N1) reveals low infectious dose and transmission barriers
items.citation: Carolyn M. Lee, Natalie N. Tarbuck, Hannah J. Cochran et al.. Dairy cows infected with influenza A(H5N1) reveals low infectious dose and transmission barriers. Nature Communications. 2026

items.title: Comprehensive Review on Candidemia: Epidemiology, Diagnosis, Treatment, and Future Directions
items.summary: This review examines candidemia, a major cause of hospital-acquired bloodstream infections that carries high mortality and significant healthcare costs. The authors synthesize current knowledge on the epidemiology, species distribution, and origins of the condition, while evaluating the limitations of conventional diagnostic methods and the emerging technologies that may improve detection. The review also covers therapeutic strategies in depth, including empiric and targeted antifungal regimens, management of mixed infections, host-drug interactions, and the growing challenge of antifungal resistance. Novel agents in clinical development and compounds targeting new fungal pathways are highlighted as promising avenues for expanding the treatment arsenal. The authors conclude by pointing toward future directions including artificial intelligence-assisted diagnostics, vaccine-based prophylaxis, and combination therapy strategies as potential tools for improving patient outcomes in candidemia management.
items.citation: Haojie Dong, Minghui Song, Jingfang Sun et al.. Comprehensive Review on Candidemia: Epidemiology, Diagnosis, Treatment, and Future Directions. iFungi. 2026

items.title: Spectral Fingerprinting of Engineered Nanomaterials for Precision Biosensing
items.summary: Biological systems contain thousands of molecular species whose identities and concentrations shift with disease states, yet conventional bioanalytical methods for detecting these changes tend to be costly and low-throughput. Spectral fingerprinting of engineered nanomaterials is an emerging approach that uses optical readouts—such as near-infrared fluorescence or surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy—from one or more related nanomaterials to detect subtle biological differences without requiring highly targeted assays. The technique can be applied across scales ranging from single or multiplexed biomarker detection to whole-cell and organism-level biological characterization. Integration with machine learning and feature extraction methods has substantially broadened its capabilities, enabling applications such as live-cell phenotyping, serum-based cancer detection, and pathogen identification with high accuracy. The authors propose that this technology will continue to advance through synergy with next-generation nano-omics and generative artificial intelligence approaches.
items.citation: Aceer Nadeem, Maryam Rahmani, Yibo Wang et al.. Spectral Fingerprinting of Engineered Nanomaterials for Precision Biosensing. ACS Nano. 2026

items.title: Behaviour-Conditioned Neural Processes for Adaptive Residential Short-Term Load Forecasting
items.summary: This paper investigates residential short-term load forecasting, which is difficult because household electricity consumption varies widely across users and over time due to differing behavioural routines. The authors propose a behaviour-conditioned Attentive Neural Process framework that embeds inferred behavioural structure directly into the forecasting mechanism, representing it through a discrete latent variable derived from clustering-based weak supervision during training, while a continuous latent variable captures broader functional uncertainty across diverse load profiles. At test time, the model infers behavioural class distributions purely from available context, requiring no ground-truth behavioural labels. Evaluated on a smart grid dataset with user-disjoint splits and varying context lengths and forecast horizons, the proposed approach outperforms a label-agnostic neural process baseline on both point and probabilistic accuracy metrics, with the greatest gains occurring when context data are limited. The results demonstrate that embedding behavioural structure within a single probabilistic model supports uncertainty-aware forecasting across heterogeneous households without sacrificing generality.
items.citation: Ramin Soleimani, Andrea Visentin, Dirk Pesch. Behaviour-Conditioned Neural Processes for Adaptive Residential Short-Term Load Forecasting. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Euclid: Calibrating distances from surface brightness fluctuations with Early Release Observations of the Fornax cluster
items.summary: This paper investigates the use of surface brightness fluctuations (SBF) — pixel-to-pixel flux variations in galaxy images caused by the statistical distribution of stars — as a redshift-independent distance measurement tool applied to data from the Euclid space mission. The authors applied the FAST-SBF code to Euclid Early Release Observations of the Fornax galaxy cluster, measuring stellar fluctuation amplitudes in the visible imaging band despite the data reduction pipeline not being optimized for such measurements. They calibrated the absolute fluctuation magnitude as a function of galaxy color using a sample of Fornax galaxies, then validated this calibration on galaxies in two additional fields, the Perseus cluster and dwarf satellite candidates around a nearby spiral galaxy. The resulting distances showed reasonable agreement with literature values, with particularly good agreement where the pipeline flagged measurements as robust. The authors also compared their findings against simple stellar population models, highlighting the additional potential of Euclid SBF measurements for probing the underlying stellar populations of galaxies beyond distance estimation.
items.citation: R. Habas, M. Cantiello, G. Riccio et al.. Euclid: Calibrating distances from surface brightness fluctuations with Early Release Observations of the Fornax cluster. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Adaptive Fault Injection Planning for Multi-Layer Self-Healing AI Infrastructure
items.summary: This paper investigates how faults propagate across multiple layers in GPU-accelerator infrastructure used by hyperscale operators, focusing on the inadequacy of conventional fault-injection testing that treats each layer in isolation. The authors developed ADA-ST, an adaptive fault-injection methodology built around a weighted fault-propagation graph that models interactions across hardware, firmware, management software, and orchestration layers, using repair ticket data from a large production system as its empirical foundation. They found that existing static test campaigns cover only a small fraction of cross-layer fault-propagation pathways, leaving the vast majority of the attack surface untested, while their iterative approach achieved full edge coverage within roughly ten iterations across multiple platform generations. A knowledge-transfer mechanism called FLAM was shown to carry propagation knowledge across hardware generations with near-complete fidelity, enabling the methodology to scale to new platforms without rebuilding from scratch. Physical validation on the newest platform confirmed cross-layer vulnerabilities including telemetry blind spots, detection gaps, multi-signal correlation failures, and unverified trust propagation between layers.
items.citation: Saurabh Kulkarni, Yuxin Yang, Rohan Kulkarni et al.. Adaptive Fault Injection Planning for Multi-Layer Self-Healing AI Infrastructure. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Radiopurity material assays and radiation exposure projections for superconducting qubit measurements at SNOLAB
items.summary: This paper addresses the problem of ionizing radiation degrading the performance and coherence of superconducting qubit devices, which are typically operated at surface laboratories where cosmic ray flux is substantial. To establish a low-background environment for quantum hardware testing, the authors prepared for underground operation of superconducting qubits at the CUTE facility at SNOLAB, which is shielded by a thick rock overburden. An extensive radiopurity assay campaign was conducted on the materials comprising the quantum device assembly, and the measured radioactivity levels were fed into detailed Monte Carlo simulations using Geant4 to estimate the expected rates and spectra of energy deposits from radiogenic sources in that environment. The simulations also characterized the dominant particle interaction types contributing to the projected background, and the study was extended using the G4CMP crystal dynamics extension to model how high-energy particle impacts propagate through the solid-state substrate. The authors conclude that this work informs broader community efforts to develop mitigation strategies for radiation-induced errors in superconducting quantum circuits.
items.citation: Y. Ahmed, B. Binoy, R. Bunker et al.. Radiopurity material assays and radiation exposure projections for superconducting qubit measurements at SNOLAB. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Quantum Fisher information of magnetic quantum phase transition on Kondo lattice
items.summary: This paper investigates quantum phase transitions in a Kondo lattice model, which serves as a paradigm for strange metals and heavy fermion systems, using quantum information tools rather than conventional characterization methods. The authors apply quantum Fisher information to study multipartite entanglement across the magnetic quantum phase transition, focusing particularly on spin components transverse to the order parameter. They find that this quantity acts as a diagnostic for the destruction of heavy quasiparticles in the Kondo-destroyed magnetically ordered phase, with the underlying physics clarified through analysis of the antiferromagnetic Heisenberg model. The authors propose that their theoretical predictions can be tested experimentally via both unpolarized and polarized inelastic neutron scattering measurements in the ordered region of the heavy fermion phase diagram. Their results demonstrate that different operators can simultaneously witness multipartite entanglement in distinct sectors and illuminate the broader physics across different parts of the phase diagram.
items.citation: Yuan Fang, Lei Chen, Mounica Mahankali et al.. Quantum Fisher information of magnetic quantum phase transition on Kondo lattice. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Eccentricity as a Magnifying Glass: Precision Population Inference Enabled by Eccentric Neutron Star--Black Hole Mergers
items.summary: This paper investigates how orbital eccentricity in neutron star–black hole (NSBH) binary mergers can improve our ability to infer how these systems formed. Because eccentric mergers yield sharper measurements of intrinsic parameters such as component masses and spins compared to circular mergers, the authors explored the downstream benefits for population-level inference using gravitational-wave detectors. They found that the improved precision on the effective spin parameter enables confident identification of spin-orbit misaligned systems at a rate of roughly one per few eccentric detections during the upcoming observing run, and that enhanced mass measurements reveal finer structure in neutron star and black hole mass distributions. Additionally, they demonstrate that the eccentricity distribution predicted for dynamically formed systems can itself be tested by the end of the next observing run, providing a direct probe of formation channels that are alternatives to isolated binary evolution.
items.citation: Alberto Salvarese, Hsin-Yu Chen, Aaron Zimmerman et al.. Eccentricity as a Magnifying Glass: Precision Population Inference Enabled by Eccentric Neutron Star--Black Hole Mergers. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Evaluation of the QuickFinder COVID-19/Flu/RSV antigen test for rapid simultaneous detection of SARS-CoV-2, influenza A/B viruses, and respiratory syncytial virus.
items.summary: This study evaluated a multiplex lateral flow immunoassay antigen rapid diagnostic test, the QuickFinder COVID-19/Flu/RSV Antigen Test, designed to simultaneously detect SARS-CoV-2, influenza A and B viruses, and respiratory syncytial virus from nasopharyngeal swab specimens. Performance was assessed by comparing results from archived clinical specimens against real-time reverse transcription polymerase chain reaction as the reference standard, while cross-reactivity was examined using a panel of respiratory viral and bacterial strains. The test demonstrated high sensitivity for SARS-CoV-2 and influenza A, with somewhat lower sensitivity for influenza B and RSV, and achieved perfect specificity for all four targets with no cross-reactivity observed. Sensitivity was consistently high in specimens with greater viral loads but declined in low viral load samples. The authors conclude that the test is a reliable rapid screening and differentiation tool for high viral load specimens but should complement rather than replace molecular testing.
items.citation: Kim JA, Kang M, Yu HJ et al.. Evaluation of the QuickFinder COVID-19/Flu/RSV antigen test for rapid simultaneous detection of SARS-CoV-2, influenza A/B viruses, and respiratory syncytial virus.. Diagnostic microbiology and infectious disease. 2026

items.title: No detection of respiratory syncytial virus at one clinic in Japan with rapid immunochromatographic antigen tests for three viruses using the same nasopharyngeal swabs from adult patients
items.summary: This study investigated the burden and detectability of respiratory syncytial virus infection among adult patients presenting with acute respiratory symptoms at a clinic in Japan. Nasopharyngeal swab samples from adult patients were tested using rapid immunochromatographic antigen tests capable of simultaneously detecting SARS-CoV-2, influenza A and B viruses, and RSV. While a substantial proportion of patients tested positive for SARS-CoV-2 or influenza A, not a single case of RSV was identified. Comparing these findings against prior studies that used polymerase chain reaction testing revealed a statistically significant difference in RSV detection rates, with PCR-based approaches identifying a considerably higher proportion of positive cases. The authors conclude that rapid immunochromatographic antigen testing has limited diagnostic utility for RSV infection in Japanese adults compared with PCR-based methods.
items.citation: Kajiwara N, Tsunetoshi Y, Shiode S. No detection of respiratory syncytial virus at one clinic in Japan with rapid immunochromatographic antigen tests for three viruses using the same nasopharyngeal swabs from adult patients. 2026

items.title: Respiratory surveillance through point of care molecular testing and its impacts on nursing home RSV, influenza, and SARS-CoV-2 awareness and reporting behavior: a pilot study.
items.citation: Davidson H, Han L, McConeghy K et al.. Respiratory surveillance through point of care molecular testing and its impacts on nursing home RSV, influenza, and SARS-CoV-2 awareness and reporting behavior: a pilot study.. Antimicrobial stewardship & healthcare epidemiology : ASHE. 2026

items.title: Performance of an antigen test for detection of SARS-CoV-2, influenza, and respiratory syncytial virus-a prospective study.
items.summary: This prospective study evaluated the diagnostic performance of a multiplex rapid antigen test capable of simultaneously detecting SARS-CoV-2, influenza A, influenza B, and respiratory syncytial virus in patients presenting with acute respiratory infection symptoms. Over a thousand symptomatic patients, including children, were tested using nasal swabs with the rapid antigen test alongside nasopharyngeal swabs processed by PCR at a central laboratory, with antigen test interpretation performed by emergency department staff. The test demonstrated high sensitivity for all four pathogens when using a PCR cycle threshold cutoff intended to reflect infectious viral loads, along with perfect specificity across all agents and age groups. An additional finding was that nasal swabs appeared to absorb less viral RNA than nasopharyngeal swabs, a difference that reached statistical significance for influenza A among the broader patient population but was not observed in children. The authors conclude that the test's performance supports its use as a marker of infectiousness in acute clinical care settings.
items.venue: Clinical microbiology and infection : the official publication of the European Society of Clinical Microbiology and Infectious Diseases
items.citation: Berger K, Vang SH, Bekkeseth RHO et al.. Performance of an antigen test for detection of SARS-CoV-2, influenza, and respiratory syncytial virus-a prospective study.. Clinical microbiology and infection : the official publication of the European Society of Clinical Microbiology and Infectious Diseases. 2026

items.title: Analytical comparison of over-the-counter multiplex tests for influenza A, influenza B, and SARS-CoV-2.
items.summary: This study evaluated the analytical performance of over-the-counter lateral flow tests designed to detect influenza A, influenza B, and SARS-CoV-2 in a controlled, head-to-head comparison. Researchers prepared contrived specimen dilution panels from propagated viral stocks in clinical matrix and applied them to multiple commercially available tests according to manufacturer instructions, with results read by blinded assessors. All eight tests performed similarly, with detection limits falling within a fourfold dilution range across all four viral targets tested. A notable finding was that at low viral concentrations, many tests initially appeared negative at the start of the interpretation window but converted to a positive result by the end of that window. The authors therefore recommend that users allow the full manufacturer-specified incubation time to elapse before concluding that a test result is truly negative.
items.citation: Lohsen S, Hoy-Schulz Y, Taing A et al.. Analytical comparison of over-the-counter multiplex tests for influenza A, influenza B, and SARS-CoV-2.. Microbiology spectrum. 2026

items.title: No detection of respiratory syncytial virus at one clinic in Japan with rapid immunochromatographic antigen tests for three viruses using the same nasopharyngeal swabs from adult patients
items.summary: The study investigated the burden of respiratory syncytial virus infection among adult patients at a Japanese clinic by testing nasopharyngeal swabs collected from adults presenting with acute respiratory symptoms. Samples were analyzed using rapid immunochromatographic antigen tests capable of simultaneously detecting RSV, SARS-CoV-2, and influenza A and B viruses. While substantial numbers of patients tested positive for SARS-CoV-2 and influenza A, and one tested positive for influenza B, no RSV-positive cases were identified. Comparison with findings from previous studies employing PCR-based testing revealed a statistically significant deficit in RSV detection, suggesting the rapid antigen test substantially underperforms relative to molecular methods. The authors conclude that rapid immunochromatographic antigen testing is not a useful diagnostic approach for RSV infection in Japanese adults.
items.citation: Kajiwara N, Tsunetoshi Y, Shiode S. No detection of respiratory syncytial virus at one clinic in Japan with rapid immunochromatographic antigen tests for three viruses using the same nasopharyngeal swabs from adult patients. 2026

items.title: The International Guideline for the Definition, Classification, Diagnosis and Management of Urticaria
items.summary: This paper presents an updated international clinical guideline for urticaria, a mast cell-driven condition characterized by the rapid appearance of wheals, angioedema, or both. The guideline was developed using Cochrane methodology and the GRADE framework, with input from a large multinational consensus process involving delegates from numerous national and international societies across dozens of countries. The work covers the definition and classification of urticaria, distinguishing between acute and chronic forms, with chronic urticaria further categorized as either spontaneous or inducible. The guideline provides expert-guided and evidence-based recommendations for diagnosis and management across the different urticaria subtypes, acknowledging that chronic urticaria is disabling and impairs quality of life, while noting that novel therapeutic options are now available.
items.citation: T. Zuberbier, Z. Abdul Hameed Ansari, A. H. Abdul Latiff et al.. The International Guideline for the Definition, Classification, Diagnosis and Management of Urticaria. Allergy. 2026

items.title: Microbiota in cancer: current understandings and future perspectives
items.summary: This review examines the relationship between the microbiota and cancer, covering how microbial communities influence carcinogenesis, tumor progression, and patient prognosis. The authors trace the historical development of this research field and analyze the molecular mechanisms by which the microbiota modulates oncogenic pathways, immune responses, and tumor metabolism. The review addresses the dual nature of microbial communities in cancer, describing how they can both promote tumor growth and serve as targets for therapeutic intervention. Particular attention is given to how the microbiota influences responses to immunotherapy, chemotherapy, and radiotherapy, as well as emerging therapeutic strategies such as probiotics, antibiotics, and fecal microbiota transplantation. The authors conclude by calling for personalized, microbiota-targeted approaches in oncology and propose a comprehensive framework to guide future research and clinical application in this field.
items.citation: Yanxi Yao, Yifei Zhu, Keji Chen et al.. Microbiota in cancer: current understandings and future perspectives. Signal Transduction and Targeted Therapy. 2026

items.title: Tumour–brain crosstalk restrains cancer immunity via a sensory–sympathetic axis
items.summary: This paper investigated how tumours in peripheral organs communicate with the brain and how this communication affects anti-tumour immunity. Using genetically engineered mouse models combined with neural tracing, tissue imaging, and single-cell transcriptomics, the researchers studied lung adenocarcinoma and its interactions with the nervous system. They found that lung tumours activate vagal sensory neurons expressing a specific receptor, which relay signals to brainstem nuclei and in turn elevate sympathetic nervous system activity within the tumour microenvironment. This heightened sympathetic output suppresses anti-tumour immune responses through adrenergic signalling in alveolar macrophages, thereby promoting tumour growth. Disrupting this sensory-to-sympathetic circuit through genetic, pharmacological, or chemogenetic means significantly inhibited lung tumour growth by unleashing immune responses, suggesting that targeting this tumour–brain axis could offer new therapeutic strategies for visceral cancers.
items.citation: Haohan Karen Wei, Chuyue Yu, Bo Hu et al.. Tumour–brain crosstalk restrains cancer immunity via a sensory–sympathetic axis. Nature. 2026

items.title: Insulin Resistance and Inflammation
items.summary: This paper examines the bidirectional relationship between insulin resistance and inflammation, focusing on how each perpetuates the other to drive cardiometabolic and vascular disease. The authors conducted a conceptual narrative review, synthesizing evidence across multiple levels of biological organization — from insulin receptor signaling and intracellular stress pathways to tissue dysfunction and systemic inflammatory amplification — while explicitly distinguishing mechanistic and interventional findings from observational data. The review identifies oxidative, endoplasmic reticulum, inflammatory, and fibrotic stress modules as key intracellular mediators that link impaired insulin signaling to endothelial dysfunction and atherogenesis. The central argument is that these stress-response pathways become maladaptive under conditions of chronic nutrient excess and persistent inflammatory signaling, creating self-reinforcing feedback loops between insulin resistance and inflammation that accelerate vascular injury. The authors propose an integrative framework intended to highlight convergence points amenable to mechanistic prioritization and translational hypothesis testing.
items.citation: Evgenii Gusev, Alexey Sarapultsev, Yulia A. Zhuravleva. Insulin Resistance and Inflammation. International Journal of Molecular Sciences. 2026

items.title: CAR-engineered cell therapies: current understandings and future perspectives
items.summary: This review examines the current state and future directions of chimeric antigen receptor (CAR)-engineered cell therapies, tracing their evolution from oncology into a broad range of non-malignant conditions. The authors surveyed the literature through the present to synthesize advances across multiple CAR platforms, including CAR-T, CAR-NK, CAR-macrophage, and CAR-NKT cells, as well as next-generation design innovations such as logic-gated CARs and off-the-shelf allogeneic products. The review details the structural and signaling principles underlying these therapies, their application in hematologic and solid tumors, and the persistent challenges posed by the tumor microenvironment, manufacturing complexity, high costs, and antigen escape. Beyond oncology, the authors highlight promising expansions into autoimmune disorders, where CD19/BCMA-targeted CAR-T cells have achieved long-term remission in lupus and rheumatoid arthritis, as well as applications in infectious disease, transplant tolerance via CAR-Tregs, and tissue fibrosis reduction using senolytic CARs. Overall, the review concludes that CAR-engineered therapies offer precise, programmable immune modulation with a favorable safety profile in autoimmune contexts, and that ongoing clinical trials signal continued progress toward broader clinical adoption.
items.citation: Mobina Bayat, Javid Sadri Nahand. CAR-engineered cell therapies: current understandings and future perspectives. Molecular Biomedicine. 2026

items.title: Tackling cuproptosis: from metabolic rewiring to therapeutic exploitation in cancer
items.summary: This review examines cuproptosis, a recently characterized form of regulated cell death driven by copper overload and mitochondrial dysfunction, in the context of cancer biology and therapy. The authors synthesize current mechanistic understanding, focusing on how copper causes aggregation of lipoylated tricarboxylic acid cycle enzymes and destabilizes iron-sulfur cluster proteins, ultimately producing proteotoxic stress, while key signaling pathways and copper homeostasis govern this process. The review also addresses how copper shapes the tumor microenvironment and antitumor immunity, raising its relevance to cancer immunotherapy. Therapeutic approaches including copper ionophores and nanomedicine platforms are evaluated for their capacity to induce cuproptosis across cancer types, with preclinical evidence supporting particular promise in malignancies characterized by copper dysregulation or altered mitochondrial metabolism. The authors conclude that successful clinical translation will depend on biomarker-driven patient stratification and improved drug delivery systems, framing cuproptosis as a tractable target for precision oncology.
items.citation: Qian Hao, Yu Gan, Xiang Zhou. Tackling cuproptosis: from metabolic rewiring to therapeutic exploitation in cancer. Cellular and Molecular Immunology. 2026

items.title: Rapid structural evolution of neutron-rich silicon isotopes toward N = 28
items.summary: This paper investigates the structural evolution of neutron-rich silicon isotopes as they approach the N = 28 shell closure, a region known for the interplay between shell closures and collective nuclear deformation. The researchers performed lifetime measurements of excited states in one isotope and simultaneously conducted lifetime and heavy-ion inelastic-scattering measurements in a neighboring odd-mass isotope, the latter being a first of its kind for that nucleus. In the even isotope at N = 26, the extracted transition strengths reveal moderate quadrupole collectivity along with signatures of triaxial nuclear shape, while in the odd isotope, two nearly degenerate low-lying states show comparable electromagnetic transition strengths from scattering but are dominated by magnetic dipole decay based on the lifetime results. Together, these findings indicate a structural evolution toward an oblate nuclear shape as N = 28 is approached, in agreement with large-scale shell-model theoretical predictions.
items.citation: G. L. Zimba, H. Iwasaki, B. A. Brown et al.. Rapid structural evolution of neutron-rich silicon isotopes toward N = 28. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Behaviour-Conditioned Neural Processes for Adaptive Residential Short-Term Load Forecasting
items.summary: This paper investigates residential short-term load forecasting, a problem complicated by the heterogeneity of household energy consumption and the diversity of behavioural routines that shape demand patterns. The authors propose a behaviour-conditioned Attentive Neural Process framework that embeds inferred behavioural structure directly into the forecasting mechanism rather than treating it merely as an external grouping signal, using a discrete latent variable to capture behavioural patterns and a continuous latent variable to represent shared functional uncertainty. Because ground-truth behavioural labels are unavailable, the model is trained using weak supervision derived from clustering, while at test time it infers behaviour from context alone without any labels. Experiments on a smart meter dataset using user-disjoint splits and variable context lengths show that the proposed approach improves both point and probabilistic forecast accuracy over a label-agnostic baseline, with the largest gains occurring when contextual information is limited. The results suggest that embedding behavioural structure within a neural process framework enables a single model to provide uncertainty-aware forecasts across heterogeneous households and forecast horizons while reducing large prediction errors compared to deterministic baselines.
items.citation: Ramin Soleimani, Andrea Visentin, Dirk Pesch. Behaviour-Conditioned Neural Processes for Adaptive Residential Short-Term Load Forecasting. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Euclid: Calibrating distances from surface brightness fluctuations with Early Release Observations of the Fornax cluster
items.summary: This paper presents the first application of the surface brightness fluctuations (SBF) distance measurement technique to data from the Euclid space mission, using early release observations of the Fornax galaxy cluster. SBF exploits pixel-to-pixel variations in galaxy image flux caused by the statistical distribution of stars to derive redshift-independent distances from photometric data alone. The authors used the FAST-SBF code to measure stellar fluctuation amplitudes in a sample of Fornax cluster galaxies, calibrating the absolute fluctuation magnitude as a function of galaxy colour, despite the Euclid data reduction pipeline not being optimised for this type of analysis. The calibrated relation was then tested on galaxies in two additional early release observation fields — the Perseus cluster and dwarf satellite candidates around an external galaxy — yielding reasonable to good agreement with distances reported in the literature. The study also explored comparisons with simple stellar population models, highlighting the potential of Euclid SBF measurements to probe the stellar populations of galaxies in addition to measuring distances.
items.citation: R. Habas, M. Cantiello, G. Riccio et al.. Euclid: Calibrating distances from surface brightness fluctuations with Early Release Observations of the Fornax cluster. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Adaptive Fault Injection Planning for Multi-Layer Self-Healing AI Infrastructure
items.summary: This paper examines the challenge of testing self-healing pipelines in GPU-accelerator platforms, where faults can propagate across hardware, firmware, management software, and orchestration layers in ways that evade detection or trigger conflicting responses. The authors developed ADA-ST, an adaptive fault-injection methodology that constructs weighted fault-propagation graphs to guide cross-layer test scenario selection, moving beyond conventional approaches that test each layer in isolation. Applied to three successive hyperscale platforms, the work demonstrates that existing static test campaigns exercise only a small fraction of cross-layer fault-propagation paths, leaving the vast majority of potential failure modes untested. ADA-ST achieves full edge coverage through iterative, coverage-maximizing scenario selection within a small number of iterations per platform, and a knowledge-transfer abstraction called FLAM allows propagation insights to carry over across hardware generations with high fidelity. Physical validation on the newest platform confirmed tested propagation edges and revealed concrete cross-layer vulnerabilities including telemetry blind spots, detection gaps based on signal absence, multi-signal correlation failures, and unverified trust propagation between management layers.
items.citation: Saurabh Kulkarni, Yuxin Yang, Rohan Kulkarni et al.. Adaptive Fault Injection Planning for Multi-Layer Self-Healing AI Infrastructure. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Radiopurity material assays and radiation exposure projections for superconducting qubit measurements at SNOLAB
items.summary: This paper investigates the challenge that ionizing radiation poses to superconducting qubit operation and explores the use of a deep underground facility to reduce this background. The authors conducted an extensive material assay program to characterize the radioactivity levels of components intended for use in a superconducting qubit assembly at the CUTE facility at SNOLAB, which is shielded by substantial rock overburden to suppress cosmic ray flux. These radioactivity measurements were fed into detailed Monte Carlo simulations using Geant4 to estimate the rates and spectral composition of energy deposits from radiogenic sources in the underground environment, as well as to identify the dominant particle interaction types. The study also demonstrates how crystal dynamics simulations via the G4CMP solid-state physics extension can help understand the effects of high-energy particle impacts on quantum devices. Together, the findings provide a framework for assessing radiation-induced decoherence and informing mitigation strategies for superconducting qubit technologies operated underground.
items.citation: Y. Ahmed, B. Binoy, R. Bunker et al.. Radiopurity material assays and radiation exposure projections for superconducting qubit measurements at SNOLAB. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Quantum Fisher information of magnetic quantum phase transition on Kondo lattice
items.summary: This paper investigates quantum phase transitions in strongly correlated electron systems, specifically the Kondo lattice model, which serves as a paradigmatic framework for understanding strange metals and heavy fermion materials. The researchers applied quantum Fisher information as a diagnostic tool to characterize multipartite entanglement across the quantum phase transition, focusing on how entanglement structure changes between different phases. They found that the quantum Fisher information associated with spin components transverse to the magnetic order parameter serves as a signature for the destruction of heavy quasiparticles in the Kondo-destroyed magnetically ordered phase, with the underlying physics clarified through analysis of the antiferromagnetic Heisenberg model. The study proposes that these theoretical predictions can be experimentally tested using both unpolarized and polarized inelastic neutron scattering in the ordered region of the heavy fermion phase diagram. The work demonstrates that carefully chosen operators can simultaneously witness multipartite entanglement in distinct sectors and illuminate the broader physics across different regions of a complex quantum phase diagram.
items.citation: Yuan Fang, Lei Chen, Mounica Mahankali et al.. Quantum Fisher information of magnetic quantum phase transition on Kondo lattice. arXiv. 2026

items.title: AI-Enhanced Point-of-Care Diagnostics for Infectious Diseases in Resource-Limited Settings: A Scoping Review.
items.summary: This scoping review systematically mapped research on artificial intelligence-enhanced point-of-care and rapid diagnostic technologies for infectious diseases in resource-limited settings, examining gaps in disease coverage, geographic representation, and validation rigor. Following established scoping review methodology, the authors searched five major databases for studies published over approximately a decade, screening over a thousand records and ultimately including more than two hundred studies, from which data were extracted using a structured charting framework. The field was found to be growing exponentially, with nearly half of all publications appearing in the most recent period reviewed, though research was heavily concentrated on a narrow triad of diseases—COVID-19, malaria, and tuberculosis—while neglected tropical diseases remained markedly underrepresented. Convolutional neural networks were the predominant artificial intelligence approach, and microscopy and molecular diagnostic modalities were most commonly studied, but the vast majority of studies lacked prospective field validation, with only a small fraction demonstrating real-world testing in target settings. The authors conclude that the field faces critical deficiencies in validation rigor, disease equity, and geographic diversity, and call for future work to prioritize field validation, broaden disease focus beyond the dominant triad, and engage end-user communities from the earliest stages of research design.
items.venue: Tropical medicine & international health : TM & IH
items.citation: Farquhar H. AI-Enhanced Point-of-Care Diagnostics for Infectious Diseases in Resource-Limited Settings: A Scoping Review.. Tropical medicine & international health : TM & IH. 2026

items.title: Point-of-care testing (POCT) for Infectious diseases: technological innovation, effectiveness evaluation, and application strategies in resource-limited regions.
items.summary: This review paper examines point-of-care testing technologies for infectious diseases, focusing on their role in early screening, epidemic surveillance, and public health prevention. The authors systematically surveyed recent innovations across molecular diagnostics, microfluidic systems, biosensing technologies, and artificial intelligence as applied to rapid disease detection. The review also analyzed quality assessment frameworks encompassing sensitivity, specificity, detection limits, stability, and field applicability. Particular emphasis was placed on strategies for deploying these technologies in resource-limited settings, including optimizing technical suitability, resource allocation, and local support infrastructure. Based on identified challenges, the authors propose future development directions intended to guide further research and implementation of point-of-care diagnostics for infectious diseases.
items.citation: Zhang YX, Ru JF. Point-of-care testing (POCT) for Infectious diseases: technological innovation, effectiveness evaluation, and application strategies in resource-limited regions.. Diagnostic microbiology and infectious disease. 2026

items.title: Infectious disease diagnostic competencies of medical students as a fundamental basis for physicians' resilience in addressing future infectious disease challenges.
items.summary: This paper examines the role of infectious disease diagnostic competencies in medical education and their importance for preparing physicians to handle future infectious disease challenges. The authors argue that training in medical microbiology, virology, and hygiene—encompassing epidemiology, prevention, diagnostics, and therapy within a One-Health framework—is essential for building long-term professional resilience. They contend that core skills such as correct sample collection, appropriate test selection, and critical interpretation of microbiological and virological findings cannot be adequately acquired through self-directed learning alone, but require structured, hands-on practical training. The paper further emphasizes that proficiency in rapid and accurate infection diagnostics underpins rational antimicrobial therapy and is therefore critical to combating the development of multidrug-resistant pathogens and controlling outbreaks.
items.citation: Brandt C, Becker K, Ciesek S et al.. Infectious disease diagnostic competencies of medical students as a fundamental basis for physicians' resilience in addressing future infectious disease challenges.. GMS infectious diseases. 2026

items.title: From Control to Eradication: The Role of Point-of-Care Testing in Modernising Australia's Bovine Viral Diarrhoea (BVD) Disease Management.
items.summary: This review examines the current state of bovine viral diarrhoea virus management in Australian cattle industries and evaluates how diagnostic innovations could transform national disease control efforts. The authors analysed Australia's existing decentralised and voluntary biosecurity framework, identifying the reliance on individual producers as a key contributor to fragmented and inconsistent disease management. Because persistently infected animals serve as the primary transmission reservoir, the review argues that rapid identification and removal of these animals is essential to any effective eradication strategy. The paper proposes that field-deployable point-of-care diagnostic technologies, including lateral flow assays, nucleic acid amplification tests, and biosensors, could enable real-time crush-side testing and large-scale surveillance to address this gap. When combined with strengthened biosecurity protocols and optimised vaccination, the authors conclude that these diagnostic advances offer a scientifically credible and economically viable route toward coordinated national eradication of the disease.
items.citation: Ogada S, Naseem MN, Ranjbar S et al.. From Control to Eradication: The Role of Point-of-Care Testing in Modernising Australia's Bovine Viral Diarrhoea (BVD) Disease Management.. Viruses. 2026

items.title: Establishing an EU-compliant diagnostic facility for infectious diseases under war conditions in Poltava, Ukraine.
items.summary: This paper describes the establishment of advanced infectious disease diagnostic capabilities in a Ukrainian regional hospital during active wartime conditions. The authors implemented European Union-compliant serological, automated PCR, and bacterial identification systems with antibiotic susceptibility testing at a hospital in Poltava, a region under significant strain from internally displaced persons and pre-existing elevated infectious disease rates. The partnership between German and Ukrainian institutions involved participatory equipment selection, hands-on staff training conducted in Germany, jointly developed standard operating procedures, and adaptive reagent supply chains to address the logistical challenges of operating under war. Over the course of the project, the laboratories conducted tens of thousands of tests and identified previously unrecognized cases of HIV, Hepatitis B and C, Lyme disease, Toxoplasmosis, and antibiotic-resistant bacterial infections. The authors conclude that high-quality diagnostic infrastructure can be successfully built under wartime conditions when the partnership emphasizes practical solutions, shared decision-making, and contingency planning, and they propose the model as a template for replication elsewhere in Ukraine.
items.citation: Zelinskyy G, Koval T, Schrammel U et al.. Establishing an EU-compliant diagnostic facility for infectious diseases under war conditions in Poltava, Ukraine.. Frontiers in public health. 2026

items.title: Navigating the Future of Respiratory Infections: Key Insights From International Congress in Singapore, 17-20 September 2025.
items.summary: This paper summarizes the major scientific themes and discussions that emerged from an international conference focused on prevention and management of respiratory infections. The authors reviewed plenary sessions, symposia, and panel discussions, organizing findings across domains including therapeutics, host response, vaccination, surveillance, diagnostics, and research infrastructure. Key themes included the development of long-acting and broadly active antivirals, combination and early treatment strategies, and growing recognition that inflammatory host responses play a substantial role in disease severity. Advances in vaccines targeting conserved viral components, long-acting monoclonal antibodies, and adaptive platform trials were also highlighted, alongside integrated surveillance approaches using genomic sequencing and point-of-care diagnostics within a One Health framework. The authors conclude that clinical care, translational science, and public health preparedness are becoming increasingly interconnected, and that sustained investment across these domains is essential for translating scientific progress into routine care and pandemic readiness.
items.citation: Morvil N, Goh WGW, Zheng C et al.. Navigating the Future of Respiratory Infections: Key Insights From International Congress in Singapore, 17-20 September 2025.. Influenza and other respiratory viruses. 2026

items.title: Gut microbiome–driven colorectal cancer via immune, metabolic, neural, and endocrine axes reprogramming
items.summary: This review examines the role of the gut microbiome as a causal driver of colorectal cancer, synthesizing molecular, ecological, and translational evidence across immune, metabolic, neural, and endocrine axes. The authors characterize how dysbiosis marked by enrichment of pathobionts and depletion of protective short-chain-fatty-acid-producing commensals promotes carcinogenesis through mechanisms including genotoxin-induced DNA damage, epithelial barrier disruption, and chronic inflammation that sustains immune suppression. Specific bacterial metabolites such as colibactin, cytolethal distending toxin, and indolimines are linked to defined mutational signatures now detectable in human cancer genomes, directly connecting microbial exposures to oncogenesis. The review conceptualizes colorectal cancer as a biofilm-structured, polymicrobial ecosystem disease in which microbial consortia coordinate barrier breakdown, immune evasion, and metabolic cooperation within the tumor microenvironment. The authors conclude by highlighting emerging microbiota-targeted therapeutic strategies—including dietary modulation, bacteriophage therapy, engineered biotherapeutics, and fecal microbiota transplantation—and argue for repositioning the microbiome from a correlative feature to a tractable determinant of disease progression and treatment response.
items.citation: Jhommara Bautista, Mónica Lamas‐Maceiras, Camila Hidalgo-Tinoco et al.. Gut microbiome–driven colorectal cancer via immune, metabolic, neural, and endocrine axes reprogramming. npj Biofilms and Microbiomes. 2026

items.title: The International Guideline for the Definition, Classification, Diagnosis and Management of Urticaria
items.summary: This paper presents an updated international clinical guideline for urticaria, a mast cell-driven disease characterized by wheals, angioedema, or both. The guideline was developed using Cochrane methodology and the GRADE framework, with input from a large multinational consensus conference involving delegates from numerous national and international societies across dozens of countries. The document addresses the definition, classification, diagnosis, and management of urticaria, covering both acute and chronic forms, the latter of which is further categorized as chronic spontaneous or chronic inducible urticaria. The guideline highlights that chronic urticaria is disabling and significantly impairs quality of life, while also noting that novel therapeutic options are now available. The resulting recommendations are intended to provide expert-guided and evidence-based approaches to the full spectrum of urticaria subtypes.
items.citation: T. Zuberbier, Z. Abdul Hameed Ansari, A. H. Abdul Latiff et al.. The International Guideline for the Definition, Classification, Diagnosis and Management of Urticaria. Allergy. 2026

items.title: EULAR recommendations for the management of rheumatoid arthritis with synthetic and biologic disease-modifying antirheumatic drugs: 2025 update
items.summary: These updated recommendations address the management of rheumatoid arthritis using synthetic and biologic disease-modifying antirheumatic drugs, incorporating the most recent evidence since the prior iteration. An international task force conducted systematic literature reviews on the safety and efficacy of relevant drug classes, then used a voting process to reach consensus on overarching principles and specific recommendations. The resulting guidance consolidates treatment into fewer recommendations than before, advising that methotrexate combined with short-term glucocorticoids remains the preferred initial strategy, with addition of a biologic agent recommended after insufficient response within three to six months. Janus kinase inhibitors remain an option at that stage but require careful individual risk assessment, particularly regarding major cardiovascular events, malignancies, and thromboembolic events. The recommendations also address sequencing after treatment failure, support a treat-to-target strategy, and advise caution with tapering in sustained remission, as discontinuation frequently leads to disease flare.
items.citation: Josef S. Smolen, Christopher J. Edwards, Victoria Konzett et al.. EULAR recommendations for the management of rheumatoid arthritis with synthetic and biologic disease-modifying antirheumatic drugs: 2025 update. Annals of the Rheumatic Diseases. 2026

items.title: Lipid metabolism in homeostasis and disease
items.summary: This review examines the role of lipid metabolism in maintaining cellular homeostasis and how its dysregulation contributes to a broad range of diseases. The authors synthesize the research history of lipid metabolism and focus particularly on how lipid functions, transport, and signaling pathways regulate immune cell behavior, including the proliferation, differentiation, and activation of macrophages, T cells, B cells, and neutrophils across both innate and adaptive immunity. The review explores multilevel regulatory mechanisms—genetic, epigenetic, posttranscriptional, and posttranslational—that govern lipid metabolic processes in immune contexts. The authors further discuss how lipid metabolism is implicated in autoimmune diseases, cancer, neurodegenerative conditions, cardiovascular diseases, aging, and metabolic disorders. Finally, the review surveys therapeutic strategies targeting lipid metabolism, summarizes ongoing clinical efforts, and identifies lipid-derived biomarkers and innovative treatment approaches as priorities for future research.
items.citation: Zhili Li, Wende Deng, Lanxuan Yang et al.. Lipid metabolism in homeostasis and disease. Signal Transduction and Targeted Therapy. 2026

items.title: Digital twins in oncology: From predictive modelling to personalised treatment strategies
items.summary: This narrative review examines the application of digital twin technology in oncology, a concept adapted from engineering that creates dynamic virtual replicas of a patient's physiological and pathological state to support personalised cancer care. The authors conducted a comprehensive literature search across multiple major databases, including grey literature and conference proceedings, to synthesise evidence on how digital twins are being developed, validated, and applied across different cancer types. The review finds that digital twins show promise across the full cancer care continuum, encompassing precision treatment selection, radiotherapy optimisation, drug development, immuno-oncology modelling, surgical planning, and survivorship care, with artificial intelligence and multi-omics data integration enabling simulation of tumour behaviour and treatment response. Despite this broad potential, significant barriers to clinical translation remain, including challenges in data integration, model validation, computational scalability, and ethical governance. The authors conclude that interdisciplinary collaboration and the development of appropriate regulatory frameworks are essential prerequisites for realising the clinical utility of digital twin technology in precision oncology.
items.citation: David B. Olawade, Emmanuel O. Oisakede, Oluwakemi Jumoke Bello et al.. Digital twins in oncology: From predictive modelling to personalised treatment strategies. Critical Reviews in Oncology/Hematology. 2026

items.title: Food and Medicine Homology Focus in 2026
items.summary: This perspective paper examines the anticipated direction of the food and medicine homology field as it transitions from traditional empirical knowledge toward modern scientific frameworks. The authors outline a vision centered on scientific rigor, mechanistic understanding through systematic analysis, and precision medicine approaches applied to substances that serve both nutritional and therapeutic roles. Research priorities identified include active component identification, gut microbiota interactions, phase separation, and immunometabolic reprogramming, alongside the establishment of evidence-based regulatory standards. Applications are expected to shift toward dynamic, personalized health management for chronic diseases and aging populations, supported by artificial intelligence and multidimensional personal data. The industrial dimension of the field is anticipated to evolve through synthetic biology, intelligent delivery systems, and sustainable manufacturing practices.
items.citation: Mengyao Li, Gui-Fen Wu, Feng Lu et al.. Food and Medicine Homology Focus in 2026. Food & medicine homology.. 2026

items.title: A Methodology for Auditable Trustworthiness Levels in AI Lifecycle Governance
items.summary: This paper addresses a gap in AI governance between high-level trustworthiness principles and the practical need to monitor and reassess AI systems throughout their operational lifecycle. The authors propose a two-part methodology combining a formal framework for representing trustworthiness levels and a lightweight governance procedure for documenting and tracking those levels over time. The formal framework models trustworthiness through context-sensitive, measurable dimensions and learns interpretable rules—using decision trees as a proof-of-concept—that yield explicit trustworthiness plateaus, readable level transitions, and two lifecycle diagnostics called boundary margins and profile drift. The governance procedure embeds these formal outputs into a conformity-oriented workflow covering design-time labeling, post-deployment monitoring, reassessment, and reporting, with defined human responsibilities and control gates at each stage. The methodology is illustrated on synthetic lifecycle traces involving system degradation, shocks, updates, and heterogeneous monitoring conditions, and is framed as an evidential support tool for governance documentation rather than a replacement for legal or expert judgment.
items.citation: Andrea Ferrario. A Methodology for Auditable Trustworthiness Levels in AI Lifecycle Governance. arXiv. 2026

items.title: CRAFT: Clustering Rubrics to Diagnose Weak LLM Capabilities and Generate Targeted Fine-Tuning Data
items.summary: This paper investigates whether evaluations of large language models can go beyond measuring performance to actively diagnosing the underlying capability failures that cause poor performance. The authors introduce CRAFT, a method that processes rubric-based evaluation datasets by extracting capability descriptions from prompt-rubric pairs, clustering them into a hierarchical capability tree, and identifying the specific nodes where a target model performs poorly. These diagnosed weak capabilities then guide the generation of targeted supervised fine-tuning data, creating a pipeline from evaluation to model improvement. Comparing CRAFT against prompt-level clustering and untargeted random data generation across four open-source models and two professional domains with held-out benchmarks, the method achieves the strongest performance improvements in the finance domain for all models tested and in the legal domain for most models. The findings suggest that diagnosing model weaknesses at the granularity of rubric criteria, rather than at the level of prompts or broad categories, yields both clearer diagnostic insight and measurably better models after fine-tuning.
items.citation: Vipul Gupta, Zihao Wang, Razvan-Gabriel Dumitru et al.. CRAFT: Clustering Rubrics to Diagnose Weak LLM Capabilities and Generate Targeted Fine-Tuning Data. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Attention-Guided Saliency Maps for Interpreting Visualization Literacy in VLMs
items.summary: This paper investigates how vision-language models allocate visual attention when interpreting data visualizations, a question relevant to ensuring reliable analytical reasoning. The authors developed a lightweight, gradient-free saliency map method that aggregates attention weights across all heads and layers of the language model component, mapping these back onto the vision encoder's patch grid to link each generated answer token to specific image regions. The approach produces direct correspondences between model outputs and the visual elements attended to during answer generation, enabling inspection of whether model focus aligns with semantically meaningful parts of a visualization. The method's causal faithfulness to actual model behavior was validated using a deletion metric, which tests whether removing attended regions degrades model outputs as expected.
items.citation: Maeve Hutchinson, Abderrahmane Wassim Mehdaoui, Pranava Madhyastha. Attention-Guided Saliency Maps for Interpreting Visualization Literacy in VLMs. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Scaling Hawkes Processes
items.summary: This paper examines the application of Hawkes processes, a class of stochastic point process models used to study contagion phenomena, to the analysis of gun violence in the United States. The authors review general strategies for fitting Hawkes processes to data and discuss how model structure affects computational scalability. They then apply a high-performance computing powered Bayesian inference approach to conduct a spatiotemporal Hawkes process analysis of a large dataset of gun violence incidents spanning a decade. The paper presents findings on model fit and outlines directions for future research in this domain.
items.citation: Seyoon Ko, Jasen Zhang, Andrew J. Holbrook. Scaling Hawkes Processes. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Non-invasive monitoring of process-relevant plasma parameters by Fiber PROES in capacitively coupled Ar/CF$_4$ plasmas
items.summary: This paper investigated whether fiber-based phase-resolved optical emission spectroscopy (Fiber PROES) could serve as a non-invasive method for monitoring key plasma parameters in industrial plasma processing reactors. The researchers operated a low-pressure capacitive radio-frequency discharge in various mixtures of argon and carbon tetrafluoride, using optical fiber ports typically available on industrial sources to track the spatio-temporally resolved dynamics of energetic electrons through a selected emission line. Invasive diagnostics, including probe measurements and a retarding field energy analyzer, were used as reference measurements to directly characterize electron density and ion flux as functions of driving voltage and pressure. The study found that changes in these process-relevant parameters, along with hysteresis effects, correlate with transitions in the electron power absorption mode detected by Fiber PROES, which alter the electron energy distribution function and thereby influence plasma behavior. The authors conclude that Fiber PROES provides a viable non-invasive diagnostic approach for monitoring and knowledge-based development of plasma processes without requiring intrusive instrumentation.
items.citation: Florian Beckfeld, Maximilian Ryppa, Constantin Neuroth et al.. Non-invasive monitoring of process-relevant plasma parameters by Fiber PROES in capacitively coupled Ar/CF$_4$ plasmas. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Spatial Normalization for Cross-Domain Retinal Layer Segmentation in Optical Coherence Tomography
items.summary: This paper investigated whether spatial normalization as a preprocessing step can improve the generalizability and robustness of deep learning models for retinal layer segmentation in optical coherence tomography images. The authors introduced a fovea-centered normalization framework, inspired by standard neuroimaging practices, that aligns OCT volumes into a common anatomical reference space to reduce geometric domain shifts across different scanners, acquisition protocols, and clinical populations. They evaluated multiple state-of-the-art deep learning architectures using a comprehensive combination of overlap-based metrics, topology-aware metrics, and thickness-based measures, and additionally proposed ground-truth-free metrics for settings where annotations are unavailable. The results demonstrated that spatial normalization meaningfully improves segmentation consistency and cross-domain performance, supporting its inclusion in OCT analysis pipelines. The authors argue this approach advances reliable extraction of retinal biomarkers for computational studies of neurodegenerative diseases.
items.citation: Iker Moran-Cavero, Monica Hernandez, Elvira Mayordomo et al.. Spatial Normalization for Cross-Domain Retinal Layer Segmentation in Optical Coherence Tomography. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Perovskite–organic tandem solar cells with a photo-transformable stabilizer
items.why_notable: This work is attracting attention because it introduces a photo-transformable stabilizer to address durability challenges in perovskite–organic tandem solar cells, a configuration that combines two promising photovoltaic technologies.
items.citation: Ruihan Wu, Shucheng Qin, Tianwei Zou et al.. Perovskite–organic tandem solar cells with a photo-transformable stabilizer. Nature. 2026

items.title: Author Correction: OR7A10 GPCR engineering boosts CAR-NK therapy against solid tumours
items.why_notable: This work is attracting attention because it describes engineering a GPCR to enhance CAR-NK cell therapy against solid tumours, representing a potentially significant advance in cellular immunotherapy for difficult-to-treat cancers.
items.citation: Luojia Yang, Paul A. Renauer, Kaiyuan Tang et al.. Author Correction: OR7A10 GPCR engineering boosts CAR-NK therapy against solid tumours. Nature. 2026

items.title: Tall and small trees are equally vulnerable to drought
items.why_notable: This work draws attention because it challenges the assumption that taller trees face greater drought vulnerability, revealing instead that the water-transport systems of towering tropical trees have evolved to counteract gravitational demands and maintain drought resistance comparable to smaller trees.
items.citation: Holly Smith. Tall and small trees are equally vulnerable to drought. Nature. 2026

items.title: Rights for rivers and ice cream for all: top reads for the summer holidays
items.why_notable: This piece is drawing attention because it offers readers a curated, personal collection of summer reading recommendations from trusted science contributors, blending intellectual curiosity with leisure appeal.
items.citation: Moriba Jah, Seema Mundoli, Blaise Agüera y Arcas et al.. Rights for rivers and ice cream for all: top reads for the summer holidays. Nature. 2026

items.title: How voluntary work can boost your career in science
items.why_notable: This work is drawing attention because it highlights how voluntary work offers scientists not just altruistic rewards but tangible professional advantages, including career advancement and the development of valuable social networks.
items.citation: Jacqui Thornton. How voluntary work can boost your career in science. Nature. 2026

items.title: The rise of evidence-based medicine and the ‘mavericks’ who championed it
items.why_notable: This work is drawing attention because it chronicles the history of how clinical trials came to reshape both health and social policy, spotlighting the unconventional figures who drove that transformation. The framing highlights the book's thorough research as it traces evidence-based medicine's rise to prominence.
items.citation: Peter John. The rise of evidence-based medicine and the ‘mavericks’ who championed it. Nature. 2026

items.title: Epigenetic editing makes its mark
items.why_notable: Researchers have developed techniques to precisely alter the chemical modifications on DNA and chromatin, offering a way to control gene activity without changing the underlying genetic sequence. This approach is drawing attention for its potential to tune gene expression in a targeted and potentially reversible manner.
items.citation: Amber Dance. Epigenetic editing makes its mark. Nature. 2026

items.title: This microbiologist endured a four-year court battle over COVID-19 tests
items.why_notable: This story is drawing attention because it highlights the personal and legal ordeal of a microbiologist who, while working to provide COVID-19 testing, became entangled in political controversy and faced criminal prosecution. Her case raises concerns about the risks scientists can face when their public health work intersects with political conflict.
items.citation: Anne Gulland. This microbiologist endured a four-year court battle over COVID-19 tests. Nature. 2026

items.title: Author Correction: Authigenic mineral phases as a driver of the upper-ocean iron cycle
items.why_notable: This is a correction notice for a previously published study on how authigenic mineral phases influence the cycling of iron in the upper ocean, indicating that an error in the original work required formal amendment.
items.citation: Alessandro Tagliabue, Kristen N. Buck, Laura E. Sofen et al.. Author Correction: Authigenic mineral phases as a driver of the upper-ocean iron cycle. Nature. 2026

items.title: Alpine crossing took a heavy toll on Hannibal’s elephants and troops
items.why_notable: This work is attracting attention because it provides quantitative insight into the immense physical cost that Hannibal's famous Alpine crossing inflicted on his army, revealing that elephants, horses, and soldiers all would have suffered substantial weight loss during the grueling mountain traverse.
items.citation: Alpine crossing took a heavy toll on Hannibal’s elephants and troops. Nature. 2026
