items.title: Briefing Chat: The 30 year-legacy of a science icon — Dolly the sheep
items.why_notable: The work is drawing attention because it marks a major anniversary of one of the most consequential experiments in biology — the first cloning of a mammal from an adult cell — prompting reflection on its lasting scientific legacy. The discussion also highlights a separate landmark achievement, the first direct observation of new ocean-floor crust forming.
items.citation: Benjamin Thompson, Flora Graham. Briefing Chat: The 30 year-legacy of a science icon — Dolly the sheep. Nature. 2026

items.title: Which ‘AI scientist’ suits your lab? A guide for the perplexed
items.why_notable: The proliferation of general-purpose AI tools marketed specifically for scientific research has created a challenging landscape for researchers trying to choose the right platform. This guide is attracting attention by helping scientists navigate which "AI scientist" tool best fits their particular lab's needs.
items.citation: Ewen Callaway. Which ‘AI scientist’ suits your lab? A guide for the perplexed. Nature. 2026

items.title: Think preprints are unreliable? Analysis of 70,000 studies might change your mind
items.why_notable: This work is drawing attention because it challenges the common assumption that preprints are less trustworthy than peer-reviewed publications, finding that the core conclusions of biomedical preprints overwhelmingly hold up through the formal publication process.
items.citation: Mohana Basu. Think preprints are unreliable? Analysis of 70,000 studies might change your mind. Nature. 2026

items.title: Daily briefing: Preprints are chock-full of authors’ private info
items.why_notable: The story is drawing attention because preprints on a major server have been found to inadvertently contain sensitive personal data—such as passwords and private notes—embedded within them, raising concerns about researchers' digital privacy. The briefing also highlights other eye-catching findings, including unusually low-density planets and a guide to choosing AI research tools.
items.citation: Flora Graham. Daily briefing: Preprints are chock-full of authors’ private info. Nature. 2026

items.title: Lab-grown sperm: scientists inch closer to fertility breakthrough
items.why_notable: This work is drawing attention because researchers have managed to generate immature human sperm from stem cells, using a mouse kidney as a nurturing environment — a step toward potentially treating certain forms of infertility that were previously considered untreatable.
items.citation: Heidi Ledford. Lab-grown sperm: scientists inch closer to fertility breakthrough. Nature. 2026

items.title: NSF plans cuts to core science programmes to fund White House initiative
items.why_notable: The story is drawing attention because the funding agency is reportedly planning to redirect money away from established core science programmes toward a White House initiative, while simultaneously trying to recover already-distributed research funds at a time when its budget is under strain and a backlog of unprocessed grant applications has accumulated.
items.citation: Dan Garisto. NSF plans cuts to core science programmes to fund White House initiative. Nature. 2026

items.title: Pair of ‘super-puff’ planets are lighter than candy floss
items.why_notable: The discovery is drawing attention because two gas giants orbiting the same star have been found to be among the least dense planets ever recorded, making them exceptionally rare and extreme examples of so-called "super-puff" worlds.
items.citation: Pair of ‘super-puff’ planets are lighter than candy floss. Nature. 2026

items.title: Graduating without a thesis: meet the people getting ‘practical’ PhDs in China
items.why_notable: This work is attracting attention because China is pioneering an alternative doctoral pathway that allows candidates to graduate by producing practical products rather than traditional written theses, marking a significant shift in how advanced degrees can be earned.
items.citation: Xiaoying You. Graduating without a thesis: meet the people getting ‘practical’ PhDs in China. Nature. 2026

items.title: Daily briefing: Mutation lets octopuses make proteins with precision
items.why_notable: This work is attracting attention because it reveals that a mutation in octopuses' protein-synthesis machinery paradoxically increases its accuracy, suggesting a surprising evolutionary trade-off. The finding offers a novel window into how errors in biological processes can be harnessed for greater precision.
items.citation: Jacob Smith. Daily briefing: Mutation lets octopuses make proteins with precision. Nature. 2026

items.title: Whoops! Most arXiv papers contain information never meant to be shared
items.why_notable: This work is drawing attention because it reveals a widespread and largely hidden privacy problem: sensitive personal data—including passwords, location information, and private communications—has been inadvertently embedded in the files of millions of preprints uploaded to a major public repository. The finding suggests that researchers routinely share far more than they intend when submitting their work.
items.citation: David Brzostowicki. Whoops! Most arXiv papers contain information never meant to be shared. Nature. 2026

items.title: ‘This time, it’s the other way around’: how Indonesia is reclaiming the science of human history
items.why_notable: This work is attracting attention because Indonesia has undergone a major restructuring of its research ecosystem that is enabling the country to lead its own palaeontological investigations into human history, reversing a longstanding pattern in which such science was driven by outside researchers.
items.citation: Dyna Rochmyaningsih. ‘This time, it’s the other way around’: how Indonesia is reclaiming the science of human history. Nature. 2026

items.title: Nukes in space? Orbital detector could sniff out warheads
items.why_notable: This work is attracting attention because a compact, shoebox-sized satellite detector could potentially identify hidden nuclear warheads from orbit, raising significant implications for arms control and global security monitoring.
items.citation: Benjamin Thompson, Nick Petrić Howe. Nukes in space? Orbital detector could sniff out warheads. Nature. 2026

items.title: Observation of Floquet rotational super-radiance
items.why_notable: This work is attracting attention because it reports the first experimental observation of a Floquet regime of rotational super-radiance, achieved using a spatiotemporally modulated ring network of resonators, opening a new frontier at the intersection of driven periodic systems and collective radiation phenomena.
items.citation: Hadiseh Nasari, Hady Moussa, Yoshiaki Kasahara et al.. Observation of Floquet rotational super-radiance. Nature. 2026

items.title: In vivo feasibility study of humanoid robots in surgery
items.why_notable: This work is attracting attention because it demonstrates, through systematic evaluation, that humanoid robots are capable of performing laparoscopic surgical tasks via teleoperation—marking a notable step toward their potential clinical use. At the same time, it candidly identifies the technical hurdles that must still be overcome before such robots could be deployed in real surgical settings.
items.citation: Zekai Liang, Nikita Thareja, Peihan Zhang et al.. In vivo feasibility study of humanoid robots in surgery. Nature. 2026

items.title: Single-phase gradient-solvation-electrolyte-stabilized Li metal batteries
items.why_notable: This work is attracting attention because it demonstrates a way to engineer a single-phase electrolyte system that simultaneously delivers long cycle life, high energy density, and high capacity retention in lithium metal batteries—key performance metrics that have historically been difficult to achieve together. The approach of incorporating a targeted ligand anti-solvent into an anion-rich ether-based electrolyte represents a promising strategy for stabilizing the lithium metal anode.
items.citation: Wujie Yang, Jianfeng Cai, Aoyuan Chen et al.. Single-phase gradient-solvation-electrolyte-stabilized Li metal batteries. Nature. 2026

items.title: Non-genotoxic transplantation and in vivo selection through epitope editing
items.summary: This study addressed the challenge that toxic chemotherapy or radiation is currently required to prepare patients for haematopoietic stem cell transplantation and gene therapy, limiting who can safely receive these treatments. The researchers engineered specific amino acid changes in the extracellular domain of the KIT receptor on haematopoietic stem and progenitor cells using adenine base editing or prime editing, rendering the cells invisible to therapeutic anti-KIT monoclonal antibodies while preserving normal KIT function and expression. These epitope-edited cells were simultaneously modified to disrupt the BCL11A erythroid enhancer, promoting fetal haemoglobin expression as a treatment strategy for sickle cell disease and beta-thalassaemia. When anti-KIT antibodies were administered, they cleared unedited host stem cells from the bone marrow niche while sparing the engineered cells, driving progressive in vivo enrichment of the therapeutically modified population without clonal skewing. The approach demonstrates that epitope editing can replace genotoxic conditioning regimens, enabling durable engraftment, preserved clonal diversity, and enhanced fetal haemoglobin induction through immune-based selection alone.
items.why_notable: This work is attracting attention because it offers a way to transplant and selectively expand gene-edited blood stem cells without the toxic chemotherapy or radiation typically required for conditioning. By modifying a surface protein on stem cells so they resist antibody-based depletion while therapeutically edited cells are enriched, the approach could make curative treatments for diseases like sickle cell disease and β-thalassemia safer and more broadly accessible.
items.citation: Gabriele Casirati, Andrea Cosentino, Marta Freschi et al.. Non-genotoxic transplantation and in vivo selection through epitope editing. Nature. 2026

items.title: Architecture of the 8 MDa Hdr–Vhu–Fwd super-assembly in class I methanogens
items.summary: This paper investigated the molecular architecture of the Hdr–Vhu–Fwd super-assembly, a key enzymatic complex central to the energy metabolism of class I methanogens, which are major biological producers of methane. Using cryogenic electron microscopy and cryo-electron tomography, the researchers determined the structure of this complex from *Methanococcus maripaludis* and confirmed it exists as an intact structure within living cells. The super-assembly forms an enormous complex of over two hundred polypeptide chains and hundreds of redox cofactors, arranged as two hexameric rings linked by a tetrameric core to create a continuous circular electron transport chain that couples hydrogen oxidation with carbon dioxide reduction through flavin-based electron bifurcation. The study also identified a modular variant in which a hydrogenase subunit is replaced by a formate dehydrogenase, suggesting the complex can flexibly incorporate different electron-input modules to support metabolic adaptation across varied environments. Taxonomic analysis further revealed that this architecture is unique to class I methanogens and distinct from the analogous complex in class II methanogens, pointing to lineage-specific evolutionary adaptation.
items.why_notable: This work is attracting attention because it resolves the architecture of an exceptionally large, lineage-specific protein super-assembly central to methane-producing microorganisms, revealing its modular organization. The structural detail offered explains how the complex can adapt to diverse anaerobic environments, with implications for understanding methanogenesis.
items.citation: S. Paul, Tomas C. Pascoa, Max A. Klamke et al.. Architecture of the 8 MDa Hdr–Vhu–Fwd super-assembly in class I methanogens. Nature. 2026

items.title: Reinforcement learning control of quantum error correction
items.summary: This paper investigated how to maintain quantum error correction performance in the face of continuously drifting environmental conditions, which ordinarily require halting computation entirely for recalibration. The researchers unified calibration and computation by repurposing the error-detection events generated during quantum error correction as a learning signal for a reinforcement learning agent, which continuously adjusts the processor's control parameters in real time without interrupting the computation. This framework was demonstrated experimentally on a superconducting processor running the surface code, where it substantially improved logical stability against injected drift and achieved record-low logical error rates for both the surface and colour codes. Numerical simulations further showed that the reinforcement learning agent's optimization speed does not grow with system size, suggesting the approach scales to processors with very large numbers of control parameters. The work establishes a paradigm in which a quantum computer autonomously learns from its own errors and self-calibrates continuously throughout operation.
items.why_notable: This work draws attention because it demonstrates that combining reinforcement learning with quantum error correction allows a quantum computer to continuously self-calibrate while computing, yielding record-low logical error rates and improved robustness against performance drift.
items.citation: Volodymyr Sivak, Alexis Morvan, Michael Broughton et al.. Reinforcement learning control of quantum error correction. Nature. 2026

items.title: Large language models can predict the results of social science experiments
items.why_notable: This work is drawing attention because it demonstrates that large language models can forecast social science experimental results with accuracy comparable to human expert forecasters, including for studies published after the models' training cutoff. However, the finding that these models systematically overestimate effect sizes points to an important limitation that tempers enthusiasm for their use as a substitute for human judgment.
items.citation: Ashwini Ashokkumar, Luke Hewitt, Isaias Ghezae et al.. Large language models can predict the results of social science experiments. Nature. 2026

items.title: Ancient feeding-related neuropeptides regulate alloparenting in ants
items.summary: This paper investigated the neural and molecular mechanisms underlying alloparental care and age-related division of labour in social insects, using the clonal raider ant as a model system. Through an unbiased pharmacological screen of neuropeptides, the researchers identified two ancient feeding-related neuropeptides, neuropeptide F and allatostatin A, as regulators of brood-care behaviour. Functional manipulations demonstrated that these two neuropeptides have opposing effects on brood care, with neuropeptide F promoting it and allatostatin A suppressing it, and that their brain levels shift naturally as workers age in a manner consistent with the known age-related transition from nursing to foraging. The study further showed that both neuropeptides remain responsive to nutritional state, which in turn influences brood-care behaviour, mirroring their ancestral role in feeding regulation found in solitary species. The authors conclude that evolution has co-opted molecular machinery originally involved in feeding to support cooperative brood care and the age-structured division of labour characteristic of insect societies.
items.why_notable: This work is drawing attention because it reveals that ancient feeding-related neuropeptides—not previously associated with social behavior—directly and oppositely control brood-care behavior in ants, while also connecting an individual's nutritional state to age-dependent shifts in how workers care for young.
items.citation: Alexander Paul, Tomas Kay, Ivan Lacroix et al.. Ancient feeding-related neuropeptides regulate alloparenting in ants. Nature. 2026

items.title: Anatomy of a seafloor spreading event captured by in situ seismogeodesy
items.summary: This study investigated the processes governing seafloor spreading at mid-ocean ridges, which have been poorly constrained on short timescales due to the difficulty of monitoring submarine plate boundaries. Researchers deployed a network of hydroacoustic, direct-path ranging, and bottom-pressure instruments across the Southeast Indian Ridge and a nearby transform fault, and the instruments captured a complete rifting event that began just two months after deployment. The event commenced with a rapidly migrating swarm of extensional earthquakes and involved several metres of subsidence and horizontal extension across the axial valley, interpreted as deflation of a sill-like magmatic reservoir feeding propagating dykes. These dykes drove a large submarine lava eruption over approximately two weeks, while simultaneously inducing both seismic and aseismic slip on bounding normal faults and triggering seismicity on adjacent transform faults. The findings suggest that magmatically driven aseismic slip may be the dominant mechanism by which mid-ocean ridge normal faults accumulate displacement, offering an explanation for their well-documented deficit in seismic moment release.
items.why_notable: This work is attracting attention because it provides rare, direct in situ observations of a seafloor spreading event as it unfolded, capturing the anatomy of rifting on yearly timescales through a novel combination of measurement techniques deployed at the ocean bottom.
items.citation: Jean‐Yves Royer, Jean‐Arthur Olive, Sara Bazin et al.. Anatomy of a seafloor spreading event captured by in situ seismogeodesy. Nature. 2026

items.title: Computational approaches and the future of urban crime research
items.why_notable: This work is drawing attention because it synthesizes how emerging datasets and computational methods are reshaping the study of urban crime, while also identifying the key challenges and future directions that researchers must navigate to use these tools effectively.
items.citation: Gian Maria Campedelli, Zubin Jelveh, Aaron Chalfin et al.. Computational approaches and the future of urban crime research. Nature. 2026

items.title: Reconfigurable mmWave microchips co-integrating hBN switches on GaN
items.why_notable: This work is attracting attention because it demonstrates a way to combine two-dimensional hBN-based switches with GaN microchips to create reconfigurable millimetre-wave circuits, addressing a key hardware challenge for next-generation wireless communication. The co-integration approach suggests a practical path toward programmable mmWave components needed for 6G technology.
items.citation: Sebastián Pazos, Andrés Fontana, Yaqing Shen et al.. Reconfigurable mmWave microchips co-integrating hBN switches on GaN. Nature. 2026

items.title: Air-permeable hydrogels through viscoelastic phase separation of aerogels
items.summary: The study addressed the fundamental challenge that conventional hydrogels poorly exchange gases such as oxygen and carbon dioxide due to the low solubility and diffusivity of non-polar gases in water-rich polar media, which limits their utility in long-term biomedical applications. The researchers developed a strategy using viscoelastic phase separation to incorporate hydrophobic, dry aerogel particles into a hydrophilic hydrogel matrix, driving them to form a stable, non-collapsible three-dimensional air-rich network throughout the material. This approach yielded a record-high oxygen permeability while maintaining high water content, representing roughly a tenfold improvement over unmodified hydrogels and overcoming the traditional trade-off between permeability and water content seen in prior methods. The technique was shown to be broadly applicable across different hydrogel chemistries and form factors, and physiological testing under continuous wear conditions confirmed that the resulting materials prevented fluid accumulation and preserved skin health over an extended period.
items.why_notable: This work is attracting attention because it introduces a method to create hydrogels that remain highly water-rich yet allow air to flow through them, overcoming a longstanding limitation of conventional hydrogels. The resulting materials achieve a dramatic improvement in oxygen permeability, opening potential applications where both hydration and gas transport are simultaneously required.
items.citation: Xiaoyun Yan, Shucong Li, Won Jun Song et al.. Air-permeable hydrogels through viscoelastic phase separation of aerogels. Nature. 2026

items.title: Verification of the Outer Space Treaty with cosmic protons
items.why_notable: This work is drawing attention because it proposes a method to detect nuclear weapons aboard satellites by monitoring neutrons produced through cosmic-proton spallation in the Van Allen radiation belts, potentially enabling verification of the Outer Space Treaty using broadly accessible sensor technologies.
items.citation: Areg Danagoulian. Verification of the Outer Space Treaty with cosmic protons. Nature. 2026

items.title: Aneuploidy selects for the acquisition of driver genes in breast cancer
items.why_notable: This work is attracting attention because it reveals that large-scale chromosomal imbalances in breast cancer are not passive bystanders but actively drive the acquisition of specific cancer-promoting genes. In particular, it identifies a previously underappreciated oncogene whose tumor-promoting effects are tied to stress-resistant mitochondria and enhanced ability to neutralize damaging reactive oxygen species.
authors: Angelica Maria Assunçao da Ponte Lopes
items.citation: Khalid N. Al‐Zahrani, Ellen Langille, Jocelyn Nurtanto et al.. Aneuploidy selects for the acquisition of driver genes in breast cancer. Nature. 2026

items.title: Universal cell embedding provides a foundation model for cell biology
items.summary: The paper developed a foundation model called the Universal Cell Embedding (UCE) that creates a unified representation space for cells across diverse tissues and species using single-cell transcriptomic data. UCE was trained via self-supervision on tens of millions of cells drawn from hundreds of experiments, dozens of tissues, and multiple species, producing a biological latent space that captures meaningful variation while tolerating experimental noise. A key feature of the model is that new cells can be embedded without any data labelling, model retraining, or fine-tuning, making it broadly applicable. The authors used UCE to construct an Integrated Mega-scale Atlas encompassing millions of cells and thousands of named cell types, and showed that the embedding space organizes cell types and tissues in biologically coherent ways. Notably, UCE exhibited emergent capabilities, correctly identifying developmental lineages and embedding data from species absent from its training set, demonstrating generalization beyond its training distribution.
items.why_notable: This work is attracting attention because it introduces a foundation model for cell biology trained on an exceptionally broad and diverse dataset spanning millions of cells across multiple species, tissues, and experiments. By learning generalizable representations of cellular organization and variation, it offers a potentially transformative tool for a wide range of biological and biomedical research.
items.citation: Yanay Rosen, Yusuf Roohani, Ayush Agrawal et al.. Universal cell embedding provides a foundation model for cell biology. Nature. 2026

items.title: LARES-2 satellite measures frame-dragging effect around the Earth
items.why_notable: This work is attracting attention because it represents the most precise measurement yet of frame-dragging — the way Earth's rotation drags spacetime around it — providing a strong confirmation of general relativity while also placing tighter constraints on alternative theories of gravity.
items.citation: Ignazio Ciufolini, Antonio Paolozzi, E. C. Pavlis et al.. LARES-2 satellite measures frame-dragging effect around the Earth. Nature. 2026

items.title: Chromatin landscape and epigenetic heterogeneity of acute myeloid leukaemia
items.summary: This study investigated the role of epigenetic alterations in the pathogenesis, heterogeneity, and clinical outcomes of acute myeloid leukaemia, a dimension that had been less thoroughly characterized than genetic mutations. Using chromatin accessibility profiling via ATAC-seq in a large cohort of newly diagnosed AML patients, combined with multiomics data including transcriptomics, DNA methylation, and histone mark analyses, the researchers classified AML into sixteen distinct epigenomic subgroups. Each subgroup displayed characteristic driver mutations, differentiation states, gene expression programs, and super-enhancer profiles, with distinct transcription factor networks governing their regulatory architecture. Single-cell analyses confirmed that leukaemic cells within each subgroup share a common chromatin accessibility fingerprint and revealed deregulated differentiation trajectories linked to both chromatin and transcriptional states. Importantly, these chromatin-based subgroups provided prognostic information independent of genomic classification and were associated with specific drug sensitivities, highlighting the value of epigenomic profiling beyond conventional genetic characterization of AML.
items.why_notable: This work is attracting attention because it uses chromatin accessibility profiling to classify a blood cancer into a detailed set of epigenomic subgroups, revealing that non-genetic mechanisms—not just mutations—play a significant role in shaping how the disease develops, behaves clinically, and responds to drugs.
items.citation: Yotaro Ochi, Markus Liew‐Littorin, Yasuhito Nannya et al.. Chromatin landscape and epigenetic heterogeneity of acute myeloid leukaemia. Nature. 2026

items.title: Diet–microbiome synergy underlies obesity-associated immunotherapy efficacy
items.summary: This study investigated how diet, obesity, and the gut microbiome interact to influence responses to immune checkpoint inhibitor cancer immunotherapy. Using a broad panel of mouse diet models spanning diverse obesity biology, the researchers characterized how diet-driven changes in metabolism, immunity, and gut microbial composition relate to immunotherapy sensitivity. They found that the enhanced immunotherapy response associated with high body mass index is not explained by metabolic dysfunction but rather depends on the diet–gut microbiome axis, with obesogenic diets fostering a resilient gut microbial ecosystem that can restore immunotherapy sensitivity through diet switching or fecal microbiota transplantation. Specific favorable bacteria, such as Lactobacillus johnsonii, combined with an obesogenic diet synergistically promoted tumor regression by enriching microbiota-derived aromatic amino acid metabolites. Transplantation of gut microbiota from high-BMI human donors into mice also enhanced immunotherapy efficacy compared with normal-BMI donors, suggesting that the immunomodulatory interplay between diet and the gut microbiota could be harnessed to improve cancer immunotherapy outcomes.
items.why_notable: This work is attracting attention because it reveals that the gut microbiome, shaped by diet, drives the paradoxical improvement in cancer immunotherapy outcomes seen in obesity. By linking dietary patterns to microbial metabolism and anti-tumour immune responses across both mouse and human-derived models, it offers a mechanistic explanation for why obesity can enhance responses to immune checkpoint inhibitors.
items.citation: Lysanne Desharnais, Anikka Swaby, Meriem Messaoudene et al.. Diet–microbiome synergy underlies obesity-associated immunotherapy efficacy. Nature. 2026

items.title: Intergenerational mobility fosters innovation in Europe
items.summary: This paper investigates how intergenerational educational mobility varies across European subnational regions and how it relates to innovation outcomes. The researchers constructed a panel database called the European Atlas of Spatially Disaggregated Intergenerational Mobility, capturing indicators of intergenerational mobility across European regions over time. Their analysis reveals that while intergenerational mobility has generally increased, this growth is driven mainly by educational improvements among individuals from lower-educated families rather than by broad reshuffling across the educational spectrum, and gains are unevenly distributed such that regions with high educational inequality also tend to have lower intergenerational mobility. Using this database, the study provides large-scale time-series evidence that European regions with greater intergenerational mobility achieve stronger innovation outcomes, a key driver of economic growth, with the relationship being nonlinear and operating through distinct mechanisms in major innovation hubs.
items.why_notable: This work draws attention by introducing a large-scale atlas that maps how intergenerational mobility has shifted across time and place in Europe, revealing a meaningful link between social mobility and regional innovation.
items.citation: Sarah McNamara, Guido Neidhöfer, Patrick Lehnert. Intergenerational mobility fosters innovation in Europe. Nature. 2026

items.title: An intrinsic cytoskeletal oscillator establishes neuronal polarity
items.summary: This paper investigated how neurons establish polarity by designating a single neurite as the axon while the remaining neurites become dendrites, a process whose mechanistic basis has been poorly understood. Using live imaging and genetic loss-of-function approaches in vivo alongside optogenetic and cytoskeletal perturbation experiments in cultured neurons, the researchers examined the respective roles of growth cones and the cell body in directing this asymmetry. They found that growth cones are not the primary drivers of polarization; instead, the soma acts as a central organizer by initiating an intrinsic oscillatory program involving periodic actin branching dependent on the ARP2/3 complex. This soma-driven branching remodels a global actomyosin network, generating an actin wave that transiently relaxes local contractility in a single neurite and biases it toward axon fate through microtubule-based protrusion, while sustained actomyosin activity suppresses axon identity in the remaining neurites. The study concludes that this cell-autonomous, soma-initiated mechanism ensures the reliable emergence of a single axon independently of external environmental cues.
items.why_notable: This work is attracting attention because it identifies an intrinsic cytoskeletal oscillator—involving the ARP2/3 complex and actomyosin acting together at the soma—as a fundamental mechanism that drives how neurons establish their polarity, revealing a previously unrecognized rhythmic program underlying a critical step in neural development.
items.citation: Tien-chen Lin, C.H. Coles, Eissa Alfadil et al.. An intrinsic cytoskeletal oscillator establishes neuronal polarity. Nature. 2026

items.title: The forest of knowledge under global change
items.summary: This paper examined how climate change and Indigenous language loss might simultaneously threaten the biological and cultural heritage of the Amazon basin. The researchers assembled a large database of reports linking native plant species to their societal uses across all Amazonian countries, then modeled how the geographic ranges of these utilized species are projected to shift under multiple future climate scenarios. They found that plant species used by humans face greater range reductions than non-utilized species, and that Indigenous cultures could lose a substantial proportion of both their utilized plant species and the ecosystem services associated with them by the latter half of this century. Compounding this biological threat, the disappearance of endangered Indigenous languages is projected to cause a major reduction in the regional pool of traditional ecological knowledge. The study concludes that Amazonian biocultural heritage faces severe dual vulnerability from climate change and linguistic erosion, while offering its publicly available dataset as a resource for guiding biocultural restoration efforts.
items.why_notable: This work is attracting attention because it highlights that climate change threatens not only the Amazon's plant species and ecological services but also the indigenous and local cultures whose knowledge and heritage are intertwined with that biodiversity.
items.citation: Rodrigo Cámara‐Leret, Patrick R. Roehrdanz, Jordi Bascompte. The forest of knowledge under global change. Nature. 2026

items.title: I want to see a prison-to-PhD pipeline
items.why_notable: This work is drawing attention because it highlights efforts to build structured pathways that allow formerly incarcerated individuals to enter and succeed in academia, addressing a significant gap in educational access and opportunity.
items.citation: Virginia Gewin. I want to see a prison-to-PhD pipeline. Nature. 2026

items.title: Regenerating people–nature relationships to counter biocultural erosion in the Amazon
items.why_notable: This work is drawing attention because it proposes that restoring reciprocal, care-based relationships between Indigenous peoples and nature in the Amazon could serve as a strategy to counteract the biodiversity impacts of climate change, framing biocultural regeneration as a conservation solution.
items.citation: Victoria Reyes‐García. Regenerating people–nature relationships to counter biocultural erosion in the Amazon. Nature. 2026

items.title: Nobel-winning chemist leaves US to direct AI materials lab in China
items.why_notable: A Nobel-winning chemist's decision to relocate from the US to lead an AI-driven materials laboratory in China is drawing attention because it illustrates how cuts to American science funding, combined with China's active recruitment of top international researchers, may be redirecting elite scientific talent away from the United States.
items.citation: Jenna Ahart, Mohana Basu. Nobel-winning chemist leaves US to direct AI materials lab in China. Nature. 2026

items.title: Volcanoes and wildfires contributed to increased stratospheric humidification
items.summary: The paper investigated the causes of increasing water vapour concentrations in the stratosphere since the mid-2000s. Using satellite observations and climate-model simulations, the researchers examined the roles of moderate volcanic eruptions and extreme wildfire events in humidifying the stratosphere. They found that aerosol-mediated processes from these volcanic and wildfire events explain roughly one-third of the observed stratospheric water vapour trend, a contribution comparable in magnitude to that attributable to surface warming. The findings highlight that episodic natural disturbances such as volcanic eruptions and large wildfires are significant, previously underappreciated contributors to long-term changes in stratospheric moisture.
items.why_notable: This work is attracting attention because it reveals that moderate volcanic eruptions and extreme wildfires have been a substantial, previously underappreciated source of stratospheric water vapour, with their aerosol-driven effects accounting for roughly a third of the observed humidification trend — comparable in magnitude to the contribution from surface warming.
items.citation: Volcanoes and wildfires contributed to increased stratospheric humidification. Nature. 2026

items.title: Those who forget
items.why_notable: The work is drawing attention for its exploration of how forgetting may actually contribute to mental clarity rather than simply representing cognitive loss.
items.citation: Michael Adam Robson. Those who forget. Nature. 2026

items.title: Ocean floor witnessed splitting apart for the first time — releasing lava
items.why_notable: This work is drawing attention because scientists captured a mid-ocean ridge actively splitting apart as it happened, directly observing seafloor motion and the accompanying lava outflows in real time—a geological process that had never been witnessed before.
items.citation: Davide Castelvecchi. Ocean floor witnessed splitting apart for the first time — releasing lava. Nature. 2026

items.title: Breast cancer driver genes found by screening chromosome aberrations in vivo
items.summary: This study investigated how chromosomal instability contributes to breast cancer development by examining aneuploidies — gains or losses of whole chromosomes or chromosome arms — in mouse models of the disease. Using an in vivo CRISPR screen approach, the researchers systematically identified cancer driver genes embedded within these chromosomal aberrations. They found that each aneuploidy typically harbors only one or two prevalent driver genes capable of promoting cancer progression without requiring the accumulation of additional chromosomal changes. A key finding was that these drivers depend on an intact tumor microenvironment to exert their effects, as altered expression of many identified drivers failed to change cell behavior when tested in vitro.
items.why_notable: This work is drawing attention because it identifies specific genes within chromosomal instabilities that actively drive breast cancer, rather than the broader aneuploidies themselves being the key factor. Notably, these driver genes appear to require an intact tumor microenvironment to exert their cancer-promoting effects, revealing a more nuanced mechanism underlying genomic instability in breast cancer.
items.citation: Breast cancer driver genes found by screening chromosome aberrations in vivo. Nature. 2026

items.title: Daily briefing: Mountain bongos still roam the forest
items.why_notable: The discovery of a breeding herd of critically endangered mountain bongos is drawing attention because it offers rare hope for a species' survival. The findings, alongside other intriguing stories, highlight both conservation prospects and broader scientific curiosities.
items.citation: Flora Graham. Daily briefing: Mountain bongos still roam the forest. Nature. 2026

items.title: Judicious use of LLMs could speed up progress in the social sciences
items.why_notable: This work is attracting attention because it highlights the potential of large language models to accelerate social science research, while also raising important unresolved questions about the appropriate ways to deploy them in that context.
items.citation: James Druckman. Judicious use of LLMs could speed up progress in the social sciences. Nature. 2026

items.title: Sea-floor spreading captured by undersea observatory
items.why_notable: This work is drawing attention because it represents the first direct, in situ measurement of oceanic crust being formed as tectonic plates spread apart, a fundamental geological process that had never before been captured by an on-site observatory.
items.citation: Ingo Grevemeyer, Lars Ruepke. Sea-floor spreading captured by undersea observatory. Nature. 2026

items.title: How to advance revolutionary science: high turnover, high risk and a licence to fail
items.why_notable: This work is attracting attention because it outlines a specific organizational philosophy for funding genuinely transformative research, emphasizing high researcher turnover, tolerance for failure, and risk-taking as deliberate structural features rather than incidental ones.
items.citation: Elizabeth Gibney. How to advance revolutionary science: high turnover, high risk and a licence to fail. Nature. 2026

items.title: Nuclear weapons lurking in space could be tracked down by satellites
items.why_notable: This work is attracting attention because it identifies a potential method for detecting hidden nuclear weapons in orbit, exploiting the fact that interactions with Earth's natural space radiation environment would cause such weapons to emit detectable neutrons that satellites could sense.
items.citation: Angela Di Fulvio. Nuclear weapons lurking in space could be tracked down by satellites. Nature. 2026

items.title: Publisher Correction: A 98-qubit trapped-ion quantum computer with all-to-all connectivity
items.why_notable: This work is attracting attention because it describes a trapped-ion quantum computer with a notably large qubit count and all-to-all connectivity, a architecture that allows any qubit to interact directly with any other — a significant advantage for running complex quantum algorithms.
items.citation: Anthony Ransford, Mark Allman, Jake Arkinstall et al.. Publisher Correction: A 98-qubit trapped-ion quantum computer with all-to-all connectivity. Nature. 2026

items.title: Listen to Gen Z when it comes to AI in education
items.why_notable: This work is drawing attention because it highlights that young people who have grown up as the primary users of AI tools have uniquely relevant perspectives on how artificial intelligence should be integrated into education. The framing suggests that educators and policymakers risk making poorly informed decisions if they fail to center the voices of this generation in those conversations.
items.citation: Ian Robertson, Vincent C. Müller, Eleonora Catena. Listen to Gen Z when it comes to AI in education. Nature. 2026

items.title: Say hello to hard helium
items.why_notable: The work is drawing attention because it represents the first-ever solidification of helium, a landmark achievement given the element's notoriously resistant-to-solidify nature, paired with a notable geological discovery of an ancient salt-water lake.
items.citation: Say hello to hard helium. Nature. 2026

items.title: AI can cause harm: safeguards must catch up
items.why_notable: The piece is drawing attention because it highlights a critical and growing gap between the harmful capabilities of AI systems and the protective measures currently in place to address them. The urgency of the message — that safeguards are lagging behind real-world AI-driven harms — frames this as an immediate policy and research challenge requiring action.
items.citation: Ziv Ben-Zion. AI can cause harm: safeguards must catch up. Nature. 2026

items.title: How long can humans live? All evidence points to a maximum of 125 years
items.why_notable: This work is attracting attention because it addresses one of biology's most fundamental questions—whether there is a hard ceiling on human lifespan—and concludes that the evidence converges on a definitive upper limit. The finding challenges ongoing speculation about whether advances in medicine or technology could push human longevity beyond what nature appears to allow.
items.citation: Brandon Milholland, X Dong, Jan Vijg. How long can humans live? All evidence points to a maximum of 125 years. Nature. 2026

items.title: ‘Humanizer’ tool can erase signs of AI-written text — alarming scientists
items.why_notable: The work is drawing concern because a newly described tool can systematically strip out the textual patterns that typically betray AI-generated writing, making it far harder to detect AI use in research papers and grant proposals.
items.citation: Edward Chen. ‘Humanizer’ tool can erase signs of AI-written text — alarming scientists. Nature. 2026

items.title: How to widen access to the critical minerals that the world needs
items.why_notable: This work is attracting attention because it proposes a legal and economic framework—rather than purely industrial expansion—as a faster route to broadening global access to critical minerals. The argument that a patent pool and licensing architecture could outpace decades of mining and refining investment challenges conventional assumptions about how mineral supply constraints are best addressed.
items.citation: Rabah Arezki, Grégoire Rota-Graziosi. How to widen access to the critical minerals that the world needs. Nature. 2026

items.title: Daily briefing: Three decades of Dolly
items.why_notable: This briefing is drawing attention by marking a major milestone in cloning history—reflecting on three decades of scientific progress since Dolly the sheep's birth—while also covering timely topics including dementia prevention research and the future preservation of the Hubble space telescope.
items.citation: Flora Graham. Daily briefing: Three decades of Dolly. Nature. 2026

items.title: Body-plan organizer in comb jellies hints at animal ancestry
items.why_notable: This work is attracting attention because transplantation experiments across evolutionarily distant animal groups suggest that the mechanisms organizing body plans during development are more ancient and conserved than previously appreciated, with implications for understanding the common ancestry of animals.
items.citation: Ruth Styfhals, Pawel Burkhardt. Body-plan organizer in comb jellies hints at animal ancestry. Nature. 2026

items.title: Save Hubble: the race to preserve the space telescope kicks off
items.why_notable: The story is drawing attention because Hubble's continued scientific value and strong public backing have sparked urgent efforts to ensure the iconic space telescope's survival, raising questions about the future of a tool still considered irreplaceable for understanding the Universe.
items.citation: Rogier Windhorst. Save Hubble: the race to preserve the space telescope kicks off. Nature. 2026

items.title: Keep the Hubble and James Webb Space Telescopes alive — the science is worth the price tag
items.why_notable: The framing highlights that these space telescopes are producing scientific results that exceed even astronomers' highest expectations, making a case that their continued operation justifies the cost.
items.citation: Keep the Hubble and James Webb Space Telescopes alive — the science is worth the price tag. Nature. 2026

items.title: How to avoid dementia — what the science really says
items.why_notable: This work is attracting attention because large-scale studies are rigorously testing whether everyday lifestyle factors like diet, exercise, and social engagement can protect against dementia, and the findings are reportedly surprising. The results challenge or complicate prevailing assumptions about what people can do to reduce their risk.
items.citation: Helen Pearson. How to avoid dementia — what the science really says. Nature. 2026

items.title: From cloning to gene-editing: the enduring legacy of Dolly the sheep
items.why_notable: Dolly the sheep's landmark place in scientific history is being revisited because the public and ethical debates surrounding her cloning are seen as offering relevant guidance for today's conversations about gene-editing and reproductive technologies.
items.citation: From cloning to gene-editing: the enduring legacy of Dolly the sheep. Nature. 2026

items.title: Daily briefing: World Cup ‘hydration breaks’ miss the mark
items.why_notable: The work is drawing attention because a physiologist argues that World Cup hydration breaks, intended to protect players from heat, may actually backfire by eroding public trust in heat-health science.
items.citation: Flora Graham. Daily briefing: World Cup ‘hydration breaks’ miss the mark. Nature. 2026

items.title: Validation of molecular detection of <i>Candida auris</i> from wastewater.
items.summary: This study addressed the challenge of monitoring *Candida auris*, a multidrug-resistant fungal pathogen that spreads readily in healthcare settings through skin colonization and shedding, by investigating whether wastewater surveillance could serve as a detection tool. The researchers adapted and validated a quantitative PCR assay originally developed for clinical colonization screening to work with wastewater samples. The assay demonstrated high amplification efficiency, strong reproducibility, and a low limit of detection in the wastewater matrix. These findings indicate that the clinical qPCR protocol can be reliably repurposed for detecting *C. auris* in wastewater, supporting its integration into existing environmental surveillance workflows as a more cost-effective alternative to direct patient screening for tracking pathogen emergence and prevalence across healthcare systems.
items.citation: Rodríguez Stewart RM, Litvintseva AP, Chavez J et al.. Validation of molecular detection of <i>Candida auris</i> from wastewater.. Microbiology spectrum. 2026

items.title: A Systematic Review of Gastrointestinal and Respiratory Pathogen Detection in Wastewater in Africa, with Focus on Rwanda: Implications for Early Warning and Public Health Surveillance.
items.summary: This systematic review examined the use of wastewater-based epidemiology for detecting gastrointestinal and respiratory pathogens, with particular attention to the African context and Rwanda as a case study. The authors conducted a narrative systematic review by searching major academic databases for peer-reviewed literature and surveillance studies published over roughly two decades, ultimately synthesizing findings from more than a hundred eligible studies. The review found that wastewater-based epidemiology offers a non-invasive and cost-effective means of capturing both symptomatic and asymptomatic infections, providing population-level epidemiological intelligence that traditional clinical surveillance systems frequently miss, especially in low- and middle-income countries where underreporting is common. Key challenges identified include variability in sampling methods, environmental factors that accelerate pathogen decay, and inconsistencies in laboratory workflows across settings. The authors conclude that integrating wastewater surveillance into existing public health infrastructure, supported by standardized protocols, laboratory capacity building, and multisectoral collaboration, could substantially enhance outbreak preparedness and response in rapidly urbanizing African settings such as Kigali.
items.citation: Bambara S, Isingizwe MC, Adegboyega TT et al.. A Systematic Review of Gastrointestinal and Respiratory Pathogen Detection in Wastewater in Africa, with Focus on Rwanda: Implications for Early Warning and Public Health Surveillance.. Pathogens (Basel, Switzerland). 2026

items.title: Multi-Pathogen Wastewater Surveillance enables Real-Time Targeted Public Health Interventions During the 2025 African Nations Championship Football Tournament
items.summary: This study examined whether multi-pathogen wastewater-based surveillance could serve as a practical public health tool during a major mass gathering event in a low- and middle-income country. During a football tournament held in Uganda, researchers deployed Moore swabs across dozens of manholes at a national stadium on multiple match days, concentrating pathogens from collected wastewater samples and analyzing them for a broad panel of enteric, respiratory, and other pathogens using quantitative PCR. The vast majority of samples tested positive for at least one pathogen, with enteric pathogens such as Shigella, Rotavirus A, and Enterovirus predominating, while mpox virus was detected in a substantial proportion of samples and respiratory pathogens including SARS-CoV-2 and Influenza B appeared at lower frequencies. Pathogen diversity varied across sampling days, with co-detection of multiple pathogens common among positive samples, and the surveillance findings directly informed real-time public health responses including sanitation improvements, hygiene promotion, environmental disinfection, and enhanced syndromic surveillance. The authors conclude that integrating multi-pathogen wastewater surveillance into mass-gathering preparedness frameworks is operationally feasible in low-resource settings and can provide actionable, population-level intelligence to support outbreak detection and response.
items.citation: Nsawotebba A, Morunyanga I, Nakintu V et al.. Multi-Pathogen Wastewater Surveillance enables Real-Time Targeted Public Health Interventions During the 2025 African Nations Championship Football Tournament. 2026

items.title: Revealing the hidden burden: wastewater-based epidemiology for underreported and emerging infectious diseases in communities.
items.summary: Wastewater-based epidemiology (WBE) has emerged as a powerful surveillance approach that captures population-level infectious disease burden beyond what traditional clinical reporting systems detect. This review examines how WBE identifies underreported, novel, and emerging pathogens across enteric, respiratory, and neglected disease categories, including many agents routinely missed by healthcare systems. Sequencing-based studies have revealed extensive diversity within the human virome and shown that multiple viral taxa are frequently co-detected, raising the possibility that co-infections influence viral evolution, disease presentation, and transmission dynamics. WBE has demonstrated the capacity to detect both known and novel pathogens before they achieve clinical recognition, despite ongoing methodological challenges in quantification and biological validation. The review argues that advances in long-read sequencing, bioinformatics, and global data integration will further establish wastewater genomics as a foundational component of early-warning and One Health surveillance frameworks worldwide.
items.citation: Greaves JC, Rodriguez RA. Revealing the hidden burden: wastewater-based epidemiology for underreported and emerging infectious diseases in communities.. Environmental monitoring and assessment. 2026

items.title: Toward a unified approach: Considerations for bioinformatic and sequencing activities & data in wastewater surveillance of biologic public health threats
items.summary: This paper examines the challenges and opportunities of applying next-generation sequencing to wastewater-based epidemiological surveillance as a public health tool. The authors reviewed the entire wastewater surveillance workflow—from sample collection and sequencing through data analysis and reporting—identifying gaps in existing guidelines, which tend to favour conventional analytical approaches over sequencing-based methods. They argue that while falling sequencing costs and advances in genomic technologies have made large-scale pathogen monitoring increasingly feasible, the absence of universally applicable, standardised protocols for sampling, sequencing, and bioinformatic analysis undermines the comparability and reliability of surveillance data across different settings. The paper provides concrete recommendations for standardisation and calls for pilot studies and iterative refinement to address implementation barriers. Crucially, the authors contend that technical standardisation alone is insufficient, and that durable, comparable surveillance also requires supportive infrastructure, sustained funding, and enabling government policy frameworks.
items.citation: Psomopoulos F, O’Cathail C, Anastasiadou N et al.. Toward a unified approach: Considerations for bioinformatic and sequencing activities & data in wastewater surveillance of biologic public health threats. 2026

items.title: Traveler-based Genomic Surveillance: A Scalable Approach to Early Pathogen Detection and Global Biosecurity
items.summary: The paper describes the implementation and evaluation of the U.S. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention's Traveler-based Genomic Surveillance program, which used genomic sequencing of nasal swabs from volunteer international air travelers and aviation wastewater to detect infectious disease threats early. Nasal samples collected anonymously from travelers arriving at multiple U.S. airports were pooled and tested by RT-PCR, while wastewater samples were collected from arriving flights and airport collection points, with sequencing performed for SARS-CoV-2 and a range of respiratory and gastrointestinal pathogens. The program successfully processed samples from hundreds of thousands of travelers, detected SARS-CoV-2 in a substantial proportion of pools and the vast majority of wastewater samples, and made sequence data publicly available within approximately two weeks of collection. Surveillance captured seasonal patterns of influenza and respiratory syncytial virus, detected gastrointestinal viruses year-round in wastewater, and was scaled in response to specific outbreak events abroad, including COVID-19, Mycoplasma pneumoniae, and monkeypox. The authors conclude that voluntary, multi-modal traveler surveillance integrating nasal and wastewater sampling with genomic sequencing represents a feasible and scalable model for early pathogen detection and pandemic preparedness.
items.citation: Bart SM, Smith TC, Rothstein AP et al.. Traveler-based Genomic Surveillance: A Scalable Approach to Early Pathogen Detection and Global Biosecurity. 2026

items.title: PHINN-EEG: Topological Time-Series Analysis of Dream-State EEG -- Dynamic Betti Curves for Dream Content Classification and Topology-Conditioned Neural Signal Synthesis
items.summary: This paper introduces a topological signal processing framework for analyzing EEG recordings associated with dream states, positioning itself against conventional approaches that rely on power spectral density and statistical moment features. The method uses sliding-window delay embeddings and Vietoris-Rips filtrations to construct Dynamic Betti Curves, which capture the geometric and topological structure of multichannel neural activity rather than its spectral energy content. The framework also incorporates topology-conditioned flow matching for dream-state EEG synthesis, alongside a spectral-conditioned baseline to isolate the contribution of topological conditioning specifically. The authors analytically project their approach to substantially outperform existing benchmarks in dream-versus-non-dream classification on a large open-access awakening dataset, and propose candidate topological archetypes linking Betti curve transitions to phenomenological dream report categories as an exploratory hypothesis. Notably, the claimed performance gains are projections rather than empirically validated results, and the authors explicitly present their phenomenological linkages as a hypothesis space pending empirical confirmation.
items.citation: Ren Takahashi, Emre Yusuf, Jayabrata Bhaduri. PHINN-EEG: Topological Time-Series Analysis of Dream-State EEG -- Dynamic Betti Curves for Dream Content Classification and Topology-Conditioned Neural Signal Synthesis. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Impact of Benign Connectivity Variations on Intrusion Detection for Encrypted OPC UA Traffic in Industrial Private 5G Networks
items.summary: This paper examined how machine learning-based intrusion detection systems behave when monitoring encrypted industrial communications over private 5G networks, specifically focusing on OPC UA traffic. The researchers investigated whether normal, benign connectivity variations in these networks could negatively affect detection accuracy. Their experiments demonstrated that legitimate connectivity events can substantially raise false positive rates even when no actual attacks are present, and that elevated anomaly scores from the intrusion detection system tend to coincide with control-plane activity associated with these connectivity events. The findings suggest that intrusion detection outputs in industrial private 5G environments should be interpreted in conjunction with control-plane context to avoid misclassifying normal network behavior as malicious.
items.citation: Song Son Ha, Florian Foerster, Henry Beuster et al.. Impact of Benign Connectivity Variations on Intrusion Detection for Encrypted OPC UA Traffic in Industrial Private 5G Networks. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Identifying and characterizing extragalactic circum-CBC exoplanets with future gravitational-wave detectors
items.summary: This paper investigates whether exoplanets orbiting compact binary coalescences outside our galaxy could be detected using future gravitational-wave observatories. The authors exploit the fact that a circum-binary exoplanet causes the compact binary's centre of mass to wobble around the shared barycentre, imprinting a characteristic signature on the emitted gravitational-wave signal. By reconstructing the centre-of-mass kinematics encoded in the waveform, they assess the detectability and characterizability of such extragalactic exoplanets for next-generation detectors including an upgraded LIGO, Einstein Telescope, and DECIGO, considering systems involving binary neutron stars, neutron star–black hole pairs, and binary black holes across a range of orbital parameters. For a substantial fraction of the parameter space explored, including both circular and eccentric exoplanet orbits, they find it is possible to confirm the presence of a circum-binary exoplanet and recover its mass to within an order-unity factor of the true value at reasonable confidence, up to a degeneracy with the unknown orbital inclination angle. The work thus establishes a proof-of-principle that gravitational-wave observations could extend exoplanet science beyond the Milky Way for the first time.
items.citation: Avinash Tiwari, Aditya Vijaykumar, Shasvath J. Kapadia et al.. Identifying and characterizing extragalactic circum-CBC exoplanets with future gravitational-wave detectors. arXiv. 2026

items.title: HD 148797: A bright F-type star with two moderate-period low-density sub-Jovian planets. Compact multi-planet architectures are common in the Neptunian savanna
items.summary: The paper reports the confirmation and characterization of two transiting sub-Jovian planets orbiting the bright F-type star HD 148797, using photometric data from the TESS and CHEOPS space missions. The two planets have orbital periods of roughly 42 and 68 days, with a period ratio close to the golden mean, and both possess similar radii of about 8.3 Earth radii alongside masses near 40 Earth masses derived from anti-correlated transit-timing variations via photodynamical modeling. Their resulting low bulk densities place both planets in the Neptunian savanna, a population of intermediate-sized planets that is sparsely populated relative to neighboring parameter space. Examining the broader population of savanna planets, the authors find that multi-planet systems are common in this regime and that most contain at least one adjacent planet pair with a modest period ratio, suggesting dynamically cold architectures consistent with disk-driven rather than high-eccentricity tidal migration. The co-evolved, similarly sized nature of the two planets and the brightness of the host star make this system a strong candidate for comparative atmospheric studies.
items.citation: J. M. Almenara, A. Castro-González, R. Mardling et al.. HD 148797: A bright F-type star with two moderate-period low-density sub-Jovian planets. Compact multi-planet architectures are common in the Neptunian savanna. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Evolution of Accuracy and Visual-Cognitive Errors in a Decade of Vision-Language AI Models
items.summary: This paper investigated how vision-language models have progressed over a decade in describing complex social scenes, addressing limitations of prior evaluations that relied on simple imagery and lacked systematic error analysis. The researchers introduced a new dataset of images depicting complex social interactions and behaviors, then assessed nine models spanning from early pre-multimodal systems to recent multimodal large language models alongside human descriptions, evaluating performance against a gold standard. They categorized errors into five visual-cognitive types, including object detection, hallucination, scene understanding, and spatial dependence, to understand not just overall accuracy but the nature of model failures. Their findings show that earlier models fell well below human performance on complex scenes, while recent multimodal large language models reached accuracy levels comparable to top-performing human descriptions and effectively closed the gap between simple and complex scene understanding. Most error types were nearly eliminated by the latest models, with the exception of spatial dependence errors, and detection, recognition, and hallucination errors were identified as having the greatest negative impact on description accuracy.
items.citation: Shravan Murlidaran, Miguel P. Eckstein. Evolution of Accuracy and Visual-Cognitive Errors in a Decade of Vision-Language AI Models. arXiv. 2026

items.title: VEXAIoT: Autonomous IoT Vulnerability EXploitation using AI Agents
items.summary: This paper investigated whether large language model-based AI agents could autonomously discover and exploit vulnerabilities in IoT systems, an application domain that had not previously been explored despite growing interest in LLM-driven penetration testing. The researchers developed a multi-agent framework called VEXAIoT, which pairs a vulnerability detection agent with an attack execution agent to perform reconnaissance, plan attack sequences, and execute exploits against IoT services. The framework was evaluated across ten attack scenarios mapped to established OWASP IoT vulnerability categories using two testbed environments designed to simulate real-world IoT and networked system weaknesses. Results demonstrated exceptionally high attack success rates, with the system achieving an overall success rate near the maximum across hundreds of attack executions, while also completing most attacks in under two minutes with low computational token overhead. The findings suggest that LLM-driven multi-agent systems can effectively automate IoT vulnerability assessment workflows in controlled settings, raising both promise for security testing and implications for the offensive security landscape.
items.citation: Katherine Swinea, Kshitiz Aryal, Lopamudra Praharaj et al.. VEXAIoT: Autonomous IoT Vulnerability EXploitation using AI Agents. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Rapid and Sensitive Detection of <i>Schistosoma mansoni</i> in the Intermediate Snail Hosts Using Loop-Mediated Isothermal Amplification (LAMP) Diagnostics.
items.summary: The study addressed the need for improved diagnostic tools to detect *Schistosoma mansoni* infection in its intermediate snail hosts, which is critical for controlling schistosomiasis but poorly served by conventional parasitological methods. The researchers developed a loop-mediated isothermal amplification assay targeting the mitochondrial NADH dehydrogenase subunit 1 gene of *S. mansoni*, optimized it under simple thermal conditions, and evaluated its performance against microscopy, conventional PCR, and real-time PCR using both laboratory-infected snails and field-collected samples from two endemic countries. In laboratory settings, the assay achieved perfect specificity and high sensitivity, outperforming microscopy and matching the performance of real-time PCR. In field surveys, the LAMP assay detected a higher proportion of infected snails than conventional PCR, while microscopy failed to identify any positive cases at all. The authors conclude that this assay represents a sensitive, specific, and practically deployable tool for routine snail surveillance and early detection of shifts in schistosomiasis transmission.
items.citation: Li HM, Qin ZQ, Lv S et al.. Rapid and Sensitive Detection of <i>Schistosoma mansoni</i> in the Intermediate Snail Hosts Using Loop-Mediated Isothermal Amplification (LAMP) Diagnostics.. Tropical medicine and infectious disease. 2026

items.title: You Only Look Once (YOLO) based machine learning algorithm for real-time detection of loop-mediated isothermal amplification (LAMP) diagnostics.
items.summary: This paper investigated the use of machine learning to automate the interpretation of loop-mediated isothermal amplification (LAMP) diagnostic results, addressing the subjectivity and error-prone nature of visual result reading. The researchers applied the YOLOv8 object detection algorithm to digital images of LAMP reaction tubes, training it to locate individual tubes and classify them as positive or negative for DNA amplification. The trained model achieved high overall accuracy in classifying LAMP results on a test set, with strong precision and recall for both positive and negative cases. The findings demonstrate that this approach is suitable for real-time LAMP diagnostics, offering an automated, rapid, and accessible alternative to manual visual interpretation that could reduce false-positive and false-negative outcomes.
items.citation: Mezgebo B, Chaffee R, Castellanos LR et al.. You Only Look Once (YOLO) based machine learning algorithm for real-time detection of loop-mediated isothermal amplification (LAMP) diagnostics.. PloS one. 2026

items.title: Broad-Temperature Polymerase in Nucleic Acid Amplification-Based Diagnostics: From Thermal Precision to Dynamic Conditions.
items.summary: This paper investigated nucleic acid amplification using a polymerase capable of functioning across a wide range of temperatures, bridging the gap between traditional PCR's precise thermal cycling and conventional isothermal methods. The researchers developed a LAMP-based amplification approach employing a Bst polymerase with strong strand displacement activity and broad thermostability, enabling DNA synthesis across a temperature range spanning from approximately body temperature to well above typical isothermal amplification conditions. They demonstrated that amplification could be initiated at either high or low starting temperatures and sustained even under dynamically changing temperature profiles, rather than requiring strict thermal control. The system was validated for detecting SARS-CoV-2 and Mycobacterium tuberculosis genomic DNA, confirming its diagnostic applicability. The authors propose this broad-temperature amplification platform as a promising tool for point-of-care and field-based diagnostics where maintaining precise temperature conditions is impractical.
items.citation: Hamidizadeh M, Bier FF. Broad-Temperature Polymerase in Nucleic Acid Amplification-Based Diagnostics: From Thermal Precision to Dynamic Conditions.. ACS sensors. 2026

items.title: Recent advances in isothermal amplification techniques for detection of animal diseases.
items.summary: This review examines isothermal amplification technologies as alternatives to conventional PCR-based diagnostic methods for detecting pathogens in livestock and companion animals. The authors surveyed recent advances in these techniques, which amplify nucleic acids at a constant temperature and therefore do not require thermocycling equipment, making them suitable for field and resource-limited settings. The review covers improvements in assay design and analytical performance, as well as the integration of these methods into portable, field-deployable detection platforms. The authors highlight that isothermal approaches offer rapid, sensitive, and user-friendly pathogen detection with potential to overcome the infrastructure and personnel constraints that limit conventional molecular diagnostics. The review also identifies remaining challenges and outlines future directions for deploying these technologies in routine veterinary diagnostics and disease surveillance programs.
items.citation: Tooryan F, Enyertornye B, Choudhury FJ et al.. Recent advances in isothermal amplification techniques for detection of animal diseases.. Frontiers in veterinary science. 2026

items.title: Development of a digital loop-mediated isothermal amplification using a digital polymerase chain reaction device.
items.summary: This study developed a digital loop-mediated isothermal amplification workflow by adapting a commercial digital polymerase chain reaction platform to perform absolute quantification without standard curves. The researchers optimized the method using a mitochondrial marker from an aquatic mussel species, employing diluted fluorescent master mix on a high-density nanoplate system that generates tens of thousands of reaction partitions. Compared to conventional turbidimetric LAMP, the digital approach showed greater quantitative stability and reliable detection down to very low copy numbers, though minor limitations emerged at both concentration extremes. The high partition density of the platform was found to reduce the likelihood of molecular dropout, improving precision for low-abundance targets. The authors propose this workflow as a practical tool for applications in diagnostics, food safety, and environmental DNA monitoring.
items.venue: Analytical sciences : the international journal of the Japan Society for Analytical Chemistry
items.citation: Fukuta S, Suzuki R. Development of a digital loop-mediated isothermal amplification using a digital polymerase chain reaction device.. Analytical sciences : the international journal of the Japan Society for Analytical Chemistry. 2026

items.title: A Quantum Path to Partial Differential Equations
items.summary: These lecture notes address the challenge of applying fault-tolerant quantum algorithms to partial differential equations, a topic that sits at the intersection of numerical analysis and quantum computation, two communities with distinct terminologies. The work organizes its treatment around the principle of block encoding, whereby discretized differential operators are embedded in unitaries and then processed using quantum primitives such as quantum singular value transformation, Hamiltonian simulation, and amplitude amplification. Each chapter traces the full computational pipeline from a continuous PDE through finite difference or finite element discretization to quantum encoding, transformation, and extraction of quantities of interest, with careful attention to sources of error and cost including state preparation, normalization, postselection, and measurement. The notes cover elliptic, hyperbolic, and parabolic equations, and conclude by treating nonlinear problems through Carleman and Koopman–von Neumann linearization techniques. The stated goal is not to survey quantum advantage broadly but to provide a mathematically transparent entry point and a common vocabulary for researchers approaching the field from either discipline.
items.citation: Xiantao Li. A Quantum Path to Partial Differential Equations. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Thermalization in a Height-Conserving Quantum Dimer Model
items.summary: This paper investigates thermalization in a strongly constrained quantum system, specifically the height-conserving quantum dimer model on the square lattice, where local plaquette flip moves conserve an emergent height field. The central question is whether dynamics within a single Hilbert space sector thermalizes after accounting for the extensive fragmentation caused by conservation laws and frozen states. The authors carefully resolve winding numbers, sublattice heights, and lattice momentum to isolate the dominant connected Krylov component of each fragmented sector, then analyze spectral statistics, entanglement entropy, and connectivity. They find a striking inconsistency between two standard chaos diagnostics: level-spacing statistics vary across momentum sectors from near-Poisson to Wigner-Dyson distributions, yet eigenstate entanglement entropy consistently collapses onto a narrow dome-shaped curve characteristic of eigenstate thermalization, with only rare low-entanglement outliers. This dissociation between spectral correlations and eigenstate thermalization demonstrates that strong kinematic constraints can produce a novel regime of constrained quantum chaos where these two signatures of thermalization need not coincide.
items.citation: Junsheng Feng, Jie Ren, Zheng Yan. Thermalization in a Height-Conserving Quantum Dimer Model. arXiv. 2026

items.title: SAGEAgent: A Self-Evolving Agent for Cost-Aware Modality Acquisition in Multimodal Survival Prediction
items.summary: This paper investigates whether accurate cancer survival prediction requires a complete set of diagnostic modalities for every patient, framing the problem as one of cost-aware sequential decision-making. The authors propose SAGEAgent, a self-evolving large language model-based clinical agent that operates along a clinically ordered workflow of escalating diagnostic burden—from basic demographics to invasive genomic profiling—and actively decides whether acquiring the next modality is justified for each individual patient. The agent integrates clinical tools that translate numerical predictions into interpretable text, an episodic memory for retrieving similar past cases, and a semantic memory that accumulates reusable decision patterns over experience. Experiments on a glioma cohort combining multiple datasets with four diagnostic modalities show that SAGEAgent achieves competitive survival prediction accuracy while substantially reducing the average diagnostic burden compared to approaches that assume full modality availability or passively handle missing data.
items.citation: Chongyu Qu, Can Cui, Zhengyi Lu et al.. SAGEAgent: A Self-Evolving Agent for Cost-Aware Modality Acquisition in Multimodal Survival Prediction. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Deep Learning for Dynamic Programming with Recursive Utility Using First-order Conditions
items.summary: This paper addresses the challenge of solving discrete-time dynamic programming problems with recursive utility, where nonlinear certainty equivalents embedded in the Bellman equation are difficult to evaluate. The authors developed a deep learning algorithm called certainty-equivalent first-order learning (CEFOL), which uses separate neural networks to represent the certainty equivalent, value functions, policy functions, and Lagrange multipliers simultaneously. The algorithm exploits both Bellman equations and model-specific first-order and Karush-Kuhn-Tucker optimality conditions as residuals for minimization, allowing it to handle general equality and inequality constraints, including occasionally binding ones, without penalty functions or problem-specific reformulations. CEFOL was applied to several economic models including risk-sensitive and Epstein-Zin consumption-saving problems, a robust-control problem, and a dynamic stochastic general equilibrium model with recursive preferences and stochastic volatility. Across these applications, out-of-sample accuracy diagnostics were generally small over relevant state regions, and the learned functions closely matched value function iteration benchmarks where available.
items.citation: Xianhua Peng, Wu Guo, Songyan Wang et al.. Deep Learning for Dynamic Programming with Recursive Utility Using First-order Conditions. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Diagnosing quantum reservoirs at scale based on expressivity and coverage
items.summary: This paper addresses the challenge of evaluating quantum reservoirs for machine learning without incurring the exponential computational costs of full state reconstruction. The authors introduce two complementary diagnostic quantities: a task-independent order-statistics expressivity score that compares only the largest output probabilities against a Haar random baseline, and a task-dependent effective rank of the feature matrix that quantifies how much input-dependent information reaches the classical readout layer.
