The expressivity score is designed to be scalable and hardware-agnostic, with a closed-form correction for depolarizing noise, allowing it to be applied directly on quantum hardware. The framework was validated against established complexity measures, tested under simulated noise and on real quantum hardware, and benchmarked across quantum extreme learning machine and reservoir computing tasks. Together, the two metrics are shown to capture the expressivity hierarchy among reservoir families and to identify when that expressivity translates into practically usable predictive information.
items.citation: Laia Domingo, Oriol Balló-Gimbernat, Fernando Vilariño. Diagnosing quantum reservoirs at scale based on expressivity and coverage. arXiv. 2026

items.title: The Statistical physics of unsaturated soil water: kinetic theory and non commutative pore water dynamics
items.summary: This paper develops a statistical-mechanical framework for describing water movement in unsaturated soil, treating pore-scale occupancy dynamics as the fundamental physical problem rather than relying on empirical macroscopic relationships. The theory operates across three scales, from microscopic inter-pore transfer rates governed by Hagen-Poiseuille flow through a mesoscale master equation to a continuum field equation for the fraction of pores of a given radius that are water-filled at any location and time. A single dimensionless group, the pore-resolved Damköhler number, organizes the full range of behavior and reveals Richards' equation as a quasi-static limiting case, with standard quantities like matric potential and hydraulic conductivity emerging only in that limit rather than being fundamental. Within the same framework, hysteresis is recast as a geometric phase arising from the topology of a forcing bundle rather than from bistability within individual pores, and preferential flow emerges naturally where the Damköhler number exceeds unity, dissolving the traditional dichotomy between these flow regimes into a continuous crossover. The theory requires only geometric properties of the pore network as inputs, in principle obtainable from micro-CT imaging, and claims to need no calibration against macroscopic experimental data.
items.citation: Riccardo Rigon. The Statistical physics of unsaturated soil water: kinetic theory and non commutative pore water dynamics. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Diagnostic value of cystatin C in acute kidney injury among patients with sepsis: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
items.summary: This systematic review and meta-analysis evaluated the diagnostic utility of cystatin C for the early detection of acute kidney injury in patients with sepsis. Researchers systematically retrieved relevant studies from electronic databases, assessed their quality using a standardized diagnostic accuracy tool, and applied a random-effects meta-analysis along with summary receiver operating characteristic curve analysis to pool diagnostic performance estimates. Cystatin C demonstrated good overall diagnostic performance for sepsis-associated acute kidney injury, and subgroup analyses revealed that studies adhering to Sepsis 3.0 criteria showed higher specificity and diagnostic odds ratios with less heterogeneity. Combining cystatin C with other biomarkers further improved diagnostic accuracy. The authors conclude that cystatin C is a promising biomarker for early clinical diagnosis of sepsis-associated acute kidney injury, though heterogeneity across included studies remains a limitation.
items.citation: Cheng L, Zhang B, Hu X et al.. Diagnostic value of cystatin C in acute kidney injury among patients with sepsis: a systematic review and meta-analysis.. Frontiers in medicine. 2026

items.title: Pancreatic Stone Protein as an Emerging Biomarker in Paediatric Nosocomial and Postoperative Sepsis: An Integrative Review of Diagnostic and Prognostic Performance.
items.summary: This narrative review examined the diagnostic and prognostic value of pancreatic stone protein (PSP) as a biomarker for sepsis in paediatric patients, particularly in the context of nosocomial and postoperative infections where distinguishing infectious from sterile inflammation is clinically difficult. The authors conducted a structured literature search across two major databases, screening studies published over a recent multi-year period, and synthesized findings from those meeting inclusion criteria. The review found that PSP levels rise significantly in children with sepsis compared to those with non-infectious systemic inflammation, and elevated PSP was associated with bacteraemia, organ dysfunction, and mortality. PSP demonstrated diagnostic performance comparable to or exceeding that of conventional markers such as C-reactive protein and procalcitonin, including strong sensitivity and specificity in neonatal and other high-risk paediatric populations. The authors conclude that PSP is a promising early biomarker for paediatric sepsis identification and risk stratification, while noting that study variability underscores the need for larger multicentre prospective trials and integration of PSP into multimarker diagnostic approaches.
items.citation: Ramírez Quintana A, Gaman EL, Badea M et al.. Pancreatic Stone Protein as an Emerging Biomarker in Paediatric Nosocomial and Postoperative Sepsis: An Integrative Review of Diagnostic and Prognostic Performance.. International journal of molecular sciences. 2026

items.title: Early diagnostic accuracy of immature platelet fraction for sepsis: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
items.summary: This systematic review and meta-analysis investigated whether the immature platelet fraction, a measure of bone marrow thrombopoietic activity, could serve as an early diagnostic biomarker for sepsis. The authors searched two major biomedical databases and pooled data from studies meeting their inclusion criteria, calculating sensitivity, specificity, and diagnostic odds ratios, with a subgroup analysis focused on adult populations. The meta-analysis found that the immature platelet fraction demonstrated only moderate diagnostic accuracy for sepsis, with pooled sensitivity and specificity both around seventy percent and moderate heterogeneity across the included studies. The authors conclude that the immature platelet fraction may serve as a complementary rather than standalone biomarker for sepsis diagnosis, but caution that the findings are based on a small number of studies with methodological variability. They call for larger, standardized prospective studies before the measure can be confidently incorporated into clinical practice.
items.citation: Wiranata S, Manuaba IBAP, Supadmanaba IGP et al.. Early diagnostic accuracy of immature platelet fraction for sepsis: a systematic review and meta-analysis.. Hematology, transfusion and cell therapy. 2026

items.title: Diagnostic performance of interleukin-27 and C-reactive protein in neonatal sepsis: an updated systematic review and meta-analysis.
items.summary: This systematic review and meta-analysis evaluated and compared the diagnostic accuracy of interleukin-27 (IL-27) and C-reactive protein (CRP) as biomarkers for neonatal sepsis, addressing inconsistencies in the existing literature. Following PRISMA guidelines, the authors searched multiple major databases and pooled data from controlled studies involving neonates, calculating sensitivity, specificity, and area under the summary receiver operating characteristic curve for each biomarker. The analysis found that IL-27 achieved higher pooled sensitivity and specificity than CRP, and its area under the curve was notably superior, indicating stronger overall diagnostic performance. The authors conclude that IL-27 demonstrates greater diagnostic accuracy than CRP for identifying neonatal sepsis and may serve as a more reliable biomarker for early clinical diagnosis.
items.citation: Liu W, Zhang Y, Liu W et al.. Diagnostic performance of interleukin-27 and C-reactive protein in neonatal sepsis: an updated systematic review and meta-analysis.. Frontiers in pediatrics. 2026

items.title: Aligning neonatal sepsis triage diagnostics with WHO target product profiles in low- and middle-income countries.
items.summary: This paper examined the state of diagnostics for neonatal sepsis in low- and middle-income countries, where the condition remains a major driver of infant death and illness despite limited progress in diagnostic development. The authors synthesized evidence across a range of current and emerging diagnostic approaches, including clinical algorithms, host-response biomarkers, haematological parameters, continuous monitoring, and artificial intelligence-based digital platforms, evaluating each for diagnostic performance and operational feasibility in resource-constrained settings. This evidence was assessed against the WHO Target Product Profile for neonatal sepsis, which sets requirements for high sensitivity, suitability for non-hospital settings, and point-of-care applicability. The analysis identified promising technologies, including more accurate host-response biomarkers and affordable tools that could enhance existing predictive algorithms. The authors conclude that integrated diagnostic strategies combining affordable point-of-care testing, routine clinical data, and digital decision support offer the most viable path toward earlier detection, more rational antibiotic use, and reduced neonatal mortality in these settings.
items.citation: Gleeson B, Blumel B, Ibarz Pavon AB et al.. Aligning neonatal sepsis triage diagnostics with WHO target product profiles in low- and middle-income countries.. BMJ global health. 2026

items.title: Calprotectin as a Diagnostic Marker for Lower Respiratory Tract Infection and Sepsis in the Emergency Department.
items.summary: The study investigated whether calprotectin, a neutrophil activation marker measured in plasma, could serve as a useful diagnostic biomarker for sepsis and for identifying the source of infection in patients presenting to an emergency department with suspected sepsis. Researchers prospectively enrolled a large cohort of patients at a university hospital and measured calprotectin levels at admission, comparing its diagnostic performance against established markers such as C-reactive protein. Calprotectin showed only modest ability to distinguish sepsis from non-infectious systemic inflammatory response syndrome and was not an independent predictor when C-reactive protein was included in multivariable analysis. However, calprotectin demonstrated utility in differentiating lower respiratory tract infections from upper respiratory tract infections and urinary tract infections, and adding calprotectin to C-reactive protein significantly improved discriminatory ability for these comparisons. The authors conclude that calprotectin may complement standard clinical assessment in identifying the source of infection in sepsis patients, though further research is needed to confirm its added diagnostic value.
items.citation: Wollmer M, Wessman T, Melander O et al.. Calprotectin as a Diagnostic Marker for Lower Respiratory Tract Infection and Sepsis in the Emergency Department.. Open forum infectious diseases. 2026

items.title: Evolution of Accuracy and Visual-Cognitive Errors in a Decade of Vision-Language AI Models
items.summary: This paper examined how vision-language models have progressed over a decade in describing complex social scenes, addressing limitations of prior evaluations that relied on simple imagery and ignored error type analysis. The researchers introduced a new dataset of images depicting complex social interactions and benchmarks and evaluated nine models spanning from early pre-multimodal systems to recent large language model-based systems, alongside human descriptions, against a gold standard. They assessed five categories of visual-cognitive errors including object detection, recognition, hallucination, scene understanding, and spatial dependence. The findings show that earlier pre-multimodal models performed substantially worse than even the lowest-ranked human descriptions, while recent multimodal large language models achieved accuracy comparable to top human performers and effectively closed the performance gap between simple and complex scenes. Nearly all error types were eliminated by modern models, with the notable exception of spatial dependence errors, while detection, recognition, and hallucination errors were identified as having the greatest influence on overall description accuracy.
items.citation: Shravan Murlidaran, Miguel P. Eckstein. Evolution of Accuracy and Visual-Cognitive Errors in a Decade of Vision-Language AI Models. arXiv. 2026

items.title: VEXAIoT: Autonomous IoT Vulnerability EXploitation using AI Agents
items.summary: This paper investigated whether large language model-based AI agents could autonomously discover and exploit vulnerabilities in Internet of Things systems, an area previously unexplored despite growing interest in AI-driven penetration testing. The authors developed a multi-agent framework called VEXAIoT, which pairs a vulnerability detection agent with an attack execution agent to conduct reconnaissance, plan attack sequences, and carry out exploits against IoT services using standard offensive security tools. The framework was evaluated across ten attack scenarios derived from OWASP IoT vulnerability categories, tested in two controlled environments representing common IoT and networked system weaknesses. The system achieved a very high overall attack success rate across hundreds of executions, with most attacks completing in under two minutes and modest computational token overhead. The results demonstrate that LLM-driven multi-agent systems can effectively automate IoT vulnerability assessment workflows in controlled settings, though the authors note the evaluations were conducted in purpose-built vulnerable environments rather than real-world deployments.
items.citation: Katherine Swinea, Kshitiz Aryal, Lopamudra Praharaj et al.. VEXAIoT: Autonomous IoT Vulnerability EXploitation using AI Agents. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Spatial decomposition of Little Red Dots with JWST/NIRSpec IFU into broad-line red cores and narrow-line blue host galaxies
items.summary: This paper investigates Little Red Dots, a population of compact red sources discovered by JWST that display distinctive V-shaped continua, broad Balmer emission lines, and in some cases Balmer absorption features whose physical origins have been unclear. To test whether these features arise from two distinct physical components — a compact central engine hosting a rapidly growing black hole and a surrounding host galaxy — the researchers used JWST/NIRSpec integral field unit spectroscopy to spatially resolve and map the spectral components across a sample of broad Hα-selected Little Red Dots at redshift approximately five. Using both prism and high-resolution grating observations, they decomposed and mapped the continuum emission, narrow and broad emission lines, and absorption features across each source. They found that blue continuum emission is co-spatial with the narrow emission line region, while the red continuum is concentrated in a compact core co-spatial with the broad Balmer emission and absorption, with spatially resolved equivalent width maps of [OIII] showing a pronounced decrease toward this central core. These results provide spatial evidence that Little Red Dot emission is produced by at least two distinct physical components: a red compact central engine embedded within a blue host galaxy.
items.citation: Yuzo Ishikawa, Anna-Christina Eilers, Rohan P. Naidu et al.. Spatial decomposition of Little Red Dots with JWST/NIRSpec IFU into broad-line red cores and narrow-line blue host galaxies. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Lean-QIT: Towards a Formal Infrastructure for Quantum Information Theory
items.summary: This paper addresses the challenge of formalizing quantum information theory within a machine-checked proof system, noting that existing frameworks lack a reusable operational layer that cleanly separates coding constructs from their information-theoretic characterizations. The authors developed Lean-QIT, a library built in Lean 4 that provides composable, kernel-verified interfaces for quantum states, channels, source and channel codes, finite-block performance criteria, hypothesis testing, one-shot quantities, and asymptotic rate constructions. Using this infrastructure, they formally verified several foundational coding theorems, including Schumacher's quantum source-coding theorem, the Holevo–Schumacher–Westmoreland classical capacity theorem, and the entanglement-assisted classical capacity theorem along with its strong converse. By modularizing achievability, converse, and asymptotic components into reusable building blocks, the library enables future formal proofs in quantum information theory to be composed from verified primitives. The authors also position Lean-QIT as a foundation for AI-assisted formalization and automated proof search in quantum information and computation.
items.citation: Chengkai Zhu, Ziao Tang, Guocheng Zhen et al.. Lean-QIT: Towards a Formal Infrastructure for Quantum Information Theory. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Toward Real-Time Sentence-Level Sign Language Translation
items.summary: This paper investigates sentence-level sign language translation with an explicit focus on real-time deployment rather than advancing translation model architecture. The researchers fine-tuned a SHuBERT-ByT5 translation stack on a subset of the How2Sign dataset using QLoRA, keeping the SHuBERT component frozen to manage computational constraints. The system achieved competitive translation quality as measured by BLEU and BLEURT scores on test data. A central contribution is a hardware-aware streaming architecture that separates lightweight client-side functions—such as camera capture and text display on a Raspberry Pi—from compute-intensive perception and translation handled by a backend server, making the system accessible from various client devices. Through optimizations including chunked ingestion, parallelized perception, and a sentence-boundary state machine, the system substantially reduced both mean and high-percentile response latencies, demonstrating a viable path toward practical real-time sign language translation.
items.citation: Thanh-Hoang Nguyen Doan. Toward Real-Time Sentence-Level Sign Language Translation. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Searching for signatures of fuzzy dark matter in cosmic filament profiles
items.summary: This paper investigates whether fuzzy dark matter, composed of ultralight particles whose de Broglie wavelengths span kiloparsec to megaparsec scales, leaves detectable interference fringe signatures in the large-scale structure of the cosmic web. The authors developed a methodology to search for periodicities in the radial distribution of galaxies around cosmic filaments, modelling any such signal as a cosine-wave density component with a characteristic wavelength and amplitude. Applying this approach to a large sample of filaments drawn from the Sloan Digital Sky Survey Main Galaxy Sample, they found that the observations are broadly consistent with the absence of any periodic signal across a wide range of model parameters. However, they were able to place constraints on the allowed amplitude of periodic density variations, excluding sufficiently large amplitudes at a given wavelength in the sub-megaparsec regime. The study demonstrates the viability of using galaxy distributions around cosmic filaments as a probe of fuzzy dark matter models, laying groundwork for stronger constraints with future surveys.
items.citation: Callum J. O'Kane, Alfonso Aragón-Salamanca, Ulrike Kuchner et al.. Searching for signatures of fuzzy dark matter in cosmic filament profiles. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Novel Rapid Phenotypic Susceptibility Testing Techniques That Enhance Antimicrobial Stewardship and Clinical Decision-Making.
items.summary: This review paper examined emerging rapid phenotypic antimicrobial susceptibility testing technologies and their role in addressing the global challenge of antimicrobial resistance. The authors surveyed the fundamental principles of phenotypic testing approaches, with particular attention to growth-based methods, and synthesized available evidence on their clinical and operational implications. The review found that rapid phenotypic susceptibility testing can meaningfully reduce the time to effective therapy compared to conventional methods, which is especially critical in severe infections such as sepsis where treatment delays worsen patient outcomes. However, the authors also identified barriers to widespread implementation, including inconsistent test performance, absence of standardized protocols, and uncertainties around cost-effectiveness. Overall, the paper concludes that rapid phenotypic testing represents an important advancement in bridging diagnostic gaps and supporting antimicrobial stewardship programs.
items.citation: Glover JS, Humphries R. Novel Rapid Phenotypic Susceptibility Testing Techniques That Enhance Antimicrobial Stewardship and Clinical Decision-Making.. Clinical chemistry. 2026

items.title: Rapid Detection of Pathogens and Resistance Genes in Positive Blood Cultures Using BioFire<sup>®</sup> FilmArray BCID2.
items.summary: This study evaluated the diagnostic performance of a molecular multiplex panel for rapidly identifying pathogens and antimicrobial resistance genes directly from positive blood cultures. Researchers compared the panel's results against conventional culture methods, mass spectrometry-based identification, and automated susceptibility testing across a series of positive blood culture samples from a university hospital, with polymerase chain reaction used to confirm detected resistance genes. The panel successfully identified at least one microorganism in the large majority of samples, showing strong concordance with culture-based methods overall, with complete agreement for certain key pathogens and slightly lower but acceptable agreement for others. Resistance gene detection correlated well with phenotypic susceptibility results, and the panel identified a notable proportion of samples as polymicrobial. The authors conclude that the panel is a reliable and rapid complement to conventional diagnostics for bloodstream infections, though its interpretation requires clinical context, particularly for organisms that may represent contamination.
items.citation: Çinar C, Çayci YT, Atakcan Z et al.. Rapid Detection of Pathogens and Resistance Genes in Positive Blood Cultures Using BioFire<sup>®</sup> FilmArray BCID2.. Polish journal of microbiology. 2026

items.title: When Faster Diagnosis Is Not Enough: Bringing Diagnostic Stewardship into Antimicrobial Stewardship.
items.citation: Mezzadri L, Bonfanti P. When Faster Diagnosis Is Not Enough: Bringing Diagnostic Stewardship into Antimicrobial Stewardship.. Le infezioni in medicina. 2026

items.title: Multiplex mismatch amplification mutation polymerase chain reaction assay for the rapid detection of antimicrobial-resistant determinants in Cutibacterium acnes.
items.summary: This study addressed the clinical problem of antimicrobial resistance in *Cutibacterium acnes*, a bacterium central to the pathogenesis of acne vulgaris, by developing a faster and more comprehensive detection method. The researchers designed a multiplex polymerase chain reaction assay combining mismatch amplification mutation assay principles to simultaneously detect point mutations in ribosomal RNA genes and a DNA gyrase gene alongside acquired resistance genes in clinical isolates. The optimized protocol completed detection in just over one hour, substantially faster than conventional susceptibility testing methods. When validated against a large collection of clinical isolates, the assay achieved perfect sensitivity and specificity for identifying the major resistance determinants. These results support the assay's utility as a rapid diagnostic tool to guide appropriate antimicrobial treatment decisions for acne and to help curb the spread of resistant strains.
items.citation: Yoshihara K, Seyama S, Hayashi N et al.. Multiplex mismatch amplification mutation polymerase chain reaction assay for the rapid detection of antimicrobial-resistant determinants in Cutibacterium acnes.. Diagnostic microbiology and infectious disease. 2026

items.title: A review of bloodstream infections-pathogens, pathogenesis, diagnostic strategies, treatment methods-challenges and future aspects.
items.summary: This review examined bloodstream infections, which occur when pathogenic microorganisms enter the circulatory system and can progress to sepsis, septic shock, multi-organ failure, and death, representing a major global healthcare burden. The authors conducted a comprehensive literature review of peer-reviewed publications, clinical guidelines, and surveillance reports spanning from 2010 to mid-2026, synthesizing evidence on bacterial, fungal, viral, and parasitic pathogens, their virulence mechanisms, immune evasion strategies, antimicrobial resistance, and both established and emerging diagnostic and therapeutic approaches. Bacterial pathogens were found to be the predominant cause of bloodstream infections, with Candida species identified as the leading fungal agents, while increasing antimicrobial resistance and diagnostic delays continue to worsen clinical outcomes. Advances in molecular diagnostics, metagenomic sequencing, biomarker-guided testing, and artificial intelligence-assisted analyses have meaningfully improved rapid pathogen detection and treatment decision-making. The authors conclude that integrating next-generation diagnostics, genomic surveillance, and precision medicine alongside robust antimicrobial stewardship programs holds considerable promise for enabling faster, individualized therapeutic interventions and ultimately reducing the high mortality associated with these infections.
items.venue: European journal of clinical microbiology & infectious diseases : official publication of the European Society of Clinical Microbiology
items.citation: Kumar A, Kumar A, Tyagi A et al.. A review of bloodstream infections-pathogens, pathogenesis, diagnostic strategies, treatment methods-challenges and future aspects.. European journal of clinical microbiology & infectious diseases : official publication of the European Society of Clinical Microbiology. 2026

items.title: Molecular insights into antimicrobial resistance in human bacterial pathogens: mechanisms, resistance genes, and translational diagnostic applications.
items.summary: This review examines the molecular basis of antimicrobial resistance in human bacterial pathogens, focusing on resistance genes and the mobile genetic elements responsible for their rapid dissemination across bacterial populations. The authors critically evaluate a range of molecular diagnostic platforms, comparing targeted amplification approaches, whole-genome sequencing, CRISPR-based technologies, and metagenomics in terms of their performance, limitations, and potential for point-of-care use. The review also considers how these diagnostic tools can be integrated into antimicrobial stewardship programmes and real-time surveillance efforts, highlighting the persistent gap between genomic data generation and actionable clinical decision-making. Artificial intelligence and machine learning are identified as promising approaches for predicting resistance phenotypes from genomic data and guiding personalized antibiotic therapy, though broad clinical implementation remains nascent. The authors conclude that the broader shift from phenotypic to genotypic diagnostic strategies could substantially improve surveillance and treatment outcomes, contingent on overcoming barriers related to infrastructure, standardization, and equitable access.
items.citation: Hossen N, Mascellino MT. Molecular insights into antimicrobial resistance in human bacterial pathogens: mechanisms, resistance genes, and translational diagnostic applications.. Frontiers in microbiology. 2026

items.title: Scalable Visual Pretraining for Language Intelligence
items.summary: This paper challenges the prevailing assumption that large language models should be pretrained exclusively on text, arguing that visually rich documents contain information in figures, equations, and layouts that text conversion discards. The authors conducted a systematic study of unsupervised visual pretraining paradigms that train directly on visual documents without extracting text. Testing across multiple model backbones and benchmarks, they found that visual pretraining on the same underlying corpora consistently outperforms text-only pretraining. The results suggest that treating documents as visual inputs rather than text representations offers a scalable and efficient pathway to improved language model intelligence.
items.citation: Yiming Zhang, Zhonghan Zhao, Wenwei Zhang et al.. Scalable Visual Pretraining for Language Intelligence. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Regional Economic Impacts of the Just Energy Transition: Lessons for Coal Regions
items.summary: The paper examined the regional economic consequences of coal phase-outs as part of the broader energy transition, focusing on the structural vulnerabilities of communities dependent on coal for employment and fiscal revenue. Using panel data on European Union regions over more than two decades, the researchers applied fixed effects models with clustered errors to isolate the economic trajectory of coal-dependent regions. They found that coal regions consistently carry a higher unemployment rate compared to non-coal regions while simultaneously exhibiting faster per-capita GDP growth, a pattern interpreted as a hollowing-out dynamic in which population outmigration inflates output per person even as labor market conditions deteriorate. Spatial analysis revealed strong geographic clustering of these effects, pointing to the value of coordinated, place-based transition policies rather than relying on market adjustment alone. Drawing on South Korea's accelerated coal phase-out and the experience of one of its major coal-power regions, the paper argues that proactive national support must be mobilized before plant closures occur to enable meaningful regional adaptation.
items.citation: Imke Rhoden, Jae-Hyuck Lee. Regional Economic Impacts of the Just Energy Transition: Lessons for Coal Regions. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Wan-Dancer: A Hierarchical Framework for Minute-scale Coherent Music-to-Dance Generation
items.summary: This paper addresses the challenge of generating long-duration, high-quality dance videos synchronized to music, a task where existing diffusion-based methods typically fail beyond around twenty seconds due to temporal drift, identity inconsistency, and repetitive motion. The authors propose a hierarchical framework that separates the generation process into global keyframe planning and local temporal refinement, using the full musical track to maintain long-range coherence. Key technical contributions include time-mapped rotary position embeddings for dynamic frame rate adaptation, an optical-flow-based loss to improve motion continuity, and a motion-speed control mechanism to preserve detail during fast movements. The framework is conditioned on both audio and text prompts and demonstrated across multiple distinct dance genres. The authors report that their approach surpasses conventional duration limits, producing stable high-definition videos exceeding one minute and achieving a new state of the art in coherent long-form music-to-dance synthesis.
items.citation: Mingyang Huang, Peng Zhang, Li Hu et al.. Wan-Dancer: A Hierarchical Framework for Minute-scale Coherent Music-to-Dance Generation. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Thermalization in a Height-Conserving Quantum Dimer Model
items.summary: This paper investigates whether strongly constrained quantum systems can thermalize within individual sectors of a fragmented Hilbert space, a question relevant to both condensed matter physics and lattice gauge theory. The authors studied the height-conserving quantum dimer model on the square lattice, where local plaquette flip moves preserve an emergent height field, leading to extensive conservation laws and Hilbert space fragmentation. By carefully resolving winding numbers, sublattice heights, and lattice momenta, they isolated the dominant connected Krylov components within fragmented sectors and examined their spectral statistics, entanglement entropy, and connectivity. They found a striking dissociation between two standard chaos diagnostics: level-spacing statistics varied across momentum sectors from near-Poisson to Wigner-Dyson distributions, yet eigenstate entanglement entropy consistently collapsed onto a dome-shaped curve characteristic of eigenstate thermalization, with only rare low-entanglement outliers. This demonstrates that strong kinematic constraints can decouple spectral correlations from eigenstate thermalization, revealing a novel form of constrained quantum chaos in which the two signatures need not coincide.
items.citation: Junsheng Feng, Jie Ren, Zheng Yan. Thermalization in a Height-Conserving Quantum Dimer Model. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Disorder-Induced Localization of Molecular Polaritons Despite Spectroscopic Strong Coupling
items.summary: This paper investigates whether molecular polaritons—hybrid light–matter quasiparticles formed when molecules couple strongly to optical cavities—remain genuinely delocalized across many molecules under realistic experimental conditions. Using a disordered Tavis–Cummings model, the authors theoretically examine how static energetic disorder and finite cavity and molecular linewidths affect the collective molecular character of electronic polaritons. They quantify delocalization through several measures including a molecular participation ratio, a density-matrix-based coherence metric, and an energy-resolved autocorrelation function. Their central finding is that energetic disorder mixes polariton states with the so-called dark-state manifold, causing the extended molecular component to collapse even when polariton peaks remain clearly visible in spectra—a condition that conventional spectroscopic strong coupling criteria would incorrectly identify as collectively delocalized. The analysis yields quantitative delocalization boundaries showing that the collective Rabi splitting must substantially exceed both the disorder width and the cavity–molecule linewidth mismatch, providing stricter and more physically meaningful criteria for assessing true polariton delocalization in realistic molecular systems.
items.citation: Amitav Sahu, Tönu Pullerits. Disorder-Induced Localization of Molecular Polaritons Despite Spectroscopic Strong Coupling. arXiv. 2026

items.title: CORAL-AUV: CFD Oriented Reinforcement Learning for Autonomous Underwater Vehicles
items.summary: This paper addresses the challenge of precise control and positioning of autonomous underwater vehicles, where traditional methods struggle with changing configurations and environmental conditions. The researchers developed a reinforcement learning framework that incorporates surrogate drag models trained on computational fluid dynamics data, enabling high-fidelity drag physics to be used during policy training without the prohibitive computational cost of running CFD simulations directly within the learning pipeline. The trained policies were evaluated in both controlled tank environments and real-world field deployments in a zero-shot transfer setting, meaning no additional fine-tuning was performed after simulation-based training. Compared to controllers using simplified physics models, the CFD-informed approach achieved substantially lower energy consumption, faster waypoint traversal, and reduced positioning error. Crucially, when domain randomization was applied to account for perturbed deployment parameters, only the CFD-based surrogate model policy successfully transferred to the real vehicle, demonstrating its superior robustness over conventional simulation approaches.
items.citation: Steven Roche, Milo Van Mooy, Nathan McGuire et al.. CORAL-AUV: CFD Oriented Reinforcement Learning for Autonomous Underwater Vehicles. arXiv. 2026

items.title: A quantitative rapid test for urine creatinine <i>via</i> Fenton's reaction and a self-driven microfluidic device.
items.summary: This paper developed a rapid, quantitative test for measuring creatinine in urine, targeting its dual role as a kidney disease biomarker and a normalizing factor for other urinary markers. The approach exploits creatinine's ability to inhibit hydroxyl radicals generated by Fenton's reaction, which are otherwise used to cleave an oligonucleotide linker connecting magnetic and polystyrene microparticles. By measuring the length of polystyrene microparticle accumulation following magnetic separation on a self-driven microfluidic device, the creatinine concentration is converted into a visible readout. The assay demonstrated a low detection limit, broad pH tolerance, and resistance to interfering substances, and when validated against a standard clinical method using patient urine samples, it showed strong concordance, indicating clinical suitability.
items.citation: Hartanto H, Li J, Szeto CC et al.. A quantitative rapid test for urine creatinine <i>via</i> Fenton's reaction and a self-driven microfluidic device.. Lab on a chip. 2026

items.title: Fabrication of photoresist SU-8 pyrolyzed carbon electrodes and their integration in a microfluidic hematocrit detection system
items.summary: This paper investigated the fabrication of pyrolytic carbon microelectrodes derived from the photoresist SU-8 and their application in microfluidic hematocrit detection. The researchers developed a three-step pyrolysis protocol to overcome adhesion, geometric distortion, and limited surface area problems associated with conventional two-step approaches, producing low-aspect-ratio planar electrodes with both micro- and millimeter-scale features. The resulting electrodes were integrated into a polydimethylsiloxane-based microfluidic device, where hematocrit was determined by measuring electrical current responses from red blood cells suspended in phosphate-buffered saline. Surface characterization revealed that the pyrolytic carbon electrodes exhibited substantially greater roughness and thickness compared to platinum electrodes, yielding increased electroactive surface area that reduced charge-transfer and concentration overpotentials. The device achieved precision and accuracy for hematocrit determination comparable to platinum-based systems, demonstrating that this low-cost, scalable fabrication approach can serve as a viable alternative to noble metal electrodes in diagnostic microdevices.
items.citation: Lee HY, Rogers J, Kendrick C et al.. Fabrication of photoresist SU-8 pyrolyzed carbon electrodes and their integration in a microfluidic hematocrit detection system. 2026

items.title: Microfluidic and MEMS-Based Biosensing Platforms for Fungal Respiratory Infections in Immunocompromised Patients: Toward Rapid, Specific, and Minimally Invasive Diagnosis.
items.summary: This review examined the current landscape and future potential of microfluidic and microelectromechanical systems-based biosensing platforms for diagnosing invasive fungal respiratory infections in immunocompromised patients. The paper surveyed a range of technologies, including on-chip nucleic acid amplification, antigen immunodetection, CRISPR-enhanced sensing, host response profiling, detection of exhaled volatile organic compounds, and mechanical biosensing of fungal biomarkers. These approaches were evaluated in the context of clinical settings such as intensive care, hematology-oncology, and transplant medicine, where conventional diagnostic methods suffer from poor sensitivity, limited sample accessibility, and slow turnaround. The authors found that while these platforms offer substantial promise for rapid, minimally invasive, and highly specific detection, significant barriers remain, including insufficient multicenter validation, biofouling from complex biological matrices, and a lack of standardized biomarker panels. They conclude that future integration of artificial intelligence-driven multianalyte interpretation and fully cartridge-based near-patient systems could enable earlier and more precise fungal diagnoses, ultimately improving outcomes in vulnerable populations.
items.citation: Georgakopoulou VE, Pitiriga VC. Microfluidic and MEMS-Based Biosensing Platforms for Fungal Respiratory Infections in Immunocompromised Patients: Toward Rapid, Specific, and Minimally Invasive Diagnosis.. Biosensors. 2026

items.title: Silicon-Germanium Heterostructures with Enhanced Valley Splitting for Spin Qubits
items.summary: The paper addresses the challenge of achieving sufficiently large valley splittings in silicon-germanium heterostructures, which is critical for reliably initializing, operating, and reading out silicon spin qubits. The authors developed a device-level optimization strategy using device-scale simulations that incorporate atomistic alloy disorder through a one-dimensional tight-binding theory, complemented by atomistic density functional theory calculations. Their proposed heterostructure design combines an unconventional combination of a narrow quantum well, a small germanium spike, and a pure-germanium cap to engineer enhanced valley splittings. This approach yields valley splittings far exceeding those reported in nearly all prior theoretical work, while also producing a tight statistical distribution across different disorder configurations—an important feature for reproducible performance across many qubits. The authors argue that their findings provide a viable path toward scalable silicon-germanium quantum processors in which valley splitting is no longer a fundamental obstacle.
items.citation: David W. Kanaar, Efrain Martinez, Peihong Zhang et al.. Silicon-Germanium Heterostructures with Enhanced Valley Splitting for Spin Qubits. arXiv. 2026

items.title: GHz-rate all-fiber active polarization state analyzer for quantum protocols
items.summary: This paper presents a high-speed optical instrument designed to perform polarization state measurements at gigahertz rates for use in quantum communication protocols. The researchers built an all-fiber polarization state analyzer using a fiber Sagnac interferometer with a lithium niobate electro-optic phase modulator, constructed entirely from off-the-shelf fiber-optic components, enabling trial-by-trial reconfiguration of the measurement basis. To validate the system, they conducted a CHSH Bell test at a nominal repetition rate of one gigahertz, achieving high polarization visibility and a clear violation of the Bell inequality that certifies entanglement at an exceptionally high rate. The system also demonstrated strong long-term stability, maintaining the Bell inequality violation for several hours without any realignment. The authors conclude that this platform offers a scalable and versatile approach for quantum protocols requiring rapid, reconfigurable polarization measurements, such as device-independent quantum key distribution and quantum teleportation with active feed-forward.
items.citation: Andrea Pompermaier, Kannan Vijayadharan, Costantino Agnesi et al.. GHz-rate all-fiber active polarization state analyzer for quantum protocols. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Thermalization in a Height-Conserving Quantum Dimer Model
items.summary: This paper investigates whether strongly constrained quantum systems can thermalize within individual sectors of a fragmented Hilbert space, after accounting for the many disconnected frozen states that such constraints produce. The authors study the height-conserving quantum dimer model on the square lattice, where local plaquette flip moves conserve an emergent height field, leading to extensive conservation laws and Hilbert space fragmentation. By carefully resolving winding numbers, sublattice heights, and lattice momentum, they isolate the dominant connected Krylov component within each fragmented sector and examine spectral statistics, entanglement entropy, and connectivity. They find a striking decoupling between two standard chaos diagnostics: level-spacing statistics vary across momentum sectors from near-Poisson to Wigner-Dyson distributions, yet eigenstate entanglement entropy consistently collapses onto a narrow dome-shaped curve indicative of eigenstate thermalization, with only rare low-entanglement outliers in select sectors. This demonstrates that strong kinematic constraints can produce a regime of constrained quantum chaos in which spectral correlations and eigenstate thermalization hypothesis signatures need not share the same universal behavior.
items.citation: Junsheng Feng, Jie Ren, Zheng Yan. Thermalization in a Height-Conserving Quantum Dimer Model. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Higher-Order Programs with Indefinite Causal Orders: a Linear Approach to Coherent Control of Quantum Processes
items.summary: This paper addresses a gap in quantum programming languages concerning processes with indefinite causal orders, where the sequence in which quantum operations are applied is placed in quantum superposition, offering computational advantages that existing languages fail to fully capture. The authors introduce a higher-order quantum functional language with a linear type system designed to handle general quantum computation—including measurement and arbitrary quantum channels—rather than being restricted to unitary operations or treating coherent control nonlinearly. The language is equipped with both a small-step operational semantics, which synchronizes measurement outcomes across superposed branches using device references and a memory function, and a denotational semantics based on completely positive maps. To ensure physical meaningfulness beyond what linearity alone guarantees, the authors impose an additional typing discipline grounded in the causal category of completely positive maps, proving that every well-typed program denotes a physically valid process in a property checkable statically and efficiently. The language is shown to express every quantum channel at first order and a large subclass of quantum circuits with quantum control at second order, including the quantum switch, and is demonstrated to be extensible to a nonlinear setting with recursion.
items.citation: Kathleen Barsse, Romain Péchoux, Simon Perdrix. Higher-Order Programs with Indefinite Causal Orders: a Linear Approach to Coherent Control of Quantum Processes. arXiv. 2026

items.title: SAGEAgent: A Self-Evolving Agent for Cost-Aware Modality Acquisition in Multimodal Survival Prediction
items.summary: This paper investigates whether accurate cancer survival prediction requires collecting the full complement of diagnostic modalities for every patient, or whether a selective, patient-specific acquisition strategy can achieve comparable performance at lower cost. The authors formulate modality acquisition as a sequential decision problem, reflecting the clinically realistic ordering of diagnostic procedures from low-burden intake data to invasive genomic profiling. They propose SAGEAgent, a large language model-based agent equipped with clinical translation tools, an episodic memory for retrieving similar past cases, and a semantic memory that accumulates reusable decision patterns, allowing it to learn and refine acquisition strategies over time. Experiments on a glioma cohort integrating multiple public datasets and spanning four diagnostic modalities show that SAGEAgent matches competitive survival prediction accuracy while substantially reducing the average diagnostic burden compared to approaches that acquire all modalities. The work demonstrates that an experience-driven agent can actively reason about when additional diagnostic information is clinically justified rather than passively accommodating missing data.
items.citation: Chongyu Qu, Can Cui, Zhengyi Lu et al.. SAGEAgent: A Self-Evolving Agent for Cost-Aware Modality Acquisition in Multimodal Survival Prediction. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Seeing is Free, Speaking is Not: Uncovering the True Energy Bottleneck in Edge VLM Inference
items.summary: This paper investigated where energy is actually consumed during vision-language model inference on edge hardware, challenging the prevailing assumption that visual processing represents the dominant computational cost. The researchers conducted systematic energy profiling across multiple models, architecture families, input resolutions, and two edge hardware platforms, measuring power draw and inference time under varying conditions. They found that average inference power remains essentially constant regardless of input resolution, image complexity, or prompt type, meaning all energy variation stems from differences in inference time rather than power consumption. The critical bottleneck they identified is output token generation, which costs an order of magnitude more time per token than input processing due to the asymmetry between prefill and decode phases, making output length the dominant driver of both latency and energy. Consequently, they demonstrate that visual token pruning approaches can save only a small fraction of total energy at best, while controlling output length can reduce energy consumption by the vast majority, with this effect growing stronger at larger model scales.
items.citation: Junfei Zhan, Haoxun Shen, Mingang Guo et al.. Seeing is Free, Speaking is Not: Uncovering the True Energy Bottleneck in Edge VLM Inference. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Multiple cross displacement amplification coupled with a lateral flow biosensor for ultra-rapid and highly sensitive detection of <i>Escherichia coli</i> in neonatal sepsis.
items.summary: The paper studied rapid detection of *Escherichia coli* as a causative pathogen in neonatal sepsis, where conventional culture methods are slow and insufficiently sensitive. The researchers developed a multiple cross-displacement amplification assay coupled with a lateral flow biosensor targeting the *uidA* gene, using a set of ten primers optimized to run at a fixed temperature for forty minutes. The assay achieved femtogram-level sensitivity with no cross-reactivity against non-*E. coli* strains, and the complete workflow from nucleic acid extraction to visual readout was completed within approximately one hour. Clinical validation using blood and cerebrospinal fluid specimens from neonates with suspected sepsis showed complete agreement with quantitative PCR and substantially higher positivity rates than conventional culture. The authors conclude that this method offers a rapid, sensitive, and operationally simple approach well suited to point-of-care and resource-limited settings for early diagnosis of neonatal *E. coli* infection.
items.citation: Xiao R, Zou P, Zhou J et al.. Multiple cross displacement amplification coupled with a lateral flow biosensor for ultra-rapid and highly sensitive detection of <i>Escherichia coli</i> in neonatal sepsis.. Microbiology spectrum. 2026

items.title: Simultaneous Dual-Gene Detection of <i>Escherichia coli</i> O157:H7 Based on a CRISPR/Cas13-Mediated Biosensor.
items.summary: This study addressed the need for rapid, accurate detection of *Escherichia coli* O157:H7, a foodborne pathogen responsible for significant public health risks. The researchers developed a one-pot CRISPR/Cas13-based biosensor that simultaneously targets two key genetic markers characteristic of this serotype, exploiting the distinct collateral cleavage preferences of two different Cas13 proteins to create dual fluorescence channels. Target amplification, transcription, and CRISPR-mediated detection were all integrated into a single reaction tube, removing the requirement for complex instrumentation. The biosensor achieved high sensitivity at a low detection limit using recombinase polymerase amplification, and its performance in analyzing fecal samples from a mouse infection model was consistent with standard PCR results. The approach offers improved serotype discrimination and specificity compared to conventional CRISPR diagnostics, presenting a simple, rapid, and field-deployable platform suitable for food safety monitoring, clinical diagnostics, and infectious disease surveillance.
items.citation: He Y, Lin X, Zhang X et al.. Simultaneous Dual-Gene Detection of <i>Escherichia coli</i> O157:H7 Based on a CRISPR/Cas13-Mediated Biosensor.. JACS Au. 2026

items.title: Target-to-signal conversion and spatial enrichment cascade boost CRISPR/Cas12a biosensing for trace-level pathogen detection.
items.summary: This study developed a biosensor for detecting *Salmonella typhimurium*, a major cause of foodborne illness, by integrating DNAzyme-catalyzed signal amplification with CRISPR/Cas12a technology. The approach works by using DNAzymes to generate single-stranded DNA that triggers hybridization chain reaction (HCR) amplification across three hairpin structures, creating branched DNA nanowires bearing PAM sequences that are subsequently cleaved by CRISPR/Cas12a to restore a quenched fluorescence signal. This cascade of target-to-signal conversion and spatial enrichment achieved an extremely low detection limit with a broad linear detection range, enabling trace-level pathogen quantification. The biosensor demonstrated rapid response, straightforward operation, and high sensitivity under optimized conditions. The authors also propose that the platform is adaptable for detecting other foodborne pathogens by substituting the recognition module and primer sequences.
items.venue: Analytical methods : advancing methods and applications
items.citation: Liu Y, Yue X, Li B et al.. Target-to-signal conversion and spatial enrichment cascade boost CRISPR/Cas12a biosensing for trace-level pathogen detection.. Analytical methods : advancing methods and applications. 2026

items.title: Graphene foam biosensors for molecular diagnostics of antimicrobial resistance and emerging pathogen threats.
items.summary: This paper developed an electrochemical biosensor designed for rapid, point-of-care detection of antimicrobial resistance genes and emerging pathogens. The researchers constructed disposable graphene foam electrodes functionalized with pyrene-modified peptide nucleic acid probes, using π-π stacking interactions to immobilize the probes without chemically altering the electrode surface. A key innovation was a heat-assisted functionalization protocol that reduced preparation time dramatically while maintaining sensor performance. The biosensor achieved amplification-free and label-free detection of a colistin resistance gene as well as a carbapenem resistance gene simply by swapping the probe sequence, demonstrating straightforward retargetability. The platform showed strong selectivity against non-complementary sequences, reproducible performance across independently fabricated devices, and detection limits in the picomolar range for bacterial plasmid samples, supporting its potential for scalable decentralized diagnostics.
items.citation: Wang KY, Schulze H, Lozano Sanchez P et al.. Graphene foam biosensors for molecular diagnostics of antimicrobial resistance and emerging pathogen threats.. Biosensors & bioelectronics. 2026

items.title: Advanced biosensing strategies for high-risk foodborne pathogens: a comprehensive review of <i>Salmonella</i> and <i>Listeria monocytogenes</i>.
items.summary: This review examines advances in biosensor technology specifically targeting two major foodborne pathogens, *Salmonella* spp. and *Listeria monocytogenes*, which are among the leading causes of foodborne illness and mortality globally. Rather than broadly surveying biosensing across many pathogens, the authors provide a pathogen-specific comparison of bioreceptor development and transducer choices for these two organisms. A particular focus is placed on how artificial intelligence tools can accelerate biosensor design, optimization, and detection decision-making for these pathogens, which the authors claim represents a novel contribution to the literature. The review also critically evaluates which biosensing strategies hold genuine potential for scalability, portability, and practical deployment in food industry settings, addressing the persistent gap between laboratory research and real-world implementation. Taken together, the review is framed as a practical roadmap intended to guide future biosensor research toward field-ready, industry-relevant solutions for these high-priority pathogens.
items.citation: Gumush SE, Al-Attar R, Sandbecker E et al.. Advanced biosensing strategies for high-risk foodborne pathogens: a comprehensive review of <i>Salmonella</i> and <i>Listeria monocytogenes</i>.. Food chemistry. Molecular sciences. 2026

items.title: Deep learning integrated plasmonic electrochemical sensing for fast and accurate pathogen detection.
items.summary: This paper investigated the integration of plasmonic electrochemical biosensing with deep learning algorithms to achieve rapid and accurate detection of bacterial pathogens. The researchers developed a nanostructured biosensor based on gold-nanoparticle graphene oxide hybrid nanocomposites that exploit localized surface plasmon resonance to enhance charge-transfer kinetics and electrochemical response, targeting Escherichia coli, Salmonella typhimurium, and Staphylococcus aureus. Detection was achieved using very small sample volumes in under ten minutes, with extremely low limits of detection for all three pathogens. Raw voltammetric signals were processed through a combined MobileNet-Transformer and Gated Recurrent Unit deep neural network pipeline, where the MobileNet-Transformer extracted spectral features and the GRU component suppressed temporal noise. The integrated system outperformed baseline convolutional and recurrent neural network models in classification accuracy and discriminative performance, demonstrating a scalable approach to AI-assisted biosensing suitable for food safety, environmental monitoring, and point-of-care diagnostics on portable, low-cost hardware.
items.citation: Mansour S, Negm N, Alhashmi AA et al.. Deep learning integrated plasmonic electrochemical sensing for fast and accurate pathogen detection.. Scientific reports. 2026

items.title: PHINN-EEG: Topological Time-Series Analysis of Dream-State EEG -- Dynamic Betti Curves for Dream Content Classification and Topology-Conditioned Neural Signal Synthesis
items.summary: This paper introduces a topological machine learning framework called PHINN-EEG for analyzing EEG signals recorded during dreaming, addressing limitations of conventional approaches that rely on power spectral density and statistical moment features. The method applies sliding-window Takens delay embeddings and Vietoris-Rips filtrations to multichannel EEG data to extract Dynamic Betti Curves, which capture the geometric and topological structure of neural activity rather than its spectral energy content. These topological features are combined with topology-conditioned flow matching for dream-state EEG synthesis, and the framework also proposes candidate Betti transition archetypes as hypothetical links between topological patterns and phenomenological dream report categories. The authors analytically project that their approach will outperform existing spectral and statistical benchmarks on a large open-access dream database, though these performance claims are presented as targets pending empirical validation rather than confirmed experimental results. The work is framed as a conceptual paradigm shift from energy-based to phase-space geometry-based neural signal analysis, with proposed applications in brain-computer interface dream monitoring.
items.citation: Ren Takahashi, Emre Yusuf, Jayabrata Bhaduri. PHINN-EEG: Topological Time-Series Analysis of Dream-State EEG -- Dynamic Betti Curves for Dream Content Classification and Topology-Conditioned Neural Signal Synthesis. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Impact of Benign Connectivity Variations on Intrusion Detection for Encrypted OPC UA Traffic in Industrial Private 5G Networks
items.summary: This paper examined how machine learning-based intrusion detection systems behave when monitoring encrypted industrial communications under realistic private 5G network conditions, specifically focusing on OPC UA traffic. The researchers investigated whether normal, benign connectivity variations in these networks could adversely affect detection system performance. Their experiments revealed that legitimate connectivity events cause a noticeable increase in false positive alerts even when no actual attacks are occurring, and that elevated anomaly scores from the detection systems frequently coincide with control-plane activity associated with these events. The findings underscore that control-plane context must be taken into account when interpreting intrusion detection outputs in industrial private 5G deployments to avoid misclassifying normal network behavior as malicious.
items.citation: Song Son Ha, Florian Foerster, Henry Beuster et al.. Impact of Benign Connectivity Variations on Intrusion Detection for Encrypted OPC UA Traffic in Industrial Private 5G Networks. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Identifying and characterizing extragalactic circum-CBC exoplanets with future gravitational-wave detectors
items.summary: This paper investigates whether exoplanets orbiting compact binary coalescences outside our galaxy could be detected using future gravitational wave observatories. The approach relies on extracting information about the centre-of-mass motion of a compact binary system — such as a binary neutron star or binary black hole — as it wobbles around the barycentre it shares with an orbiting planet, with this motion leaving a characteristic imprint on the emitted gravitational wave signal. The authors evaluated the sensitivity of next-generation detectors to this effect across a range of planetary orbital parameters, including both circular and eccentric orbits. They found that for a substantial portion of the parameter space considered, future detectors could not only identify the presence of such a circum-compact-binary exoplanet but also recover its mass to within an order-unity factor at meaningful statistical confidence, albeit with a degeneracy from the unknown orbital inclination. This work constitutes a proof-of-principle demonstration that gravitational wave observations may open an entirely new channel for discovering the first extragalactic exoplanets.
items.citation: Avinash Tiwari, Aditya Vijaykumar, Shasvath J. Kapadia et al.. Identifying and characterizing extragalactic circum-CBC exoplanets with future gravitational-wave detectors. arXiv. 2026

items.title: HD 148797: A bright F-type star with two moderate-period low-density sub-Jovian planets. Compact multi-planet architectures are common in the Neptunian savanna
items.summary: The paper reports the confirmation and characterization of two transiting sub-Jovian planets orbiting the bright F-type star HD 148797, using photometric data from the TESS and CHEOPS space missions. The two planets have orbital periods near a golden-mean ratio and nearly identical radii and masses, with both exhibiting very low bulk densities that place them among the sparse population of planets in the so-called Neptunian savanna. Planetary masses were derived from anti-correlated transit-timing variations rather than radial velocities, yielding a photodynamical solution consistent with a dynamically cold, compact multi-planet architecture. A broader survey of known savanna systems reveals that multi-planet configurations are common in this regime, with most pairs having period ratios below three, pointing toward smooth disk-driven migration rather than disruptive high-eccentricity tidal pathways as the dominant formation route. The system's brightness and its hosting of two co-evolved, nearly twin planets also make it an attractive candidate for comparative atmospheric studies.
items.citation: J. M. Almenara, A. Castro-González, R. Mardling et al.. HD 148797: A bright F-type star with two moderate-period low-density sub-Jovian planets. Compact multi-planet architectures are common in the Neptunian savanna. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Evolution of Accuracy and Visual-Cognitive Errors in a Decade of Vision-Language AI Models
items.summary: This paper investigated how vision-language models have progressed over a decade in their ability to describe complex social scenes, addressing gaps in prior evaluations that relied on simple imagery and limited human benchmarks. The researchers introduced a new dataset of images depicting complex social interactions and behaviors, then systematically evaluated nine models spanning from early approaches to modern multimodal large language models, alongside human descriptions, using a gold-standard accuracy measure and a taxonomy of five visual-cognitive error types. They found that earlier models performed substantially worse than even the lowest-ranked human describers on complex scenes, while modern multimodal large language models reached accuracy levels comparable to the highest-ranked human descriptions. Notably, modern models have largely closed the performance gap between simple and complex scene description, and have nearly eliminated most error categories, with the exception of spatial dependence errors reflecting differences in which image regions models versus humans attend to. The analysis also revealed that object detection, recognition, and hallucination errors carry the greatest impact on overall scene description accuracy.
items.citation: Shravan Murlidaran, Miguel P. Eckstein. Evolution of Accuracy and Visual-Cognitive Errors in a Decade of Vision-Language AI Models. arXiv. 2026

items.title: VEXAIoT: Autonomous IoT Vulnerability EXploitation using AI Agents
items.summary: This paper investigated whether large language model-based agents could autonomously discover and exploit vulnerabilities in Internet of Things systems, addressing the lack of scalable security testing approaches for constrained IoT devices. The researchers developed a multi-agent framework called VEXAIoT that pairs a vulnerability detection agent with an attack execution agent, enabling automated reconnaissance, attack planning, and exploit execution using offensive security tools. The system was evaluated across ten attack scenarios drawn from OWASP IoT vulnerability categories within two established testbed environments, IoTGoat and Metasploitable. Results demonstrated very high overall attack success rates with low computational overhead, with most attacks completing in under two minutes. The findings suggest that LLM-driven multi-agent frameworks hold considerable promise for automating IoT vulnerability assessment and offensive security workflows in controlled settings.
items.citation: Katherine Swinea, Kshitiz Aryal, Lopamudra Praharaj et al.. VEXAIoT: Autonomous IoT Vulnerability EXploitation using AI Agents. arXiv. 2026
